1 CONC t two of his nephews, Zach and Philip Hagedorn to town to h

1 CONC ŮCbŮDBiographical Note
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDJacob Loeb was born in Reichshoffen in the Alsace regio
1 CONC n of France. He emigrated to the United States after the Un
1 CONC ited States Civil War, having spent the war years in Mexico
1 CONC . He married Mary Gross on 2 January 1866 and settled in Ca
1 CONC nton, Miss. Jacob Loeb and Mary Gross had ten children, man
1 CONC y of whom settled in Shreveport, La. As of the printing o
1 CONC f the family history in June 1998, there were more than 12
1 CONC 0 living descendents of Jacob Loeb and Mary Gross. Notabl
1 CONC e descendants include brothers Samuel Gross Wiener and Will
1 CONC iam Wiener, architects who were among the earliest proponen
1 CONC ts of the International Style of architecture in America, a
1 CONC nd Jacques Wiener, Jr., a judge on the United States Fift
1 CONC h District Court of Appeals.

3 CONC clock last night at his residence, 221 Clayton Street, afte
3 CONC r an illness of only a few days. Mr. Loeb was taken suddenl
3 CONC y ill last Saturday and later pneumonia symptoms were recog
3 CONC nized.

3 CONC dent of Montgomery for more than forty years. He was connec
3 CONC ted with the Winter-Loeb Company, a large wholesale firm o
3 CONC f this city.
3 CONT      Surviving relatives are his widow, two daughters, Mis
3 CONC s Carrie Loeb and Miss Adele Loeb, and one son, Ralph Loeb
3 CONC , all of them residents of Montgomery. Funeral arrangement
3 CONC s will be announced later.

1 NOTE @NI252@
0 @NI252@ NOTE
1 CONC Visited the Family History Library in Salt Lake City June 3
1 CONC 0-July 2, 2003.  Prior to leaving home, I had found Amanda'
1 CONC s birth notation in the IGI file...her given name was show
1 CONC n as "Maria".
1 CONT
1 CONT In the FHL catalog, I found "Birth returns, 1860-1903,file
1 CONC d by the attending physician, midwife or hospital for the c
1 CONC ity of Philadelphia."  Narrowed Amanda down to one microfil
1 CONC m , and spent a good deal of time perusing it,
1 CONT
1 CONT Amanda's was (hand-)written as "Mina".  Probably the way th
1 CONC e midwife ( whose first language was probably not English
1 CONC ) heard her name.  Then, in another hand-written microfil
1 CONC m of "Birth authentications, 1867-1891, for births occurrin
1 CONC g 1829-1882, Philadelphia" the name was less legible...bu
1 CONC t had become "Minna", "Minni", or "Maria".
1 CONT
1 CONT Never did find the actual birth certificate copy (it woul
1 CONC d be fascinating to know if her name had been corrected the
1 CONC re), but at least the "Maria" mystery seems to be solved!
1 CONT
1 CONT                                              --------------
1 CONC --------------
1 CONT
1 CONT Amanda's brother-in-law, Samuel Snellenburg, witnessed he
1 CONC r marriage certificate.
1 CONT
1 CONT Samuel S. Snellenburg was a partner, with Joseph J. Snellen
1 CONC burg, Nathan N.  Snellenburg, and Samuel L. Bloch, in "N. S
1 CONC nellenburg & Co., clothiers", located at 1140 Market Stree
1 CONC t and 601 S. 5th street, in Philadelphia.   Samuel S. Snell
1 CONC enburg lived at 534 Spruce Street; Nathan lived at 905 Fran
1 CONC klin, and Joseph lived at 706 N. 5th Street...all in Philad
1 CONC elphia.  This store later became "Snellenburgs"...a promine
1 CONC nt Philadelphia department store in the mid-20th century.
1 CONT
1 CONT There was also a Meyer Snellenburg, grocer, and Morris Snel
1 CONC lenburg, clerk  (father and son?),  both of whom lived at 2
1 CONC 10 Bainbridge in Philadelphia.
1 CONT
1 CONT Groom name Rosenbaum
1 CONT Bride name Amanda Frohsin
1 CONT Marriage year 1888
1 CONT Section or license number 23077 Image number 00309 Digita
1 CONC l GS number 4140424

1 CONC From March 14, 2002 email from Ralph Loeb, Jr. (Adele Loe
1 CONC b Frohsin's nephew):
1 CONT
1 CONT "When Adele died, Ellen and I went to Pikesville to help Jo
1 CONC e- one of the main things that we did was help get Adele'
1 CONC s clothes to Goodwill, and help clean out the attic. Anothe
1 CONC r couple, Joe and Adele's best friends were there also to h
1 CONC elp. In the attic we found Louella's ashes in a plain cardb
1 CONC oard box. Joe had just left them up there and had done noth
1 CONC ing with them. While Joe was out of the house on business
1 CONC , the four of us took the box to the back of the back yard
1 CONC . We said the Kaddish, and then dumped the ashes. We all th
1 CONC ought it very ironic that Louella would wind up that way.
1 CONT
1 CONT You asked how Louella was able to afford a fancy apartmen
1 CONC t in New York at the time that Joe and Adele married. I hav
1 CONC e often wondered that too, but haven't a clue."
1 CONT
1 CONT (Also)..."Louella's second husband's name was George.  I th
1 CONC ink that Joe was in business with George, but my memory tel
1 CONC ls me that business went bankrupt - I think it was a clothi
1 CONC ng store.  Anyhow, according to what I can recall, they nev
1 CONC er repaid the debts and after Joe died his attorney told u
1 CONC s that there might be a claim against the estate.  Since th
1 CONC e bankruptcy took place so long ago, there was never a clai
1 CONC m."
1 CONT

1 CONC .Louella married George F. SULTZBACH at the Biltmore Hote
1 CONC l in New York City on August 24, 1918.  Announcement phot
1 CONC o shown in "Pictures".

2 TITL Rosenbaum Building
2 NOTE 310 E Main Street, Kalamazoo, Michigan 49007
3 CONT The Rosenbaum Block
3 CONT When Samuel Rosenbaum's Kalamazoo Pant Company outgrew it
3 CONC s quarters in the William A. Doyle Building Rosenbaum had t
3 CONC his six story steel and concrete structure erected just dow
3 CONC n the street. The company moved into the block in 1907. It
3 CONC s successor, Redwood and Ross, remained here until 1998. Th
3 CONC e building exhibits the simple modern style that had develo
3 CONC ped in the early years of the 20th century. It was one of t
3 CONC he first buildings in Kalamazoo to exceed 4 stories
2 _SCBK Y
2 _TYPE PHOTO
1 OBJE
2 FORM jpg
2 FILE C:\Users\Sid\Pictures\ORIGINAL Kalamazoo Pant Co. building.jpg
2 TITL ORIGINAL Rosenbaum Building
2 NOTE Rosenbaum Block: 1906-07, 300 E. Michigan Ave.
3 CONT The William A. Doyle Building
3 CONT The William A. Doyle Building is a sharp contrast to the mo
3 CONC dern Ihling Building just next door. The Doyle looks to th
3 CONC e past for its inspiration. It bears strong Romanesque styl
3 CONC ing with Queen Anne details. The facade features rough ston
3 CONC e and face brick. Large arched windows add to the Romanesqu
3 CONC e flavor.
3 CONT
3 CONT The structure originally housed Samuel Rosenbaum's Kalamazo
3 CONC o Pant Company until it moved to its own quarters in the Ro
3 CONC senbaum Building. The building then went through a period o
3 CONC f multiuse that included a stint as the Kalamazoo Hotel
3 CONC , a 75 room establishment. The building has been restored a
3 CONC s an office building.

1 NOTE @NI287@
0 @NI287@ NOTE
1 CONC Known to my mother and her siblings as "Gropa".
1 CONT
1 CONT He was a "free thinker", and a disciple of Robert Ingerso
1 CONC l  (the famous agnostic) and Elbert Hubbard (Roycrofters, e
1 CONC tc.).
1 CONT
1 CONT Samuel emigrated to America because of religious difference
1 CONC s with his strict Orthodox father, Sussman (and NOT for eco
1 CONC nomic or military reasons.).
1 CONT
1 CONT He sailed from Bremen, Germany, on the SS Indiana, and arri
1 CONC ved in the Port of New York on September 23, 1857, bound fo
1 CONC r Michigan.  (Ship records erroneously show his age at th
1 CONC e time as "30"...in fact, he was 19 years old.
1 CONT
1 CONT He settled in Three Rivers, and didn't arrive in Kalamazo
1 CONC o until after the Civil War.
1 CONT
1 CONT He came to Kalamazoo in 1867 because his older brother, Sim
1 CONC on, was already established there.
1 CONT
1 CONT He joined the Unitarian church in Kalamazoo in  1858 and wa
1 CONC s a life member although he much later affiliated with th
1 CONC e Reformed Jewish Congregation for social reasons.
1 CONT
1 CONT He was naturalized as an American citizen in Kalamazoo on F
1 CONC riday, January 26, 1866.
1 CONT
1 CONT In early 1866, he returned to Germany with his wife, Henrie
1 CONC tta and his son, Louis; they returned, along with daughte
1 CONC r Caroline who was born while her parents were in Germany
1 CONC , on the SS America, sailing again from Bremen, and arrivin
1 CONC g at the port of New York on October 15, 1866.
1 CONT
1 CONT Samuel was a peddler for ten years, and was given a "grubst
1 CONC ake" by either Simon or Mannes Israel to open a store in Th
1 CONC ree Rivers.  When he had accumulated sufficient capital, h
1 CONC e started the Henrietta Skirt Company (named after his wife
1 CONC , Henrietta Cohn ) -- later to become the Kalamazoo Pant Co.
1 CONT
1 CONT A story is told about a "George HANDLEMAN" (called "Hanselm
1 CONC an" by all of the family), to whom Samuel loaned the mone
1 CONC y to begin the Handleman Candy Co.  Consequently, every Ros
1 CONC enbaum family had a five pound box of candy delivered to th
1 CONC em each Christmas. Port Of New York
1 CONT

1 CONT Samuel Rosenbaum came to Kalamazoo in 1867 after runnin
1 CONC g a dry-goods store in Three Rivers since 1857. He went int
1 CONC o business for himself, eventually organizing the Kalamazo
1 CONC o Pant and Overall Co. The Rosenbaum Building still stand
1 CONC s on East Michigan Avenue at Edwards Street.

1 CONT From:ŮCbŮD
1 CONT MICHIGAN JEWISH HISTORY
1 CONT Volume 44 Fall 04 Tishrei 5765
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDTHE JOURNAL OF THE JEWISH HISTORICAL SOCIETY OF MICHIGAN

1 CONT FEATURED ARTICLES
1 CONT  Kalamazoo's Jewish Community: An Overview of the First 7
1 CONC 5 Years ŮCiŮD by Tom Dietz..................................
1 CONC . ŮCbŮDŮC/iŮD23ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT
1 CONT Pgs. 23-24:
1 CONT Jeans and Skirts: Samuel Rosenbaum
1 CONT Samuel Rosenbaum presents a similar success story. He was b
1 CONC orn in Pyrmont, Germany in 1838, immigrating to the Unite
1 CONC d States in 1858. He first settled in Three Rivers, Michiga
1 CONC n, a small town some 25 miles south of Kalamazoo, where h
1 CONC e opened a dry goods store. For nine years, he ran a succes
1 CONC sful business there. His customers, mostly farmers, often p
1 CONC aid him with grain and produce that he then transported t
1 CONC o Kalamazoo every Thursday.
1 CONT
1 CONT After nine years, Rosenbaum, tired of the hauling, decide
1 CONC d Kalamazoo offered better opportunities. He sold his Thre
1 CONC e Rivers store for $10,000. In Kalamazoo, he became a trave
1 CONC ling salesman, carrying his dry goods and notions to the su
1 CONC rrounding farms by wagon. Several years later, he noticed t
1 CONC hat denim overalls were his most popular item. He bought so
1 CONC me sewing machines, hired a few skilled seamstresses, and t
1 CONC he Kalamazoo Overall Company was born.
1 CONT
1 CONT By the mid-1880s, Rosenbaum had gone full time into manufac
1 CONC turing. The quality and modest cost of his goods extended h
1 CONC is sales beyond the local market. His sons joined the firm
1 CONC , and the company added a line of skirts produced by a subs
1 CONC idiary, the Henrietta Skirt Company, named after Samuel's w
1 CONC ife. In 1903, shortly before his death, the firm incorporat
1 CONC ed as Sam Rosenbaum and Sons Co. Samuel Rosenbaum died in l
1 CONC ate 1903, but the company continued to prosper. In 1907, i
1 CONC t moved into a six-story factory building that still stand
1 CONC s on the southeast corner of East Michigan Avenue and Edwar
1 CONC ds Street. Over the years, the company reorganized, merge
1 CONC d and changed product lines. Known today as Kazoo, Inc., i
1 CONC t manufactures industrial and business uniforms as part o
1 CONC f the Edwards Garment Company

1 CONC Sussman Rosenbaum was a very well-to-do velvet and satee
1 CONC n  merchant. His LARGE house became a pension after if pass
1 CONC ed from the family --- it was known as Haus Rosenbaum.
1 CONT
1 CONT He was very orthodox and very strict re law of Kosher and o
1 CONC ther restrictions and observances.
1 CONT
1 CONT Only his two sons, Simon and Samuel, are known, although h
1 CONC e reportedly came from a "large family".
1 CONT
1 CONT His Hebrew name may have been Moses.  A grave marker with t
1 CONC his name and these birth/death dates was found in the Pyrmo
1 CONC nt Jewish Cemetery by Ned Rosenbaum...he said there was a H
1 CONC ebrew inscription on the other side of the marker, but he c
1 CONC ouldn't reach it.  But the stone had been vandalized, and h
1 CONC e surmised (as the dates would have been correct for Sussma
1 CONC n) that the stone marked the head of the Bad Pyrmont clan'
1 CONC s final resting place.

1 CONC The story is that she was Napoleon's translator during hi
1 CONC s Austrian campaign.  He gave her a large emerald, which "M
1 CONC ike"  (Sylvan) claimed in 1986 to have "seen before Aunt Ca
1 CONC rrie lost it.  She was a legatee in his will.  "Groma" [Car
1 CONC oline's daughter-in-law]  ultimately got a few thousand."
1 CONT
1 CONT Caroline's birth and death dates are unknown.  However, th
1 CONC e Napoleonic Austrian campaign was in 1809.  Her sons wer
1 CONC e born in 1832 and 1840. Assuming she had the first one a
1 CONC s late as age 40, she would have been only about 17 in 1809
1 CONC .  So, the "folklore" doesn't seem to ring true.

1 CONT
1 CONT On Chicago's South Side 60 years ago Jacob Portis proved be
1 CONC tter at raising a family than at selling real estate: his e
1 CONC ight boys had to sell newspapers. Milton Portis, the eldes
1 CONC t (now 62), worked his way through medical school. The tw
1 CONC o youngest, Bernard and Sidney (now 42 and 45), were put th
1 CONC rough by their older brothers. Three others, Isadore, Arnol
1 CONC d and Theodore, went to work for a hat firm and in 1914 the
1 CONC y and the remaining two brothers, Lyon and ŮCbŮDHenry,ŮC/bŮD se
1 CONC t up Portis Brothers Hat Co. They had $23,000 to start with
1 CONC , half borrowed from Dr. Milton, half from, others.
1 CONT
1 CONT Came the War. First their business dwindled, then they go
1 CONC t established. Today Portis Brothers makes about 1,000,00
1 CONC 0 hats a year, ranks about tenth in the industry. Last wee
1 CONC k at its 25th annual meeting Treasurer Henry Portis announc
1 CONC ed that its capital stood at $400,000, its surplus at $100,
1 CONC 000, its 1938 net at $39,000. Its profits do not make Porti
1 CONC s Brothers Hat Co. big business, but its management is uniq
1 CONC ue.
1 CONT
1 CONT Soon after the five Portis boys formed their firm, they dec
1 CONC ided to run the company on the basis of all for one, one fo
1 CONC r all. For 25 years, they have drawn identical salaries. Ea
1 CONC ch took a title for the sake of convention, but each has a
1 CONC n equal voice in the management. Today they are as indistin
1 CONC guishable as five hats on a closet shelf. All are stocky, a
1 CONC ll about five-feet-ten.
1 CONT
1 CONT Not only the five hatters but their three doctor brothers
1 CONC , 82-year-old mother and three sisters live within eight bl
1 CONC ocks of each other. Six of the Portis clan drive Buicks. Al
1 CONC l have bridge and golf as hobbies. The four who work in th
1 CONC e Chicago plant drive there together, arriving sharply at 8
1 CONC :15. Seven of the brothers have two children apiece. One ha
1 CONC s three. In 25 years they have hardly ever disagreed. Say
1 CONC s Henry proudly: "We put business ahead of profits and it w
1 CONC orked."

1 CONC ŮCbŮDRose Beal, 107, Traveled Around Globe 4 Times
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDJuly 13, 2002
1 CONT
1 CONT Her life spanned three centuries and bore witness to the ri
1 CONC se of technology, from the advent of electricity to the ag
1 CONC e of computers. She outlived two husbands and traveled th
1 CONC e world by all manner of transport.
1 CONT
1 CONT After 107 years on Earth, Rose Beal died Thursday at Homewo
1 CONC od Assisted Living Facility in Coconut Creek. Her son Edwar
1 CONC d Sedlis said Mrs. Beal died peacefully after complication
1 CONC s from a stroke.
1 CONT
1 CONT "We regret her passing but rejoice in her life," he said.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mrs. Beal's life was indeed rich. The Boston native marrie
1 CONC d a dress manufacturer and raised two sons in the upscale B
1 CONC oston suburb of Brookline. Her husband, William Sedlis, wa
1 CONC s a wealthy philanthropist who helped found Brandeis Univer
1 CONC sity; Mrs. Beal helped found the Brandeis Women organizatio
1 CONC n in 1948, a group that today has numerous chapters.
1 CONT
1 CONT An ardent Zionist, Mrs. Beal and four women friends toure
1 CONC d Palestine in 1935 and witnessed the arrival of European J
1 CONC ews fleeing Nazi persecution. She later sent her two sons o
1 CONC ff to World War II. Both survived, but her husband died o
1 CONC f a heart attack in 1943.
1 CONT
1 CONT Through it all, Mrs. Beal maintained an unflagging optimism
1 CONC . "Life is what you make it," she once said. "If you make i
1 CONC t with a smile, then you're happy. If you're happy, then yo
1 CONC u smile. It's a circle."
1 CONT
1 CONT A witness to history, Mrs. Beal traveled around the world f
1 CONC our times and once spent four months aboard a Chinese freig
1 CONC hter. "She did everything," Edward Sedlis said. "You coul
1 CONC d never describe her as a little old lady, that was one thi
1 CONC ng she was not."
1 CONT
1 CONT Mrs. Beal lost her second husband, Joseph Beal, in the mid-
1 CONC 1970s and moved from Boston to South Florida to be close t
1 CONC o her sons. In her later years she enjoyed playing rummy cu
1 CONC e, a game of tiles that's a combination of mah-jongg and gi
1 CONC n rummy. Despite her years, she remained active and alert.
1 CONT
1 CONT "She was cogent and aware and alive," Sedlis said.
1 CONT
1 CONT Action kept Mrs. Beal young. "I just loved to do things, an
1 CONC d I did them," Mrs. Beal once told a reporter in what coul
1 CONC d serve as her epitaph. "All things being equal, I can sa
1 CONC y that I've had a very good life."
1 CONT
1 CONT Besides Sedlis, of Coconut Creek, Mrs. Beal is survived b
1 CONC y a second son, Herbert, of Boca Raton.
1 CONT
1 CONT A memorial service will be held at 10 a.m. Sunday at Rivers
1 CONC ide Menorah Chapel, 6701 W. Commercial Blvd., Tamarac. Th
1 CONC e family asks that in lieu of flowers, donations be made t
1 CONC o the person's charity of choice.

1 CONC Emigrated from Pyrmont, Waldeck Germany (near Hamburg)to Ka
1 CONC lamazoo, Michigan, in 1855 to work for his cousin,  Manne
1 CONC s Israel, in M. Israel Dry Goods...a large  variety store t
1 CONC hat Mannes Israel owned and operated there.   The store lat
1 CONC er became Rosenbaum & Speyer.  The "Rosenbaum" was Simon.
1 CONT It is not known what brought Mannes Israel to Kalamazoo i
1 CONC n 1844(?), but he was definitely an EARLY settler...,"the f
1 CONC irst Jew to arrive in Kalamazoo".
1 CONT Mannes was much admired and loved by the Rosenbaums (a "fir
1 CONC st cousin" of Simon and Samuel, ,although precise nature o
1 CONC f the relationship is not established.)
1 CONT Simon married Rieckchen Romberg of Berlin, Prussia, and ha
1 CONC d one daughter, Emma.
1 CONT
1 CONT Database: Full Context of Michigan Biographies, 1878
1 CONT American Biographical History of Eminent and Self-Made Me
1 CONC n with Portrait Illustrations on Steel, Volumes I-II
1 CONT
1 CONT Name: Simon Rosenbaum Dry-Goods Merchant, Kalamazoo, Michig
1 CONC an, is a son of Susman and Caroline (Bermann) Rosenbaum. H
1 CONC e was born in Pyrmont, Waldeck, Germany, April 13, 1832. Hi
1 CONC s early educational advantages were confined to the commo
1 CONC n schools of his native place. After leaving school, wher
1 CONC e he was distinguished for his rapid progress in learning
1 CONC , he continued his self-education by careful study of Germa
1 CONC n literature, devoting his attention to the works of its mo
1 CONC st eminent authors, with an interest bordering on enthusias
1 CONC m. At the age of fourteen, he entered the dry-goods store o
1 CONC f his father, in which he continued, as clerk, for nine yea
1 CONC rs; and, by his untiring industry, contributed greatly to i
1 CONC ts financial success. At the end of this time, he receive
1 CONC d from a friend an urgent invitation to come to America, wh
1 CONC ere be arrived in 1855, and found a situation awaiting hi
1 CONC m in the dry-goods store of Mr. Israel, in Kalamazoo, Michi
1 CONC gan. Here, by his business talent, energy, and fidelity, h
1 CONC e soon won for himself the confidence and friendship of hi
1 CONC s employer. In 1865 he was admitted as a partner; and, at t
1 CONC he death of Mr. Israel, in 1868, took the entire charge o
1 CONC f the business. He has conducted it ever since, giving, b
1 CONC y his untiring zeal and sterling integrity, full satisfacti
1 CONC on to all. He is noted for the salutary influence he exert
1 CONC s upon the young men in his employment. He is frugal in hi
1 CONC s habits, strict towards himself, but lenient in his judgme
1 CONC nt of others. He has been several times to Europe, and ha
1 CONC s visited all the important cities on this continent. He wo
1 CONC rships with the Israelites, but evinces no prejudice toward
1 CONC s those of a different faith. Since he became a citizen o
1 CONC f the United States, he has always voted with the Republica
1 CONC n party. He married, February 27, 1865, Rieckchen Romberg
1 CONC , a lady of superior intellectual culture, having receive
1 CONC d her education in the most celebrated schools of Berlin, P
1 CONC russia. They have one child,--a daughter. Mr. Rosenbaum i
1 CONC s unassuming in his manners, and shrinks from parade of an
1 CONC y kind. The late financial depression has left him unharmed
1 CONC , and he is now in a more prosperous condition than ever be
1 CONC fore.
1 CONT

1 CONT
1 CONT Death of Mrs. Rosenbaum
1 CONT Mrs. Simon Rosenbaum, died at the Belvidere house, New Yor
1 CONC k City, Saturday evening, of Bright's disease.  The remain
1 CONC s will arrive in Kalamazoo this afternoon, at 2:36, on th
1 CONC e day express.  Carriages will be in waiting at the depot t
1 CONC o convey friends to the cemetery.  Mr. Lipmansohn will offi
1 CONC ciate at the grave.
1 CONT The deceased was a lady very highly esteemed by all who mad
1 CONC e her acquaintance.  She was a very charitable and sympathe
1 CONC tic person.  It will be remembered that she lost a belove
1 CONC d daughter about one year ago, and immediately wento int
1 CONC o a decline, although under the best medical treatment tha
1 CONC t could be procured in New York.

3 CONT Heart Attack Ends Life of G. ROSENBAUM - Illness, at Firs
3 CONC t Thought Inconsequential, Proves Fatal - Ill But Few Hour
3 CONC s - Former Police and Fire Commissioner Was Well-Known Manu
3 CONC facturer; Goddie ROSENBAUM, treasurer of the Samuel ROSENBA
3 CONC UM and Sons Company of this city, a lifelong resident of Ka
3 CONC lamazoo, died suddenly at his home, 305 West Vine street a
3 CONC t 1:30 Friday morning from a heart. He was 62 years of age
3 CONC . Thursday Mr. ROSENBAUM appeared to be in his usual good h
3 CONC ealth until late in the afternoon when he complained of no
3 CONC t feeling so well and left his office for his home, where h
3 CONC e summoned a physician. His condition was not considered se
3 CONC rious but was thought to be an attack of indigestion, to wh
3 CONC ich he was subject. He continued to grow worse until the en
3 CONC d. Seldom Absent From Desk; Mr. ROSENBAUM, who was one of K
3 CONC alamazoo's best know citizens, was an indefatigable worke
3 CONC r in whatever was undertaken by his, not alone in the compa
3 CONC ny with which he had been affiliated since he was 15 year
3 CONC s of age, but in the civic and social positions he had occu
3 CONC pied. This quality was only exceeded by his intense loyalt
3 CONC y to his friends. When the Kalamazoo Pant Company, as the c
3 CONC oncern is best known to people of this city, was incorporat
3 CONC ed in 1902, by Samuel ROSENBAUM and his three sons, Louis
3 CONC , Edward S., and Goddie, the latter was made treasurer, a
3 CONC n office he held until the time of his death. During the 2
3 CONC 0 years intervening he was seldom absent from his desk an
3 CONC d had been a big factor in the development of the concern w
3 CONC hich had experienced a remarkable growth. On Safety Commiss
3 CONC ion; For six years Mr. ROSENBAUM was a member of the polic
3 CONC e and fire commission of this city. When the appointment t
3 CONC o that body was first suggested to him by the late Frank H
3 CONC . Milham in 1907 he declined with the excuse that he was to
3 CONC o busy with his own affairs. Later he relented and receive
3 CONC d similar appointments under the administrations of Charle
3 CONC s H. Farrell, Charles B. Hays and A. B. Connable. While ser
3 CONC ving on the commission he spent many evenings at the polic
3 CONC e and fire stations mingling with the men of the two depart
3 CONC ments and in that manner getting first hand knowledge of wh
3 CONC at was needed to build up the organizations to the highes
3 CONC t degree of efficiency. He became the friend and confidan
3 CONC t of every police officer and fireman he encountered and wa
3 CONC s ready to go to the front for them in encounters with th
3 CONC e old city councils, when necessary. It was during the tim
3 CONC e that Mr. ROSENBAUM was a member of the commission that th
3 CONC e present police station was built and that Charles Russel
3 CONC l present fire chief, received his appointment. Prominent i
3 CONC n Elks Lodge; On Feb. 17, 1897 Mr. ROSENBAUM became a membe
3 CONC r of the Kalamazoo lodge of Elks. During the 35 years sinc
3 CONC e his initiation no one has given more time and attention t
3 CONC o the upbuilding of that organization. It was the two broth
3 CONC ers, Louis and Goddie, who did much to keep the lodge aliv
3 CONC e during its early days. Mr. ROSENBAUM was keenly intereste
3 CONC d in bowling. He had served as president of the Michigan St
3 CONC ate Association the Michigan Elks Association and a directo
3 CONC r of the International Bowling Association. Backed Basebal
3 CONC l Club; When Kalamazoo was represented in the old Souther
3 CONC n Michigan Association league. Mr. ROSENBAUM, the late J. W
3 CONC . Ryder, Charles A. Blaney, and Edgar F. Raseman were the b
3 CONC ackers of the team. The club lost money but never once di
3 CONC d the enthusiasm of Mr. ROSENBAUM and his associates dwindl
3 CONC e. They put victory on the diamond ahead of personal gain a
3 CONC nd pocketed a loss each season without complaint. The decea
3 CONC sed for several years was at the head of the Kalamazoo Amus
3 CONC ement Company which operated the Fuller, Orpheum, and Elit
3 CONC e theatres at the time. He, also was one of the builders o
3 CONC f the Empress theatre in Grand Rapids. He took a keen inter
3 CONC est in all theatrical matters. Mr. ROSENBAUM also was a dir
3 CONC ector of the Home Saving bank, a Mason and Shriner, membe
3 CONC r of the Rotary club, the Kalamazoo County club and the Gul
3 CONC l Lake Country club. In all these organizations he took a k
3 CONC een interest. Friend of Employes; Out side the family circl
3 CONC e it will be in the factory of the plant company that Mr. R
3 CONC OSENBAUM will be missed most of all. He was a true friend t
3 CONC o every employe in the place. They came to him with their t
3 CONC roubles and Goddie, as he was addressed by those in his emp
3 CONC loy, did his best to smooth over their difficulties. Goddi
3 CONC e ROSENBAUM was the son of Samuel and Henrietta ROSENBAUM
3 CONC . The father, a pioneer musician came to the city in 1857 a
3 CONC nd secured employment as a clerk in one of the downtown sto
3 CONC res. Later the father went in business for himself on wha
3 CONC t was then Main street. The present company was started i
3 CONC n 1867. The father, president of the concern, died in Janua
3 CONC ry, 1903 and the mother's death occurred in July 1921. Gre
3 CONC w Up With Business; The family for years lived in Portage s
3 CONC treet, near the present location of old Borgess. It was the
3 CONC re that Goddie and the other children were born. Goddie att
3 CONC ended the public schools of this city until old enough to e
3 CONC nter the employ of his father and grow up with the business
3 CONC . Mr. ROSENBAUM was married in Sept. 1893 to Miss Maud POWE
3 CONC RS, of this city, who survives him. There are two children
3 CONC , Samuel ROSENBAUM, who is on his was back from Tulsa, Okla
3 CONC ., and Mrs. Anita R. JUDSON, who resides at home. The survi
3 CONC ving brothers and sisters are Louis and Edwin S., presiden
3 CONC t and vice-president of the Kalamazoo Pant Company, Menz RO
3 CONC SENBAUM, Chicago, Mrs. Ludwig ROSENBAUM, Kalamazoo, and Mrs
3 CONC . Blanch ROSENBAUM, Hanover, Germany. Also there is a grand
3 CONC child, Robert Edwards JUDSON. Services will be held at 2 Su
3 CONC nday afternoon at the Harrington funeral chapel, West Sout
3 CONC h Street. Rabbi Philip Waterman, Grand Rapids will officiat
3 CONC e. Burial in the Jewish Mountain Home cemetery. source: Kal
3 CONC amazoo Gazette Friday June 10, 1932 (submitted by a volunte
3 CONC er, no family connection)

1 CONC At age 12 (1872), ran away from home because of a difficul
1 CONC t father.  He wandered from town to town, was an excellen
1 CONC t singer, so he got food and lodging as a boy Cantor; he wa
1 CONC s also a Talmudic scholar.  In about 1875, he met Fannie, h
1 CONC is future wife, who was about ten years old.  Simon and Fan
1 CONC nie's father, Morris Kaplan (later Copland) lived first i
1 CONC n Syracuse, New York; later, in Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania
1 CONC , where Simon and Fannie were  married in 1882 -- Rabbi Cop
1 CONC land presiding.
1 CONT

1 CONC o September 9, 1904)   Page 94

1 CONC egation Adas Jeshurun, Louisville, Ky.  Born September 29
1 CONC , 1860, in Poland.  Son of Joseph Salinger, M.D. (sic).  We
1 CONC stern University of Pennsylvania, 1897.  Was Rabbi in Mehls
1 CONC ack, Germany, for one year; London, Eng., for one year; Wil
1 CONC kes-Barre, Pa.; Cleveland, Ohio; and Pittsburg, Pa., for si

1 CONT Herbert Hart Abrams, life resident of Indianapolis, passe
1 CONC d away November 18, 2008. Herb was born April 14, 1922 to B
1 CONC irdie and Sam Abrams. He attended IPS #66 and graduated fro
1 CONC m Shortridge High School. He was an accomplished musician-p
1 CONC laying both clarinet and saxophone--and was in the Shortrid
1 CONC ge Band and the Earl Breech Dance Band. Herbert served in t
1 CONC he U S Army during World War II at Fort Riley, Kansas, an
1 CONC d in New Guinea. He was president and CEO of Best Roofing a
1 CONC nd Remodeling for over 50 years, retiring 1 year ago. Survi
1 CONC vors include; his loving wife, Mary Alboher Abrams; sons, D
1 CONC r. Stephen Abrams, Scott, and William; brother, Dr. Jerom
1 CONC e Abrams; cousin, Dorothy Benjamin, of Bloomfield Hills, Mi
1 CONC chigan. Funeral services will be held on Friday, November 2
1 CONC 1, 2008 at 2:00 pm at Aaron-Ruben-Nelson Mortuary. Burial w
1 CONC ill follow in Indianapolis Hebrew Cemetery South. Memoria
1 CONC l contributions may be made to the Birdie Abrams Memorial F
1 CONC und, Jewish Community Center; or Multiple Myeloma Fund, I.U
1 CONC . Medical Center, c/o Dr. Abanour. Arrangements entrusted t
1 CONC o Aaron-Ruben-Nelson Mortuary. Online condolences may be se
1 CONC nt to www.arnmortuary.com

1 CONT NEW YORK TIMES January 29, 2010 ŮCbŮDJ. D. Salinger, Literar
1 CONC y Recluse, Dies at 91 ŮC/bŮDBy CHARLES McGRATH J. D. Salinger
1 CONC , who was thought at one time to be the most important Amer
1 CONC ican writer to emerge since World War II but who then turne
1 CONC d his back on success and adulation, becoming the Garbo o
1 CONC f letters, famous for not wanting to be famous, died on Wed
1 CONC nesday at his home in Cornish, N.H., where he had lived i
1 CONC n seclusion for more than 50 years. He was 91. Mr. Salinger
1 CONC 's literary representative, Harold Ober Associates, announc
1 CONC ed the death, saying it was of natural causes. "Despite hav
1 CONC ing broken his hip in May," the agency said, "his health ha
1 CONC d been excellent until a rather sudden decline after the ne
1 CONC w year. He was not in any pain before or at the time of hi
1 CONC s death." Mr. Salinger's literary reputation rests on a sle
1 CONC nder but enormously influential body of published work: th
1 CONC e novel "The Catcher in the Rye," the collection "Nine Stor
1 CONC ies" and two compilations, each with two long stories abou
1 CONC t the fictional Glass family: "Franny and Zooey" and "Rais
1 CONC e High the Roof Beam, Carpenters and Seymour: An Introducti
1 CONC on." "Catcher" was published in 1951, and its very first se
1 CONC ntence, distantly echoing ŮCuŮDMark Twain ŮC/uŮD, struck a bras
1 CONC h new note in American literature: "If you really want to h
1 CONC ear about it, the first thing you'll probably want to kno

1 CONC d me, and all that ŮCuŮDDavid Copperfield ŮC/uŮD kind of crap
1 CONC , but I don't feel like going into it, if you want to kno
1 CONC w the truth." Though not everyone, teachers and librarian

1 CONC e an almost immediate best seller, and its narrator and mai

1 CONC om prep school, became America's best-known literary truan
1 CONC t since Huckleberry Finn. With its cynical, slangy vernacul

1 CONC rica and quickly attained cult status, especially among th

1 CONC rmit. The novel's allure persists to this day, even if som

1 CONC ck. ŮCuŮDMark David Chapman ŮC/uŮD, who killed ŮCuŮDJohn Lennon ŮC

1 CONC n anyone else, has not turned his back on the times but, in

1 CONC f significance is going on today between self and culture.
1 CONC " Many critics were more admiring of "Nine Stories," whic
1 CONC h came out in 1953 and helped shape writers like Mr. Roth

1 CONC n quality they have, the way they don't snap shut." Mr. Sal
1 CONC inger also perfected the great trick of literary irony - o
1 CONC f validating what you mean by saying less than, or even th

1 CONC /uŮD in The New York Times in 1963, "Rarely if ever in liter

1 CONC t," a movie so bad that Mr. Salinger was never tempted to s
1 CONC ell film rights again. Befriended, Then Betrayed In the fal
1 CONC l of 1953 he befriended some local teenagers and allowed on
1 CONC e of them to interview him for what he assumed would be a
1 CONC n article on the high school page of a local paper, The Cla

1 CONC he press again, ŮCuŮDexcept in 1974 ŮC/uŮD when, trying to fen
1 CONC d off the unauthorized publication of his uncollected stori
1 CONC es, he told a reporter from The Times: "There is a marvelou
1 CONC s peace in not publishing. It's peaceful. Still. Publishin
1 CONC g is a terrible invasion of my privacy. I like to write
1 CONC . I love to write. But I write just for myself and my own p
1 CONC leasure." And yet the more he sought privacy, the more famo
1 CONC us he became, especially after his appearance on ŮCuŮDthe cov
1 CONC er of Time ŮC/uŮD in 1961. For years it was a sort of journal
1 CONC istic sport for newspapers and magazines to send reporter

1 CONC e toward her mother, and to the homeopathy and food fads sh

1 CONC anta Hinduism, Christian Science, ŮCuŮDScientology ŮC/uŮD and a
1 CONC cupuncture. Mr. Salinger drank his own urine, she wrote, an
1 CONC d sat for hours in an orgone box. But was he writing? The q
1 CONC uestion obsessed Salingerologists, and in the absence of re
1 CONC al evidence, theories multiplied. He hadn't written a wor
1 CONC d for years. Or, like the character in the ŮCuŮDStanley Kubri
1 CONC ck ŮC/uŮD film "The Shining," he wrote the same sentence ove
1 CONC r and over again. Or like ŮCuŮDGogol ŮC/uŮD at the end of his l
1 CONC ife, he wrote prolifically but then burned it all. Ms. Mayn
1 CONC ard said she believed there were at least two novels locke
1 CONC d away in a safe, though she had never seen them. Early Lif
1 CONC e Jerome David Salinger was born in Manhattan on New Year'
1 CONC s Day, 1919, the second of two children. His sister, Doris
1 CONC , who died in 2001, was for many years a buyer in the dres
1 CONC s department at Bloomingdale's. Like the Glasses, the Salin
1 CONC ger children were the product of a mixed marriage. Their fa
1 CONC ther, Sol, was a Jew, the son of a rabbi, but sufficientl
1 CONC y assimilated that he made his living importing both chees
1 CONC e and ham. Their mother, Marie Jillisch, was of Irish desce
1 CONC nt, born in Scotland, but changed her first name to Miria
1 CONC m to appease her in-laws. The family was living in Harlem w
1 CONC hen Mr. Salinger was born, but then, as Sol Salinger's busi
1 CONC ness prospered, moved to West 82nd Street and then to Par
1 CONC k Avenue. Never much of a student, Mr. Salinger, then know
1 CONC n as Sonny, attended the progressive McBurney School on th
1 CONC e Upper West Side. (He told the admissions office his inter
1 CONC ests were dramatics and tropical fish.) But he flunked ou
1 CONC t after two years and in 1934 was packed off to Valley Forg
1 CONC e Military Academy, in Wayne, Pa., which became the model f
1 CONC or Holden's Pencey Prep. Like Holden, Mr. Salinger was th
1 CONC e manager of the school fencing team, and he also became th
1 CONC e literary editor of the school yearbook, Crossed Swords, a
1 CONC nd wrote a school song that was either a heartfelt pastich
1 CONC e of 19th-century sentiment or else a masterpiece of irony
1 CONC : Hide not thy tears on this last day Your sorrow has no sh
1 CONC ame; To march no more midst lines of gray; No longer play t
1 CONC he game. Four years have passed in joyful ways - Wouldst st
1 CONC ay those old times dear? Then cherish now these fleeting da
1 CONC ys, The few while you are here. In 1937, after a couple o
1 CONC f unenthusiastic weeks at ŮCuŮDNew York University ŮC/uŮD, Mr
1 CONC . Salinger traveled with his father to Austria and Poland

1 CONC ness. Deciding that wasn't for him, he returned to Americ
1 CONC a and drifted through a term or so at Ursinus College in Co
1 CONC llegeville, Pa. Fellow students remember him striding aroun
1 CONC d campus in a black chesterfield with velvet collar and ann
1 CONC ouncing that he was going to write the Great American Novel

1 CONC n was an evening class he took at Columbia in 1939, taugh
1 CONC t by Whit Burnett, and under Mr. Burnett's tutelage he mana

1 CONC . He subsequently sold stories to Esquire, Collier's and Th

1 CONC int of real originality. In 1941, after several rejections

1 CONC e goal of any aspiring writer back then, with a story, "Sli

1 CONC ine then had second thoughts, apparently worried about seem
1 CONC ing to encourage young people to run away from school, an
1 CONC d held the story for five years - an eternity even for Th

1 CONC n drafted. He served with the Counter-Intelligence Corps o
1 CONC f the Fourth Infantry Division, whose job was to intervie
1 CONC w Nazi deserters and sympathizers, and was stationed fo
1 CONC r a while in Tiverton, Devon, the setting of "For Esmâe - wi
1 CONC th Love and Squalor," probably the most deeply felt of th

1 CONC , and he later saw action during the Battle of the Bulge. I
1 CONC n 1945 he was hospitalized for "battle fatigue" - often a e
1 CONC uphemism for a breakdown - and after recovering he stayed o

1 CONC ries. He married a German woman, very briefly - a doctor ab

1 CONC nger always called her Saliva. A Different Kind of Writer B
1 CONC ack in New York, Mr. Salinger moved into his parents' apart
1 CONC ment and, having never stopped writing, even during the war
1 CONC , resumed his career. "A Perfect Day for Bananafish," auste
1 CONC re, mysterious and Mr. Salinger's most famous and still mos
1 CONC t discussed story, appeared in The New Yorker in 1948 and s
1 CONC uggested, not wrongly, that he had become a very differen
1 CONC t kind of writer. And like so many writers he eventually fo
1 CONC und in The New Yorker not just an outlet but a kind of hom
1 CONC e and developed a close relationship with the magazine's ed
1 CONC itor, William Shawn, himself famously shy and agoraphobi
1 CONC c - a kindred spirit. In 1961 Mr. Salinger dedicated "Frann
1 CONC y and Zooey" to Shawn, writing, "I urge my editor, mentor a

1 CONC , Fla. Mrs. Koch was the mother of James V. Rosenbaum, vice
1 CONC -president of Kazoo, Inc. and president of the YMCA. She li
1 CONC ved here from 1922 to 1950. She was the daughter of Herma
1 CONC n Friedman, proprietor of Peoples Outfitter Co., and manage
1 CONC d her father's business during World War II. The company wa
1 CONC s subsequently sold to Leath Furniture Co. on N. Burdick St
1 CONC reet. In additional to her husband and son, Mrs. Koch is su
1 CONC rvived by one brother, Philip Friedman of Miami, Fla; one d
1 CONC aughter, Judy R. Hirsch of New York City and three grandchi
1 CONC ldren. Funeral services will be held in Florida. source: Ka
1 CONC lamazoo Gazette Tues 28 March 1972 (submitted by a voluntee
1 CONC r, no family connection)

1 CONC n both coasts, after a decade as a successful actor. He cre

1 CONC e films, all independent, all outside the studio system. Th

1 CONC mbodia, with Martin Sheen and Academy Award-winner Haing S

1 CONC d Alfred Molina Little City with Jon Bon Jovi, Annabella Sc
1 CONC iorra and Penelope Ann Miller Jay Craven's adaptation of Ve

1 CONC end-up of life in Hollywood called Hacks, starring Stephe
1 CONC n Rea, Lisa Kudrow and Tom Arnold Meeting Daddy, a precurso
1 CONC r to Meet the Parents and Lloyd Bridges' last film Stacy Ti
1 CONC tle's modern day Hamlet story, Let the Devil Wear Black, st
1 CONC arring Jacqueline Bisset, Mary-Louise Parker and Jamey Sher
1 CONC idan. With partner Steven Hoffman, he wrapped Four Dogs Pla

1 CONC ng Forest Whitaker, Tim Curry and Olivia Williams (The Sixt
1 CONC h Sense). And his biggest budget film to date here in New Y

1 CONC Robert James Cayton, Sr. (Jim) set sail on the final cruis
1 CONC e of his life at mid-day Saturday, May 23, 2003, embarkin
1 CONC g from St. John's Hospital after heart failure. An avid cru
1 CONC iser, together Jim and Lucille had made over 150 voyages vi
1 CONC siting over 200 countries in their 59 years of marriage. Ji
1 CONC m's first cruise was April 21, 1915, one week after he wa
1 CONC s born in Honolulu and sailed back to his home on the big i
1 CONC sland of Hawaii. Later, Jim moved back to Honolulu where gr
1 CONC ew up as the quintessential island boy attending Punahou Sc
1 CONC hool, surfing with the early wave pioneers, and honing hi
1 CONC s laid back personality. In 1932, with his first pair of sh
1 CONC oes and his duffel bag, Jim attended Stanford University. I
1 CONC n 1937, unable to return to Hawaii due to a shipping strike
1 CONC , Jim settled in Los Angeles.
1 CONT
1 CONT While waiting for the shipping strike to end, Jim was invit
1 CONC ed to invest in a Venetian blind company. With about $100
1 CONC 0 in borrowed funds, Jim launched Vogue Venetian Blinds. Ji
1 CONC m, being ever curious and a tinkerer, heard about the compl
1 CONC aints of Venetian blinds, "They are a pain to clean!". In 1
1 CONC 943, while serving as the first President of the Venetian B
1 CONC lind Association of America, he had a conversation with a g
1 CONC entleman who held the patent for a "Vertical Blind". "Ahhh
1 CONC , no dust!", Jim thought to himself. The rest is history. J
1 CONC im renamed his company Louver Drape and in 1971 he had buil
1 CONC t a 70,000 square foot factory in Santa Monica. When Jim so
1 CONC ld the company in 1981, Louver Drape's facilities include
1 CONC d more than Ůa million square feet, 700 employees, and ove
1 CONC r 100 distributors in 100 countries world-wide. Despite thi
1 CONC s success, Jim maintained his Hawaiian lifestyle playing sa
1 CONC nd volleyball three days a week well into his seventies.
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1944, Jim married Lucille Ferderber of Chicago. The inse
1 CONC parable couple built not only a successful business, bu
1 CONC t a family and community of close friends. Jim and Lucill
1 CONC e were coaches and scout leaders, active in PTA, and well k
1 CONC nown at the old Sand and Sea Beach Club. Jim was a devote
1 CONC d father teaching and sharing with his four children everyt
1 CONC hing, from swimming, volleyball and surfing, to his invalua
1 CONC ble life lessons of values and wisdom. A very close family
1 CONC , the Caytons worked, played and traveled together.
1 CONT
1 CONT Jim is survived by Lucille and his four children, sons Bob
1 CONC , wife Amy, and John, wife Pam, of Santa Cruz, daughters, K
1 CONC iki of San Diego, Lori of Madison, WI, and 6 grandchildren
1 CONC , Ami, Meagan, Kelsey, Jhana, Arya and Kelden.
1 CONT
1 CONT Jim was also involved in a wide variety of community activi
1 CONC ties and humanitarian concerns. Jim is a past president o
1 CONC f both the Santa Monica Chamber of Commerce and Rotary Clu
1 CONC b of Santa Monica, and continued to be an active member i
1 CONC n both of these organizations. True philanthropists, Jim an
1 CONC d Lucille have demonstrated their generosity and compassio
1 CONC n by sponsoring scholarships at Stanford University and San
1 CONC ta Monica College, buildings at Santa Monica College and th
1 CONC e Desert Rehabilitation Services, the pool at the YMCA of S
1 CONC anta Monica, The Center for Healthy Aging, The Wellness Com
1 CONC munity of West Los Angeles, The Boys and Girls Club of Sant
1 CONC a Monica, the Betty Ford Center and many more.
1 CONT
1 CONT Despite Jim's love of people and playful nature, Jim felt i
1 CONC t an unnecessary burden to have a Bon Voyage party for thi
1 CONC s final cruise. Therefore, there will not be a memorial ser
1 CONC vice. Keeping in spirit of Jim's commitment to helping othe
1 CONC rs, he would like you to offer your donations, in his nam
1 CONC e to any of his favored charities: The Wellness Community o
1 CONC f West Los Angeles, The Center for Healthy Aging, Santa Mon
1 CONC ica College, the YMCA of Santa Monica, or the Rotary Club o
1 CONC f Santa Monica.
1 CONT
1 CONT Los Angeles Times Thursday May 29, 2003

1 CONC Salt Lake Tribune , 17 October 1952 , Page 12B
1 CONT The Tower Theater, 9th South and 9th East, has been lease
1 CONC d by the Tower Corporation, L. Howard Marcus, corporation p
1 CONC resident, announced Thursday. The new company plans to ope
1 CONC n its season late this month.
1 CONT "Our intention is to operate the Tower as a truly 'art thea
1 CONC ter,'" Mr. Marcus said. "We plan an entirely new type of th
1 CONC eater operation, patterned after the fine entertainment ent
1 CONC erprises so successful in metropolitan centers throughout t
1 CONC he United States.
1 CONT "We feel there is a constant demand for high quality artist
1 CONC ic films, both imported and domestic, which we will presen
1 CONC t at the Tower."
1 CONT The president said that the Tower's pictures will be in kee
1 CONC ping with the city's reputation as a western cultural and m
1 CONC usical metropolis.
1 CONT Mr. Marcus, son of the late Louis Marcus, former Salt Lak
1 CONC e City mayor and operator of theaters throughout the Interm
1 CONC ountain states, is a Stanford University graduate and has b
1 CONC een identified with local theaters, advertising industry, t
1 CONC he Utah Symphony Orchestra and various civic and philanthro
1 CONC pic organizations.
1 CONT
1 CONT Howard Marcus was born on April 22, 1919, to Louis and Gert
1 CONC rude Levin Marcus. Louis Marcus was a civic and business le
1 CONC ader, as he served as mayor of Salt Lake City from 1931-193
1 CONC 5. He graduated from East High School in Salt Lake City, th
1 CONC en went on to get his bachelor's from Stanford, and went t
1 CONC o Harvard Graduate School of Business Administration. He he
1 CONC ld various positions, including: vice chairman of the Sal
1 CONC t Lake County Planning Commission, appointee of the Tracy A
1 CONC dvisory Board, trustee of the National Hemophilia Foundatio
1 CONC n, chairman of the Salt Lake Aviary Commission, member of t
1 CONC he Utah Symphony board of trustees (1948-1951), president o
1 CONC f Duckville Gun Club and Utah Skeet Shooting Association, m
1 CONC ember of Holladay Gun Club, vice president of R.T. Harris A
1 CONC dvertising Agency and president of Tower Theater, presiden
1 CONC t and general manager of First Capitol Corp of Utah (1962-1
1 CONC 965), member of Congregations Kol Ami and B'nai B'rith, cha
1 CONC irman of Israel drive for Utah and trustee and treasurer fo
1 CONC r Congregation B'nai Israel. He married Wilma Ferderber o
1 CONC n April 10, 1947, in Los Angeles and had three sons: Stepha
1 CONC n, William, and Roger. He died on Feb. 13, 1984, of heart c
1 CONC omplications. This collection contains: letters, financia
1 CONC l papers, receipts, newspaper clippings, misc. papers, a fe
1 CONC w pictures, theatre and opera pamphlets and clippings, broc
1 CONC hures,and travel papers. Readers who would find an interes
1 CONC t in this collection include those with an interest in Uta
1 CONC h politics, as there are many papers from mayor Louis Marcu
1 CONC s; those with an aviaion interest, as Gertrude was the pres
1 CONC ident of the Salt Lake chapter of the Women's National Aero
1 CONC nautical Association, which along with information on the a
1 CONC ssociation, are many newspaper articles on various pilots
1 CONC , including: Amelia Earhart, Charles Lindbergh, and "The ba
1 CONC t man," Clem Sohn; those with World War II interests, as th
1 CONC ere are many letters from the war between soldiers and thei
1 CONC r family and friends back home, as well as a few newspapers
1 CONC ; a few Stanford records and course offers could be of inte
1 CONC rest for colleges in 1930s- 40s; vacation interests becaus
1 CONC e of 1930-1940s brochures and maps (including one of Havan
1 CONC a from 1940s); and those with an interest in theatre durin
1 CONC g the early 1900s. There is also a Flag of Destiny Vol. I
1 CONC I piece by C. Switzer, with a picture portraying Francis Sc
1 CONC ott Key and the flag, a text about the piece and a copy o
1 CONC f the original manuscript of the The Star-Spangled Banner.

1 CONC ŮCbŮDSuperior Court of California
1 CONT County of Alameda
1 CONT 2003 Court News
1 CONT
1 CONT July 25, 2003 Glenn P. Oleon, Appointed Court Commissioner
1 CONC , Superior Court, County of Alameda
1 CONT ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT -----------------------------------------------------------
1 CONC -----------------------------------------------------------
1 CONC ---------
1 CONT
1 CONT Contra Costa TIMES
1 CONT Posted on Thu, Jul. 31, 2003
1 CONT VALLEY BRIEFS
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCbŮDAlameda CountyŮC/bŮD
1 CONT Orinda lawyer new court commissioner
1 CONT
1 CONT An Orinda lawyer with more than 2 1/2 decades of experienc
1 CONC e practicing family law in Castro Valley, Hayward and Oakla
1 CONC nd has been appointed Alameda County's newest superior cour
1 CONC t commissioner.
1 CONT
1 CONT Glenn P. Oleon has begun hearing juvenile, traffic and unla
1 CONC wful detainer cases in Department 504 at the Hayward courth
1 CONC ouse.
1 CONT
1 CONT Oleon has worked a great deal with the Alameda County Bar A
1 CONC ssociation, the Volunteer Legal Services Corporation and mo
1 CONC st recently served as an officer and director of the Lega
1 CONC l Aid Society of Alameda County. He also was on the
1 CONT
1 CONT Alameda County Bar Association's board of directors and ser
1 CONC ved as a judge pro tem.
1 CONT
1 CONT Oleon attended UC Berkeley for his undergraduate educatio
1 CONC n before attending Cal's Boalt Hall where he received his l
1 CONC aw degree. He was admitted to the state bar in 1976.
1 CONT
1 CONT He and his wife, Ricki, are the parents of three children.
1 CONT
1 CONT Oleon replaces retired Commissioner Hilde Olds.
1 CONT
1 CONT Commissioner Glenn Oleon has served as a family court commi
1 CONC ssioner for the Superior Court of California, County of Ala
1 CONC meda, since 2003. He began his private law practice in 1976
1 CONC , which emphasized family law, estate planning, and probat
1 CONC e litigation. In 2011, ACBA Volunteer Legal Services Corpor
1 CONC ation honored him with the "Mentor of the Year" award. Addi
1 CONC tionally, Commissioner Oleon has served regularly as a judg
1 CONC e pro tempore at the Superior Court of California. He earne
1 CONC d his B.A. and J.D. from the University of California Berke
1 CONC ley.
1 CONT
1 CONT Commission Oleon was born July 18, 1952 in St. Louis, Misso
1 CONC uri. In May 1976 he graduated from the University of Califo
1 CONC rnia at Berkeley School of Law. He has been a member of th
1 CONC e California State Bar since December 1976 and a member o
1 CONC f the Alameda County Bar Association since 1977 where he se
1 CONC rved as a member of the Board of Directors and former chai
1 CONC r on the Family Law Committee. While a member of the Alamed
1 CONC a County Bar Association he also served on numerous other c
1 CONC ommittees and sections, including Estate Planning, Trust an
1 CONC d Probate Law Section; Fee Arbitration Committee; Lawyer Re
1 CONC ferral Service Committee and Solo Practice Section.
1 CONT
1 CONT Additionally, he sat as Judge Pro Tem for Alameda County Su
1 CONC perior Court, Traffic Court, Probate Court, Family Law an
1 CONC d Small Claims departments.
1 CONT
1 CONT He has conducted numerous pro bono clinics, attorney traini
1 CONC ng sessions and family law workshops and represented numero
1 CONC us pro bono clients through the Alameda County Bar Associat
1 CONC ion Volunteer Legal Services Corporation.
1 CONT
1 CONT If all the above hasn't kept him busy enough, he was vice-p
1 CONC resident, director and former Brotherhood president of Temp
1 CONC le Sinai in Oakland, California. Other volunteer communit
1 CONC y organizations include: Oakland Coalition of Congregations
1 CONC ; Cub Scout leader; youth baseball and basketball coach (Pi
1 CONC edmont Little League and Orinda Youth Association); Jewis
1 CONC h Community Relations Council of the Greater East Bay; Oakl
1 CONC and-Piedmont Jewish Community Center; National Commission o
1 CONC n Social Action of Reform Judaism; and Northern Californi
1 CONC a Council of the Union of American Hebrew Congregations.
1 CONT
1 CONT He and his wife, Ricki Peters Oleon have been married for 2
1 CONC 4 years and have been residents of Orinda, California sinc
1 CONC e 1996. They have three children, Jessica 24, Hilary 19 an
1 CONC d Michael 16.
1 CONT
1 CONT CALDA is honored to have such an upstanding member of the l
1 CONC egal community accept our invitation to be a speaker and pa
1 CONC nel member at our 25th anniversary conference! He is back b
1 CONC y popular demand after rave reviews received at the Bay Are
1 CONC a Chapter seminar in March 2012. Don't miss this opportunit
1 CONC y to experience what you may have missed!

2 NOTE Mr. Lester F. ROSENBAUM - 4417 Lake Forest Drive, Kalamazoo
3 CONC , passed away November 17, 1971 at Bronson Hospital. Mr. RO
3 CONC SENBAUM was born October 18, 1892 in Kalamazoo, son of Lewi
3 CONC s and Amanda ROSENBAUM and had been a lifetime resident her
3 CONC e. Graduated from Kalamazoo Public Schools and the Universi
3 CONC ty of Michigan in 1914. Spent all his working life at the K
3 CONC alamazoo Pant Company where he had achieved positions wher
3 CONC e he had achieved positions as president and chairman of th
3 CONC e board, retiring in 1966. member of Temple Bi'Nai Isrial (
3 CONC sic), Kalamazoo Country Club. Surviving are: one son and on
3 CONC e daughter, Dr. Louis ROSENBAUM, Ishpeming, Mich., Mrs. Mil
3 CONC dred (Tood) ACKERMAN, Cambridge, Mass.; seven grandchildren
3 CONC ; one brother, two sisters, Walter ROSENBAUM of Baltimore
3 CONC , Md., Mrs. Norman ROSENWEIG, Miami, Fla., and Mrs. Helen
3 CONC e RAYCE of Phoenix, Arix.; many nieces and nephews, Mr. ROS
3 CONC ENBAUM was preceded in death by his wife, Rita, in May 16
3 CONC , 1966. A memorial service will be held, Rabbi Steven Stein
3 CONC berg officating, time to be announced. Arrangements by th
3 CONC e Truesdale Downtown Funeral Home. Memorial contributions m
3 CONC ay be made to charity of choice. source: Kalamazoo Gazett
3 CONC e Wed 17 Nov 1971 (submitted by a volunteer, no family conn
3 CONC ection) Dead at 79, Former President of Pant Company - Lest
3 CONC er ROSENBAUM, 79, of 4417 Lake Forest former president an
3 CONC d chairman of the board the Kalamazoo Pant Co., died this m
3 CONC orning in Bronson Hospital. A native of Kalamazoo, ROSENBAU
3 CONC M graduated from Kalamazoo Central High School in 1910 an
3 CONC d went on to the University of Michigan where he gained a B
3 CONC achelor of Arts degree in 1914. Following graduation from t
3 CONC he U of M he joined the company founded in Kalamazoo in 186
3 CONC 7 by his grandfather, Samuel ROSENBAUM. His grandfather beg
3 CONC an business in Three Rivers prior to the Civil War, then mo
3 CONC ved to Kalamazoo. Lester succeeded his father Louis as pres
3 CONC ident of the company and was named chairman of the board wa
3 CONC s named chairman of the board before his retirement in 1966
3 CONC , the same year his wife Rita died. He was a member of Temp
3 CONC le B'nai Israel, the Kalamazoo County Club and for many yea
3 CONC rs was active on boards and committees for the Kalamazoo Ci
3 CONC vic Theatre, the Kalamazoo Art Center and the Kalamazoo Sym
3 CONC phony Orchestra. He was also active in the Kalamazoo Societ
3 CONC y for Crippled Children and Adults. An accomplished artis
3 CONC t in his own right, ROSENBAUM had won national recognitio
3 CONC n for his work in both pottery and collages. A past preside
3 CONC nt of the former Kalamazoo Art Institute, Rosenbaum was hon
3 CONC ored in 1969 for his long service to the arts in this cit
3 CONC y by being named an honorary board member of the Gilmore Ar
3 CONC t Center. source: Kalamazoo Gazette (submitted by a volunte
3 CONC er, no family connection)

1 CONC No relation to the Rosenbaum family.  He was "bought" to pr
1 CONC ovide a husband for Carolyn ("Carrie").  Samuel went to Ne
1 CONC w York to find Ludwig, and Louis, Goddie and Edwin were al
1 CONC l required to come up with the purchase price, or dowry.
1 CONT
1 CONT Ludwig was a tailor, and took the dowry to open his own tai
1 CONC lor shop in New York.  In a short time, he went broke so th
1 CONC e family brought Ludwig and Carrie to Kalamazoo and give hi
1 CONC m a job in the stock room at the factory.  He was a kindly
1 CONC , loveable, ineffectual man.
1 CONT
1 CONT Some time later, Ludwig's brother, Adolph, arrived in Kalam
1 CONC azoo and went to work in the cutting room at the factory
1 CONC .   He was a handsome man with rosy cheeks -- he died of "c
1 CONC onsumption" (TB).   Adolph was quite a womanizer and report

1 CONC ŮCbŮDStanley Ned Rosenbaum, Ph.D.ŮC/bŮD is Professor Emeritus o
1 CONC f Religious and Classics, and former Coordinator of the Jud
1 CONC aic Studies Program, at Dickinson College in Carlisle, Penn
1 CONC sylvania. His publications include Amos of Israel: A New In
1 CONC terpretation and a number of articles in scholarly and popu
1 CONC lar publications. His religious activities for many years i
1 CONC ncluded regular leading of Sabbath services for Congregatio
1 CONC n Beth Tikvah, the Carlisle Jewish community. In addition t
1 CONC o giving lectures, workshops, and invocations at Christia
1 CONC n and ecumenical events, he is Adjunct Professor of Religio
1 CONC n at Louisville Presbyterian Seminary in Kentucky.

1 CONC nything about Judaism, having been raised in a household th
1 CONC at was Jewish, but non-observant. Mary, raised with a non-o
1 CONC bservant Lutheran father and a strongly observant Catholi
1 CONC c mother, was adamant that Ned explore his own religion a
1 CONC s well as work on ways to accept hers. Now married nearly f
1 CONC orty years, the Rosenbaums each have strong individual reli
1 CONC gious identities and a terrific partnership. They raised th
1 CONC eir children with exposure to both faiths and allowed the
1 CONC m to choose for themselves how they would maintain their sp
1 CONC irituality.  ...Ned and Mary Rosenbaum have a reassuringl
1 CONC y healthy relationship, having nurtured each other throug
1 CONC h forty years of religious and personal exploration, and ke
1 CONC eping a delightful sense of humor about the struggles coupl
1 CONC es can face about religion."

2 NOTE Message Board Post: ROSENBAUM, Edna Mae Wieland May 15, 191
3 CONC 0 - June 19, 2005 Edna grew up on the Wieland Farm in Manis
3 CONC tique, MI, the eldest of six children. During her girlhood
3 CONC , she attended the one room Kiawatha Grade School and assis
3 CONC ted her family on the farm. She washed clothes on a washin
3 CONC g board with Fels Naptha soap and wrung them through the ri
3 CONC nger on wash day, worked in the family garden, went berryin
3 CONC g, canned food for the winter months, picked potato bugs of
3 CONC f the vines, picked the mustard stalks out of the oat and b
3 CONC arley fields, drove the team of horses for her father durin
3 CONC g haying season, watched over her younger siblings, and coo
3 CONC ked, cleaned, and sewed like most girls of her day. Edna gr
3 CONC aduated from high school at the age of 16 and graduated fro
3 CONC m the nursing program at Michael Reese Hospital in Chicago
3 CONC , IL in 1932. That same year, she met Sylvan "Mike" Rosenba
3 CONC um, who was working as a lawyer in Chicago. They were marri
3 CONC ed in Chicago, IL in 1935. Their daughter, Carolyn Marcia
3 CONC , was born in 1937, followed by a son, Edwin S. "Butch" i
3 CONC n 1940. During WW II, Edna trained over 200 nurses for th
3 CONC e war as a Red Cross volunteer. These were happy years whe
3 CONC n Mike worked at the Kalamazoo Pant Company and the famil
3 CONC y enjoyed their summer cottage at Gull Lake, where Butch an
3 CONC d Carolyn raced sailboats as teenagers. Mike and Edna Rosen
3 CONC baum retired to Sarasota, FL in 1968. There they were invol
3 CONC ved with the New College Music Festival (Mike was a board m
3 CONC ember), and The John and Mable Ringling Museum of Art, wher
3 CONC e Edna worked as a volunteer lecturer. As a Master Needle C
3 CONC rafter, Edna also contributed articles to the Embroiderer'
3 CONC s Guild. The Rosenbaums enjoyed traveling, eating out, al
3 CONC l the cultural events Sarasota had to offer, walks along th
3 CONC e Gulf beaches, and frequent visits from family and friends
3 CONC . After Mike's death, Edna moved to Tempe, AZ to be near he
3 CONC r son, Butch. She died at the ripe age of 95, having seen m
3 CONC ost of the 20th century unfold. As a girl, her home had n
3 CONC o running water, indoor bathrooms, electricity, or centra
3 CONC l heat. ! She lived from horse and buggy and dirt road time
3 CONC s to the modern age, learning to play FreeCell on the compu
3 CONC ter at 89! Edna will be fondly remembered for her wisdom, h
3 CONC er graciousness, her creativity, and her warmth. She is sur
3 CONC vived by three of her siblings, Mildred, Marian and Bud; he
3 CONC r son, Butch (and Joni) Rosenbaum; her son-in-law, Garry Ha
3 CONC ma- cher; her five grandchildren, Kara (and Rob) Girvin, Sc
3 CONC ott Rosenbaum (and Gretchen Piper), Mike Jessop, Laura (an
3 CONC d Hugh) Carr, and Anne Duret; and her nine great-grandchild
3 CONC ren, James, Paige, and Ruth Jessop; Daniel and Stuart Shaw
3 CONC , Anais and Aniele Duret; and Louise and Samuel Rosenbaum
3 CONC . Should you wish to remember Mrs. Rosenbaum, donations ar
3 CONC e being accepted in her name to The John and Mable Ringlin
3 CONC g Museum of Art, 5401 Bay Shore Road, Sarasota, Florida 342
3 CONC 43, (941) 359-5838. Published in the Kalamazoo Gazette on 8
3 CONC /14/2005. (Posted by a volunteer-no family connection)

1 CONC e one room Kiawatha Grade School and assisted her family o
1 CONC n the farm. She washed clothes on a washing board with Fel
1 CONC s Naptha soap and wrung them through the ringer on wash day
1 CONC , worked in the family garden, went berrying, canned food f
1 CONC or the winter months, picked potato bugs off the vines, pic
1 CONC ked the mustard stalks out of the oat and barley fields, dr
1 CONC ove the team of horses for her father during haying season
1 CONC , watched over her younger siblings, and cooked, cleaned, a
1 CONC nd sewed like most girls of her day. Edna graduated from hi
1 CONC gh school at the age of 16 and graduated from the nursing p
1 CONC rogram at Michael Reese Hospital in Chicago, IL in 1932. Th
1 CONC at same year, she met Sylvan "Mike" Rosenbaum, who was work
1 CONC ing as a lawyer in Chicago. They were married in Chicago, I
1 CONC L in 1935. Their daughter, Carolyn Marcia, was born in 1937
1 CONC , followed by a son, Edwin S. "Butch" in 1940. During WW II
1 CONC , Edna trained over 200 nurses for the war as a Red Cross v
1 CONC olunteer. These were happy years when Mike worked at the Ka
1 CONC lamazoo Pant Company and the family enjoyed their summer co
1 CONC ttage at Gull Lake, where Butch and Carolyn raced sailboat
1 CONC s as teenagers. Mike and Edna Rosenbaum retired to Sarasota
1 CONC , FL in 1968. There they were involved with the New Colleg
1 CONC e Music Festival (Mike was a board member), and The John an
1 CONC d Mable Ringling Museum of Art, where Edna worked as a volu
1 CONC nteer lecturer. As a Master Needle Crafter, Edna also contr
1 CONC ibuted articles to the Embroiderer's Guild. The Rosenbaum
1 CONC s enjoyed traveling, eating out, all the cultural events Sa
1 CONC rasota had to offer, walks along the Gulf beaches, and freq
1 CONC uent visits from family and friends. After Mike's death, Ed
1 CONC na moved to Tempe, AZ to be near her son, Butch. She died a
1 CONC t the ripe age of 95, having seen most of the 20th centur
1 CONC y unfold. As a girl, her home had no running water, indoo
1 CONC r bathrooms, electricity, or central heat. She lived from h
1 CONC orse and buggy and dirt road times to the modern age, learn
1 CONC ing to play FreeCell on the computer at 89! Edna will be fo
1 CONC ndly remembered for her wisdom, her graciousness, her creat
1 CONC ivity, and her warmth. She is survived by three of her sibl
1 CONC ings, Mildred, Marian and Bud; her son, Butch (and Joni) Ro
1 CONC senbaum; her son-in-law, Garry Hamacher; her five grandchil
1 CONC dren, Kara (and Rob) Girvin, Scott Rosenbaum (and Gretche
1 CONC n Piper), Mike Jessop, Laura (and Hugh) Carr, and Anne Dure
1 CONC t; and her nine great-grandchildren, James, Paige, and Rut
1 CONC h Jessop; Daniel and Stuart Shaw, Anais and Aniele Duret; a
1 CONC nd Louise and Samuel Rosenbaum. Should you wish to remembe
1 CONC r Mrs. Rosenbaum, donations are being accepted in her nam
1 CONC e to The John and Mable Ringling Museum of Art, 5401 Bay Sh
1 CONC ore Road, Sarasota, Florida 34243, (941) 359-5838.
1 CONT Published in the Kalamazoo Gazette on 8/14/2005. (Posted b
1 CONC y a volunteer-no family connection)

1 CONC e Police who were investigating the complaint, seventy-eigh
1 CONC t purebred Cornish Rex cats and three Rat Terrier pups wer
1 CONC e removed from the Ishpeming home of ŮCbŮDGrace Aalto-Rosenba
1 CONC umŮC/bŮD, who was operating ŮCbŮDCascading Rex catteryŮC/bŮD, an
1 CONC d ŮCbŮDLouis RosenbaumŮC/bŮD.
1 CONT These animals were held as evidence in a criminal neglect c
1 CONC ase. The Rosenbaums were charged with ten counts of anima
1 CONC l cruelty. In a plea agreement, the Rosenbaums pled no cont
1 CONC est to one count of animal cruelty. As part of the judgment
1 CONC , they forfeited all ownership interest in the 78 cats an
1 CONC d 3 pups, and they agreed to pay $13,964.31 which represent
1 CONC ed veterinary care, staff time and transport for the seizur
1 CONC e of the animals, and only 25% of sheltering costs incurre
1 CONC d as of the date of the agreement.

1 CONC James Sloss Ackerman (USA) 2001 Balzan Prize for History o
1 CONC f Architecture (including town planning and landscape desig
1 CONC n) For his outstanding work on the history of Renaissance a
1 CONC rchitecture which contributed to the modern approach to arc
1 CONC hitectural history based on a systematic critical examinati
1 CONC on of written and visual sources. James Sloss Ackerman, on
1 CONC e of the leading historians of art and architecture, was bo
1 CONC rn in San Francisco in1919. He taught at Berkeley and the
1 CONC n at ŮCuŮDHarvard ŮC/uŮD until 1990. During the Second World Wa
1 CONC r he served in the US Army in Italy. This allowed him to im
1 CONC prove his knowledge of the Italian Renaissance which will t
1 CONC hen become his major area of expertise. His books are consi
1 CONC dered classics in their field, both in terms of rigor and o
1 CONC f method: a monograph on architecture, the biography of a
1 CONC n artist, the study of a particular architectural style. Th
1 CONC e first is exemplified by ŮCiŮDThe Cortile del Belvedere ŮC/iŮD
1 CONC (1954). The second is well illustrated by his fundamental w
1 CONC orks in the sixties on the architectural works by Michelang
1 CONC elo and Palladio. Finally the third is to be found in ŮCiŮDTh
1 CONC e Villa: Form and Ideology of Country Houses ŮC/iŮD(1990). I
1 CONC n this Ackerman analyses the common characteristics as wel
1 CONC l as the specific elements of this type of building, from t
1 CONC he Roman villa to Wright's Waterfall House. For all the gre
1 CONC at variety of forms that the villa has taken on over time
1 CONC , it appears that the underlying ideology has remained subs
1 CONC tantially unchanged from its origins to our days. In additi
1 CONC on to these major works Ackerman has also written hundred
1 CONC s of essays on the history of Renaissance architecture, stu
1 CONC dies on the relation between art and science and on the int
1 CONC ellectual, moral and social foundations of teaching.
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCuŮDŮCbŮDJames Sloss AckermanŮC/uŮDŮC/bŮD James Sloss Ackerman i
1 CONC s the doyen of the international community of architectura
1 CONC l historians studying the epoch of the Renaissance. He is o
1 CONC ne of the scholars who created modern architectural histor
1 CONC y based on a systematic approach to the critical examinatio
1 CONC n of all the written and visual sources. His ability to us
1 CONC e scholarship, keen observation and responsiveness to archi
1 CONC tecture to present great architects of the past as immediat
1 CONC e, almost contemporary figures, who are constantly presen
1 CONC t in our culture, is perhaps the greatest of Ackerman's ach
1 CONC ievements. His work has had considerable influence on bot
1 CONC h architectural historians and practical architects. Ackerm
1 CONC an's first major work, ŮCiŮDThe Cortile del BelvedereŮC/iŮD, wh
1 CONC ich was published in 1954, deals with the question of style
1 CONC , significance and the relation to the antique. It becam
1 CONC e a model for monographs on individual buildings, above al
1 CONC l in the Renaissance and Baroque periods,  critically exami
1 CONC ning all the written and visual sources and arriving at a p
1 CONC lausible reconstruction of the original architect's intenti
1 CONC ons.  In a succession of influential learned articles, man
1 CONC y of which were reprinted in 1991 in ŮCiŮDDistance Points: Es
1 CONC says in Theory and Renaissance Art and ArchitectureŮC/iŮD, h
1 CONC e opened up new ways of looking at the Italian Renaissance
1 CONC . He drew attention to such neglected areas of Renaissanc
1 CONC e architecture as the buildings of Milan prior to Bramant
1 CONC e and the relations between the mentality of Renaissance ar
1 CONC chitects and late Gothic architectural theory. His researc
1 CONC h on architectural practice in Italy in the 15th and 16th c
1 CONC entury laid the foundations for the whole modern study of a
1 CONC rchitectural drawings and design methods. A major phase o
1 CONC f Ackerman's work was dedicated to Michelangelo and culmina
1 CONC ted in the outstanding monograph ŮCiŮDThe Architecture of Mic
1 CONC helangeloŮC/iŮD,  which appeared in English in 1961 and was l
1 CONC ater published in Italian, Spanish, French, Japanese and Ge
1 CONC rman.  This book focuses on the relation between the idea
1 CONC s of Michelangelo about architecture and his built works, p
1 CONC rovides lucid analysis of the works themselves and the prel
1 CONC iminary drawings for them and stresses the physical and men
1 CONC tal contexts in which they had to be created. After writin
1 CONC g on Michelangelo, Ackerman turned to Palladio, discussin
1 CONC g him as an architect and as an intellectual, in his brilli
1 CONC ant book ŮCiŮDPalladioŮC/iŮD, published in 1966. This is stil
1 CONC l considered the best introduction to Palladio and is avail
1 CONC able not only in English, but also in Italian, Spanish, Fre
1 CONC nch, Japanese and German. The influence of this book has be
1 CONC en immense, particularly in its innovative approach to patr
1 CONC onage and the role of clients in the creation of the cultur
1 CONC al meaning of architecture, especially of the villas.  Acke
1 CONC rman published his scholarly catalogue ŮCiŮDPalladio's Villas
1 CONC ŮC/iŮD in 1967 and developed this sphere of research up to 19
1 CONC 90, when his book ŮCiŮDThe Villa: Form and Ideology of Countr
1 CONC y HousesŮC/iŮD appeared. This time he put the villa in broa
1 CONC d historical perspective, from Antiquity to the Enlightenme
1 CONC nt. In recent years Italian, French and Spanish translation
1 CONC s of this fundamental work have appeared. James Ackerman i
1 CONC s a very productive and original scholar, a skilful write
1 CONC r on architecture and a teacher who has guided many of th
1 CONC e leading scholars in their first steps. He continues to gi
1 CONC ve fruitful ideas to the development of the architectural h
1 CONC istory in America and in Europe. His work has constantly gr
1 CONC own and developed taking account of changes in other discip
1 CONC lines, and in the world. It has always been inspired by a s
1 CONC ense of the cultural role and responsibility of the archite
1 CONC ctural historian.
