1 CONC Gulfshore Life
1 CONT January, 2010
1 CONT ŮCbŮDŮCiŮDHERE COMES THE STYLE By: Marsha Fottler
1 CONT Fabulous Vera Wang wedding gowns, amazing Annieglass and tw
1 CONC o new collections of furniture at Robb & Stucky.ŮC/bŮDFashio
1 CONC n is finally handing bridesmaids a break. Vera Wang is th
1 CONC e couture design genius who made it happen and Kerry Waterm
1 CONC an (owner of The Bridal Path and The Paper Mill) is the loc
1 CONC al savvy retail source for women who say, "I do" to partici
1 CONC pating in a wedding party. Formerly, that meant wearing a f
1 CONC ussy dress so unflattering that "bridesmaid gown" had becom
1 CONC e synonymous with stuff-in-the-closet-and-never-look-at-aga
1 CONC in. But no more.
1 CONT The new look in bridesmaids' dresses isn't about dresses a
1 CONC t all, it's all about the two-piece ensemble. It works thi
1 CONC s way. A bride selects the color, fabric and the style o
1 CONC f a skirt -long, short, A-line, and so on. Then, each bride
1 CONC smaid chooses a matching top that suits her body type and m
1 CONC aximizes her best feature--shoulders, bust, gorgeous long n
1 CONC eck, whatever. Each top is the same shade and material as t
1 CONC he skirt, so all the attendants are uniform, but each look
1 CONC s slightly individual. And all of them feel pretty.
1 CONT "Vera Wang is definitely at the top of her form with this i
1 CONC dea," says ŮCbŮDŮC/iŮDKerry WatermanŮC/bŮDŮCiŮD. "I sell more of th
1 CONC ese two-piece outfits than any other type of gown for bride
1 CONC smaids."
1 CONT Waterman notes that 95 percent of women who come into her b
1 CONC outique end up with a strapless wedding dress: "Even whe
1 CONC n a bride tells me strapless is the last thing on her mind
1 CONC , that's what she'll ultimately select. Strapless is reall
1 CONC y in right now and Florida weddings make the choice ideal.
1 CONC " What other trends does the wedding expert notice?
1 CONT * The black-and-white wedding is over. It was on the way ou
1 CONC t but the events of 9/11 finished the concept. The aftermat
1 CONC h of the September tragedy has had other ramifications: Big
1 CONC , family-type wedding celebrations are more common. Wedding
1 CONC s are seen as a major celebration of life and family unity
1 CONC , a colorful reunion.
1 CONT * Deeper, richer shades for bridesmaids' ensembles. Popula
1 CONC r colors right now include purple, dark moss, tangerine, an
1 CONC d ocean blue. Celadon and sky blue are always desirable an
1 CONC d pink will always be in.
1 CONT * More children in wedding parties.
1 CONT * People are paying more attention to designer invitations
1 CONC . The ones by William Arthur and Anna Griffin are sought af
1 CONC ter and quite expensive, about $1,000 for 100 invitations (
1 CONC and that's before postage). Traditional in script, paper st
1 CONC ock and wording, these invitations feature a modern but tas
1 CONC teful twist. For example, the inside of the envelope will b
1 CONC e lightly brushed with a pearl finish or the envelope migh
1 CONC t be square instead of rectanglular.
1 CONT Waterman got into the couture wedding dress business abou
1 CONC t six years ago when people coming into her Paper Mill bout
1 CONC ique (established in 1982) to select upmarket wedding invit
1 CONC ations complained about the selection of luxury wedding gow
1 CONC ns in this part of Florida. Waterman, who has loved fashio
1 CONC n since she was an English major at the University of Michi
1 CONC gan and made her own clothes for her tiny size 4 frame, too
1 CONC k off for New York and sat through a season of bridal shows
1 CONC . These big events occur every October and April in Manhatt
1 CONC an, which is the world center for the bridal industry.
1 CONT For the first round of shows, she just observed and said sh
1 CONC e was too overwhelmed to buy. On a second visit she ordere
1 CONC d sample gowns from Christian Dior, Jim Helm and St. Pucci
1 CONC , selecting conservative styles more appropriate for the So
1 CONC uthwest Florida market. The following year, Waterman met Ve
1 CONC ra Wang and decided that Wang creations would form the cent
1 CONC erpiece of The Bridal Path. A Vera Wang wedding dress start
1 CONC s at about $4,000. The 40-year-old designer is credited wit
1 CONC h overhauling the wedding dress industry by offering wome
1 CONC n sophisticated and sexy gowns of impeccable tailoring mad
1 CONC e from only the finest materials, such as duchesse silk sat
1 CONC in.
1 CONT Impressed with the designs of Alvina Valenta (starting pric
1 CONC e about $2,800), Waterman immediately stocked them, too. To
1 CONC day, she has an inventory of about 30 different dresses dur
1 CONC ing any one season. They are all in a size 8 or 10, even th
1 CONC ough 25 percent of her clients wear upwards of a size 12.
1 CONT "All the dresses are samples," she explains. "A bride trie
1 CONC s them on for color and style, and then once she has mad
1 CONC e a decision, the dress is sewn just for her, with regula
1 CONC r fittings taking place during the five-month process." Wat
1 CONC erman admits that in extreme cases (and only if the sampl
1 CONC e dress fits nearly perfectly) she will sell a sample. "I
1 CONC f a bride is here visiting from another state or country, t
1 CONC ries on the perfect gown and has major time constraints, I'
1 CONC ll sell the sample," she says. " But that's rare."
1 CONT Waterman's clients come from all over Florida and the world
1 CONC . She recently provided the wedding dresses for two DuPon
1 CONC t sisters. "The parents were vacationing here with relative
1 CONC s in Sanibel," she says. "First one daughter came in and bo
1 CONC ught. She was married in Annapolis. Then the other daughte
1 CONC r came in and selected a gown. Her wedding was at the Gaspa
1 CONC rilla Inn in Boca Grande. Both young women went with Vera W
1 CONC ang gowns. My business is mostly through word of mouth. On
1 CONC e bride's family tells another. My showroom is open only b
1 CONC y appointment."
1 CONT Equally as important as style is a wedding gown's color, wh
1 CONC ich ranges from white to ivory and to darker shades of cham
1 CONC pagne and rum with a delicate blush undertone. "The differe
1 CONC nce in shading is so subtle that as a guest in the church
1 CONC , you'd never be able to tell the difference between whit
1 CONC e and ivory," Waterman says. "The color differences are mea
1 CONC nt to complement a bride's skin tones. For example, a blond
1 CONC e, blue-eyed, fair-skinned woman is shown to best advantag
1 CONC e in an ivory gown. If she has raven hair, dark eyes, she'
1 CONC s probably best in white. And the Irish good looks of blac
1 CONC k hair and blue eyes will want white."
1 CONT Waterman observes that darker shades are usually selected b
1 CONC y older, independent women who want to make a fashion state
1 CONC ment. These women are buying their own gowns and don't com
1 CONC e to the showroom with their mothers. The average age of he
1 CONC r brides runs between 25 and28. Sometimes, young women brin
1 CONC g photos of dresses from bridal magazines. Sometimes they c
1 CONC ome in with pre-conceived notions about what they think the
1 CONC y need based on self-perception.
1 CONT "Full-figured women will always tell me they want a simple
1 CONC , straight gown," says Waterman. "I listen, then I bring ou
1 CONC t what I think they should have, and they are amazed. A col
1 CONC umn dress just calls attention to the fact that a bride i
1 CONC s full figured. What that woman needs is a gown that flatte
1 CONC rs her best features. And today's luxury gowns are made t
1 CONC o do that. It's just a matter of being willing to try on se
1 CONC veral to tell what's going on."
1 CONT Not all of Waterman's clients are brides or members of a we
1 CONC dding party. Many buyers are local gala-goers who call an
1 CONC d make an appointment to try on party shoes from the Italia
1 CONC n house of Fenaroli. These beauties sell for $185 to $285 a
1 CONC nd can be dyed to match a gown or left white. Many styles f
1 CONC eature crystal and faux jewels. "My necklaces are another g
1 CONC ood buy at $200 to $300, and lots of local women will sto
1 CONC p in to buy them for themselves or as gifts," Waterman says
1 CONC .
1 CONT The bridal expert's daughter, Julie Magardino (now an attor
1 CONC ney with two sons), became one of mom's first clients six y
1 CONC ears ago. Daughter ultimately selected an ivory modified A-
1 CONC line Alvina Valenta dress designed by Victoria McMillan. I
1 CONC t had a full skirt with silk roses and off-the-shoulder top
1 CONC . Waterman says she has noticed over the years that Alvin
1 CONC a Valenta does a particularly good job with gowns for the p
1 CONC etite bride. "If you're five-foot-two or under, I always br
1 CONC ing out the Valenta samples," she says. "The cut of her gow
1 CONC ns really flatter a petite bride. Alternatively, the Vera W
1 CONC angs seem to have a longer torso and most of them, but cert
1 CONC ainly not all, work better on a taller woman."
1 CONT When Waterman isn't in her bridal salon or in The Paper Mil
1 CONC l helping people select wedding invitations, gift items, o
1 CONC r cards, she can be tracked down on horseback. "The Brida
1 CONC l Path name is actually double entendre," explains the eque
1 CONC strian. "I'm a student of dressage and have been training w
1 CONC ith Joan Humphry for two years and competing. My life toda
1 CONC y has me back and forth on two roads, bridal path and bridl
1 CONC e path. I love them both."
1 CONT The Bridal Path and The Paper Mill
1 CONT Cypress Square, 13451 McGregor Blvd., Fort Myers

1 CONC From The Grand Rapids (MI) Press
1 CONT
1 CONT HAMP - Dr. Arthur Kautz Hamp. Dr. Arthur K. Hamp, highly-re
1 CONC garded physician of internal medicine, devoted father and h
1 CONC usband, avid outdoorsman and conservationist, and informe
1 CONC d student of world affairs and history, died on January 1
1 CONC 1 after a lengthy illness. Dr. Hamp was born in Kokomo, Ind
1 CONC iana on November 29, 1921 and attended Butler University i
1 CONC n Indianapolis, where he was a third generation member of P
1 CONC hi Delta Theta Fraternity and graduated with a BS degree i
1 CONC n 1943. At Butler, he met and later married fellow studen
1 CONC t Sarah Jane (Sally) Steinbaugh on April 22, 1945. Dr. Ham
1 CONC p attended Indiana University Medical School, earning his M
1 CONC D degree in 1945 in a program accelerated due to WWII. He p
1 CONC erformed his medical internship at Indiana University Hospi
1 CONC tal from 1945 to 1946 and then enlisted as Captain US Arm
1 CONC y Medical Corps stationed at Percy Jones General Hospital
1 CONC , Battle Creek, MI, from 1946 to 1948. He served a genera
1 CONC l internal medicine residency at Blodgett Memorial Hospita
1 CONC l in Grand Rapids, MI from 1948 to 1951, punctuated by a on
1 CONC e year residency at University of Michigan Medical School i
1 CONC n 1949. Dr. Hamp received his Board Certification for Inter
1 CONC nal Medicine in 1953 and became a staff member at Blodget
1 CONC t Memorial Hospital from 1951 to 1982, serving as Chief o
1 CONC f Staff from 1971 to 1973. After his medical certification
1 CONC , he joined the pioneering Grand Rapids internal medicine p
1 CONC artnership of Beeman and Avery, remaining an active partne
1 CONC r until his retirement in 1982. Dr. Hamp became a Fellow i
1 CONC n the American College of Physicians in 1963. Dr. Hamp marr
1 CONC ied Jerrie Waterman in 1992, widow of medical colleague Dr
1 CONC . Donald Waterman after the death of Sally. His many and va
1 CONC ried interests included music, camping, hunting, skiing, sa
1 CONC iling and boating, photography and travel, and he possesse
1 CONC d considerable knowledge of natural phenomena, flora and fa
1 CONC una. He was keenly interested in the geopolitics of the Col
1 CONC d War and the medical profession's role in maintaining a he
1 CONC althy and growing democracy. He exhibited a powerful intell
1 CONC ect and a genuine curiosity about the world, the very embod
1 CONC iment of a true lifelong learner. He was a tireless gathere
1 CONC r and keeper of information and a self-described "Jack of A
1 CONC ll Trades". This unique and wonderful man, beloved by his f
1 CONC amily and his patients, is survived by Jerrie Waterman Hamp
1 CONC ; his four children, James Arthur Hamp of Ashland, WI (Lesl
1 CONC ie), Steven Kautz Hamp of Ann Arbor, MI (Sheila), Melissa H
1 CONC amp of Davison, MI (Michael Lechner), Philip David Hamp o
1 CONC f Chicago, IL (Laura Hochuli); ten grandchildren (Andrew, E
1 CONC ric, Michael, Kiff, Peter, Sarah, Alison, Claire, Leigh, Ja
1 CONC mes. Three step-children include Susan Gordon of Grand Rapi
1 CONC ds, Philip F. Waterman of Ft. Myers, FL, and G. Scott Water
1 CONC man of Charlotte, VT (Sandy Steingard) and six step-grandch
1 CONC ildren, Meredith Hedeman, Laura Gordon, Julie Magardino, Le
1 CONC e Mumme, Martha and Donna Waterman. In addition, Dr. Hamp i
1 CONC s survived by three siblings, Robert J. Hamp, Jr. of Hought
1 CONC on Lake, MI, Julianna Mehlig of Kokomo, IN and David F. Ham
1 CONC p of Mercer Island, WA. The family extends its sincere than
1 CONC ks and gratitude to the caregivers and staff at Porter Hill
1 CONC s for the many kindnesses given to Dr. Hamp during his stay
1 CONC . Memorial services will be held on Saturday, January 17, 2
1 CONC 009 at 11 a.m. in the Meeting House of Porter Hills Village
1 CONC , with a reception to follow. In lieu of flowers, please ma
1 CONC ke donations in Dr. Hamp's name to The Alzheimer's Associat
1 CONC ion, 213 Sheldon Blvd., Grand Rapids, MI 49503 or The Porte
1 CONC r Hills Benevolent Fund, 4450 Cascade Rd., Grand Rapids, M
1 CONC I 49546. The family is being served by Metcalf & Jonkhoff F
1 CONC uneral Service

1 CONC n March 31, 2009. The son of Gladys and Milton Orwin, Jim w
1 CONC as born on February 7, 1931 and spent most of his life in K
1 CONC alamazoo. He graduated from State High where he was a stand
1 CONC out in his favorite sports, basketball and tennis. Jim wa
1 CONC s a high school state champion and nationally ranked tenni
1 CONC s player. After attending WMU and Michigan State, he serve
1 CONC d in the U.S. Army. Jim opened the first Redwood & Ross sto
1 CONC re in Columbus, Ohio before returning to Kalamazoo and expa
1 CONC nding Redwood & Ross to 22 stores throughout the Midwest. H
1 CONC e served on the boards of Industrial State Bank and Arcadi
1 CONC a Bank. His best times were spent with his beloved wife, Me
1 CONC lissa, his three sons and their families and a huge circl
1 CONC e of friends, young and old. Jim enjoyed paddle tennis, pla
1 CONC ying cards, investing, winters in Florida, and taking trip
1 CONC s to the NCAA Final Four, Las Vegas and Biloxi. He fough
1 CONC t a long battle with cancer with determination and grace. J
1 CONC im was profoundly loved and will be greatly missed. His wif
1 CONC e of 14 years, Melissa (Fleming), survives along with his t
1 CONC hree sons, John (Holly), of Madison, WI, Jeffrey (Charlene)
1 CONC , and Jerry (Carla) of Kalamazoo; his brother, Dr. John Wei
1 CONC l; and six grandchildren who brought him joy and pride, Gre
1 CONC gory, Carly, Colleen, Michael, Audrey and Ben. Also survivi
1 CONC ng is his former wife, Janet, cousins, nieces and nephews
1 CONC . Jim was preceded in death by his parents; his stepmother
1 CONC , Pauline; and his brother and sister-in-law, Duke and Bett
1 CONC y Orwin. The family will hold a memorial service at the Gul
1 CONC l Lake Country Club at 3:30 PM on Monday, April 6, 2009. Me

1 CONT Formerly of Kalamazoo
1 CONT Betty Orwin, 76, a resident of Kalamazoo for more than 70 y
1 CONC ears, passed away March 13, 2006, in Annapolis, Maryland wh
1 CONC ere she had been living for the past 5 years. Born Elizabet
1 CONC h Ann Hutton on October 31, 1929, she was the daughter of t
1 CONC he late Frederick and Helen (Dye) Hutton. She attended West
1 CONC ern Michigan University, graduating from its secretarial pr
1 CONC ogram and worked at Sutherland Paper Company. She married W
1 CONC alter Lawrence "Duke" Orwin on October 16, 1954. They enjoy
1 CONC ed more than 36 years of marriage until his death in 1991
1 CONC . She and her husband were members of Temple B'nai Israel a
1 CONC nd the Kalamazoo Country Club. She was an active voluntee
1 CONC r at SCORE and in later years worked as an administrative a
1 CONC ssistant for the Kalamazoo Public Schools and the America
1 CONC n Red Cross. She was also a member of the Service Club of K
1 CONC alamazoo, which became the Junior League, volunteering at P
1 CONC retty Lake Vacation Camp, the Child Guidance Clinic and oth
1 CONC er Service Club charities. She devoted her time to many int
1 CONC erests, especially to her family, gardening, golf, bowlin
1 CONC g and animals. She is survived by her daughter and son-in-l
1 CONC aw Angela and Dan Dunlap of Annapolis, Maryland; her son an
1 CONC d daughter-in-law Larry and Heide Orwin of Brecksville, Ohi
1 CONC o; and four grandchildren, Madeline and Emily Dunlap and Br
1 CONC ittany and Troy Orwin. Funeral services will be held 11:0
1 CONC 0 Saturday morning at Langeland Family Funeral Homes, Wests
1 CONC ide Chapel, 3926 S. 9th Street. Burial in Mountain Home Cem
1 CONC etery will follow services. Visitation will be held Frida
1 CONC y from 6-8pm at the funeral home. Memorial contributions ma
1 CONC y be made to the Kalamazoo Community Foundation, Hospice o
1 CONC f Greater Kalamazoo or a charity of personal choice.

1 CONC From March 14, 2002 email from Ralph Loeb, Jr. (Adele Loe
1 CONC b Frohsin's nephew):
1 CONT
1 CONT "Louella's second husband's name was George.  I think tha
1 CONC t Joe was in business with George, but my memory tells me t
1 CONC hat business went bankrupt - I think it was a clothing stor
1 CONC e.  Anyhow, according to what I can recall, they never repa
1 CONC id the debts and after Joe died his attorney told us that t
1 CONC here might be a claim against the estate.  Since the bankru
1 CONC ptcy took place so long ago, there was never a claim."
1 CONT
1 CONT Later, found in New York Times, August 25, 1918 (page 19)..
1 CONC .Louella married George F. SULTZBACH at the Biltmore Hote
1 CONC l in New York City on August 24, 1918.  Announcement phot
1 CONC o shown in "Pictures".
1 CONT  Marriage Certificate No. 23121
1 CONT
1 CONT "George F. George" was the professional name of George F. S
1 CONC ultzbach, Clothier
1 CONT
1 CONT WPCH - 810 AM, Hoboken
1 CONT The origins of this station date back to July 15, 1924 whe
1 CONC n it signed on as WFBH, owned by the Concourse Radio Corp.
1 CONC , on 1100 AM, sharing time with New York stations WRW and W
1 CONC EBJ.
1 CONT Studios were located on the ground floor of the Hotel Majes
1 CONC tic at Central Park West and 72nd St, with the transmitte
1 CONC r on the roof of the hotel.
1 CONT A typical day's schedule consisted mostly of "potted palm m
1 CONC usic," including occasional recitals by the Majestic Strin
1 CONC g Ensemble.
1 CONT One program that got the station in trouble was "Health Tal
1 CONC k," which was sponsored by the House of Health organization
1 CONC , operated by Dr. Leonard Lincoln Landis.
1 CONT However, it was not sanctioned by the New York Medical Soci
1 CONC ety or the Hotel Majestic.
1 CONT It got to the point that the hotel would shut down the stat
1 CONC ion's power when the show was scheduled to be on.
1 CONT After many court battles, the station was able to stay on
1 CONC , however, by the autumn of 1926, WFBH was sold to ŮCbŮDGeorg
1 CONC e F. George (the professional name of clothier George Sultz
1 CONC bach)ŮC/bŮD d/b/a the Peoples Broadcasting Co. and was rename
1 CONC d WPCH on November 6, 1926.

2 TYPE Obituary
2 DATE Dec 1923
2 NOTE AUTO VICTIM DIES THURSDAY EVENING Mrs. Florine Loeb Succumb
3 CONC s to Injuries; Wright Charged with Manslaughter
3 CONT
3 CONT      Mrs. Florine Loeb, died at 6 o'clock Thursday night a
3 CONC t a local hospital, where she had not quitted her bed sinc
3 CONC e she was struck by an automobile last Monday.
3 CONT      Ernest Wright, 16-year-old school boy driver of the ca
3 CONC r which is said to have struck Mrs. Loeb, near Court Stree
3 CONC t Methodist church, was arrested two hours after the latter
3 CONC 's death. He was later released in $1,000 bail, charged wit
3 CONC h manslaughter. Hearing for Wright in police court was se
3 CONC t for the evening of December 20.
3 CONT      When a change was noted in the condition of Mrs. Loe
3 CONC b during the early morning, police officials visited Sidne
3 CONC y Lanier High School and carried Wright to headquarters, wh
3 CONC ere he was later released. Prior to the detention of Wrigh
3 CONC t he had been free in bail on a charge of collision preferr
3 CONC ed the night of the running down of the victim.
3 CONT      Mrs. Loeb was a native of Reichshoffen, Alsace, France
3 CONC , coming to Montgomery when but a young woman. She was marr
3 CONC ied to the late Leon Loeb in 1858 and during the many year
3 CONC s of her residence in Montgomery won the esteem of a larg
3 CONC e circle of friends who will be grieved to learn of her dea
3 CONC th.
3 CONT      She was a member of Temple Beth Or, the Hebrew Ladies
3 CONC ' Benevolent Society and the Council of Jewish Women.
3 CONT      Mrs. Loeb is survived by two daughters, Misses Carri
3 CONC e and Adele Loeb, and one son, Ralph Loeb, all of Montgomer
3 CONC y; one sister, Mrs. Emelie Winter of Philadelphia, Pa., an
3 CONC d one brother, Michael Loeb of Montgomery.
3 CONT      The funeral services will be held from the residence
3 CONC , 221 Clayton Street, at 2 o'clock Friday afternoon. Rabb
3 CONC i William B. Schwartz officiating. Interment will be at Oak
3 CONC wood.
3 CONT Montgomery Advertiser

1 CONC KASLE Barbara Lee Rosenzweig Barbara Lee Rosenzweig Kasle
1 CONC , 81 years, died August 29, 2009, while under the care of E
1 CONC beid Residence Hospice. The daughter of Harry and Blossom R
1 CONC osenzweig and a graduate of Scott High School, she attende
1 CONC d the University of Illinois and Ohio State University marr
1 CONC ying Richard Kasle also of Toledo who survives. An outstand
1 CONC ing homemaker who had unusual qualities, talents, and socia
1 CONC l graces, she became a source for family and friends in pla
1 CONC nning events and gatherings. Many close friends relied on h
1 CONC er for their etiquette questions and she became known unoff
1 CONC icially as Toledo's "Miss Manners". Elegant but simple wer
1 CONC e her tastes as evidenced by her home's decor and her wardr
1 CONC obe choices. Love of the arts including Opera and Ballet, h
1 CONC er involvement in Toledo's Jewish Community as the first Pr
1 CONC esident of the Local Chapter of ORT, a Life Member of Hadas
1 CONC sah, Council of Jewish Women and support of numerous loca
1 CONC l charities helped her lead a fruitful and giving life. Sur
1 CONC viving besides husband, Richard are sons, Thomas (Jan) Kasl
1 CONC e, Andrew (Donna) Kasle of Cleveland, OH, and Joseph (Debor
1 CONC ah) Kasle of Milwaukee, WI; sister, Luanne Schoenberg of Lo
1 CONC s Angeles, CA; also grandchildren, Joshua, Dorie, Ari, Nic
1 CONC k and Lucas. Thanks is extended by the family to the Neurol
1 CONC ogy Intensive Care Unit at Toledo Hospital and Ebeid Reside
1 CONC nce Hospice for their care and support. A Memorial is plann
1 CONC ed for Sunday, September 6, 2009. For arrangement details i
1 CONC ncluding location and times please contact the Robert H. Wi
1 CONC ck/Wisniewski Funeral Home at 419-535-5840. Memorial tribut
1 CONC es suggested in the form of contributions to the United Jew
1 CONC ish Council of Greater Toledo.

1 CONC ŮCbŮDAlfred E. Perlman
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDAlfred Edward Perlman was born November 22, 1902, in  S
1 CONC t. Paul, Minnesota,  the son of Louis and Leah Perlman. H
1 CONC e married Adele Sylvia Emrich, June 15, 1937. Together the
1 CONC y had three children: Michael Louis; Lee Alfred; Constance
1 CONT ŮCbŮD
1 CONT Family ŮC/bŮDAlfred Edward Perlman was born November 22, 1902
1 CONC , in St. Paul, Minnesota , the son of Louis and Leah Perlma
1 CONC n. He married Adele Sylvia Emrich, June 15, 1937. Togethe
1 CONC r they had three children: Michael Louis; Lee Alfred; Const
1 CONC ance.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDEducation ŮC/bŮDPerlman graduated from the Massachusetts I
1 CONC nstitute of Technology  with a Bachelor of Science in civi
1 CONC l engineering  in 1923. He graduated from the Harvard Busin
1 CONC ess School  with a Masters degree in railway transportatio
1 CONC n in 1931. He was awarded honorary degrees from Clarkson Un
1 CONC iversity  and Depauw University .
1 CONT ŮCbŮDCareer ŮC/bŮDPerlman joined the Northern Pacific Railwa
1 CONC y  in field construction in 1923. Over the next nine year
1 CONC s he served as a track laborer, draftsman, roadmaster, an
1 CONC d finally assistant to the vice-president. In 1934 he joine
1 CONC d the Reconstruction Finance Corporation  as a consultant
1 CONC . He joined the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy Railroad  a
1 CONC s an assistant engineer in 1935, helping reconstruct Midwes
1 CONC tern lines hit by heavy flooding. In 1936 he joined the Den
1 CONC ver and Rio Grande Western Railroad  as engineer in charg
1 CONC e of maintenance-of-way work; in 1941 he was promoted to be
1 CONC come chief engineer of the Rio Grande. From 1947 to 1952 Pe
1 CONC rlman was general manager of the Rio Grande, and from 195
1 CONC 2 to 1954, executive vice-president. In 1954 Perlman was ap
1 CONC pointed president of the New York Central Railroad  in th
1 CONC e wake of the suicide of Robert R. Young . For the next fou
1 CONC rteen years Perlman worked to strengthen and improve the Ce
1 CONC ntral in the face of ever increasing automobile, truck, an
1 CONC d airline competition. Within six years Perlman succeeded i
1 CONC n reducing the Central's long-term debt by nearly $100 mill
1 CONC ion; reduced its passenger service deficit from $42 to $24.
1 CONC 8 million; and by 1959 had increased earnings to $1.29 pe
1 CONC r share, double that of 1958. Included in Perlman's effort
1 CONC s was the famous Jet Train of 1966, when a Budd Rail Diese
1 CONC l Car numbered M-497 was mated to a General Electric  jet e
1 CONC ngine. Running in Ohio, it set a speed record of 183.85 mil
1 CONC es per hour. Perlman pushed for modernization of the Centra
1 CONC l's physical plant, which included paring down main line ro
1 CONC utes to single track with centralized traffic control, an
1 CONC d the construction of modern freight facilities. On of th
1 CONC e largest projects was at Selkirk Yard outside of Albany, N
1 CONC ew York . Billed as a twenty million dollar project circa 1
1 CONC 966, the yard was rebuilt and automated, increasing capacit
1 CONC y to handle 8,329 cars a day from 2,300. When the project w
1 CONC as completed in 1968, the New York Central had spent $29 mi
1 CONC llion, including $4 million for a diesel locomotive servic
1 CONC e facility. When opened, the rebuilt terminal was named fo
1 CONC r Alfred E. Perlman Yard. Beginning February 1, 1968, Perlm
1 CONC an was president, director, and chief administrative offic
1 CONC e of the Penn Central Transportation , the ill-fated merge
1 CONC r of the New York Central with the Pennsylvania Railroa
1 CONC d . Penn Central operated a system with 40,000 miles of tra
1 CONC ck in fourteen states and two Canadian provinces. It had to
1 CONC tal assets of $6.3 billion and annual revenues of nearly $
1 CONC 2 billion. Yet, within two years of merger, competition an
1 CONC d a bungled integration of the merged companies resulted i
1 CONC n the largest corporate bankruptcy  in American history t
1 CONC o that time. Perlman, along with Stuart T. Saunders  (1909-
1 CONC 1987) and David C. Bevan , both from the Pennsylvania Railr
1 CONC oad, were dismissed from the Penn Central on June 8, 1970
1 CONC . Saunders, an attorney, returned to private practice, havi
1 CONC ng held onto his stock and suffered a personal loss of $700
1 CONC ,000 (in 1970 dollars). Bevan was tried and acquitted for e
1 CONC mbezzling $4 million in Penn Central funds. In the wake o
1 CONC f the Penn Central debacle, however, Perlman was hired to r
1 CONC evitalize the Western Pacific Railroad . He served as the w
1 CONC estern road's president from December 1, 1970 to January 1
1 CONC , 1973, and chairman from 1970 to 1976. He retired June 24
1 CONC , 1976, and that year the Western Pacific was sold to a pri
1 CONC vate investment group led by Perlman protege Robert Flanner
1 CONC y. The Western Pacific was merged into the Union Pacific Ra
1 CONC ilroad  in 1982. Perlman died April 30, 1983 in San Francis
1 CONC co, California . He is remembered as one of ŮCiŮDTrainsŮC/iŮD m
1 CONC agazine's ten most prominent railroad presidents of the Twe
1 CONC ntieth century. ŮCbŮD

1 CONC Colonel Goldsby built the present-day Tepper's Building aro
1 CONC und 1850. Tepper's was originally two buildings with a cent
1 CONC er wall, but the floor and roof were destroyed by fire in 1
1 CONC 865. Abraham Oberndorf combined the two buildings and insta
1 CONC lled a new front in 1904, making it the only five-story bui
1 CONC lding in Selma.
1 CONT Ben and Jake Tepper started Tepper Bros. Mercantile Co. i
1 CONC n 1906. The store moved to 25-29 Broad Street in 1913, wher
1 CONC e Ben Tepper and Max Hagedorn sold dry goods, men's and wom
1 CONC en's apparel, house furnishings, toys and shoes. The busine
1 CONC ss was renamed Tepper's Department Store in 1967.
1 CONT Tepper's Department Store operated in the Tepper's Buildin
1 CONC g through 1972. For the majority of the past 35 years, thi
1 CONC s building has stood empty and uncared for. The Freedom Fou
1 CONC ndation bought the building in January 2007 and began renov
1 CONC ations in March 2007, when they removed the green aluminu
1 CONC m siding that covered the original stone exterior.

1 CONC Edward N. Wolf, 71, Indianapolis, passed away on February 3
1 CONC , 2009. Edward was born on April 4, 1937 in Indianapolis t
1 CONC o the late Samuel and Ruth Wolf. He was the owner and presi
1 CONC dent of Sam Wolf, Inc., a company that spanned three genera
1 CONC tions of the Wolf family. In 1953, Edward earned his pilot'
1 CONC s license. He participated in the Mt. Comfort Air Show an
1 CONC d in various other air shows as well as many competitions
1 CONC . Edward was a U.S. veteran, having served in the Air Forc
1 CONC e Reserves. Also, he was a member of the Congregation Beth-
1 CONC El Zedeck, Beth-El Zedeck Men's Club, International Aerobat
1 CONC ic Club, Zeta Beta Tau Fraternity, Aircraft Owners and Pilo
1 CONC ts Association, Quiet Birdmen, and Monument Masonic Lodge.
1 CONT
1 CONT Survivors include his beloved wife, Bernice "Bonnie" Anders
1 CONC on Wolf; son, John (Anne) Wolf of California; and brother
1 CONC , Dr. Harry (Jackie) Wolf. Services will be held at Aaron-R
1 CONC uben-Nelson Mortuary on Friday, February 6, 2009 at 10:00 a
1 CONC .m. Burial will follow at Beth-El North Cemetery. Memoria
1 CONC l contributions may be made to Congregation Beth-El Zedeck
1 CONC , the American Cancer Society, or to the donor's favorite c
1 CONC harity.

1 CONT ŮCbŮDPERSONAL DATA:ŮC/bŮD Born August 23, 1956, in Indianapolis
1 CONC , Indiana. He enjoys sport aerobatic flying, scuba diving
1 CONC , handball, running, and water skiing. His parents, Dr. an
1 CONC d Mrs. Harry Wolf, reside in Indianapolis.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDEDUCATION:ŮC/bŮD Graduated from North Central High School
1 CONC , Indianapolis, Indiana, in 1974; received a bachelor of sc
1 CONC ience degree in Electrical Engineering from Purdue Universi
1 CONC ty in 1978, and a Doctor of Medicine from Indiana Universit
1 CONC y in 1982. He completed his medical internship (1983) at Me
1 CONC thodist Hospital in Indianapolis, Indiana, and USAF fligh
1 CONC t surgeon training at Brooks Air Force Base in San Antonio
1 CONC , Texas. Dr. Wolf has completed U.S. Astronaut and Russia
1 CONC n Cosmonaut training.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDSPECIAL HONORS:ŮC/bŮD Recipient of the NASA Exceptional En
1 CONC gineering Achievement Medal (1990) was NASA Inventor of th
1 CONC e Year, 1992. Dr. Wolf graduated "With Distinction" from th
1 CONC e Honors Curriculum in Electrical Engineering at Purdue Uni
1 CONC versity and received an Academic Achievement Award upon gra
1 CONC duation from I.U. Medical School (combined research program
1 CONC ). He is a Purdue "Distinguished Engineering Alumnus." He r
1 CONC eceived the Carl R. Ruddell scholarship award for researc
1 CONC h in medical ultrasonic digital signal and image processing
1 CONC . He is a member of Eta Kappa Knu and Phi Eta Sigma honorar
1 CONC y societies. Dr. Wolf has received 15 U.S. Patents, publish
1 CONC ed over 40 technical publications or papers, and received o
1 CONC ver 20 Space Act Awards primarily for 3-dimensional tissu
1 CONC e engineering technologies for which he received the Texa
1 CONC s State Bar Patent of the Year in 1994. Dr. Wolf has receiv
1 CONC ed an additional Honorary Doctorate from Indiana Universit
1 CONC y and 4 Spaceflight Medals.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDEXPERIENCE (PRIOR):ŮC/bŮD As a research scientist at the I
1 CONC ndianapolis Center for Advanced Research from 1980 to 1983
1 CONC , Dr. Wolf established himself as a pioneer in the developm
1 CONC ent of modern medical ultrasonic image processing technique
1 CONC s. This technology applied pulse compression digital RF pul
1 CONC se echo signal processing to improve image resolution and e
1 CONC nable target parameter extraction, techniques now utilize
1 CONC d by most commercial systems. He also developed novel doppl
1 CONC er demodulation techniques extending the range velocity pro
1 CONC duct limitations inherent to conventional pulsed doppler sy
1 CONC stems. He served as a USAF senior flight surgeon in the Ai
1 CONC r National Guard (1983 to 2004) achieving the rank of Lt. C
1 CONC olonel. He has logged over 2000 hours of flight time includ
1 CONC ing air combat training as a weapons systems officer (F4 Ph
1 CONC antom jet), T-38 Talon, and flies competition sport aerobat
1 CONC ics (Christen Eagle).
1 CONT ŮCbŮDEXPERIENCE (NASA):ŮC/bŮD Dr. Wolf served as Chief of the A
1 CONC stronaut Office Extravehicular Activity (EVA) Branch for mu
1 CONC ch of Space Station assembly. He led a team responsible fo
1 CONC r the development, test, and execution of spacewalks from t
1 CONC he International Space Station and Space Shuttle. This tea
1 CONC m plays a critical role for Space Station assembly, mainten
1 CONC ance, and repair; requiring innovations to extend EVA capab
1 CONC ility in areas of hardware, techniques, and human performan
1 CONC ce. Dr. Wolf has logged 168 days, 12 hours, 56 minutes, 0
1 CONC 4 seconds in space over four separate missions, including l
1 CONC ong duration (128 day) on the Russian MIR space station, tr
1 CONC ained and conducted completely in the Russian language. H
1 CONC e has conducted a total of 7 spacewalks, utilizing both th
1 CONC e American and Russian spacesuits, and has logged 47 hour
1 CONC s and 05 mins of extravehicular activity. He is an active p
1 CONC ublic speaker and is called upon to represent NASA in a wid
1 CONC e variety of venues to communicate the experience and impor
1 CONC tance of human spaceflight.
1 CONT In 1983, Dr. Wolf joined the Medical Sciences Division, Joh
1 CONC nson Space Center, Houston, Texas. He was responsible for d
1 CONC evelopment of the American Flight Echocardiograph utilized
1 CONC , in space, for investigating cardiovascular physiology i
1 CONC n microgravity. This work required synthesis of spacecraf
1 CONC t avionics integration, human physiology, and space operati
1 CONC ons to acquire fundamental cardiovascular data for human sp
1 CONC ace exploration and revealing new Earth based physiologica
1 CONC l principles. Upon completion he was assigned as chief engi
1 CONC neer for design of the Space Station Medical Facility, no
1 CONC w operational on orbit. This work pioneered concepts in tel
1 CONC emedicine, medical informatics, and bio-instrumentation. I
1 CONC n 1986 he became Chief Engineer (and later Program Manager
1 CONC ) of the "Space Bioreactor," a biotechnology based tissue e
1 CONC ngineering and cancer research program. This team, under Dr
1 CONC . Wolf's leadership, achieved the development of "state o
1 CONC f the art" tissue engineering systems now widely used for b
1 CONC oth commercial and research purposes on Earth. Dr. Wolf fos
1 CONC tered the successful technology transfer to private industr
1 CONC y and to academic laboratory applications. Special skills d
1 CONC eveloped include real time computer process control, commun
1 CONC ications, power systems, bioprocessing, fluid dynamics, aer
1 CONC ospace physiology, and aerospace medicine. In these roles D
1 CONC r. Wolf was responsible for technical and multidisciplinar
1 CONC y team leadership, mult-million dollar budget, systems desi
1 CONC gn, safety (electrical, biological), and spacecraft integra
1 CONC tion. This "on schedule" program is now a core biotechnolog
1 CONC y research facility on the International Space Station.
1 CONT Selected as a NASA astronaut in January 1990, Dr. Wolf beca
1 CONC me qualified for space flight in July 1991. His technical a
1 CONC ssignments have included Orbiter vehicle processing and tes
1 CONC t at Kennedy Space Center (1991-1992) and spacecraft commun
1 CONC ications (CAPCOM, 1994-1995) on console for the 1st and 3r
1 CONC d Shuttle-MIR rendezvous and docking. He is a senior EVA (S
1 CONC pacewalk) Instructor and has qualified with the Shuttle Rob
1 CONC otic Manipulator System (Robot Arm). Dr. Wolf completed Cos
1 CONC monaut Training at the Gagarin Cosmonaut Training Center, S
1 CONC tar City, Russia. Dr. Wolf consults at all levels of NASA m
1 CONC anagement in development of spaceflight policy and progra
1 CONC m execution.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDSPACE FLIGHT EXPERIENCE:ŮC/bŮD ŮCbŮDSTS-58 ColumbiaŮC/bŮD (Oc
1 CONC t 16-Nov 1, 1993) was a dedicated Spacelab life sciences re
1 CONC search mission. The crew conducted neurovestibular, cardiov
1 CONC ascular, cardiopulmonary, metabolic, and musculoskeletal re
1 CONC search utilizing microgravity to reveal fundamental human p
1 CONC hysiology otherwise masked by earth gravity. Mission durati
1 CONC on 14 days, 12 minutes, 32 seconds, a record at that time.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDNASA-MIR 6ŮC/bŮD (Sept 25, 1997 - Jan 31, 1998), this 6t
1 CONC h mission of the joint "Shuttle-MIR" long duration spacefli
1 CONC ght program, immediately following "the" fire and collision
1 CONC , and recovering from multiple total power failures, playe
1 CONC d a core role to establish the international relationship
1 CONC s serving the foundation of the current International Spac
1 CONC e Station (ISS) Program. Dr. Wolf performed Cosmonaut engin
1 CONC eering and scientific duties on the Russian MIR Space Stati
1 CONC on, including 9 EVA hours in the Russian ORLAN spacesuit. M
1 CONC ission duration was 128 days. Wolf launched on STS-86 and r
1 CONC eturned on STS-89.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDSTS-112 AtlantisŮC/bŮD (Oct 7-18, 2002) and ŮCbŮDSTS-127 End
1 CONC eavorŮC/bŮD (July-August, 2009). These were on-orbit heavy IS

1 CONC rol, and power management systems. Wolf's primary duties we
1 CONC re as lead spacewalker (EV1), and rendezvous navigation spe
1 CONC cialist. He performed a total of 6 spacewalks (19 hours an

1 CONC n Beverly Hills, says his hairpiece trade has gone up at le
1 CONC ast 200% in just the past year. "It used to be men of 50 o

1 CONC 0 or 35. It's part ego and part it's just annoying to be ba
1 CONC ld." Though show biz types like Bing Crosby and Frank Sinat
1 CONC ra are still leaders in the wiggy set, "ordinary people ar
1 CONC e going in for the same routine," says Mandel.

1 CONC Dr. Howard L. Kahn, 95, passed away peacefully December 2
1 CONC , 2010 in Hollywood, FL. Howard was born in Indianapolis, I
1 CONC N, July 28, 1915. Howard graduated from Manuel High School
1 CONC , received a Rector Scholarship to DePaw University and gra
1 CONC duated from Indiana University Medical School. He complete
1 CONC d his residency in Obstetrics and Gynecology at the Univers
1 CONC ity of Chicago. He and his brother Dr. Alexander Kahn pract
1 CONC iced medicine together for many years in Indianapolis. Howa
1 CONC rd served in World War II in the Asian Pacific Theatre, ret
1 CONC iring as a Major. Howard was an avid rosarian winning man
1 CONC y trophies and competitions, an accomplished bridge playe
1 CONC r and a Life Master. He was a photographer specializing i
1 CONC n Minox photography, loved to travel and was a supporter o
1 CONC f the arts. Howard was a member of the American Medical Ass
1 CONC ociation, a Fellow of the American College of Obstetrics an
1 CONC d Gynecology, and a Fellow of the American College of Surge
1 CONC ry. He was a member of Congregation Beth-El Zedek and a Had
1 CONC assah Associate. Immediate survivors include his loving wif
1 CONC e Leah Dee (Deedle) of almost 60 years, his daughters, Kati
1 CONC e (David DeBlander), Dr. Janis Kahn (Jeffrey Greve), and so
1 CONC n Raymond (Motoko) Kahn, beloved grandchildren, Kahlia (Jos
1 CONC hua) Brigham, Benjamin and Alexander Greve, and Remi Kahn
1 CONC , and great grandchildren, Braeden and Madelyn Brigham. Sur
1 CONC vivors also include his dear sister, Ann Glasser and dear s
1 CONC ister in law, Margaret Hansen Kahn. He was predeceased by h
1 CONC is brother, Dr. Alexander Kahn. Funeral services will be he
1 CONC ld Tuesday, December 7 at 2 PM, at Aaron Rubin-Nelson Mortu
1 CONC ary, 11411 N. Michigan Rd. Memorial Contributions may be ma
1 CONC de to Hadassah, 1040 E. 86th St. Indianapolis, 46240.

1 CONC JOSEPH ABRAHAM FRIEDLAENDER, a reformed rabbi in Germany i
1 CONC n the Eighteenth Century, is the first ancestor of whom we
1 CONC * have any record.  His daughter Emille married JOSEPH HAGE
1 CONC DORN, the first HAGEDORN of whom we* have any knowledge.  T
1 CONC his is the family of JOSEPH and EMILLE FRIEDLAENDER HAGEDOR
1 CONC N.
1 CONT
1 CONT * "we" = charts provided by Helene Herzfeld Parsons in 200
1 CONC 0 and Henry Stern in 2002.
1 CONT
1 CONT There are listed here the direct descendents of Joseph Abra
1 CONC ham Friedlander.
1 CONT
1 CONT There appears to be an entry about him in The Jewish Encycl
1 CONC opedia by Funk & Wagnals Company 1903.
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCbŮDŮCiŮDFRIEDLčANDER, JOSEPH ABRAHAM: ŮCuŮD ŮC/uŮDŮC/bŮDGerman rabbi
1 CONC ; born at Kolin, Bohemia, 1753; died at Brilon, Westphalia
1 CONC , Nov. 26, 1852. He was the nephew of David Friedlčander, fr
1 CONC om whom he imbibed a great enthusiasm for progressive Judai
1 CONC sm. After attending the Talmud school of Ezekiel Landau a
1 CONC t Prague, he went to Presburg. In 1784 he became chief rabb
1 CONC i of Westphalia and the principality of Wittgenstein, retai
1 CONC ning this office until his death.  Friedlčander was one of t
1 CONC he first German rabbis to advocate through speech and pen t
1 CONC he reform of Judaism. He abolished in his district the seco
1 CONC nd day of the festivals; openly and decisively opposed man
1 CONC y obsolete Jewish mourning customs; and declared, in his re
1 CONC sponsum on "Die Vertrčaglichkeit der Freien Forschung mit de
1 CONC m Rabbineramte," that the dicta of the Mishnah and the Talm
1 CONC ud are not binding for all time. He published "Shoresh Yose
1 CONC f," on abolishing the second day of the festivals (in Hebre
1 CONC w and German; Hanover and Brilon, 1834), and "Mahadura Bath
1 CONC ra," a supplement to the foregoing, and containing a corres
1 CONC pondence with Aaron Chorin on questions of Reform (Hanover
1 CONC , 1835).  Bibliography: Jost, Neuere Gesch. der IsraelitenŮC
1 CONC /iŮD, i. 316, iii. 175;  L. Stein, ŮCiŮDIsraelitischer Volksle
1 CONC hrerŮC/iŮD, ii. 295 ŮCiŮDet seq.ŮC/iŮDS.

1 CONT FROHSIN, MR. RALPH age 92, of Alexander City, Ala. died Thu
1 CONC rsday, October 13, 1994. He was Chairman of local Red Cros
1 CONC s Chapter for 20 years through the Great Depression and aft
1 CONC er World War II... In his Memoirs, ""I'm Still Here'' Publi
1 CONC shed 1989--again 1991.. He said, ""A Man is nothing withou
1 CONC t a strong family around him, and I am grateful for my chil
1 CONC dren and grandchildren and for the happiness they have brou
1 CONC ght

1 CONC During the 1930's the Red Cross assisted in a program to el
1 CONC iminate pellagra, which was epidemic in the South.  Pellagr
1 CONC a is disease caused by a deficiency of niacin (one of th
1 CONC e B complex vitamins) in the diet. Symptoms of the diseas
1 CONC e include scaly skin sores, diarrhea, and mental illness
1 CONC .  Mrs. Leon Frohsin served as Chairman of the campaign t
1 CONC o eliminate pellagra in Atlanta.

1 CONC Kim Frohsin moved to California 1979.
1 CONT
1 CONT She studied at San Diego State University, San Francisco Ar
1 CONC t Academy, L'Institut pour les Etudiants, and at L'Ecole de
1 CONC s Arts Decoratifs in France. Her inspiration is drawn fro
1 CONC m the Bay Area Figurative Movement artists such as Manual N
1 CONC eri, Richard Diebenkorn, Nathan Oliveira, and Paul Wonner
1 CONC . Her extraordinary knowledge of color she attributes to th
1 CONC e work of Wayne Thiebaud. Following in the tradition of the
1 CONC se artists, Frohsin creates vital and passionate new figura
1 CONC tive paintings with an abstract quality. Her work evolves f
1 CONC rom the figure (model) as a base, extending her sensual mar
1 CONC ks in exquisitely gestural ways. These subjects "are simple
1 CONC , solitary objects or themes that are real, recycled by m
1 CONC y imagination and my perception and memory of it," Frohsi
1 CONC n says.
1 CONT
1 CONT Frohsin currently lives in North Beach, a block away from t
1 CONC he San Francisco Art Institute. Her work has been purchase
1 CONC d by many major collectors throughout the United States an
1 CONC d has been exhibited at: The Wichita Art Center, Wichita Ka
1 CONC nsas; Boise State University, Boise Idaho; Napa Art Center
1 CONC , Napa, CA; Southern Exposure Gallery, San Francisco, CA; C
1 CONC asandre Kersting Gallery, Oakland, CA; Montata Institute o
1 CONC f the Arts, Great Falls, MT; Lubbock Fine Arts Center, Lubb
1 CONC ock, TX; and Santa Cruz Art League, Santa Cruz, CA.
1 CONT
1 CONT EDUCATION
1 CONT 1988 B.F.A., Academy of Art College, San Francisco, CA
1 CONT 1984 B.A., Humanities, San Diego State University, San Dieg
1 CONC o, CA
1 CONT 1984 B.A., French, L'Institut pour les Etudiants Etrangers
1 CONC , Aix-en-Provence, France
1 CONT
1 CONT COPYRIGHT 2000 VNU Business Media
1 CONT
1 CONT Bay Area artist Randall Sexton defines the essence of a ple
1 CONC in air painter when producing his small, impulsive cityscap
1 CONC es and landscapes.
1 CONT
1 CONT First thing each morning, plein air painter Randall Sexto
1 CONC n throws open the windows of his North Beach, San Francisco
1 CONC , apartment and takes the pulse of the day. The microclimat
1 CONC es--sunny here, foggy there--make the weather a notoriousl
1 CONC y difficult call. When he can't view enough, Sexton heads u
1 CONC p to the roof. There he can see well beyond his old Italia
1 CONC n neighborhood to the shimmering bay and north to Marin Cou
1 CONC nty's blue-misted Mount Tamalpais.
1 CONT
1 CONT Some days he need go no farther than that, as his views Nor
1 CONC th Beach Brewery or Rooftop Party demonstrate. Other mornin
1 CONC gs, rain sends him scuttling to his basement studio, part o
1 CONC f an old parking garage. Sometimes he heads for blue-colla
1 CONC r Crockett and other days to Berkeley Flats. More often tha
1 CONC n not, lured by the small lagoons and peninsulas encirclin
1 CONC g Mount Tamalpais, he heads up to Marin, where, on certai
1 CONC n glorious days when the woodpeckers woo, he feels like he'
1 CONC s living Aaron Copland's "Appalachian Spring."
1 CONT
1 CONT Sexton was born and raised in rural East Hampton, Connectic
1 CONC ut. In 1980, directly after garnering a B.F.A. in paintin
1 CONC g from the University of Connecticut in Storrs, he fled t
1 CONC o the Bay Area. For the better part of a decade, he worke
1 CONC d there on large-scale abstract canvases couched in the inv
1 CONC ented imagery of action and color-field painting. Upon real
1 CONC izing that his work bore no enduring resonance or lasting p
1 CONC ower, he began referencing the real world and by 1990 had f
1 CONC ound his metier.
1 CONT
1 CONT Today, the palpable qualities of sunlight link what he prou
1 CONC dly refers to as studies--observations from evanescent life
1 CONC --in the form of cityscapes or landscapes. The artist prefe
1 CONC rs to work small because he feels it promotes quicker, mor
1 CONC e spontaneous compositions. Taping four or five 6"-x-8" bir
1 CONC ch panels onto a larger piece of wood, he moves easily fro
1 CONC m one to the next, letting impulse and intuition be his gui
1 CONC des. His dealer of the past five years, John Pence, calls S
1 CONC exton "a true, on-the-spot plein air painter."
1 CONT
1 CONT Indeed, Sexton may throw his easel into the trunk of his 19
1 CONC 75 Mercedes-Benz sedan and barrel along the freeway--boun
1 CONC d for Bolinas, Lagunitas Lake, or Point Reyes National Seas
1 CONC hore--only to see the perfect view framed in his rearview m
1 CONC irror. At that moment, the artist has glimpsed his first "p
1 CONC ortrait of a day," to borrow the felicitous phrase of Ameri
1 CONC can Impressionist Charles Webster Hawthorne (1872-1930).
1 CONT
1 CONT Such painting, Sexton says, represents "an environmentall
1 CONC y friendly exchange" between him and the world beyond. He e
1 CONC xplains, "The end result is a record of the experience, a p
1 CONC ortrait of the moment, a blending or mixture of myself an
1 CONC d what lies before me."
1 CONT Visually, the results are fresh and charming. Painterly stu
1 CONC dies marked by broad brushwork--such as Cypress in the Fo
1 CONC g or Rocks/Slide/Pat--present their subjects in vivid, abst
1 CONC racted terms. Even panels reborn as oil-on-canvas studio pi
1 CONC eces, such as Second Bo', retain a vigorous immediacy.
1 CONT
1 CONT Sexton does not always work en plein air. He also pursues s
1 CONC till-life painting, which has deepened his understanding o
1 CONC f the medium and now makes up a third of the artist's outpu
1 CONC t. "I grew up in close proximity to an East Hampton purveyo
1 CONC r of circus memorabilia," he adds, "and that may have plant
1 CONC ed the seed for the close contemplation of objects." Sexto
1 CONC n relishes this meditative counterpoint to plein air painti
1 CONC ng--the chance to paint slowly, study his subject, and poli
1 CONC sh his drawing and color-mixing skills.
1 CONT
1 CONT For the past four years Sexton has also taught at the Acade
1 CONC my of Art College in San Francisco. Teaching life-drawing a
1 CONC nd painting, he currently spends two days a week guiding hi
1 CONC s students through their paces. "You learn twice when you t
1 CONC each," he says with chuckle.
1 CONT As such, it comes as a surprise when Sexton hesitates to of
1 CONC fer an analysis of his working methods. "I do pretty much w
1 CONC hat artists have been doing for hundreds of years," he expl
1 CONC ains. "More important than technique is being unself-consci
1 CONC ous about what I'm doing at the time, being intuitive, thou
1 CONC gh not automatic and not formulaic."
1 CONT
1 CONT When pressed for more, he says he works "fairly broadly" an
1 CONC d lately has been reaching for larger brushes. He uses hog-
1 CONC hair filberts and flats as well as smaller camel- and sable
1 CONC -hair brushes for detail. His palette? "I used to buy any o
1 CONC ld color," he admits. "Every time I went to buy art supplie
1 CONC s I'd be like a kid in a candy store." Eventually he realiz
1 CONC ed that sticking to the same palette brought greater contro
1 CONC l. Now he uses 12 or 13 colors consistently: ultramarine, c
1 CONC obalt and compose blue, viridian green, transparent red oxi
1 CONC de, alizarin crimson, terra rosa, cadmium scarlet, cadmiu
1 CONC m yellow deep, yellow ochre, cadmium lemon, titanium whit
1 CONC e and sometimes ivory black.
1 CONT
1 CONT Sexton often travels with a 25-pound box easel and a backpa
1 CONC ck, which together hold all the supplies he needs. Such mob
1 CONC ility lets the artist follow his nose to visual discoveries
1 CONC . Once, Sexton and Paul Stempen, his frequent painting part
1 CONC ner, scrambled down a cliff to find themselves on a nude be
1 CONC ach surrounded by hundreds of mud-caked revelers, some play
1 CONC ing Frisbee.
1 CONT
1 CONT The artist admits that things might have gone quite differe
1 CONC ntly had circumstances not changed for him in the late 1980
1 CONC s and early 1990s. At the time, he recalls, he was chafin
1 CONC g under the self-imposed onus of De Kooning's style of abst
1 CONC raction. Then, in 1988 he strolled into the John Pence Gall
1 CONC ery and discovered the work of Michael Lynch. "It was every
1 CONC thing I liked: painterly, loose, gestural," Sexton describe
1 CONC s. "These were abstract paintings about the real world, an
1 CONC d they totally turned me on."
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1990, Sexton enrolled in the first of three workshops wi
1 CONC th Lynch at the Fechin Institute in Taos, New Mexico, wher
1 CONC e he completed his first landscapes. Meanwhile, Sexton bega
1 CONC n taking Bob Gerbracht's celebrated weekly figure-paintin
1 CONC g class in San Francisco, which, he says, "provided a focu
1 CONC s and a springboard that I found intensely rewarding." Unde
1 CONC r Gerbracht's tutelage, Sexton gained a more formal groundw
1 CONC ork for painting than he had received when "splashing pain
1 CONC t on canvas," as he says, at the University of Connecticut
1 CONC . He also took courses at the Academy of Art College in Sa
1 CONC n Francisco, to much the same effect.
1 CONT
1 CONT More important was a chance encounter with the figurative p
1 CONC ainter ŮCbŮDKimŮC/bŮD ŮCbŮDFrohsinŮC/bŮD, a student at the academy
1 CONC , who later became his wife. "She saved me," he admits. The
1 CONC y continue to paint each other as they did when they firs
1 CONC t met, critique each other's work, and hone their skills b
1 CONC y drawing the figure from life once a week.
1 CONT
1 CONT Sexton, who describes himself as a "painterly contemporar
1 CONC y realist," exhibits at the John Pence Gallery as well as w
1 CONC ith the BayWood Artists, a group of Marin County-based pain
1 CONC ters who show and sell their work to raise public awarenes
1 CONC s of various environmental and open-air issues. A recent ex
1 CONC hibit benefited the Oakwood Valley Restoration Project, whi
1 CONC ch seeks to preserve an oldgrowth stand of live oaks in th
1 CONC e Golden Gate National Parks in San Francisco.
1 CONT Michael J. Burlingham is a writer who lives in New York Cit
1 CONC y.
1 CONT ŮCbŮD
1 CONT Source CitationŮC/bŮD
1 CONT BURLINGHAM, MICHAEL J. "Portrait of a Day." ŮCiŮDAmerican Art
1 CONC istŮC/iŮD Aug. 2000: 24. ŮCiŮDGeneral OneFileŮC/iŮD. Web. 16 Nov
1 CONC . 2009.

1 CONC irror. At that moment, the artist has glimpsed his first "p
1 CONC ortrait of a day," to borrow the felicitous phrase of Ameri
1 CONC can Impressionist Charles Webster Hawthorne (1872-1930).
1 CONT
1 CONT Such painting, Sexton says, represents "an environmentall
1 CONC y friendly exchange" between him and the world beyond. He e
1 CONC xplains, "The end result is a record of the experience, a p
1 CONC ortrait of the moment, a blending or mixture of myself an
1 CONC d what lies before me."
1 CONT Visually, the results are fresh and charming. Painterly stu
1 CONC dies marked by broad brushwork--such as Cypress in the Fo
1 CONC g or Rocks/Slide/Pat--present their subjects in vivid, abst
1 CONC racted terms. Even panels reborn as oil-on-canvas studio pi
1 CONC eces, such as Second Bo', retain a vigorous immediacy.
1 CONT
1 CONT Sexton does not always work en plein air. He also pursues s
1 CONC till-life painting, which has deepened his understanding o
1 CONC f the medium and now makes up a third of the artist's outpu
1 CONC t. "I grew up in close proximity to an East Hampton purveyo
1 CONC r of circus memorabilia," he adds, "and that may have plant
1 CONC ed the seed for the close contemplation of objects." Sexto
1 CONC n relishes this meditative counterpoint to plein air painti
1 CONC ng--the chance to paint slowly, study his subject, and poli
1 CONC sh his drawing and color-mixing skills.
1 CONT
1 CONT For the past four years Sexton has also taught at the Acade
1 CONC my of Art College in San Francisco. Teaching life-drawing a
1 CONC nd painting, he currently spends two days a week guiding hi
1 CONC s students through their paces. "You learn twice when you t
1 CONC each," he says with chuckle.
1 CONT As such, it comes as a surprise when Sexton hesitates to of
1 CONC fer an analysis of his working methods. "I do pretty much w
1 CONC hat artists have been doing for hundreds of years," he expl
1 CONC ains. "More important than technique is being unself-consci
1 CONC ous about what I'm doing at the time, being intuitive, thou
1 CONC gh not automatic and not formulaic."
1 CONT
1 CONT When pressed for more, he says he works "fairly broadly" an
1 CONC d lately has been reaching for larger brushes. He uses hog-
1 CONC hair filberts and flats as well as smaller camel- and sable
1 CONC -hair brushes for detail. His palette? "I used to buy any o
1 CONC ld color," he admits. "Every time I went to buy art supplie
1 CONC s I'd be like a kid in a candy store." Eventually he realiz
1 CONC ed that sticking to the same palette brought greater contro
1 CONC l. Now he uses 12 or 13 colors consistently: ultramarine, c
1 CONC obalt and compose blue, viridian green, transparent red oxi
1 CONC de, alizarin crimson, terra rosa, cadmium scarlet, cadmiu
1 CONC m yellow deep, yellow ochre, cadmium lemon, titanium whit
1 CONC e and sometimes ivory black.
1 CONT
1 CONT Sexton often travels with a 25-pound box easel and a backpa
1 CONC ck, which together hold all the supplies he needs. Such mob
1 CONC ility lets the artist follow his nose to visual discoveries
1 CONC . Once, Sexton and Paul Stempen, his frequent painting part
1 CONC ner, scrambled down a cliff to find themselves on a nude be
1 CONC ach surrounded by hundreds of mud-caked revelers, some play
1 CONC ing Frisbee.
1 CONT
1 CONT The artist admits that things might have gone quite differe
1 CONC ntly had circumstances not changed for him in the late 1980
1 CONC s and early 1990s. At the time, he recalls, he was chafin
1 CONC g under the self-imposed onus of De Kooning's style of abst
1 CONC raction. Then, in 1988 he strolled into the John Pence Gall
1 CONC ery and discovered the work of Michael Lynch. "It was every
1 CONC thing I liked: painterly, loose, gestural," Sexton describe
1 CONC s. "These were abstract paintings about the real world, an
1 CONC d they totally turned me on."
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1990, Sexton enrolled in the first of three workshops wi
1 CONC th Lynch at the Fechin Institute in Taos, New Mexico, wher
1 CONC e he completed his first landscapes. Meanwhile, Sexton bega
1 CONC n taking Bob Gerbracht's celebrated weekly figure-paintin
1 CONC g class in San Francisco, which, he says, "provided a focu
1 CONC s and a springboard that I found intensely rewarding." Unde
1 CONC r Gerbracht's tutelage, Sexton gained a more formal groundw
1 CONC ork for painting than he had received when "splashing pain
1 CONC t on canvas," as he says, at the University of Connecticut
1 CONC . He also took courses at the Academy of Art College in Sa
1 CONC n Francisco, to much the same effect.
1 CONT
1 CONT More important was a chance encounter with the figurative p
1 CONC ainter ŮCbŮDKimŮC/bŮD ŮCbŮDFrohsinŮC/bŮD, a student at the academy
1 CONC , who later became his wife. "She saved me," he admits. The
1 CONC y continue to paint each other as they did when they firs
1 CONC t met, critique each other's work, and hone their skills b
1 CONC y drawing the figure from life once a week.
1 CONT
1 CONT Sexton, who describes himself as a "painterly contemporar
1 CONC y realist," exhibits at the John Pence Gallery as well as w
1 CONC ith the BayWood Artists, a group of Marin County-based pain
1 CONC ters who show and sell their work to raise public awarenes
1 CONC s of various environmental and open-air issues. A recent ex
1 CONC hibit benefited the Oakwood Valley Restoration Project, whi
1 CONC ch seeks to preserve an oldgrowth stand of live oaks in th
1 CONC e Golden Gate National Parks in San Francisco.
1 CONT Michael J. Burlingham is a writer who lives in New York Cit
1 CONC y.
1 CONT ŮCbŮD
1 CONT Source CitationŮC/bŮD
1 CONT BURLINGHAM, MICHAEL J. "Portrait of a Day." ŮCiŮDAmerican Art
1 CONC istŮC/iŮD Aug. 2000: 24. ŮCiŮDGeneral OneFileŮC/iŮD. Web. 16 Nov
1 CONC . 2009.

1 CONC ŮCbŮDJohn M. Couric, former journalist, P.R. executive, fathe
1 CONC r of broadcaster Katie Couric
1 CONT By Adam Bernstein, Published: June 22
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDJohn M. Couric, a wire service editor who said he gav
1 CONC e up the "high priesthood of journalism" for a public relat
1 CONC ions career, in part to support a growing family that inclu
1 CONC ded the future television journalist Katie Couric, died Jun
1 CONC e 22 at Virginia Hospital Center in Arlington. He was 90.
1 CONT His death, of complications from Parkinson's disease, was c
1 CONC onfirmed by his daughter Katie, the former anchor of the "C
1 CONC BS Evening News" and co-host of NBC's "Today Show" who is s
1 CONC cheduled to start a daytime talk show for the ABC network.
1 CONT "I encouraged her to go into broadcasting because I though
1 CONC t it was more promising than print, having been in print my
1 CONC self," John Couric, an Arlington resident, told The Washing
1 CONC ton Post in 1991.
1 CONT He covered Georgia politics and the state capitol for the A
1 CONC tlanta Constitution before joining the United Press wire se
1 CONC rvice in the late 1940s. He reported from throughout the So
1 CONC uth for UP, chronicling the rise of then-Gov. Herman Talmad
1 CONC ge of Georgia and a hurricane that in 1949 devastated the e
1 CONC ast coast of Florida.
1 CONT He joined the news service's Washington bureau in 1951 an
1 CONC d subsequently wrote about then-Senate Majority Leader Lynd
1 CONC on Johnson's heart attack, among other stories of nationa
1 CONC l interest.
1 CONT He was an editor with UP before leaving in 1957 to begin hi
1 CONC s public relations work with a series of trade associations
1 CONC , including the National Association of Broadcasters and th
1 CONC e American Health Care Association.
1 CONT He retired in 1985 after six years with the Food and Drug A
1 CONC dministration, where he wrote articles and speeches.
1 CONT John Martin Couric Jr. was born Aug. 28, 1920, in Brunswick
1 CONC , Ga., and grew up in Dublin, Ga. He graduated in 1941 wit
1 CONC h a journalism degree from Mercer University in Macon, Ga.
1 CONC , and was a newspaper reporter in Macon before serving in t
1 CONC he Navy during World War II.
1 CONT Stationed in the Mediterranean and then the Pacific, Mr. Co
1 CONC uric participated in the invasion of Sicily before servin
1 CONC g in the campaigns for Tarawa, Peleliu, the Philippines an
1 CONC d Okinawa. He retired from the Navy Reserve in 1965 at th
1 CONC e rank of lieutenant commander.
1 CONT Besides his daughter Katie, of New York, survivors includ
1 CONC e his wife of 67 years, Elinor Hene Couric of Arlington; tw
1 CONC o other children, Clara Batchelor of Brookline, Mass., an
1 CONC d John M. Couric Jr. of Arlington; nine grandchildren; an
1 CONC d four great-grandchildren. Their oldest child, Virginia St
1 CONC ate Sen. Emily Couric (D-Charlottesville), died in 2001.
1 CONT Mr. Couric received a master's degree in communications fro
1 CONC m American University in 1968 and was an adjunct professo
1 CONC r of journalism and public relations in AU's graduate progr
1 CONC am and the University of Maryland for the next 27 years.
1 CONT He was a longtime Arlington resident and member of the Nati
1 CONC onal Presbyterian Church in Washington. He was involved i
1 CONC n volunteer work for the American Heart Association and, i
1 CONC n the early 1960s, the President's Committee on Employmen
1 CONC t of the Handicapped. He served on the executive committe
1 CONC e of both groups.

1 CONC Obituary from Alexandria City OUTLOOK Feb. 2, 2005
1 CONT
1 CONT Graveside services for Mrs. Frances Koch Frohsin of Alexand
1 CONC er City will be at 11 a.m., Wednesday, February 2, 2005, a
1 CONC t the Alexander City Cemetery. Rabbi Ken Segal will officia
1 CONC te.
1 CONT
1 CONT Frances Koch Frohsin, an Alabama native devoted to her fami
1 CONC ly, friends and community, died Tuesday, February 1, 2005
1 CONC , at age 92. Mrs. Frohsin proudly headed a large family wh
1 CONC o adored the loving matriarch they called Nanny.
1 CONT
1 CONT Born 1912 in Demopolis in western Alabama, Mrs. Frohsin wa
1 CONC s the sixth and youngest child of Hannah Carolyn and Henr
1 CONC y Koch.
1 CONT
1 CONT She received a Bachelor of Science degree in 1934 from Alab
1 CONC ama College, now
1 CONT
1 CONT known as the University of Montevallo.   In 1935, she cam
1 CONC e to Alexander City as the bride of Ralph Frohsin Sr., wh
1 CONC o ran a downtown retail store his father founded in 1891. T
1 CONC hey remained in Alexander City for the rest of their lives
1 CONC , building the Frohsin's store from a dry-goods mercantil
1 CONC e to a retail apparel store widely known for fashion. Frohs
1 CONC in's was a vital part of downtown Alexander City until th
1 CONC e 1990s when it closed after more than 100 years in busines
1 CONC s.
1 CONT
1 CONT Not only did Mrs. Frohsin participate in the community's ec
1 CONC onomic development, but she also helped fulfill its civic n
1 CONC eeds. She served 34 years - 29 years as vice chairwoman -
1 CONC - of the Tallapoosa County Department of Human Resources, w
1 CONC hich oversaw the public welfare system. The board recognize
1 CONC d Mrs. Frohsin's decades-long contributions when she retire
1 CONC d in 1987.
1 CONT
1 CONT She served her community in several ways, most notably thro
1 CONC ugh the Red Cross where she volunteered for many years. Dur
1 CONC ing World War II, she headed up the Red Cross motor corp
1 CONC s - which, among other things, taught women how to drive am
1 CONC bulances.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mrs. Frohsin loved gardening and bird watching. She often d
1 CONC elivered roses from the Frohsin garden to folks in the hosp
1 CONC ital or friends who needed brightening. In her later year
1 CONC s she loved just being outside, enjoying the colors of he
1 CONC r yard and watching birds, many of which she could identify
1 CONC . Mrs. Frohsin shared her love of the outdoors, teaching gr
1 CONC andchildren the names of birds and showing them how to cas
1 CONC t a fishing rod and reel. She was president of the Alexande
1 CONC r City Federation of Garden Clubs and active in the Five Po
1 CONC ints Garden Club.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mrs. Frohsin was a member of the Tuesday Study Club. She wa
1 CONC s a golfer and a member of the Willow Point Ladies Golf Ass
1 CONC ociation. She also was an avid bridge player and participat
1 CONC ed for many years in several bridge clubs. She loved to ent
1 CONC ertain. She also was a longtime member of Temple Beth-Or i
1 CONC n Montgomery as well as its sisterhood.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mrs. Frohsin reveled in her seven grandchildren and four gr
1 CONC eat-grandchildren. A
1 CONT
1 CONT grandchild and great-grandchild are named in her honor.
1 CONT
1 CONT She is survived by her grandchildren and great-grandchildre
1 CONC n as well as her three children: Carolyn F. Heller of Tampa
1 CONC , Fla., Henry I. Frohsin (Carolyn M. Frohsin) of Birmingham
1 CONC , and Ralph Frohsin Jr. (Allyn M. Frohsin) of Alexander Cit
1 CONC y.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mrs. Frohsin was preceded in death by her husband, Ralph Fr
1 CONC ohsin Sr., and her siblings: Josephine Patterson, Isidore K
1 CONC och, Jacob Koch, Henry Koch and Florian Koch.
1 CONT
1 CONT In lieu of flowers the family requests memorial donations m
1 CONC ay be made to The West Tallapoosa Chapter of the American R
1 CONC ed Cross, P.O. Box 246, Alexander City, AL 35011 or to th
1 CONC e Parkinson's Foundation, 1501 North West 9th Ave., Miami
1 CONC , FL 33136.
1 CONT
1 CONT After the funeral, the family will receive at the Frohsin h
1 CONC ome, 739 Ridgeway Drive.
1 CONT
1 CONT Memorial messages may be sent to the family at www.radneyfu
1 CONC neralhome.com.
1 CONT
1 CONT Radney Funeral Home is in charge of arrangements.
1 CONT

1 CONT Article from Alexander City OUTLOOK - February 1, 2005 Froh
1 CONC sin leaves lasting legacy By Kelly Caldwell
1 CONT
1 CONT The Alexander City community lost a true leader Tuesday whe
1 CONC n Mrs. Frances Koch Frohsin died.
1 CONT
1 CONT Known to many as Miss Frances, Frohsin came to Alexander Ci
1 CONC ty in 1935 to help her husband Ralph Frohsin, Sr. run his f
1 CONC amily's retail store.
1 CONT
1 CONT "She excelled in everything she did whether it was runnin
1 CONC g the store, throwing a dinner party or tending to her gard
1 CONC en," Virginia Bradshaw, long time friend of Frohsin, said
1 CONC . "She fit into the community beautifully."
1 CONT
1 CONT During her lifetime, Frohsin worked with the Red Cross fo
1 CONC r a number of years including volunteering with the Red Cro
1 CONC ss Motor Corps during World War II where she taught other w
1 CONC omen to drive ambulances.
1 CONT
1 CONT "She always looked beautiful, but she could get in the tren
1 CONC ches and work hard when she needed to," Bradshaw said.
1 CONT
1 CONT Described as a "true southern lady," Frohsin enjoyed garden
1 CONC ing and bird watching.   "I always enjoyed being around her
1 CONC ," Barbara Sokol said. "She radiated graciousness and was a
1 CONC lways a delight to be around."
1 CONT
1 CONT She also enjoyed playing golf and was a member of the Willo
1 CONC w Point Ladies Golf Association.
1 CONT
1 CONT "She was an avid golfer," Sokol said. "I remember when I fi
1 CONC rst came to town, I played with her."
1 CONT
1 CONT Frohsin was preceded in death by her husband and is survive
1 CONC d by her three children, Carolyn Heller, Henry L. Frohsin a
1 CONC nd Ralph Frohsin, Jr. as well as many grandchildren and gre
1 CONC at-grandchildren.
1 CONT
1 CONT "I have known her all my life and I hope Alexander City i
1 CONC s producing more people like her," Bradshaw said. "I hope t
1 CONC he young people (of Alexander City) will have the chance t
1 CONC o know someone like her."
1 CONT
1 CONT Frohsin donated her time freely working with the Tallapoos
1 CONC a County Department of Human Resources overseeing the publi
1 CONC c welfare system.
1 CONT
1 CONT "She was very giving of her time," Jack Coley said. "She wa
1 CONC s a very fine person and I would say she was very beloved b
1 CONC y all who knew her."
1 CONT

1 CONC TBO.com (Tampa Bay Online) Published: August 26, 2011 ŮCbŮDTa
1 CONC mpa artist was 'vibrant, full of life' By Cloe Cabrera ŮC/bŮD
1 CONC People who knew Tampa artist Carolyn Frohsin Heller appreci
1 CONC ated her as much for her wit as they did her way with a pai
1 CONC ntbrush. "You knew you were going to be entertained when yo
1 CONC u were around her," said her daughter, Janet Heller, of Oak
1 CONC land, Calif. "She liked to tell jokes and had a very larg
1 CONC e personality." Heller, who was 74, died unexpectedly Monda
1 CONC y of a gastrointestinal clot. A painter for more than 40 ye
1 CONC ars, Heller was known for her serigraphs and original acryl
1 CONC ic paintings. But her specialty was fabric and decorative a
1 CONC rt; she would paint lively, original designs on jewelry, gu
1 CONC est towels, pillows, aprons, placemats, umbrellas - you nam
1 CONC e it. Her works have been featured in numerous galleries, a
1 CONC rt shows and exhibitions. One of her paintings, "Hot Flashe
1 CONC s," is on display in Tampa General Hospital's Women's Cente
1 CONC r. "She was vibrant, full of life, and kind of larger tha
1 CONC n life, really," said longtime friend and fellow artist, Jo
1 CONC sette Urso. "She really lived her work." Heller was born i
1 CONC n Alexander City, Ala., where her parents, Ralph and France
1 CONC s Koch Frohsin, founded Frohsin's department store. She att
1 CONC ended Stratford Hall in Danville, Va., and the Sophie Newco
1 CONC mb College at Tulane University, where she graduated wit
1 CONC h a degree in fine arts. She married Edward Heller in 1958
1 CONC , and two years later, the couple moved to Tampa. Heller co
1 CONC ntinued her art studies at the University of South Florid
1 CONC a and the Tampa Museum. She later worked with well-known ar
1 CONC tists such as Ida Kohlmeyer, Syd Solomon and William Pachne
1 CONC r. But it wasn't until after her four children went away t
1 CONC o college that Heller began to focus on her art full time
1 CONC . "It's bright and it's uplifting, just like she was," sai
1 CONC d longtime friend and neighbor Adelaide Few, whose home i
1 CONC s filled with Heller's work. "When you see Carolyn's work
1 CONC , you know its Carolyn." Heller's accent stood out as muc
1 CONC h as her work, Few said. "She had an Alabama accent you cou
1 CONC ld cut with a molasses spoon. "Anything she said could neve
1 CONC r have been said by anyone else in any other way," Few adde
1 CONC d. "She used every syllable to her advantage. She was a glo
1 CONC rious, wonderful dame." Heller was a charter member of th
1 CONC e Hillsborough County Public Art Committee and served on th
1 CONC e board of the Hillsborough County Arts Council for eight y
1 CONC ears. "She liked to paint in her studio, which was her gara
1 CONC ge, with the television on watching 'South Park' or it wa
1 CONC s 'Beavis and Butt-Head,' " said Art Keeble, executive dire
1 CONC ctor of the Arts Council of Hillsborough County. "Carolyn w
1 CONC as a little ribald, so things I could tell you would not b
1 CONC e printable. But that's what was endearing about her." Hell
1 CONC er's passion went beyond the visual arts. She loved good fo
1 CONC od and often hosted holiday parties at her home, where sh
1 CONC e loved to make gumbo for guests and tell stories. Heller i
1 CONC s survived by four children: Alan Heller, Emily Heller, Jan
1 CONC et Heller and Fran Heller; five grandchildren and two broth
1 CONC ers. A celebration of her life is planned for October at Ta
1 CONC mpa Theatre.

1 CONC Ralph Frohsin, Jr., Alexander City
1 CONT
1 CONT Born in Alexander City, Ralph Frohsin received his BS in Co
1 CONC mmerce and a JD in Law in 1964 from the University of Alaba
1 CONC ma. His many civic activities include the Chamber of Commer
1 CONC ce, Red Cross, United Way, Junior Achievement, Operation Do
1 CONC wntown Alexander City, Boys and Girls Club and the Children
1 CONC 's Hospital of Alabama. In 1991, Mr. Frohsin was named Alex
1 CONC ander City's Man of the Year. He has been president of th
1 CONC e Alex City Jazz Festival since 1991 and from 1993 to 199
1 CONC 6 was a director of the Sarah Towery Art Colony. Mr. Frohsi
1 CONC n serves on the Council's Grants Review Committee.
1 CONT
1 CONT Frohsin's roots run deep in Alexander City. His family foun
1 CONC ded Frohsin's Department Store, which operated in downtow
1 CONC n Alexander City for more than 100 years, before closing se
1 CONC veral years ago. He currently serves on the board of direct
1 CONC ors for SouthTrust Bank of Central Alabama and Operation Do
1 CONC wntown and is chairman of the JazzFest committee. He also s
1 CONC erves as a board member for the Alabama State Council on th
1 CONC e Arts. He was president of the Alexander City Chamber of C
1 CONC ommerce in 1969 and was named Alexander City Man of the Yea
1 CONC r in 1991.
1 CONT
1 CONT "It's a privilege to be part of Russell Medical Center," Fr
1 CONC ohsin said. "The future of healthcare at our facility is s
1 CONC o bright. I hope that I can be an asset to Russell Medica
1 CONC l Center as it continues to grow."
1 CONT
1 CONT Frohsin is a graduate of the University of Alabama and th
1 CONC e University of Alabama School of Law. He is a longtime mem
1 CONC ber of the Alabama Bar Association.
1 CONT
1 CONT
1 CONT Ralph Frohsin graduated from the University of Alabama wit
1 CONC h a BS in Commerce and a JD in Law. After college, he serve
1 CONC d in the US Army in Germany, attaining the rank of Captain
1 CONC . After returning to the states, he joined his family's ret
1 CONC ail apparel business in Alexander City as President of Fros
1 CONC hin's.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mr. Frohsin's many civic activities include the Industria
1 CONC l Revenue Board, the Chamber of Commerce, the American Re
1 CONC d Cross, the United Way, Junior Achievement, Operation Down
1 CONC town Alexander City, the Children's Hospital of Alabama an
1 CONC d the Russell Medical Center. In 1992, Mr. Frohsin was name
1 CONC d Alexander City's Man of the Year. He has been president o
1 CONC f the Alex City Jazz Festival and a director of the Sarah T
1 CONC owery Art Colony. Recently reappointed to the Council's boa
1 CONC rd, Mr. Frohsin serves on the Grants Review Committee. Mr
1 CONC . Frohsin is married to Allyn Moses Frohsin and together th
1 CONC ey have one son, Mark Adler Frohsin.
