1 CONT   ŮCbŮDNews:  ŮC/bŮD   ŮCbŮDArchaeologists to look at artifacts f
1 CONC rom Lake Martin areaŮC/bŮD Friday, February 8, 2008 12:33 P
1 CONC M CST  ŮCbŮDBy Patrick McCreless  ŮC/bŮDFor those who have foun
1 CONC d American Indian artifacts in the Lake Martin area and wan
1 CONC t to know more about their history, help is on the way. Arc
1 CONC haeologists from Auburn University Montgomery will bring th
1 CONC eir expertise to Alexander City Feb. 9 as part of a recentl
1 CONC y organized artifact fair. The event will be held from 10 a
1 CONC .m. to 3 p.m. in the Lake Martin Area United Way building o
1 CONC n Main Street.  "A lot of people have found Indian artifact
1 CONC s but do not know how old they are," said Ralph Frohsin, or
1 CONC ganizer of the artifact fair. "Arrowheads can be 200-years-
1 CONC old or 2,000-years-old."  Like others in the area, Frohsi
1 CONC n has collected artifacts around the shores of Lake Marti
1 CONC n for years.    "I've been collecting since I was 10," Froh
1 CONC sin said. "I've got pipes and even a flintlock rifle mechan
1 CONC ism. Almost all of it I've found on the lakeshore."  Frohsi
1 CONC n said several tables would be set up at the event for peop
1 CONC le to show their collections to the archaeologists, who wil
1 CONC l then discern what the artifacts are and during what tim
1 CONC e period they were made. While American Indian artifacts wi
1 CONC ll be the focus of the event, Frohsin said collectors are w
1 CONC elcome to bring other items as well.  "Anything that an arc
1 CONC haeologist will be able to identify," Frohsin said.  Frohsi
1 CONC n said he got the idea to try and organize the fair after s
1 CONC peaking with Dr. Craig Sheldon at AUM. He said Sheldon, wh
1 CONC o is one of the archaeologists coming to the event, was fam
1 CONC iliar with several other people who were collecting America
1 CONC n Indian artifacts in the Lake Martin area.  "He was awar
1 CONC e people were searching with the lake down so low," Frohsi
1 CONC n said. "He asked if people would like to have an artifact
1 CONC s fair."  After peaking the interest of a few other collect
1 CONC ors in the area, Frohsin arranged for the archaeologists t
1 CONC o come to Alexander City. In addition, he received the back
1 CONC ing of the Hillabee Archaeological Society, a group made u
1 CONC p of collectors from Tallapoosa, Coosa and Clay Counties
1 CONC .  "We hope maybe if it's successful and people enjoy it, t
1 CONC hen maybe we'll do it periodically," Frohsin said of the ev
1 CONC ent.  Sheldon is an associate professor of anthropology
1 CONC , a registered professional archaeologist and head of the D
1 CONC epartment of Sociology at AUM. He has conducted archaeologi
1 CONC cal research on Iron Age burial caves in the southern Phili
1 CONC ppines, Classic Maya sites in Yucatan, Mexico and early Ame
1 CONC rican Indian sties in Oregon. For the past 20 years, Sheldo
1 CONC n has concentrated his research on historic Creek Indian si
1 CONC tes in central Alabama as part of the cooperative Histori
1 CONC c Creek Project of AUM and Auburn University.  Accompanyin
1 CONC g Sheldon will be Ned Jenkins, senior archaeologist for th
1 CONC e Alabama Historical Commission at Fort Toulouse - Fort Jac
1 CONC kson State Historic Site in Wetumpka, Ala.  While the major
1 CONC ity of responses he's has received about the event have bee
1 CONC n positive, Frohsin said a few collectors have expressed so
1 CONC me worry about bringing their items into the open.  "Some p
1 CONC eople were leery to bring in their findings because of rumo
1 CONC rs that it could be confiscated," Frohsin said. "People ca
1 CONC n be assured that no one's going to confiscate them."

1 CONT Insider:
1 CONT
1 CONT Saturday, October 25, 2008
1 CONT ŮCbŮDFrohsin & Barger hangs out shingle: ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT The Birmingham legal team that successfully defended a Hunt
1 CONC sville defense contractor against government prosecution la
1 CONC st year is striking out on its own.
1 CONT Partners Henry Frohsin and Jim Barger and associate Ellio
1 CONC t Walthall are forming Frohsin & Barger after working toget
1 CONC her at Baker, Donelson, Bearman, Caldwell & Berkowitz.
1 CONT The three spearheaded the defense of Axion Corp., which wa
1 CONC s tried last year by the federal government for exportin
1 CONC g a drawing of a military helicopter to China. The trial co
1 CONC llapsed after seven days, and was dismissed by the judge, w
1 CONC ho called the government's case weak and flawed.
1 CONT The new firm will concentrate on government whistleblower c
1 CONC ases and white-collar criminal defense.
1 CONT "The economy is at its worst since 1929, I am 65 years old
1 CONC , and I am starting a new business," said Frohsin, a forme
1 CONC r prosecutor in the Birmingham U.S. attorney's office. "I m
1 CONC ust be crazy."
1 CONT
1 CONT Ă 2008 The Birmingham News. All rights reserved.

1 CONT Henry I Frohsin received his undergraduate degree in 1966 a
1 CONC nd his law degree in 1967 from the University of Alabama. H
1 CONC e was Assistant United States Attorney for the Northern Dis
1 CONC trict of Alabama for fifteen years, including seven years a
1 CONC s First Assistant U.S. Attorney. Mr. Frohsin has extensiv
1 CONC e experience in virtually all areas of federal and state tr
1 CONC ial practice. He formerly served as Alabama State Bar deleg
1 CONC ate to the United States Court of Appeals for the Elevent
1 CONC h Circuit Judicial Conference. He is General Counsel to Cam
1 CONC p Smile-A-Mile (a children's oncology camp).

1 CONC rohsin Named Chairman of Birmingham Area Chapter During th
1 CONC e May 3rd annual business meeting of the Birmingham Area Ch

1 CONC e position of Chairman of the board of directors. "The peop

1 CONC shments and to make our community the haven for those wher
1 CONC e suffering and needs are our first priority" Frohsin said
1 CONC . Frohsin is a partner in the law firm of Baker Donelson Be
1 CONC arman Caldwell and Berkowitz, and comes from a family tha
1 CONC t has long supported the American Red Cross. He began his a

1 CONC r City, and became a leadership volunteer with the Birmingh

1 CONC City on Recycling Roll By Sebastian Kitchen, Montgomery Adv
1 CONC ertiser, Ala. Mar. 6--
1 CONT Louisa Weinrib started her children recycling at an early a
1 CONC ge. She would send them to neighbors to collect paper and b
1 CONC ottles in their little red wagons. She would drive them t
1 CONC o recycling companies, where they were given some spendin
1 CONC g money. "I probably used up more in fuel than they earned
1 CONC , but it taught a good lesson in conservation in the kids w
1 CONC hen they were young," Weinrib said. So, decades later, whe
1 CONC n the city started its curbside recycling program, Weinri
1 CONC b was thrilled. "We have very little garbage that leaves ou
1 CONC r house," Weinrib said. City crews collect more than 100 to
1 CONC ns of paper, cardboard, cans and glass from curbsides throu
1 CONC ghout Montgomery every year, but the trash is not buried a
1 CONC t the landfill. The items are collected, recycled and sen
1 CONC t to market to be transformed into other goods. The city ha
1 CONC s collected thousands of tons of recyclable goods since th
1 CONC e program started in Montgomery in 1989. The program bega
1 CONC n with four neighborhoods and expanded over four years to i
1 CONC nclude the entire city. "As time goes by and the public i
1 CONC s better educated about the need for recycling, the partici
1 CONC pation rates have grown," said Daniel Dickey, the city's sa
1 CONC nitation director. Now about 27,000 people, about 40 percen
1 CONC t of the city's household residents, participate in the pro
1 CONC gram. "I'd like to see it increase much more," said Susan C
1 CONC armichael, the city's recycling coordinator and superviso
1 CONC r of the Montgomery Clean City Commission. "Unfortunately
1 CONC , we don't have funding to really put a lot of advertisin
1 CONC g out there." Carmichael said officials nationwide agree mo
1 CONC re than 30 percent participation in voluntary recycling i
1 CONC s respectable. She said participation is as high as 70 perc
1 CONC ent in some Montgomery neighborhoods, but drops to 15 perce
1 CONC nt in others. The Montgomery Clean City Commission does no
1 CONC t have a marketing budget, she said, and people hear abou
1 CONC t the program from other residents or after they call the c
1 CONC ity to inquire. She said they send out about 50 orange bag
1 CONC s every week, along with instruction sheets at people's req
1 CONC uest. Mayor Bobby Bright said the city could expand the rec
1 CONC ycling program, but that is one of many areas the city coul
1 CONC d direct more funding. "Recycling is a program there need
1 CONC s to be more of," the mayor said. In Montgomery, people pla
1 CONC ce all of their items together in the orange bag and then l
1 CONC eave the bag at the curb on a specified day, and a city cre
1 CONC w picks it up. "We think we have made it as simple as possi
1 CONC ble for people," Carmichael said. "There is no reason for p
1 CONC eople not to recycle." Weinrib and her husband drop their o
1 CONC range bag at the curb every Wednesday evening for it to b
1 CONC e ready for pick-up early Thursday morning by city crews. "
1 CONC I don't know how it could be easier," she said.

1 CONC tor South on their honeymoon.  The date of the wedding ha
1 CONC s not been set.

1 CONC (Medical):Cecelia was diagnosed as "senile" in 1938 at th
1 CONC e age of 69,and was admitted to Friends Hospital in Head, w
1 CONC here she remained for the rest of her life (11 years) at th
1 CONC e age of 80.  Friends is a psychiatric hospital; a furthe
1 CONC r cause of Cecelia's death was "senile psychosis". *
1 CONT
1 CONT * from: The Merck Manual of Medical Information--Second Hom
1 CONC e Edition
1 CONT
1 CONT "Symptoms and Signs
1 CONT
1 CONT Apathetic hyperthyroidism: Apathy and inactivity dominate t
1 CONC he clinical picture and replace the usual hyperkinesis. Ass
1 CONC ociated cardiac or other organ system findings may occur. P
1 CONC atients look extremely old and wizened but, with treatment
1 CONC , rapidly lose some wrinkling and become more youthful-look
1 CONC ing. They have been described as having a "characteristic s
1 CONC enile appearance" of mild chronic illness, but when afflict
1 CONC ed with an acute illness or stress, they "quietly and peace
1 CONC fully sink into coma and die an absolutely relaxed death wi
1 CONC thout activation."

1 CONC John J. Hagedorn came to West Point, Georgia, in 1872, esta
1 CONC blished J.J. Hagedorn & Company, a department store servin
1 CONC g several generations of Valley residents.  While visitin
1 CONC g in Germany, he died at the age of 66 years, on October 9
1 CONC , 1902.  Mrs. Hagedorn was Alice Herzberg before her marria
1 CONC ge.
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCbŮDŮCiŮDManuscript MS-025ŮC/iŮD HEYMAN- HERZBERG FAMILY COLLECT
1 CONC ION Manuscript Collection No. 25 ŮC/bŮD In 1872 John J. Haged
1 CONC orn came to West Point and established a mercantile busines
1 CONC s which became one of the most prominent in the community
1 CONC . This collection contains memorabilia, clippings, post car
1 CONC ds and photographs documenting the business interests, trav
1 CONC els, and religious activities of the West Point Jewish comm
1 CONC unity.
1 CONT
1 CONT Prussian-born John Hagedorn came to West Point in 1872, an
1 CONC d later opened the store J.J. Hagedorn & Co. He soon brough
1 CONC t two of his nephews, Zach and Philip Hagedorn to town to h
1 CONC elp with the business.
1 CONT
1 CONT Prussian-born John Hagedorn came to West Point in 1872, an
1 CONC d later opened the store J.J. Hagedorn & Co. He soon brough
1 CONC t two of his nephews, Zach and Philip Hagedorn (ŮCiŮDleŮC/iŮDft
1 CONC ), to town to help with the business. In 1883, the foundati
1 CONC on of the Jewish community of Lanett was cemented when Zac
1 CONC h and Philip Hagedorn married two of Heyman and Betty Heyma
1 CONC n's daughters, Jennie and Lula. The Heyman and Hagedorn fam
1 CONC ilies formed the nucleus of the area's Jewish community fo
1 CONC r much of the next century.

1 CONC House, at 401 North 14th Street, Lanett, was built in old B
1 CONC luffton in 1886.  The style of the handsome house is more r
1 CONC estrained than some of the towering, over-ornamented house
1 CONC s of the era.  Designed by the architectural firm of Fa
1 CONC y & Eichberg, Atlanta, GA, the large rooms and central hal
1 CONC l are outstanding with their excellent woodwork and craftsm
1 CONC anship.  The hall features an elaborate screen of turned sp
1 CONC indles, and original louvered doors.
1 CONT
1 CONT Phillip and Lulu Heyman Hagedorn, the original builders an
1 CONC d owners, were married in a double wedding ceremony with Za
1 CONC ch Hagedorn and Jennie Heyman at the home of the brides' pa
1 CONC rents, Heyman and Betty Merz Heyman, at 201 North 14th Stre
1 CONC et.  The marriage was a great social event of Bluffton an
1 CONC d West Point on October 31, 1883.
1 CONT
1 CONT A newspaper clipping of the double wedding of Zach Hagedor
1 CONC n and Jennie Heyman, and Phillip Hagedorn and Lula Heyman
1 CONC , solemnized on Wednesday evening, October 31, 1883, by th
1 CONC e Rabbi S. Hecht of Atlanta, GA, has been preserved from wh
1 CONC ich the following is quoted:
1 CONT
1 CONT "The ceremony by the gifted Rabbi was beautifully impressiv
1 CONC e.  Beginning with an admirable invocation and excellent ad
1 CONC dress to the couples about to be united, after the exchang
1 CONC e of the marriage vows and of the ring, in token of their e
1 CONC ndless nature, he concluded with the priestly benediction
1 CONC .  Through Hebrew was used in part, each sentence was trans
1 CONC lated as soon as uttered, and all was thus made intelligibl
1 CONC e to the large company present, who listened with intense i
1 CONC nterest to the close.  We have never seen a more brillian
1 CONC t gathering in this city of many weddings.  Two hundred inv
1 CONC itations were issued.
1 CONT
1 CONT The decorations of the residence were simple, but artistic
1 CONC .  In the rear of the hallway hung a floral bell, and under
1 CONC neath was a table, brilliantly lighted with candles, befor
1 CONC e which the misister and the couples stood.  All around wer
1 CONC e pendant evergreens, wreathed into forms graceful and appr
1 CONC opriate.
1 CONT
1 CONT The brides were most becomingly attired in white satin,wit
1 CONC h trains, and the grooms in full dress.  ŮCiŮDEn passantŮC/iŮD
1 CONC , we may remark that the former are intelligent and accompl
1 CONC ished ladies, popular and beloved, as well as versed in al
1 CONC l domestic lore, and will be helpmeets indeed to the worth
1 CONC y and successful young merchants who have been so fortunat
1 CONC e as to win their affections and their hands.
1 CONT
1 CONT The attendants were Louis Mayer of LaGrange, and Miss Emm
1 CONC a Hagedorn; Marcus Herzfeld of Alexander City, AL, and Mis
1 CONC s Bertha Heyman; Master Louis and Miss Hetty Marx, of Wes
1 CONC t Point.
1 CONT
1 CONT The banquet tables were two --- each in the form of an H -
1 CONC - and spread in separate rooms, and every variety of the go
1 CONC od things of this earth crowning them  The cakes were bake
1 CONC d by the fair hands of the brides, and were beautifully emb
1 CONC ossed and trimmed by that ladie of exquisite taste, Miss Ka
1 CONC tie Smith.  The ornaments were ordered from London.  The pr
1 CONC esents were arranged on two tables -- one for each couple."

1 CONC Julius Solomon Hagedorn arrived in Opelika in 1917 and boug
1 CONC ht out the store H. Blumenfeld's, changing the name to "Hag
1 CONC edorn's." The store remained in the Hagedorn family until i
1 CONC t closed in 1976. Julius Hagedorn was known for his busines
1 CONC s achievement and helping with the growth and development o
1 CONC f Opelika's economy. He was also civically active as the pr
1 CONC esident of the Opelika Chamber of Commerce and the Kiwani
1 CONC s Club. Hagedorn's bargain basement attracted a mostly Afri
1 CONC can American clientele and he was well liked by the Black c
1 CONC ommunity of Opelika. Despite this positive business relatio
1 CONC nship, there was little social interaction between Blacks a
1 CONC nd Jews in Opelika, as Jews were careful not to challenge t
1 CONC he status quo of segregation directly.
1 CONT In 1937, Hagedorn rescued his family still living in Westph
1 CONC alia, Germany by sending them six tickets to come to the Un
1 CONC ited States. One of those rescued was his nephew, Henry Ste
1 CONC rn, who continues to live in Opelika today. In Germany, Ste
1 CONC rn's family had owned land where enthusiastic Zionists cam
1 CONC e to learn how to farm before moving to Palestine. Stern, w
1 CONC ho doesn't speak German or Yiddish, calls himself a "rednec
1 CONC k from Alabama." He came to Opelika as a six-year old and l
1 CONC earned English from the Sears Roebuck catalogue.

1 CONT Almost 70 Years After Escaping the Holocaust, Henry Stern L
1 CONC ocates Surviving Family By Anna Asbury Larkin

1 CONT The Boy on the Back Row
1 CONT
1 CONT At age five, most children know nothing of religious persec
1 CONC ution, seeing soldiers march in front of their homes, or mo
1 CONC v- ing to a foreign country where no one speaks their langu
1 CONC age. But for Henry Stern, that's exactly what his young lif
1 CONC e was like. Growing up a Jewish boy in Germany during Hitle
1 CONC r's reign, Stern's life wasn't easy. Jews in the country we
1 CONC re being deported to concentration camps, ghettos and othe
1 CONC r countries. His life changed drastically due to World Wa
1 CONC r II and the Holocaust. He moved to America, faced challeng
1 CONC es in a new country and lost family members.
1 CONT
1 CONT Born Heinz Julius Stern to Arnold and Hedwig Stern on Septe
1 CONC mber 4, 1931, his parents and older sis- ter, Hannalora, li
1 CONC ved in Westheim, Westfalen, Germany; the only Jewish famil
1 CONC y in a small town with only a depot, a creamery and a few s
1 CONC hops. When the family went outside, they were required to w
1 CONC ear the yellow Star of David pinned to their clothing to id
1 CONC entify them as Jews. Stern's great-aunt and great-uncle, Ju
1 CONC lius and Amelia Hagedorn, a highly respected businessman wh
1 CONC o owned a department store in Opelika, Ala., came to visi
1 CONC t and tried desperately to persuade Stern's parents to joi
1 CONC n them in America. However, the Sterns wouldn't leave unti
1 CONC l almost a year later when the Hagedorns sent six ship tick
1 CONC ets for the family to get to America. Over the next six mon
1 CONC ths, the Sterns sold all of their possessions and bought ne
1 CONC w goods to be sent to the states since the family was onl
1 CONC y allowed to travel with $10. Then, on June 16, 1937, the s
1 CONC ix Sterns went to Hamburg, Germany, for a family farewell w
1 CONC ith cousins and other relatives. Just a few minutes befor
1 CONC e boarding the ship, a photograph was taken of all the fami
1 CONC ly members in attendance at the farewell and, along with 33
1 CONC 0 other passengers, the Sterns boarded the S.S. Washington
1 CONC , the last ship of Jews to legally leave Germany. On June 2
1 CONC 4, 1937, Stern walked on deck to find a crowd of people sta
1 CONC r- ing off into the distance as the Statue of Liberty stoo
1 CONC d directly in front of them. "It was probably the most beau
1 CONC tiful sight I had ever seen," Stern remembers. Once off th
1 CONC e ship, the family stayed in a hotel for three nights befor
1 CONC e board- ing the New York Crescent train to Opelika. "Whil
1 CONC e we were on the train, my sister and I were told we neede
1 CONC d to Americanize our names," Stern said. "That's when Hanna
1 CONC lora became just Lora, and I became Henry." The family rece
1 CONC ived a hero-like welcome when they arrived in Opelika on Ju
1 CONC ne 27, 1937. Opelika Mayor John Crawsley met the family a
1 CONC t the Opelika Depot and presented a proclamation welcomin
1 CONC g them to the United States. The Sterns were then escorte
1 CONC d by police to the Hagedorn's home on Third Avenue as citiz
1 CONC ens lined the streets to cheer the family's arrival. Stern'
1 CONC s first week in the United States was confusing at best. H
1 CONC e had never been free to go outside and play. He didn't hav
1 CONC e to wear the Star of David, and he could even play with ot
1 CONC her children - concepts as foreign as the country he now ca
1 CONC lled home. The merchandise his parents shipped to America a
1 CONC rrived in boxcars and was immediately sold to buy items fo
1 CONC r their new home. Stern and his sister made new friends, th
1 CONC e first being Judson and Anna Salter (Asbury), who lived u
1 CONC p the street, and who remain dear friends today. It was dif
1 CONC ficult at first since Stern and Lora didn't speak any Engli
1 CONC sh. But local school teacher, Louise Tollison, had heard ab
1 CONC out the family coming to America, so she brushed up on he
1 CONC r German and began teaching English to the children. Ruth M
1 CONC eadows taught the adults by using a Sears-Roebuck cata- log
1 CONC . They would point to an item, and Meadows would translate
1 CONC . The children attended Northside Intermediate School fro
1 CONC m first through
1 CONT

1 CONC s Opelika High School, from seventh to 12th. Stern played f
1 CONC ootball and basketball at OHS one year and following gradua
1 CONC tion, attended Alabama Polytechnic Institute (now Auburn Un
1 CONC iversity) where he played a year of basketball. He served i
1 CONC n the U.S. Navy from 1951 to 1954 and graduated from Aubur

1 CONC fter Escaping the Holocaust, Henry Stern Locates Survivin
1 CONC g Family By Anna Asbury Larkin The Boy on the Back Row At a
1 CONC ge five, most children know nothing of religious persecutio
1 CONC n, seeing soldiers march in front of their homes, or mov- i
1 CONC ng to a foreign country where no one speaks their language
1 CONC . But for Henry Stern, that's exactly what his young life w
1 CONC as like. Growing up a Jewish boy in Germany during Hitler'
1 CONC s reign, Stern's life wasn't easy. Jews in the country wer
1 CONC e being deported to concentration camps, ghettos and othe
1 CONC r countries. His life changed drastically due to World Wa
1 CONC r II and the Holocaust. He moved to America, faced challeng
1 CONC es in a new country and lost family members. deported and k
1 CONC illed in Auschwitz Camp. A cousin, Eric Weinberg, was depor
1 CONC ted to Riga Concentration Camp, and his aunt Erna Meyburg a
1 CONC nd Stern's paternal grandparents were all murdered by the N
1 CONC azis. The surviving family members were sent to ghettos an
1 CONC d spent the remainder of their lives picking up the shatter
1 CONC ed pieces.
1 CONT
1 CONT For 50 years, Stern had searched desperately for someone, a
1 CONC nyone, from his family who may still be alive. Then, in th
1 CONC e wee hours of November 21, 2004, Stern got a break. Unabl
1 CONC e to sleep, he got out of bed at 4:30 a.m. and sat down a
1 CONC t his computer. A friend had emailed a link to a new websit
1 CONC e that tracks Holocaust victims and their families. After l
1 CONC iterally thousands of failures over the years, but never gi
1 CONC ving up hope, Stern typed in his grandmother's name and ama
1 CONC zingly, unbelievably, for the first time - something came u
1 CONC p: a Fred Hertz in Durham, North Carolina.
1 CONT
1 CONT Stern anxiously waited until 9:00 a.m. to call the stranger
1 CONC . He explained who he was and that he had spent years searc
1 CONC hing for surviving family. For some reason, to Stern, thi
1 CONC s time felt different. He e-mailed Hertz the last family ph
1 CONC otograph taken in 1937 just minutes before the Stern's boar
1 CONC ded the ship to set sail to America. He asked Hertz if he r
1 CONC ecognized or could identify anyone in the picture. A shor
1 CONC t time later, the phone rang. It was Hertz. He wanted to kn
1 CONC ow if Stern was sitting down. "I said I was."
1 CONT
1 CONT What Hertz said next would send shivers down the spine an
1 CONC d tears to the eyes of anyone who heard it.
1 CONT
1 CONT "He said, 'Henry, I'm in this picture. I'm the boy on the b
1 CONC ack row'," Stern said with as much emotion today as the da
1 CONC y his dream came true.
1 CONT
1 CONT The boys were cousins who, for over 60 years, thought the o
1 CONC ther was dead. They started e-mailing constantly and

1 CONT
1 CONT "There was so much to talk about - almost 70 years of los
1 CONC t time," Stern said.
1 CONT
1 CONT Two months later, the cousins and their families would fina
1 CONC lly meet faceto- face for the first time since 1937. With t
1 CONC elevision cameras rolling, the men embraced in a tearful re
1 CONC union in the driveway of the Hertz home in Durham. To thi
1 CONC s family, it was much more than a reunion; it was a miracle!
1 CONT
1 CONT "Finding Fred closed a chapter for the Stern family," Ster
1 CONC n said. "It was something I had prayed for for many, many y
1 CONC ears, and my prayers were answered."
1 CONT
1 CONT For the next three years, Stern and Hertz shared a lifetim
1 CONC e of memories and stories with each other until Hertz passe
1 CONC d away in early 2008.
1 CONT
1 CONT "There was never enough time to get all the questions answe
1 CONC red," Stern said. "But Fred wrote several books about the f
1 CONC amily, and I will treasure those memories forever. Even tho
1 CONC ugh I only knew him for three short years, we shared an inc
1 CONC redible bond. I am so grateful for our time together, an
1 CONC d I miss him so much."
1 CONT
1 CONT Many call the Holocaust the most heinous crime ever committ
1 CONC ed against humanity, but because of persistent relatives i
1 CONC n Opelika, Ala., Stern escaped the atrocity and now is on
1 CONC e of the last known survivors east of the Mississippi River.
1 CONT
1 CONT He faced challenges in his life most could never imagine, b
1 CONC ut he has always remained positive and grounded.
1 CONT
1 CONT "Nobody should feel they are any different than anyone else
1 CONC ," Stern analyzed. "Just because I was born in Germany an
1 CONC d I'm Jewish doesn't make me any different."
1 CONT
1 CONT If Stern's mindset had been practiced over 70 years ago, pe
1 CONC rhaps the tragedy of the Holocaust would never have happene
1 CONC d.

1 CONC Henry Sefton was the engineer that laid out many of the roa
1 CONC ds in upper Huerfano County, and built the toll road over M
1 CONC osca Pass. When he felt it was his time to die, he wanted t
1 CONC o be cremated on the top of the hill. He hauled dry timbe
1 CONC r to the spot to build his funeral pyre and instructed hi
1 CONC s fellow men to cremate his remains there.

1 CONC New York Times
1 CONT Published: September 14, 1981
1 CONT
1 CONT Elizabeth Jane Freedgood and John F. Breyer Jr., graduate
1 CONC s of the Tulane University Law School and lawyers in Washin
1 CONC gton, were married in Brooklyn Heights yesterday.
1 CONT Rabbi David Glazer performed the ceremony in the garden a
1 CONC t the home of the bride's parents, Mr. and Mrs. Frederic E
1 CONC . Freedgood. The bride is a cum laude graduate of the Unive
1 CONC rsity of Pennsylvania. The bridegroom, son of Mr. and Mrs
1 CONC . Breyer of Birmingham, Ala., was graduated from Pennnsylva
1 CONC nia's Wharton School.
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCbŮDJohn F. Breyer Jr.ŮC/bŮD specializes in the representatio
1 CONC n of financial institutions, including savings and loan ass
1 CONC ociations, savings banks, commercial banks and holding comp
1 CONC anies. A graduate of the Tulane University Law School and t
1 CONC he University of Pennsylvania's Wharton School of Business
1 CONC , Mr. Breyer has over 20 years of experience and has repres
1 CONC ented numerous financial institutions located throughout th
1 CONC e United States in various transactions. These transaction
1 CONC s include chartering issues, charter conversions, formatio
1 CONC n of denovo institutions, holding company formations, secur
1 CONC ity offerings and regulatory issues.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mr. Breyer also represents financial institutions in prepar
1 CONC ing periodic & annual federal securities reports and in exe
1 CONC cuting mergers & acquisitions.
1 CONT
1 CONT Prior to entering private practice he was an Honors Progra
1 CONC m Attorney with the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation a
1 CONC nd a banking analyst with the Federal Reserve Bank of Phila
1 CONC delphia, Pennsylvania. Mr. Breyer is a member of the Distri
1 CONC ct of Columbia Bar.

1 CONC per Allyn Moses Frohsin - e-mail - 18 May 2006 Walter Isaac
1 CONC son has become well known for writing Benjamin Franklin's a
1 CONC utobiography.  He was also head of CNN and managing edito
1 CONC r of Time magazine. He now runs the Aspen Institute.

1 CONC Heyman Heyman and Louis Merz came to West Point, GA when th
1 CONC e Atlanta & West Point Rail Road was completed in 1854.  Th
1 CONC e young men began a very modest dry goods business, selecti
1 CONC ng as their motto, "Dieu Ma Forte"...God is my Strength.  T
1 CONC he War between the States ended their association, for Loui
1 CONC s Merz joined the West Point Guard, in April, 1861, and wa
1 CONC s killed at Sharpesburg (Antietam) Maryland September 18, 1
1 CONC 862.  Mr. Heyman and Daniel Merz, brother of Louis, operate
1 CONC d a tannery in Bluffton for the Confederacy during he war y
1 CONC ears.
1 CONT
1 CONT The interesting old house of Heyman and Betty Merz Heyman
1 CONC , at 210 North 14th Street, was built about 1875-77 by Dr
1 CONC . Benjaman Thomas Smith.  Henry Harrington was the builde
1 CONC r in charge of the job.
1 CONT
1 CONT Heyman Heyman came to West Point in 1854.  He and Louis Mer
1 CONC z founded the department store that bore their names, Heyma
1 CONC n & Merz, which served the area almost a century.
1 CONT
1 CONT Heyman Heyman was sometimes referred to as Herman Heyman.
1 CONT

1 CONC h century several Jewish families who had emigrated from Ge
1 CONC rmany settled in West Point. Among them were Heyman Heyma
1 CONC n and Louis Merz who came to Troup County in 1853. They wor
1 CONC ked as bookkeepers and in the mercantile business, and duri
1 CONC ng the Civil War operated a tannery. Several West Point Jew
1 CONC s served in the Confederate Army. Of particular note was Le
1 CONC wis Merz, who served as a war correspondent for the LaGrang
1 CONC e Reporter until he was killed at the battle of Sharpsbur
1 CONC g in 1862.

1 CONC Heyman Heyman and Louis Merz came to West Point, GA when th
1 CONC e Atlanta & West Point Rail Road was completed in 1854.  Th
1 CONC e young men began a very modest dry goods business, selecti
1 CONC ng as their motto, "Dieu Ma Forte"...God is my Strength.  T
1 CONC he War between the States ended their association, for Loui
1 CONC s Merz joined the West Point Guard, in April, 1861, and wa
1 CONC s killed at Sharpesburg (Antietam) Maryland September 18, 1
1 CONC 862.  Mr. Heyman and Daniel Merz, brother of Louis, operate
1 CONC d a tannery in Bluffton for the Confederacy during he war y
1 CONC ears.
1 CONT
1 CONT After the war, Mr. Heyman and Mr. Merz, who had been in th
1 CONC e firm since 1860, continued their business of dry goods a
1 CONC s Heyman & Merz.  Though short of funds, they had the asse
1 CONC t of pre-war confidence in their record of dealings with No
1 CONC rthern concerns.  Mr. Heyman set out on a trip to eastern m
1 CONC arkets and soon West Point and Bluffton residents were trea
1 CONC ted to nbew goods arriving by rail express.  Louis Philli
1 CONC p Heyman and sister, Miss Bertha Heyman, who went to work i
1 CONC n the store as a cashier in 1885, after the death of her fa
1 CONC ther, served the area through their merchandising for mor
1 CONC e than 40 years.  Louis Phillip Heyman died in in 1935.

1 CONC , Nova Scotia. Because of a mix-up with their visas, they h
1 CONC ad to get off there and take the train to Birmingham. The
1 CONC y lived upstairs of sons, Sam and Henri, on St Charles St a
1 CONC nd 15th St South. [Source: Taped interview with daughter, C
1 CONC elia Grusin Danenberg, March 1977.]

1 CONT IMMIGRATION: 28 June 1914 Immigration: Chana Grusin, age 49
1 CONC , housewife, with husband, Jankel-Motel, daughter, Czernia
1 CONC , and nephew, Leib [Leon] Meilach, arrived in Canada, por
1 CONC t of Halifax, Nova Scotia having sailed on the steamer, S
1 CONC S Dvinsk, 2nd class, from Libau (now called Liepaja; SE o
1 CONC f Riga), Latvia on 16 June 1914.  Said she was born and hi
1 CONC s last residence was Glasmanka, province of Witebsk [Vitesk
1 CONC ]; nationality, Russian; race, Hebrew. Height: 5'2"; dark h
1 CONC air, brown eyes. She has no distinguishing marks. Can rea
1 CONC d and write. Closest relative in country she came from [Lat
1 CONC via] was cousin L. Meilach. Going to Birmingham, Alabama. C
1 CONC losest relative in country going to is son, S [Sam] Grusin
1 CONC , City Paper Co., Birmingham. Passage paid by self. Never b
1 CONC efore in U.S. Entered U.S. via Port Huron, Michigan, 1 Jul
1 CONC y 1914, G.T.R. Note: brother-in-law and sister-in-law, Mose
1 CONC s and Reva Grusin stayed on the ship until it landed in Ne
1 CONC w York Jun 29, 1914. [Source: Ships Passenger List, Group 1
1 CONC 8, List 3, Serial 305/13/3, Ship Volume #5362, LDS Microfil
1 CONC m #1401025 & G.T.R. Manifest immigration at Port Huron, MI
1 CONC , M1464, Roll 257 & M1461, Roll 179, LDS Microfilm #1561343
1 CONC .] Note: Gerson May did not work for City Paper, it was Ger
1 CONC son's friend, Mannie Bann, who worked there. [Source: Bett
1 CONC y Jo May letter of Oct 2002.]

1 CONC s old. Lives with husband,  Joseph Grusen [Yankel Mutel Gru
1 CONC sin] 65 years old, rents home at 1620 11th Ave, Birmingham
1 CONC , AL. Yankel's occupation: House work at home. Came to U.S
1 CONC . with wife and children in 1914. Lives with daughter, Celi
1 CONC a, 23 years old, Bookkeeper in a Shoe Store; and son, Henry
1 CONC , 25 years old, Watchmaker at Limehan [sp?]. Also in hous
1 CONC e is a roomer, Simon Balsam [Balsum?], 25 years old, Peddle
1 CONC r for Dry Goods, came to the U.S. in 1914. Yankel, Chana, a
1 CONC nd Celia are aliens, not naturalized. Henry came to the U.S
1 CONC . in 1912 and was naturalized in 1919. Except for Yankel'
1 CONC s mother and father whose 'Mother Tongue' was Yiddish, ever
1 CONC yone elses was German. All born in Riga [actually Glazmanka
1 CONC ?], Latvia. [Source: AL Census 1920, Birmingham, Jefferso

1 CONC Obituary - from the Birmingham News
1 CONT
1 CONT MARKSTEIN, JR., D. HARRY, died at home on August 9, 2009. H
1 CONC e was born in Birmingham on January 16, 1913. He was awarde
1 CONC d a law degree by the University of Alabama in 1934, and h
1 CONC e practiced law in Birmingham for 75 years. Having been pre
1 CONC ceded in death by his wife of 66 years, Elaine Adler Markst
1 CONC ein, he is survived by his children, Helene M. (Philip A.
1 CONC ) Tucker of Chevy Chase, Md., Daniel H. (Rosalind W.) Marks
1 CONC tein, III, and Carolyn M. (Henry I) Frohsin of Birmingham
1 CONC , seven grandchildren and eight great-grandchildren. Afte
1 CONC r a private graveside service on August 12, the family wil
1 CONC l receive friends at Mr. Markstein's residence commencing a
1 CONC t 5 p.m. The family suggests that memorials be sent to Th
1 CONC e University of Alabama Law School Foundation or a favorit
1 CONC e charity.

1 CONC ALVIN M. FERST, JR.
1 CONT
1 CONT born May 6, 1922, died peacefully at his home in Lenbrook S
1 CONC quare, Atlanta, on the afternoon of September 30, 2009 afte
1 CONC r a brief illness. Mr. Ferst was an Atlanta native, the so
1 CONC n of Alvin M. Ferst, Sr. and Doris Marks Ferst. He grew u
1 CONC p in the Druid Hills neighborhood of Atlanta, and lived o
1 CONC n Springdale Road near Emory University. Like many other me
1 CONC n in his family, he went to Georgia Tech, an institution wi
1 CONC th which he maintained a close and lifelong connection. H
1 CONC e was very active in alumni affairs and served on the Edg
1 CONC e Building Campaign Steering Committee, as Chairman of th
1 CONC e Presidentâis Committee To Set Research Direction at Georgi
1 CONC a Tech and of the Committee to Establish A New Organization
1 CONC al Structure of Georgia Tech. As part of his lifelong suppo
1 CONC rt of Georgia Tech, he served as President of both the Geor
1 CONC gia Tech National Alumni Association and the Georgia Tech F
1 CONC oundation. He also served as Secretary of the Georgia Tec
1 CONC h Centennial Campaign Committee. He was inducted into the C
1 CONC ollege of Management Hall of Fame. Alvin Ferst was a sophom
1 CONC ore at Georgia Tech when the United States entered World Wa
1 CONC r II after the attack on Pearl Harbor. He completed Tech i
1 CONC n February 1943, with a degree in Industrial Management. H
1 CONC e was a member of ANAK and ODK, the principal Georgia Tec
1 CONC h honorary societies and of Phi Epsilon Pi social fraternit
1 CONC y. He was a star on Tech's tennis team and also served as V
1 CONC ice-president of the Interfraternity Council. After graduat
1 CONC ion, he joined Philco Corporation as an industrial engineer
1 CONC , but soon joined the US Navy as a member of the Seabees (C
1 CONC onstruction Battalions), the famous construction and engine
1 CONC ering units that built airstrips and bases in the Pacific
1 CONC . He was stationed in Trinidad and the war ended before hi
1 CONC s unit shipped out to the Pacific. After the war, he return
1 CONC ed to Atlanta in 1946 and, inspired by Frank Neely, began t
1 CONC o work at Rich's department stores as an industrial enginee
1 CONC r and manager. He met Charlotte Elizabeth Boyette at Rich's
1 CONC . They married on March 15, 1950 in Atlanta. They lived bri
1 CONC efly in the first high-rise apartments in Buckhead, at 37
1 CONC 4 East Paces Ferry Road, where their first daughter Lane wa
1 CONC s born in 1952. The Fersts soon moved to Golfview Road an
1 CONC d in 1954, their twin daughters Leigh and Leslie were born
1 CONC . Mr. and Mrs. Ferst lived on Golfview Road until Mrs. Fers
1 CONC t's death in 2003, after which Mr. Ferst moved to Lenbroo
1 CONC k Square. Mr. Ferst advanced in management positions at Ric
1 CONC h's and strongly supported and believed in the company poli
1 CONC cy that the customer was always right - that any item woul
1 CONC d be accepted for refund or exchange, no questions asked. M
1 CONC r. Ferst oversaw the expansion of Rich's Department Store f
1 CONC rom its one downtown location to its many satellite store
1 CONC s that we all take for granted today. Rich's and Alvin Fers
1 CONC t were a perfect fit. Mr. Ferst's vision of involving busin
1 CONC ess to meet the cultural and moral needs of the community e
1 CONC xtended far beyond his professional responsibilities. Afte
1 CONC r thirty-five years at Rich's, he retired as Executive Vic
1 CONC e President and Treasurer, and started Alvin Ferst Associat
1 CONC es, Inc., a business and real estate consulting firm. Mr. F
1 CONC erst was a civic leader and served as President of the Atla
1 CONC nta Traffic and Safety Council, Vice-President of the Socie
1 CONC ty for Advancement of Management (Georgia Chapter), directo
1 CONC r of the National Safety Council of the Liberty Mutual Advi
1 CONC sory Board, Atlanta chairman of the National Alliance of Bu
1 CONC sinessmen, Vice-President of the Metro Atlanta YMCA, Presid
1 CONC ent of the Atlanta Kiwanis Foundation, Treasurer of Literac
1 CONC y Action, Chairman of the Board of The Wellness Community o
1 CONC f Atlanta, and as a Director of United Way, Central Atlant
1 CONC a Progress, the Atlanta Chamber of Commerce, the Atlanta Ki
1 CONC wanis Club and the Georgia Engineering Foundation. He and h
1 CONC is late wife Charlotte Boyette Ferst were visionaries in th
1 CONC e Atlanta arts community. With their many friends they help
1 CONC ed to shape the lives and direction of many institutions an
1 CONC d events we take for granted today. He is survived by his t
1 CONC hree daughters, Mary Lane Ferst, Sylvia Leigh Ferst, and Do
1 CONC ris Leslie Ferst; by his brother, Harold W. Ferst; and by h
1 CONC is sister, Babette Ferst Herzfeld. A memorial service wil
1 CONC l be held at The Temple, 1589 Peachtree Street, at 10:00 A.
1 CONC M. on Monday, October 5. In lieu of flowers the family ask
1 CONC s that donations be sent to The Georgia Tech Foundation, 76
1 CONC 0 Spring Street, Suite 400, Atlanta, GA 30308, designated f
1 CONC or the Charlotte and Alvin M. Ferst Memorial Scholarship Fu
1 CONC nd.
1 CONT
1 CONT Alvin Ferst, made Rich's more than a department store
1 CONT By Rick Badie and Larry Harstein Atlanta Journal-Constituti
1 CONC on
1 CONT
1 CONT To Alvin Ferst, Rich's was more than a department store.
1 CONT
1 CONT Alvin Ferst was a decades-long executive for Rich's.
1 CONT
1 CONT
1 CONT He met his future wife there. She was a children's clothin
1 CONC g buyer; he was a rising young executive.
1 CONT
1 CONT In a 35-year career with the company, Mr. Ferst oversaw Ric
1 CONC h's expansion from one downtown store to satellite location
1 CONC s that included the opening of the Lenox Square Mall ancho
1 CONC r in 1959. The Georgia Tech grad handled land purchases an
1 CONC d design aspects.
1 CONT
1 CONT "It wasn't just about building a store to make money," sai
1 CONC d a daughter, Doris Leslie Ferst of Cambridge, N.Y. "It wa
1 CONC s about building a great place for the community."
1 CONT
1 CONT In the early 1980s, Ferst retired as Rich's executive vic
1 CONC e president and treasurer. He then started Alvin Ferst Asso
1 CONC ciates, Inc., a business and real estate consulting firm th
1 CONC at he ran for 10 years.
1 CONT
1 CONT The lifelong Atlanta resident was an admired community supp
1 CONC orter whose impact spread far beyond Rich's. He helped wit
1 CONC h various causes, from affordable housing to inner-city dev
1 CONC elopment and literacy.
1 CONT
1 CONT "The city has lost a great friend and a great contributor,
1 CONC " said Atlanta architect-developer John Portman. "He helpe
1 CONC d the city with maintaining the airport at its present loca
1 CONC tion; helped with the support for the World Congress Center
1 CONC ; helped with the MARTA system; helped with the infrastruct
1 CONC ure in metro Atlanta. He took a tremendous interest in th
1 CONC e community."
1 CONT
1 CONT Wade Burns, a former Carter Center employee, worked with Mr
1 CONC . Ferst on affordable housing issues.
1 CONT
1 CONT "He took under his wing many folks [who were] trying to d
1 CONC o difficult things," Mr. Burns said. "He would volunteer hi
1 CONC s very valuable expertise."
1 CONT
1 CONT A memorial service for Alvin Meinhard Ferst will be at 10 a
1 CONC .m. today at The Temple. He died Wednesday of heart failur
1 CONC e at his home in Lenbrook Square. He was 87.
1 CONT
1 CONT A Georgia Tech industrial management grad, Mr. Ferst wa
1 CONC s a former president of the Georgia Tech Foundation and it
1 CONC s national alumni association.
1 CONT
1 CONT The Tech tennis star was a fixture at Yellow Jackets sporti
1 CONC ng events. He often attended road football games. Four day
1 CONC s before his death, he was at Bobby Dodd Stadium to watch T
1 CONC ech beat North Carolina.
1 CONT
1 CONT "We took him in his wheelchair," his daughter said. "He wan
1 CONC ted to sit in the stands. Everybody was coming over and say
1 CONC ing, 'hi.'"
1 CONT
1 CONT Ferst volunteered and held posts for an array of organizati
1 CONC ons. They included the Metro Atlanta YMCA, Central Atlant
1 CONC a Progress, the Atlanta Chamber of Commerce and the Atlant
1 CONC a Kiwanis Club.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mr. Ferst's wife, Charlotte Elizabeth Boyette Ferst, a majo
1 CONC r arts supporter, died in 2003.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mr. Ferst enjoyed golf and woodworking. His respite was a m
1 CONC ountain get-away in Murray County.
1 CONT
1 CONT "He loved to go up there, look at the view and piddle aroun
1 CONC d," his daughter said.
1 CONT

1 CONC e Ferst of Davis, Cal.; and Sylvia Leigh Ferst of New Yor
1 CONC k City; his brother, Harold W. Ferst of Monroe; and a siste
1 CONC r, Babette Ferst Herzfeld of White Plains, N.Y.

1 CONT
1 CONT John Paul Monahan Jr., World War II veteran
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDJohn Paul Monahan Jr., 87, a resident of New Canaan, Co
1 CONC nn., passed away at Stamford Hospital Saturday, Sept. 25, 2
1 CONC 010. Born Nov. 26, 1922, in Portland Ore., he was the son o
1 CONC f the late John Paul Sr. and Jennie McDonald Monahan. Joh
1 CONC n attended Wilson Teacher's College in Washington, D.C., be
1 CONC fore being inducted into the U.S. Army in 1942, then comple
1 CONC ted a bachelor's degree in political science from Ohio Univ
1 CONC ersity in Athens, Ohio, on the GI bill, then attended The C
1 CONC atholic University of America in Washington, D.C., for grad
1 CONC uate work in political science.
1 CONT
1 CONT He was inducted into the U.S. Army and served in the 5th Ar
1 CONC my, 34th Division, Company K, during World War II from 194
1 CONC 2 to 1945 in North Africa and Italy. He was awarded the Sil
1 CONC ver Star, Bronze Star and Purple Heart for his service. Joh
1 CONC n began his professional life working for the U.S. Air Forc
1 CONC e at the Pentagon. Then he worked in the textile industry f
1 CONC or the Henry J. Tully Co. in New York City, and retired a
1 CONC s vice president of planning and budget. After retiring fro
1 CONC m the textile business he finished his teaching degree an
1 CONC d taught high school history and economics. Later on in hi
1 CONC s retirement he worked in the Motion Picture and Recorded S
1 CONC ound Division of the U.S. Library of Congress.
1 CONT
1 CONT He was a member of the Rehoboth Beach chapter of Kiwanis In
1 CONC ternational and also a member of the St. Vincent De Paul So
1 CONC ciety in Rehoboth. While living in Darien, Conn., for the p
1 CONC ast 11 years, he was a member of the Darien Senior Men's As
1 CONC sociation and St. John Church. He also was a member of Pi G
1 CONC amma Mu National Social Science Honor Society.
1 CONT
1 CONT John has six surviving children –  Barbara Monahan of Lewes
1 CONC , Clare Myers (Jeff) of Darien, Christopher Monahan (Kathy
1 CONC ) of Westport, Conn. and Walpole, N.H., Kathryn Monahan o
1 CONC f Levittown, N.Y., Henry Monahan (Jean) of Santa Fe, N.M.
1 CONC , and Sally Zogry (Michael) of Lawrence, Kan. He is also su
1 CONC rvived by a brother, Terrance Delano Monahan (Loretta) of L
1 CONC akewood, Colo., and two sisters, Patricia Ann Wallace of D
1 CONC e Bary, Fla., and Maureen Monahan Maskrey (Bill) of Thiensv
1 CONC ille, Wis. He is also survived by a sister-in-law, Marian T
1 CONC ully Conroy, of Charlotte, N.C. He had 15 grandchildren - R
1 CONC achel and Julia O'Neal; Ellie and Carrie Monahan; Henry, Ch
1 CONC arlotte and Virginia Myers; Kayleigh, Owen and Jeffrey Mona
1 CONC han; Nancy Haas; Henry and Kate Monahan; Sophia and Danie
1 CONC l Zogry; as well as numerous nieces, nephews, great-niece
1 CONC s and great-nephews. He was preceded in death by his wife C
1 CONC arol Tully Monahan on Sept. 11, 1999, and by a son, John Pa
1 CONC ul Monahan III on Jan. 24, 1998. He was also preceded in de
1 CONC ath by a brother, Edward Dean Monahan, in 1973.
1 CONT

1 CONC ohn Roman Catholic Church in Darien. Burial will be private
1 CONC . In lieu of flowers the family suggests memorials to the J
1 CONC ay Monahan Center for Gastrointestinal Health, New York-Pre
1 CONC sbyterian Hospital/Weill Cornell Medical Center, 1315 Yor
1 CONC k Ave., Floor 1, New York, NY 10021.

1 CONC ŮCbŮDRobert Schoenhof Weil
1 CONT From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
1 CONT Robert Schoenhof WeilŮCuŮDŮC/bŮD[1]ŮC/uŮD (born November 29, 191
1 CONC 9 in ŮCuŮDMontgomery, Alabama ŮC/uŮD) is chairman emeritus of W
1 CONC eil Brothers-Cotton, Inc. He was President and Director o
1 CONC f the American Cotton Shippers Association, and has serve
1 CONC d on the Boards of the Atlantic Cotton Association, the ŮCuŮD
1 CONC Liverpool, England ŮC/uŮD, Cotton Association, and the ŮCuŮDNe
1 CONC w Orleans Cotton Exchange ŮC/uŮD. He has also served as a del
1 CONC egate to the National Cotton Council, International Federat
1 CONC ion of Cotton and Allied Textile Industries at London, Inte
1 CONC rnational Cotton Advisory Committee, and the White House Co
1 CONC nference on Export Trade Expansion. Weil has worked with th
1 CONC e ŮCuŮDUnited States Congress ŮC/uŮD and the ŮCuŮDDepartment of A
1 CONC griculture ŮC/uŮD in developing legislation and cotton policy.
1 CONT His civic affairs background includes work with or philanth
1 CONC ropy for: the ŮCuŮDAmerican Cancer Society ŮC/uŮD, ŮCuŮDSalvatio
1 CONC n Army ŮC/uŮD, ŮCuŮDUnited Way ŮC/uŮD, ŮCuŮDUnited Negro College Fu
1 CONC nd ŮC/uŮD, Eye Foundation Hospital of Birmingham, St. Margare
1 CONC t's Hospital in Montgomery, ŮCuŮDAuburn University ŮC/uŮD, ŮCuŮDH
1 CONC untingdon College ŮC/uŮD, ŮCuŮDDartmouth College ŮC/uŮD, and ŮCuŮDW
1 CONC heaton College, Massachusetts ŮC/uŮD. He is a founder of th
1 CONC e ŮCuŮDMontgomery Academy ŮC/uŮD.
1 CONT He has been on the boards of both the ŮCuŮDMontgomery Museu
1 CONC m of Fine Arts ŮC/uŮD and the Montgomery Symphony Orchestra.
1 CONT In 1994 Weil, along with his brother, was named co-"Citize
1 CONC n of the Year" by the Montgomery Advertiser.ŮCuŮD[2]ŮC/uŮD
1 CONT ŮCbŮDFamily
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDRobert Weil is one of four children of Adolph Weil an
1 CONC d Rossie Weil (ŮCiŮDnâeeŮC/iŮD Schoenhof). Adolph was a second g
1 CONC eneration partner and principal of Weil Brothers-Cotton, In
1 CONC c. Rossie was the daughter of a bookstore owner from Boston
1 CONC . ŮCuŮDSchoenhof's Foreign Books ŮC/uŮD was founded in 1856. Lo
1 CONC cated in ŮCuŮDHarvard Square ŮC/uŮD, it is the oldest foreign-l
1 CONC anguage bookseller in the ŮCuŮDUnited States ŮC/uŮD. It is repu
1 CONC ted as having been a rendezvous point of ŮCuŮDLongfellow ŮC/uŮD
1 CONC , ŮCuŮDRalph Waldo Emerson ŮC/uŮD, and ŮCuŮDThoreau ŮC/uŮD. Regardl
1 CONC ess of whether this was the case, Carl Shoenhof, owner of S
1 CONC choenhof books, took pride in corresponding regularly wit
1 CONC h ŮCuŮDCharles Dickens ŮC/uŮD.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDEarly life
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDWeil graduated from ŮCuŮDCulver Military Academy ŮC/uŮD a
1 CONC t 16 and entered Dartmouth College in 1936. (He had skippe
1 CONC d second and fourth grades.) In 1940, he began study at ŮCuŮD
1 CONC Harvard Business School ŮC/uŮD. Upon completion of the busine
1 CONC ss program, he joined the US Army as a ŮCuŮDsecond lieutenan
1 CONC t ŮC/uŮD. He married Virginia Loeb of Montgomery, Alabama i
1 CONC n January of 1942.
1 CONT ŮCbŮDCareer
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDWeil joined Weil Brothers-Cotton, Inc. not long after t
1 CONC he end of the ŮCuŮDSecond World War ŮC/uŮD. At the time, he wa
1 CONC s to work for his father and his uncle. As the story goes
1 CONC , Weil had to negotiate his salary with his uncle rather th
1 CONC an Adolph. This caused a bit of a stir. But it also initiat
1 CONC ed Weil into the trading aspect of the business. "Cotton me
1 CONC n" are notoriously difficult negotiators.
1 CONT By taking a position at WBCI, Weil joined his brother, Adol
1 CONC ph Jr., as the third generation of Weils at the company. Th
1 CONC e two brothers were made to start as "squidges;" essentiall
1 CONC y janitors for the sample room. A sample room is a room i
1 CONC n a cotton trading firm's building where cotton samples ar
1 CONC e handled and evaluated. A large amount of loose cotton mak
1 CONC es it's way to the floor and must be cleaned sometimes seve
1 CONC ral times in a day.
1 CONT Weil's handling of the broom eventually changed into handli
1 CONC ng of samples and then into handling of accounts and sales
1 CONC . By 1949, he was making calls on mills overseas. And in 19
1 CONC 68 he and Adolph, Jr. took over the business as president a
1 CONC nd chairman, respectively.

1 CONC Obituary - Birmingham News & Montgomery Advertiser (Alabama
1 CONC )
1 CONT 6 October, 2008
1 CONT
1 CONT WEIL, Virginia Loeb, 86, a lifelong resident of Montgomery
1 CONC , AL, died peacefully at her home Friday, October 3, 2008
1 CONC . Funeral services will be held at Leak-Memory Chapel on Tu
1 CONC esday, October, 7, 2008, at 2:00 p.m., Rabbi Elliot Steven
1 CONC s officiating, with visitation there beginning at 1:00 p.m
1 CONC . Interment will follow immediately at Greenwood Cemetery
1 CONC . Mrs. Weil was preceded in death by her parents, Anita an
1 CONC d Victor Loeb, as well as her sister, Frances Loeb Block. S
1 CONC he is survived by her devoted husband of 66 years, Robert S
1 CONC choenhof Weil; her children, Virginia Ann (Vicki) Weil of W
1 CONC ashington, DC, Rosalind Weil Markstein (Daniel H. III) of B
1 CONC irmingham, AL, and Robert Schoenhof Weil, II; her grandchil
1 CONC dren, Daniel Harry Markstein, IV (Eileen) of Birmingham, AL
1 CONC , Nicholas Weil Langman (Jennifer), Virginia Weil Markstein
1 CONC , Andrea Ross Langman, Robert Schoenhof Weil, III (Alicia)
1 CONC , and Georgia Ruth Cardinal Weil as well as 3 great-grandch
1 CONC ildren and numerous nieces and nephews. Mrs. Weil has lon
1 CONC g been known for her great love of family and friends, he
1 CONC r extraordinary beauty, wit and charm. She was generous i
1 CONC n deed and spirit, which led to her many community commitme
1 CONC nts. She was involved in the establishment of the Montgomer
1 CONC y Academy from its inception. As an arts student and advoca
1 CONC te, she was a member of the Alabama State Arts Council, ser
1 CONC ved as president of the Montgomery Museum of Fine Arts, vic
1 CONC e-president of the Montgomery Symphony, and president of th
1 CONC e Antiquarians Society. Mrs. Weil was a lifetime member o
1 CONC f Temple Beth-Or and served much of that time on its Sister
1 CONC hood Committee. For numerous years she was a Pink Lady fo
1 CONC r St. Margaret's Hospital. More recently, she helped to est
1 CONC ablish the Montgomery Area Food Bank. Mrs. Weil attended Mi
1 CONC ss Margaret Booth's School, Sidney Lanier High School and W
1 CONC heaton College in Norton, Massachusetts. Pallbearers will b
1 CONC e her grandsons, Daniel Markstein, IV, Nicholas Langman an
1 CONC d Robert Weil III as well as her great nephews, Josh Mandel
1 CONC l, Charles Mandell, Drew Weil and Dustin Weil. Honorary pal
1 CONC lbearers will be her beloved doctors, Wick Many, Jr. and Du
1 CONC ncan McRae, Jr. as well as her good friends,Jim Levy, Taylo
1 CONC r Dawson and Will Hill Tankersley, and the members of The T
1 CONC hirteen. In Lieu of flowers, contributions may be directe
1 CONC d to Temple Beth-OR, 2246 Narrow Lane Road, Montgomery, 361
1 CONC 06, The Montgomery Area Food Bank, 521 Trade Center Street
1 CONC , Montgomery, 36108, or the charity of your choice. Leak-Me
1 CONC mory Chapel Directing

1 CONC In a world where cotton was king, no one was ever more wort
1 CONC hy of the title of crown prince than Adolph I. Weil, Jr. Th
1 CONC e Montgomery native would rise through the ranks of his fam
1 CONC ily business to become a legend in the rough-and-tumble cot
1 CONC ton industry. A Dartmouth and Harvard graduate, Weil was ad
1 CONC mitted to the Alabama Bar Association in 1938. Weil went t
1 CONC o work for his father and uncle at Weil Brothers-Cotton
1 CONC , a company founded by Weil grandfather in 1878. Weil start
1 CONC ed at the bottom, sweeping floors and learning the art of g
1 CONC rading cotton. In 1941, Weil was drafted into the U.S. Arm
1 CONC y Medical Service Corps. At the end of World War II, he ret
1 CONC urned to Weil Brothers and Butler, a branch of Weil Brother
1 CONC s-Cotton, Inc. in New York. While there he met and marrie
1 CONC d Jean Kaufman. In 1950, Weil and his brother Bobby becam
1 CONC e directors and officers in the company. Weil served as th
1 CONC e president of the American Cotton Shippers Association an
1 CONC d Atlantic Cotton Association, and as a director of the Ne
1 CONC w York Cotton Exchange and National Cotton Council. Followi
1 CONC ng their father death in 1968, Weil became chairman and hi
1 CONC s brother president of Weil Brothers-Cotton, Inc. Weil an
1 CONC d his brother formed a holding company in 1980, Weil Enterp
1 CONC rises and Investment, Ltd. The brothers assumed correspondi
1 CONC ng roles in the holding company. Weil was involved in chari
1 CONC table organizations including American Cancer Society, Unit
1 CONC ed Way and the Montgomery Food Bank. A project Weil is mos
1 CONC t revered for was his personal crusade to save the Meals-On
1 CONC -Wheels program in Montgomery. Weil was killed on Decembe
1 CONC r 12, 1995, in an automobile accident.

1 CONC Dr. Laurie Jean Weil
1 CONT ...
1 CONT Laurie Jean Weil is a native of Montgomery and received a B
1 CONC .A. degree in psychology at Smith College and a D.V.M. degr
1 CONC ee with honors from Tuskegee University.Her children are Jo
1 CONC shua and Mary Virginia Butler Mandell of Montgomery and Cha
1 CONC rles D. Mandell of San Francisco.
1 CONT
1 CONT Dr. Weil founded and directs Camp Sunshine which provides s
1 CONC ummer camping opportunities, year-round Boy and Girl Scou
1 CONC t activities, and self-esteem building experiences for yout
1 CONC h-at-risk.She currently serves as Chairman of the Board o
1 CONC f Huntingdon College and the co-chair of Envision 2020.Sh
1 CONC e is the chairman of the eight million dollar capital and e
1 CONC ndowment campaign for the Montgomery Museum of Fine Arts.
1 CONT
1 CONT Dr. Weil has received training and works with groups on wel
1 CONC coming diversity and prejudice reduction.She is co-founde
1 CONC r of Interchange, a community-building process developed fo
1 CONC r and sponsored by Leadership Montgomery.In addition to ser
1 CONC ving as one of the four Envision 2020 Co-Chairs, the board
1 CONC s upon which she serves include the Alabama Shakespeare Fes
1 CONC tival, the Montgomery Museum of Fine Arts, the Central Alab
1 CONC ama Community Foundation and the Federation of Child Care C
1 CONC enters of Alabama (FOCAL).
1 CONT
1 CONT Community recognition includes Voluntary Action Center Volu
1 CONC nteer of the Year Award The Montgomery Advertiser Women o
1 CONC f Achievement Award National Association of Community Leade
1 CONC rship Organizations Distinguished Leadership Award
1 CONT
1 CONT
1 CONT By Martha Rouse Gates
1 CONT T here's no stopping Dr. Laurie Jean Weil. A retired Montgo
1 CONC mery veterinarian,
1 CONT she is always on the go and doing something good. Now she i
1 CONC s doing something good that tastes good, too.
1 CONT A dessert that Weil made in her kitchen in 2003 has
1 CONT evolved into a gourmet treat that is being manufactured
1 CONT at Priester's Pecan Co. in Fort Deposit.
1 CONT Weil is passionate about her latest venture. She is running
1 CONT her own business —             Turtle Tree Treat
1 CONC s —             that produces gourmet chocolate confection
1 CONC s filled with chopped fruits and nuts. Making additions an
1 CONC d improvements seems to be in Weil's nature. She
1 CONT says a new treat with a "sophisticated flavor" will be ou
1 CONC t within the year.
1 CONT
1 CONT y, Turtle Tree Treats, was fun, risky, and
1 CONT involved making many complex decisions.
1 CONT  Weil said attending business education programs is
1 CONT "a must." She suggests The Montgomery Area Chamber
1 CONT of Commerce's Small Business Resource Center, which
1 CONT offers workshops, seminars and classes on all aspects of
1 CONT starting a business. From attending a Chamber course,
1 CONT Weil learned that the name of her company needed to
1 CONT reflect what it does. The Alabama Cooperative Extension
1 CONT Service also is a helpful resource. It offers workshops
1 CONT that bring together food processors and producers
1 CONT from around the state. Here Weil learned about health
1 CONT department inspections and labeling requirements.
1 CONT  Operating a company requires a business
1 CONT license. Certain food business must obtain a
1 CONT Health Department Inspection Certificate.
1 CONT  Retailers want to know a product's shelf lif
1 CONC e –           the
1 CONT length of time the product can be stored without
1 CONT deterioration. A testing lab will determine the shelf lif
1 CONC e of
1 CONT a product. A list of food testing laboratories is available
1 CONT from The Alabama Cooperative Extension Service.
1 CONT  Weil suggests going to trade shows such as The
1 CONT Atlanta Gourmet Market at AmericasMart to check out
1 CONT the competition, and talk to exhibitors and suppliers.
1 CONT "Lots of Alabama people are represented there," she
1 CONT said. The National Association for the Specialty Food
1 CONT Trade, Inc. (NASFT) offers International Fancy Food
1 CONT and Confection Shows in New York, California and
1 CONT Chicago. Weil says NASFT sets high standards, and
1 CONT people all over the world attend their shows. Last
1 CONT yWeil attended the food show in Chicago.
1 CONT "The workshops for people like me were outstanding,"
1 CONT Weil said. From attending the Chicago Fancy Food
1 CONT and Confection Show, Weil learned it would be more
1 CONT beneficial for her to have a co-packer, a company
1 CONT that manufactures and packages her products, rather
1 CONT than develop a commercial kitchen on her own. This
1 CONT led to her relationship with Priester's Pecan Co.
1 CONT Weil's warm smile widens as she talks openly about
1 CONT Turtle Tree Treats. A sense of order and harmony
1 CONT permeates her inviting home, which is casually elegant
1 CONT with fresh flowers, family photographs, books and
1 CONT works of art. Her purring cat and friendly dog, Courage,
1 CONT are as welcoming as their owner.
1 CONT Her family loves to gather at her house for meals,
1 CONT Weil says. She introduced her first batch of confections
1 CONT to her family for dessert at a Passover meal. The recipe
1 CONT she created was a variation on charoses, a tasty Jewish
1 CONT dish made of nuts and fruits. Weil's recipe included her
1 CONT favorite chopped dried fruits formed into small balls,
1 CONT which she rolled in cinnamon. Her family thought the
1 CONT treats were delicious.
1 CONT Weil said she later covered the balls in chocolate
1 CONT instead of cinnamon, and again presented them to her
1 CONT family. Her mother thought the chocolate treats tasted
1 CONT divine, and came up with the name Heavenly Balls.
1 CONT The original formula includes an extra fancy grade
1 CONT of dried apricots, black mission figs, prunes, raisins
1 CONT and Medjhool dates. Weil says Heavenly Balls are
1 CONT made with the finest ingredients she can find. The
1 CONT dried fruits come from California.
1 CONT "Once we have chopped the fruits and the roasted
1 CONT nuts, we combine these with honey, dark brown sugar,
1 CONT spices and a dash of Concord grape wine for flavoring
1 CONT –           just as you might use a bit of vanilla in you
1 CONC r cooking,"
1 CONT she explains. "The result is a very sticky mixture from
1 CONT which we form and weigh each individual ball by
1 CONT hand."
1 CONT "Next, we enrobe each ball in the highest quality
1 CONT chocolate. Each ball is dipped in either white chocolate,
1 CONT milk chocolate or dark chocolate, not once, but twice to
1 CONT ensure that with each bite, you have enough chocolate
1 CONT to complement fully the fruit and nut center."
1 CONT Weil said she especially enjoys the creative
1 CONT processes of her business. In addition to the creativity
1 CONT of her recipes, she loves the writing, design and
1 CONT marketing aspects of having her own company. LWT
1 CONT Communications, a Montgomery advertising firm,
1 CONT worked closely with her to develop the company's logo
1 CONT and packaging designs, but Weil maintains control of
1 CONT every aspect of Turtle Tree Treats.
1 CONT "I love that this product seems to make people happy,
1 CONT whether they're talking about it or tasting it," Weil
1 CONT said.
1 CONT She has a knack for making people happy.
1 CONT Weil founded and directs Camp Sunshine, which
1 CONT has been providing area at-risk youth with camping
1 CONT and self-esteem building opportunities for 20 years.
1 CONT She has received many leadership awards including
1 CONT the Montgomery Advertiser Citizen of the Year Award,
1 CONT the Voluntary Action Center Volunteer of the Year
1 CONT Award, and the National Association for Community
1 CONT 10  Montgomery's Elite
1 CONT  Leadership's Distinguished Leadership Award. She
1 CONT serves on several boards of directors and is co-chair
1 CONT of Envision 2020, an improvement planning effort
1 CONT involving citizens and leaders in the River Region.
1 CONT Weil has plans for her treats to be on the go, just
1 CONT as she is. "My goals include moving the products up
1 CONT the East Coast into metropolitan areas and then going
1 CONT west," she said. In addition to tasting divine, Weil says
1 CONT Heavenly Balls reflect the care and pride of the people
1 CONT in Alabama who make the gourmet treats.
1 CONT The new treat will be rolled in cinnamon, like the
1 CONT ones Weil first made for her family's Passover meal in

1 CONC By  Steven G. Vegh   The Virginian-Pilot Ă September 29, 20
1 CONC 08
1 CONT ŮCbŮDVIRGINIA BEACH ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT In the 1960s, the voices teenaged Howard Mandell heard loud
1 CONC est were Martin Luther King Jr., Bobby Kennedy and Jews i
1 CONC n the civil rights movement. "I was at an early age impress
1 CONC ed by the role the Jewish people were playing" as freedom r
1 CONC iders and social justice activists, said Mandell, a Conserv
1 CONC ative Jew. "When you're a minority, you're especially attun
1 CONC ed to what other members of your minority group are doing.
1 CONC " Mandell became an activist himself as the first staff att
1 CONC orney for the Southern Poverty Law Center, and then as a ca
1 CONC reer civil rights lawyer for indigent clients in Montgomery
1 CONC , Ala. But in the 1990s, he was distracted again by a voice
1 CONC , one detectable only to himself. "It was the calm, still v
1 CONC oice within," an utterance Mandell recognized as coming fro
1 CONC m God. He answered the call by chucking his law career an
1 CONC d graduating this spring from Jewish Theological Seminary i
1 CONC n New York as a rabbi. Tonight, he'll lead his first High H
1 CONC oly Days service as the new rabbi of Temple Emanuel at th
1 CONC e Oceanfront. "Being a rabbi is doing the same work I was d
1 CONC oing before, but doing it again under the aegis and leaders
1 CONC hip of God," Mandell said. Both vocations help repair the w
1 CONC orld, a mission Judaism calls tikkun olam, he said. Mandell
1 CONC , 63, has brown eyes, salt-and-pepper hair and a long, pati
1 CONC ent face. His voice is hardly louder than the soothing, gen
1 CONC tle crash of waves from an ambient sound machine in his off
1 CONC ice. The mildness belies his intense convictions. "In cour
1 CONC t he wasn't always soft-spoken - he could be impassioned wh
1 CONC en he needed to be," said Bobby Segall, a Montgomery lawye
1 CONC r and board member of the American Civil Liberties Union o
1 CONC f Alabama. But behind the passion is compassion, friends sa
1 CONC y. Richard Cohen, president of the Southern Poverty Law Cen
1 CONC ter, said he watched Mandell talk with murderers in New Yor
1 CONC k's Sing Sing prison about personal transformation. "Howar
1 CONC d was incredibly good at getting people to express their fe
1 CONC elings and feel safe in front of each other," Cohen said. "
1 CONC I don't think anyone had a dry eye." ŮCbŮDGrowing up ŮC/bŮDin t
1 CONC he Jewish community of Providence, R.I., Mandell never thou
1 CONC ght about being a rabbi. As an artsy-minded college student
1 CONC , his first career choice was writing fiction. His mother w
1 CONC as a law school graduate, but it was his father, an MIT-tra
1 CONC ined engineer, who implored him to try law school. "Becaus
1 CONC e of all the anti-Semitism at the time, he wanted me to hav
1 CONC e something I could fall back on and be my own boss," Mande
1 CONC ll said. As a good son, he reluctantly agreed to try law sc
1 CONC hool. The decision changed his life. Under professors' ment
1 CONC oring at Georgetown University's law school, Mandell got ho
1 CONC oked on constitutional law and civil rights work. His instr
1 CONC uctors included Philip J. Hirschkop, a lawyer who helped wi
1 CONC n the U.S. Supreme Court's rejection in 1967 of Virginia'
1 CONC s ban on interracial marriage. After graduating in 1970, Ma
1 CONC ndell became a clerk for Frank M. Johnson, a federal judg
1 CONC e in Montgomery. Johnson is honored today for his landmar
1 CONC k desegregation rulings, but in 1970, he was a pariah in Al
1 CONC abama, as Mandell quickly discovered after moving there fo
1 CONC r his new job. At a party, he told neighbors he was clerkin
1 CONC g for a local judge. Which judge? the neighbors asked. "Jud
1 CONC ge Johnson," Mandell replied. Conversation froze. "No one s
1 CONC poke to me, and after 15 minutes, I left." It was an earl
1 CONC y taste of how deeply locals might revile his career choice
1 CONC . After a year's clerkship, Mandell became the first lawye
1 CONC r hired by the SPLC, which represents indigent clients, esp
1 CONC ecially minorities, in civil rights cases. "He excelled i
1 CONC n telling the stories of those who were accused or injured,
1 CONC " said SPLC co-founder Morris Dees, who hired Mandell. "H
1 CONC e was able to win over juries because he told their storie
1 CONC s in a real-life way." After two years Mandell formed his o
1 CONC wn civil rights law practice, Alabama's first racially inte
1 CONC grated law firm. "We were going to save the world. We ha
1 CONC d a cause and so much to be done - it was hard to say no t
1 CONC o people being victimized," Mandell said. In 1977 he helpe
1 CONC d start the state's first federally funded legal services g
1 CONC roup. "He was incredibly devoted to his clients and had a d
1 CONC eep, deep, abiding sense of justice," Cohen said. "If you h
1 CONC ad Howard on your side, you were going to prevail, more oft
1 CONC en than not." Eventually, Mandell's practice expanded to in
1 CONC clude general law cases and represented Major League Baseba
1 CONC ll players including Oscar Gamble, a black slugger from Ala
1 CONC bama. Family also was a focus - he married and had two son
1 CONC s - but not religion. Mandell said he did not attend synago
1 CONC gue regularly. ŮCbŮDAnd yet,ŮC/bŮD the voice eventually began t
1 CONC o speak. "I started asking myself, 'God, is there somethin
1 CONC g else you want me doing with my life?' " Mandell said. I
1 CONC n 1996, he took time off from his practice - a lot of time
1 CONC . "I thought I'd be back in a week." Instead, during a year
1 CONC s-long sabbatical, he played piano, slowed his life's tempo
1 CONC , increased his synagogue attendance and studied Torah in I
1 CONC srael. In 2000, the new mayor of Montgomery recruited Mande
1 CONC ll to be city attorney. "I said, 'I sue the city of Montgom
1 CONC ery, I don't represent it.' " The mayor implored him to hel
1 CONC p reform the city's image. Mandell held the job for two yea
1 CONC rs, while his inner voice pestered him. Ultimately, a chanc
1 CONC e - or divinely engineered - encounter with the visiting Je
1 CONC wish Theological Seminary's dean convinced Mandell to switc
1 CONC h vocations in 2002. "I'd probably never thought of rabbini
1 CONC cal school, but it's what I needed to hear, wanted to hear
1 CONC . I don't think it's all serendipity." ŮCbŮDNow, preparing ŮC/
1 CONC bŮDfor Rosh Hashana and Yom Kippur, Mandell is a bit nervous
1 CONC . "The anxiety comes from this being my first time, reall
1 CONC y the first time where I'm totally responsible as the rabbi
1 CONC ," he said. Rosh Hashana, the Jewish new year, starts the 1
1 CONC 0 days of High Holy Days that conclude with Yom Kippur, als
1 CONC o known as the Day of Atonement. Mandell said his sermons w
1 CONC ill stress traditional themes. "The High Holidays are a tim
1 CONC e for self-reflection, introspection and how can I improv
1 CONC e my relationship with God and with other human beings," Ma
1 CONC ndell said. "It's an opportunity to get in touch with tha
1 CONC t divine part of us."

1 CONT The Kansas City StarŮC/bŮD - August 23, 2009ŮCbŮD
1 CONT John Weil Uhlmann
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDJohn W. Uhlmann, 64, of Prairie Village, KS, passed awa
1 CONC y Friday, August 21, 2009. Funeral services will be at 2:0
1 CONC 0 p.m. Sunday, August 23, at The Temple, Congregation B'na
1 CONC i Jehudah, 12320 Nall, Overland Park, KS. Private family bu
1 CONC rial. In lieu of flowers, the family suggests contribution
1 CONC s to The Jewish Federation of Greater Kansas City, Nationa
1 CONC l Center for Fathering or the Autism Society of Kansas City
1 CONC . John was Chairman of the board of The Uhlmann Company
1 CONC , a family-owned grocery products firm. He was president o
1 CONC f Hyman Brand Hebrew Academy, The Jewish Federation of Grea
1 CONC ter Kansas City, the Kansas City Art Institute and the Loos
1 CONC e Foundation. John was Chairman of the Kansas City Operatio
1 CONC n Exodus. He was also Chairman of the Barbecue Industry Ass
1 CONC ociation of America. He served on the boards of United Isra
1 CONC el Appeal and United Jewish Communities where he was a memb
1 CONC er of the Overseas Needs Assessment and Distribution (ONAD
1 CONC ) Committee. He was on the national boards of the Jewish In
1 CONC stitute for National Security Affairs (JINSA), Toward Tradi
1 CONC tion, and Voices United for Israel. John was member of th
1 CONC e board of UMB Bank and Menorah Hospital. He was a member o
1 CONC f the Young Presidents Organization, Center for National Fa
1 CONC thering, AIPAC, United Jewish Communities Board and the Int
1 CONC ernational Board of UJA and the Oakwood Country Club. Joh
1 CONC n was a member of The Temple, Congregation B'nai Jehudah, t
1 CONC he New Reform Temple and was a former member of their board
1 CONC s. He was a Sunday school teacher at The Temple and tutore
1 CONC d math at an inner city Catholic school. He was a Bridge Li
1 CONC fe Master and was a member of Mensa. John was a graduate o
1 CONC f the Pembroke Country Day School and attended Dartmouth Co
1 CONC llege and Kansas State University where he studied Millin
1 CONC g Technology. He was born in Kansas City, MO, and was a lif
1 CONC elong area resident. John was a Vietnam veteran of the U.S
1 CONC . Army serving in Special Psychological Operations. John ha
1 CONC s been actively involved in national politics and nationa
1 CONC l political organizations. He ran for the Republican nomina
1 CONC tion for the third Congressional District in Kansas in 1994
1 CONC . He was preceded in death by his parents, R. Hugh Uhlman
1 CONC n and Helen Jane Weil Uhlmann and a sister and brother- in-
1 CONC law, Patricia and Marvin Rich. John is survived by his wif
1 CONC e of 37 years, Patricia Uhlmann, of the home; daughters an
1 CONC d sons-in-law: Katherine and Dennis Aikman and their childr
1 CONC en, Jennifer and Taylor, all of Marblehead, MA; Meg and Jus
1 CONC tin Shaw and their children, Joshua, Madeline and Adam, al
1 CONC l of Leawood, KS; brother and sister-in-law: Robert and Sal
1 CONC ly Uhlmann, Bozeman, MT; mother-in-law: Heloise Werthan Kuh
1 CONC n, Nashville, TN; sister-in-law: Joni P. Werthan, Nashville
1 CONC , TN; uncles: Paul Uhlmann, Jr., Kansas City, MO and Rober
1 CONC t S. Weil, Sr., Montgomery, AL; aunt: Jean K. Weil, Montgom
1 CONC ery, AL; nieces and nephews: Alison Caponetto, Meredith Ang
1 CONC rist, Anya Holmes, Lex and Sam Uhlmann. Many cousins also s
1 CONC urvive. John was the pillar of his family and his community
1 CONC . He was a man of dignity, loving kindness, courage, wisdom
1 CONC , generosity of spirit and was always there for everyone. H
1 CONC e was a true Mensch. We were all blessed to have had him i
1 CONC n our lives and he will always be in our hearts. John's str
1 CONC ongest conviction was "What is hateful to you, do not do un
1 CONC to others." Online guest book available @ www.louismemorial
1 CONC chapel.com (Arr: The Louis Memorial Chapel 816-361- 5211.)

1 CONT The Kansas City
1 CONT JEWISH CHRONICLE
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDŮCiŮDWritten by Rick Hellman, Editor
1 CONT 28 August 2009 12:00
1 CONT ŮCbŮDSpot lightŮC/bŮD
1 CONT ŮC/iŮDJohn Uhlmann, who died Friday, Aug. 21, at age 64, wa
1 CONC s recalled this week by Jewish Kansas Citians as a warm, ge
1 CONC nerous and dedicated community leader distinguished by a pr
1 CONC obing intellect.ŮCiŮD ŮC/iŮD
1 CONT He served as president of the Jewish Federation (1997-99) a
1 CONC nd of the Hyman Brand Hebrew Academy (2004-06), among his o
1 CONC ther important volunteer leadership positions.
1 CONT He was president of The Uhlmann Co., a family business whos
1 CONC e interests include the Heckers and Ceresota flour brands
1 CONC . A one-time candidate for U.S. Congress, Uhlmann was know
1 CONC n as a political conservative in a largely liberal Jewish c
1 CONC ommunity, a fact recalled several times at his funeral Sund
1 CONC ay. So was his good-humored, respectful approach to philoso
1 CONC phical differences.
1 CONT Rabbi Arthur Nemitoff of The Temple, Congregation B'nai Jeh
1 CONC udah, read the words of Robert Uhlmann regarding his late b
1 CONC rother: "It was his friendly, open nature that allowed hi
1 CONC m to see beyond a person's particular affiliation, to see t
1 CONC he real person underneath."
1 CONT Robert Uhlmann said that, as an adult, John "taught himsel
1 CONC f Hebrew, read through the Bible and immersed himself in al
1 CONC l sorts of Jewish thought."
1 CONT "He was a very spiritual person," said former Jewish Federa
1 CONC tion Executive Director Bob Gast, who served with John Uhlm
1 CONC ann. "He was not an individual who may have been regarded a
1 CONC s observant, but he was deeply religious and spiritual."
1 CONT "He was a man of conviction, but he didn't force it on anyb
1 CONC ody," said Merilyn Berenbom, who succeeded him as Federatio
1 CONC n president and who worked with Uhlmann on any number of pr
1 CONC ojects. "He was a man of ideals; very willing to engage i
1 CONC n a respectful debate."
1 CONT ŮCbŮDCommon groundŮC/bŮD
1 CONT Berenbom credited Uhlmann with helping to revamp Federation
1 CONC 's approach to fundraising in the 1990s.
1 CONT "I think that he really sought a common ground where togeth
1 CONC er we could strengthen our community," Berenbom said. "He l
1 CONC ooked at everybody as stakeholders. He used the term commun
1 CONC ity conversation for so many years. … He really sought to k
1 CONC now what was important; Why do you support Federation? What
1 CONC 's meaningful to you? It became interactive. Instead of a s
1 CONC olicitation, it's a community conversation.
1 CONT "He was an extraordinary conversationalist. He was extremel
1 CONC y respectful of every perspective, realizing there is grea
1 CONC t value in providing a wide spectrum of input. As a Jewis
1 CONC h community, we are not homogenous, and that's our strength
1 CONC . However, we still have a common denominato
1 CONC r —              that we are responsible, one for the other
1 CONC . And he found ways to make that meaningful on an individua
1 CONC l basis."
1 CONT Federation Associate Executive Director Alan Edelman said t
1 CONC hat, "among the volunteer leaders I have had the honor to w

1 CONC g the journey. The trip got off to a hectic start. Fischbac
1 CONC h had nearly backed out when she had a panic attack as sh
1 CONC e was about to board her plane in Orlando; she went home an
