1 CONC orters arranged for by the Norgays. On the third day, i
1 CONC n a remote part of the Annapurna range, the group spent al
1 CONC l morning on a grueling climb of about 2,000 feet in 90-deg

1 CONC e uniforms are really flimsy. There's cold, loneliness, an
1 CONC d boredom." At night, Uhlmann reads McCorkle's journals aga
1 CONC in before sleeping, and they give her "the most horrendous
1 CONC " dreams. "I know a lot of my dreams are me being her. I he
1 CONC ar the noise, I feel the fluorescent light and the cold
1 CONC . I wake up cold a lot because she's so darn cold. I just h
1 CONC ave to shake myself out of it. If it weren't for my yoga I'
1 CONC d go nuts." ŮCbŮDBut Uhlmann has felt that sameŮC/bŮD kind of c
1 CONC old in more than just her dreams. In 1968, Uhlmann was a 19
1 CONC -year-old hippie with a business designing one-of-a-kind ou
1 CONC tfits for rock stars and celebrities like Goldie Hawn, Jani
1 CONC ce Joplin, The Rolling Stones, and Jefferson Starship. Sh

1 CONC za, Spain, and on a trip to Beirut to buy Syrian bedspread
1 CONC s and cloth for robes, Uhlmann was arrested at the airpor
1 CONC t and thrown in prison. Her crime: being listed in the addr
1 CONC ess book of an American celebrity for whom she designed clo
1 CONC thes and who had become involved in the international dru
1 CONC g and weapons trade. Uhlmann had no idea what was going t
1 CONC o happen to her. She was told to keep her mouth shut or sh
1 CONC e would be killed. She was thrown in a basement dungeon wit
1 CONC h rancid water up to her thighs, with feces and water rats

1 CONC om of her bare feet with a sand-filled hose until her arche
1 CONC s were destroyed. Soldiers tried to rape her, and she watch

1 CONC gainst a wall because it wouldn't stop crying. After two ye
1 CONC ars, prison officials released her with an apology -- but n
1 CONC o explanation. Uhlmann says the country had been on the edg
1 CONC e of a civil war, and the political upheaval contributed t
1 CONC o her detainment. She sees it as a "badge of honor" that sh

1 CONC n America are so bad, so barbaric," Uhlmann says. "That, an

1 CONT Amy Victoria Oberdorfer, the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Joh
1 CONC n Oberdorfer of Washington, was married last evening to Da
1 CONC n Fredrik Nyberg, the son of Mr. and Mrs. Bengt Nyberg of J
1 CONC arfalla, Sweden. The Rev. Jan H. Kesker, a Lutheran ministe
1 CONC r, officiated at the Willard Hotel in Washington. Rabbi Jul
1 CONC ie Greenberg took part in the ceremony.
1 CONT Mr. and Mrs. Nyberg graduated from Yale, where they met.
1 CONT Mrs. Nyberg, 29, received a master's degree in internationa
1 CONC l affairs from the Institut d'âEtudes Politiques in Paris an
1 CONC d is now a second-year law student at Georgetown University
1 CONC . Her father is a partner in Patton Boggs, the Washington l
1 CONC aw firm. Her mother is an artist.

1 CONT 5 February 2010
1 CONT
1 CONT LOEB, James Lucien was born on April 6, 1922, and died Febr
1 CONC uary 4, 2010. James Loeb was a native of Montgomery, and gr
1 CONC aduated from the University of North Carolina. He was als
1 CONC o a World War II veteran. James Loeb entered the cotton bus
1 CONC iness in 1946 with Weil Brothers Cotton, Inc. and founded L
1 CONC oeb & Company, Inc. in 1969. He retired in 2007. He was com
1 CONC mitted to the well-being of the Montgomery community and de
1 CONC dicated to civic life here. Historic preservation was a foc
1 CONC us of his life. He founded Landmarks Foundation, and what i
1 CONC s now Old Alabama Town, where he was president for many yea
1 CONC rs. Old Alabama Town now has 50 buildings, including the Lo
1 CONC eb Education Center, named after him. His other board invol
1 CONC vement included: Past President, Atlantic Cotton Associatio
1 CONC n, founding member and long time board member of Friends o
1 CONC f the Alabama Archives, former member and President of th
1 CONC e Chancellor's Advisory Board, Auburn Montgomery, Huntingdo
1 CONC n College Board of Trustees, Board Member, Montgomery Museu
1 CONC m of Fine Arts, Advisory Board of Trustees, National Trus
1 CONC t for Historic Preservation, Past President, Temple Beth Or
1 CONC , Past President, Montgomery Symphony Orchestra, and Past P
1 CONC resident, Landmarks Foundation, and Past President of the J
1 CONC ewish Federation of Montgomery. Awards received include th
1 CONC e Alabama Historical Commission, Business Friends of the Ar
1 CONC ts, and Montgomery Alabama Council on Aging. He is precede
1 CONC d in death by his parents, Helen Weil Loeb, and Lucien S. L
1 CONC oeb. He is survived by his loving wife of 53 years, Joan By
1 CONC ck Loeb, and two sisters, Eda Loeb Newhouse of Scarsdale, N
1 CONC Y and Katherine Loeb Wiener of Jackson, MS; three daughters
1 CONC , Helen Ruth Loeb of Montgomery, Elizabeth Loeb Friedman (G
1 CONC len) of Los Angeles, CA, Patricia Loeb Rosenbaum (Maurice
1 CONC ) of Atlanta, GA., and his son James Lucien Loeb, Jr. (Caro
1 CONC l) of Montgomery; and eight grandchildren Andrew Friedman
1 CONC , Jonathan Friedman, Jack Loeb, Sarah Claire Loeb, and Cath
1 CONC erine Loeb, Allison Rosenbaum, Michelle Rosenbaum, and Laur
1 CONC a Rosenbaum. Visitation will be at Temple Beth Or at 12:3
1 CONC 0 on Sunday, February 7th, 2010, followed by a 2:00PM servi
1 CONC ce. A family burial service will follow at Oakwood Eterna
1 CONC l Rest Cemetery. Rabbi Elliott Stevens will be officiating
1 CONC . Pallbearers will be: Ken Burton, David Byck, Edwin Byck
1 CONC , Littleton Carothers, Douglas Newhouse, James Newhouse, Fr
1 CONC ed Phelps, John Snead and John Wiener. Honorary pallbearer
1 CONC s will be: Sanford Cohen, Lanny Crane, Leo Drum, James Levy
1 CONC , Employees of Loeb & Company, Paul Miles, Jeffrey Oppenhei

1 CONC JULES LOEB FOGEL, JR, 70, died October 23, 2010 after a lon
1 CONC g battle with prostate cancer. Born in Shreveport, LA to Ma
1 CONC ry Loeb Fogel and Jules Fogel, Sr., Mr. Fogel attended Tula
1 CONC ne University and graduated from the University of Missour
1 CONC i where he received a Bachelor of Journalism degree. He wa
1 CONC s editor and publisher of "The Shreveport Social Register
1 CONC " (a newspaper that he started), a former managing editor o
1 CONC f "Vivian's Caddo Citizen", and a reporter for "The New Orl
1 CONC eans Times-Picayune". Mr. Fogel was owner and manager of Fo
1 CONC gel Properties in Shreveport. In 1970 Mr. Fogel launched th
1 CONC e Laubach Literacy Program in Shreveport and certified 36 i
1 CONC nstructors as well as personally teaching many people to re
1 CONC ad and write. Mr. Fogel was elected president of the Shreve
1 CONC port Art Guild in 1960, having previously served on the boa
1 CONC rd of directors for three years, and was president of The E
1 CONC nglish-Speaking Union, 1996-97. He was a board member of th
1 CONC e Shreveport Opera. He was also an active member !
1 CONT of Tarshar, a men's club modeled after Benjamin Franklin'
1 CONC s Philosophic Society. He served in 1987 as the first Presi
1 CONC dent of the International Brotherhood of Magicians' (IBM) A
1 CONC rk-La-Tex Ring 267. At that time he introduced a rehabilita
1 CONC tion program, David Copperfield's "Project Magic," to the S
1 CONC hriner's Crippled Children's Hospital in which he and othe
1 CONC r local magicians worked with occupational therapists teach
1 CONC ing children therapeutic tricks. Mr. Fogel was preceded i
1 CONC n death by his parents. He is survived by a daughter, Lauri
1 CONC n Fogel Porter of Memphis, TN, and her husband, Waring; thr
1 CONC ee grandchildren, Alex, Mary Grace and Andrew; a sister Flo
1 CONC ra Lebowitz of Memphis; and two nephews, Scott (Carie) an
1 CONC d Bennett Lebowitz, both of Memphis. Visitation will be fro
1 CONC m 5-7 p.m. Wednesday, October 27, at Canale Funeral Directo
1 CONC rs. Funeral services will be 2:30 p.m. Thursday, October 28
1 CONC , at the Lord's Chapel in Elmwood Cemetery. The family requ
1 CONC ests memorials be sent to the Memphis Animal Shelte!
1 CONT r or LeBonheur Children's Medical Center. Canale Funeral Di
1 CONC rec!
1 CONT tors 901
1 CONT -452-6400

1 CONC ŮCbŮDNew York TImesŮC/bŮD
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCbŮDClinton O. Mayer Jr., Broker, 91
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDPublished: November 20, 1989ŮCbŮD
1 CONT ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT Clinton O. Mayer Jr., a broker with Drexel Burnham Lamber
1 CONC t who was believed to be the oldest member of the New Yor
1 CONC k Stock Exchange, died of heart failure Saturday at New Yor
1 CONC k Hospital. He was 91 years old, lived in Manhattan, and co
1 CONC ntinued to go to his office each day, his family said.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mr. Mayer was a native of Philadelphia and a graduate of Pr
1 CONC inceton University. He served in the cavalry in World Wa
1 CONC r I and in Alaska in World War II, attaining the rank of ma
1 CONC jor. He was a former president of the Jewish Home and Hospi
1 CONC tal for the Aged.
1 CONT
1 CONT Surviving are a daughter, Jane Eisner of Madison, Wisc.
1 CONC ; a son, Clinton 3d, of Manhattan; a sister, Elinor Brunswi
1 CONC ck of Philadelphia, and five grandchildren.

1 CONC In Montgomery, Alabama, Charlotte Alexander Weil kept a coo
1 CONC kbook journal from the 1950s through the 1980s in which sh
1 CONC e described dinner parties, brunches, barbecues, and cockta
1 CONC il gatherings, as well as her guests' reactions to particul
1 CONC ar recipes and dishes that she served.[28] Jewish foods wer
1 CONC e served on Jewish holidays, and dishes made with shrimp an
1 CONC d sausages were used for secular entertaining. Recipes fo
1 CONC r "tomato aspic for 20," "cocktail shrimp for 25," "Blood
1 CONC y Mary's for 12," "Frances' Punch for 30," "sauce for ham,
1 CONC " "5 doz. 'pigs'" (sausage and biscuits), "Jo Baum's Lepkuc
1 CONC hen," "Bee's matzo balls," "Julia Pake's 1//2 pound cake,
1 CONC " "Egg nog—   very stiff," and "Charoses for 10" suggest ho
1 CONC w Weil's social world was tied to both the German Jews in M
1 CONC ontgomery's Reform Temple Beth Or and upper-class Gentile M
1 CONC ontgomery society.[29] After her Passover haroset recipe We
1 CONC il noted "very good. People ate 2nd and 3rds."[30]
1 CONT Charlotte Weil's daughter Judith Weil Shanks recalls that h
1 CONC er mother's closest Jewish friends were Frances Baum, Juli
1 CONC a Pake, Jo Baum, and Julia Wise, women whose names frequent
1 CONC ly appear on recipes in her journal.[31] Charlotte Weil an
1 CONC d her German Jewish friends prepared desserts, holiday an
1 CONC d party foods, pickles, and preserves and oversaw menus fo
1 CONC r daily meals prepared by African American cooks such as Ru
1 CONC th Lewis, who worked for the Weil family for more than thir
1 CONC ty years. These Jewish women saw that their families ate tr
1 CONC aditional foods such as haroset and matzoh balls at Passove
1 CONC r, but they also took care to never appear "too Jewish." Ch
1 CONC opped liver was served like a mousse, and buttered matzoh b
1 CONC alls were served as a side dish.
1 CONT

1 CONC Among the leading merchants of Winchester may be classed th
1 CONC e Hainsfurther Brothers. They have been reared in the busin
1 CONC ess and from early childhood merchandising has been almos
1 CONC t a second nature with them. The name Hainsfurther is almos
1 CONC t a household word in Scott county. Way back yonder in 185
1 CONC 9 Moses and Solomon Hainsfurther embarked in business i
1 CONC n a moderate way in the Chapman building on the south sid
1 CONC e of the square. For nine years they dealt with the peopl
1 CONC e from this building and then moved to the Flynn building o
1 CONC n the west side of the square, where they remained for thir
1 CONC teen years. Their trade increased and with that increase ca
1 CONC me the increase in stock, and, in order to obtain more room
1 CONC , rented two rooms in the Smith building, just south of the
1 CONC ir old stand and opened out with a large stock of dry goods
1 CONC , carpets and notions in one room and boots, shoes and clot
1 CONC hing in the other. In 1890 they built a new store buildin
1 CONC g (now occupied by Sol Hainsfurther as a shoe store) and us
1 CONC ed it exclusively for clothing, hats, caps and gents furnis
1 CONC hing goods. The business thus divided into three department
1 CONC s they made many improvements and added largely to their st
1 CONC ock. In June, 1889, they purchased the shoe business of Dan
1 CONC iel Smith, on the north side of the square and for a year c
1 CONC onducted business there in conjunction with their other sto
1 CONC res, but finally merged the stock into their own store on t
1 CONC he west side. After the fire, in 1895, they, sharing with D
1 CONC aniel Smith, erected a splendid two story building, and whe
1 CONC n completed, moved their goods and occupied the entire buil
1 CONC ding. During this same year (1895) they incorporated unde
1 CONC r the name of the Hainsfurther Mercantile Co., with the fol
1 CONC lowing incorporators: Solomon Hainsfurther, Sr., Moses Hain
1 CONC sfurther, Albert Hainsfurther, David Hainsfurther and Solom
1 CONC on Hainsfurther, Jr. This continued until February, 1901, w
1 CONC hen the corporation ceased to exist and the shoe departmen
1 CONC t was sold to Solomon Hainsfurther, Jr., and the clothing
1 CONC , furnishings and dry goods departments to Albert and Davi
1 CONC d Hainsfurther.
1 CONT
1 CONT David Hainsfurther was born at Winchester, October 26, 1867
1 CONC , and received his education at Winchester and at a Chicag
1 CONC o business college. At Quincy, June 8, 1892, he was marrie
1 CONC d to Miss Nellie Berger, daughter of Simeon and Theresa (We
1 CONC inberg) Berger. Mrs. Hainsfurther was born in Quincy, Octob
1 CONC er 8, 1869. Two children - Dorothy and Madeline - have com
1 CONC e to bless their home. Mr. Hainsfurther enjoys membership w
1 CONC ith the Masons and Odd Fellows.

1 CONC Marcus was born in Brooklyn, New York, January 9, 1880, an
1 CONC d came to Salt Lake City in 1907. He established a movie th
1 CONC eater chain in Utah and other intermountain states, and ser
1 CONC ved as the director of two Salt Lake City banks. A Republic
1 CONC an, he was the only Jewish mayor of Salt Lake, serving fro
1 CONC m 1932-1936.
1 CONT
1 CONT Louis Marcus was the city's only Jewish mayor. He served fr
1 CONC om 1932-1936. E.B. Erwin defeated Marcus in an upset.  Loui
1 CONC s Marcus was a Salt Lake motion picture pioneer as well a
1 CONC s one of the city's more efficient and honest mayors.
1 CONT
1 CONT 1913: The building we know as Capitol Theatre opens in down
1 CONC town Salt Lake City. 1927: Utah movie pioneer Louis Marcu
1 CONC s buys the theater for $300,000,  and remodels it, changin
1 CONC g the name to Capitol Theatre. A "mighty Wurlitzer" theate
1 CONC r organ is installed to accompany silent movies, with Alexa
1 CONC nder Schreiner (the Tabernacle organist) as its primary mus
1 CONC ician.
1 CONT When the theatre raised its curtain on September 29, 1927
1 CONC , it had a new name. The Orpheum was now Capitol Theatre. T
1 CONC he "all-talking" picture was introduced to Capitol Theatr
1 CONC e in 1929 when On Trial, a Warner Brothers feature was proj
1 CONC ected on the screen with a Victaphone bringing the star's v
1 CONC oice to the audience.
1 CONT

1 CONT Tuesday, July 28, 1936
1 CONT MARCUS, Louis
1 CONT Dated: Tuesday, July 28, 1936
1 CONT Headline: Marcus' Rites Set Thursday-Hour As Yet Undecided
1 CONC ; Arrival of Kin Awaited
1 CONT SALT LAKE CITY, July 28---(AP)---Tentative arrangements fo
1 CONC r funeral services Thursday for former Mayor Louis Marcus
1 CONC , who died yesterday, were made today pending the arrival o
1 CONC f relatives.
1 CONT En Route to Salt Lake City are an only son, Louis Howard Ma
1 CONC rcus, coming from Yellowstone park, and two brothers, Harry
1 CONC Marcus of Denver, and Eugene Marcus of this city, who was i
1 CONC n Philadelphia.

1 CONC as credited by business men with fiscal efficiency which sa
1 CONC w the city through a trying depression period with unimpair
1 CONC ed credit and with excellent financial resources

1 CONC BIRTH: 10 Jan 1923. Samuel (Shmuel) Manevich, born in Varak
1 CONC lani, Latvia. [Source: [Source: Sam Manevich letters of 194
1 CONC 5 and 1977; phone call and notes of Aug 1998.]
1 CONT
1 CONT FAMILY NOTES:  Family moved to Riga in 1938. Was in Riga pr
1 CONC ison when parents, two sisters, and many relatives who wer
1 CONC e in the Riga ghetto were murdered by the Nazis. Then, Sa
1 CONC m was sent to a Nazi concentration camp. The last 6 month
1 CONC s he was in Stuthof concentration camp near Danzig, Poland
1 CONC . Sam was liberated on 11 March 1945. In May 1945, he was s
1 CONC ent to an English displaced persons camp in Berlin, Germany
1 CONC .  From there Sam was sent to Antwerp, Marsailles, and fina
1 CONC lly made his way to Israel.
1 CONT     Sam joined his brother, Paltiel (Palet) in Tel Aviv, Is
1 CONC rael. He stayed in Israel for 4 years. Joined the Israeli a
1 CONC rmy stationed in Gallilee, near Safed, and was also a membe
1 CONC r of the Urgun underground. Worked for the Elite Company (c
1 CONC hocolate factory), where other friends from Riga, Latvia we
1 CONC re working.
1 CONT     In 1952, Sam immigrated to the United States. He lande
1 CONC d in New York City and then went to Cincinnati, OH. Sam mov
1 CONC ed back to New York City and went into the fur business, a
1 CONC s his cousin, Isaac Meilach, was already in the business. [
1 CONC Source: Sam Manevich letters of 1945 and 1977; phone call a
1 CONC nd notes of Aug 1998.]
1 CONT
1 CONT OCCUPATION:  July 1967: Worked in the fur business. His bus
1 CONC iness card said: Samuel Manevich, Chilewich Sons & Co, Hide
1 CONC s and Skins, 120 Wall Street, New York Whitehall 4-5400. Ho
1 CONC me: 666 West End Ave, NYC 799-3226 (SE 4-8200 ext 12P). [So
1 CONC urce: A business card found among Ben Gruzen's family tre
1 CONC e folder.]
1 CONT

1 CONC in, in Chicago, IL. [Source: Sam Manevich notes of Aug 1998
1 CONC .]
1 CONT

1 CONC rk City, Sam moved to Chicago, IL. [Source: Sam Manevich ph
1 CONC one call of Aug 1998.]

1 CONC RUTH SAGER HAD TWO distinguished careers. In the first sh
1 CONC e was a leading exponent of organelle, non-nuclear genetics
1 CONC ; in the second she was a major innovator in cancer genetic
1 CONC s, proposing, discovering, and investigating roles of tumo
1 CONC r suppressor genes. At the pinnacle of research on the prob
1 CONC lem of non-nuclear or cytoplasmic genetics for many years
1 CONC , she almost single-handedly developed this subject of non-
1 CONC Mendelian, cytoplasmic genetics ("A vast, unexplored regio
1 CONC n of genetics was opened here today" [1963]). The very exis
1 CONC tence of hereditary determinants other than nuclear genes w
1 CONC as doubted by a large part of the scientific community, alt
1 CONC hough it was proposed in 1908 from observations on higher p
1 CONC lants. Sager gathered data and argued in support of a secon
1 CONC d genetic system in the face of great skepticism and finall
1 CONC y made this a respectable and exciting major area of geneti
1 CONC cs. During her final 25 years she transferred her efforts t
1 CONC o the genetics of cancer. Among her outstanding contributio
1 CONC ns, she devised the first cell lines and culture medium cap
1 CONC able of culturing and comparing normal and cancer cells. Sh
1 CONC e emphasized the major role of chromosome rearrangements an
1 CONC d the accelerated evolution of cancer cells and the require
1 CONC ment in a cancer of more than one mutated gene, importantl
1 CONC y of tumor suppressor genes in addition to oncogenes. She p
1 CONC roposed as early as 1974 that individual genetic defects co
1 CONC uld be corrected by transferring DNA into cells. "One nee
1 CONC d not be doomed by one's genes."1 She was a pioneer in th
1 CONC e novel subject she named "expression genetics," the identi
1 CONC fication by their mRNAs of genes that are functionally modi
1 CONC fied in cancers. She successfully identified numerous gene
1 CONC s that are not mutated but whose expressions are altered i
1 CONC n breast cancers, such as the mammary serpin maspin. She wo
1 CONC rked to the end, publishing innovative articles and obtaini
1 CONC ng a National Institute of Health grant in the month befor
1 CONC e her death. During her career she published two books (196
1 CONC 1, 1972) and more than 200 research articles. Ruth Sager wa
1 CONC s born in Chicago on February 7, 1918, daughter of Leon Sag
1 CONC er, a businessman with strong intellectual interests, and D
1 CONC eborah Borovik Sager, who died in the 1918 influenza epidem
1 CONC ic. She and her sisters, Esther and Naomi, were bought up b
1 CONC y her stepmother, Hannah, in an atmosphere honoring learnin
1 CONC g. She graduated at 16 from New Trier High School. She rece
1 CONC ived an S.B. in mammalian physiology in 1938 at the Univers
1 CONC ity of Chicago: "the best thing that ever happened to me.
1 CONC " Her interest in science was sparked by Anton J. Carlson'
1 CONC s lectures: "He was just a fantastic teacher."2 In 1944 sh
1 CONC e received an M.S. in plant physiology at Rutgers Universit
1 CONC y. Her World War II years were spent as a secretary and a
1 CONC n apple farmer. Her 1948 Ph.D. in maize genetics was unde
1 CONC r Marcus M. Rhoades at Columbia University. From 1949 to 19
1 CONC 51 she was a Merck postdoctoral fellow with Sam Granick a
1 CONC t the Rockefeller Institute, working on the chloroplast. Fr
1 CONC om 1951 to 1955 she was a staff member at Rockefeller, wher
1 CONC e she chose the alga Chlamydomonas reinhardi as a model org
1 CONC anism. She was a research scientist from 1955 to 1965 at Co
1 CONC lumbia University and worked for a year in Edinburgh durin
1 CONC g that period. For 20 years, until the age of 48, she coul
1 CONC d not obtain a faculty position. "I guess I knew I was righ
1 CONC t, and I wasn't terribly upset."2 Beginning in 1966 and unt
1 CONC il 1975 she was a professor at Hunter College. Finally, i
1 CONC n 1995, she was appointed professor of cellular genetics a
1 CONC t Harvard Medical School--among the first women to gain a f
1 CONC ull professorship at Harvard--and chief of the Division o
1 CONC f Cancer Genetics at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute. Sh
1 CONC e was also a Guggenheim fellow at the Imperial Cancer Resea
1 CONC rch Fund, London, during 1972-73, and was elected to member
1 CONC ship of the National Academy of Sciences in 1977. Sager wa
1 CONC s first married to Seymour Melman in 1944 and then to the a
1 CONC uthor of this memoir in 1973. She had no children. She die
1 CONC d March 29, 1997, of bladder cancer in her home in Brooklin
1 CONC e, Massachusetts, at the age of 79. She is survived by he
1 CONC r sisters, Esther Altschul and Naomi Sager, and her husband
1 CONC , Arthur Pardee. Ruth Sager was innovative, highly intellig
1 CONC ent, enthusiastic, very dedicated to her science, and hard-
1 CONC working; she had high standards and expected equal dedicati
1 CONC on from her coworkers. She did not suffer fools gladly. He
1 CONC r views of science as a career were:3 The first thing is t
1 CONC o be sure of your own abilities. Science is very demanding
1 CONC , you have to be able to think very well and also have a ve
1 CONC ry good memory. You have to really love it. Science is a wa
1 CONC y of life. I think it all comes from the inside. It reall
1 CONC y gets to the very core of your existence. It is much lik
1 CONC e being an artist or a dancer. It's something that demand
1 CONC s everything from you that you are capable of. I have alway
1 CONC s been intrigued by the physicists' approach to scientifi
1 CONC c inquiry, particularly in the fact that the way to find ou
1 CONC t something really new is to question the basic tenet of ex
1 CONC isting theory. Very early Sager believed that genetics wa
1 CONC s the core of biology; she knew she was right and she set o
1 CONC ut to prove it. She never ceased introducing new technique
1 CONC s and concepts into her field, but she found her work ignor
1 CONC ed until her discoveries proved the majority wrong. But sh
1 CONC e never really paid a lot of attention to what other peopl
1 CONC e think. She was described in her fifties as "a calmly arti
1 CONC culate and attractive woman (who looks younger by about 1
1 CONC 5 years) . . . a tall, striking brunette with a ready smil
1 CONC e and a voice that carries a merry lilt."4,5 She early desc
1 CONC ribed herself as "probably the happiest person I know."5 No
1 CONC t at all narrowly devoted to her science, Sager had numerou
1 CONC s outside interests: modern art, travel, music and theater
1 CONC , a rich social life, and she was a fine cook. She took u
1 CONC p tennis late in life and played it with great enthusiasm--
1 CONC in spite of limited ability. She was especially fond of rel
1 CONC axing at Woods Hole, where she had a cherished second home
1 CONC , and where she is buried. Among her honors and distinction
1 CONC s were Phi Beta Kappa in 1938, Sigma Xi in 1947, Guggenhei
1 CONC m Fellowship in 1972, Schneider Memorial Lecture Award in 1
1 CONC 973, National Academy of Sciences membership in 1977, Ameri
1 CONC can Academy of Arts and Sciences membership in 1978, Harve
1 CONC y Society Lecture in 1984, outstanding investigator at th
1 CONC e National Cancer Institute in 1985, Gilbert Morgan Smith M
1 CONC edal from the National Academy of Sciences in 1988, members
1 CONC hip in the Institute of Medicine in 1992, Princess Takamats
1 CONC u Award (Japan) in 1992, alumni medallist of the Universit
1 CONC y of Chicago in 1994, and membership on the Advisory Counci
1 CONC l of the National Institute on Aging. At the beginning of h
1 CONC er career Sager saw the advantages of studying genetics wit
1 CONC h a model microorganism that had a chloroplast, a sexual li
1 CONC fe cycle, grew rapidly, and could readily be manipulated fo
1 CONC r controlling growth and mating. She chose the single cel
1 CONC l alga "Chlamydomonas, a peerless group of organism
1 CONC s . . . nutritious, esthetically pleasing, and amenable t
1 CONC o laboratory experimentation."2 With talented coworkers, es
1 CONC pecially her long-time collaborator Zenta Ramanis, she: Dev
1 CONC eloped a mating system for the organism. Early investigate
1 CONC d the genetics of the organism--both Mendelian and non-Mend
1 CONC elian--with clear demonstration of the maternal inheritanc
1 CONC e pattern of the latter. Discovered with Y. Tsubo the firs
1 CONC t specific "cytoplasmic" gene mutagen, streptomycin, and id
1 CONC entified mutants by their resistance to this drug. Discover
1 CONC ed ribosomes in the choloroplast of Chlamydomonas, differen
1 CONC t from those in the cytoplasm, thus providing evidence tha
1 CONC t expression of genetic information as proteins is carrie
1 CONC d out by a different system. Discovered with M. R. Ishida t
1 CONC hat unique DNA is located in isolated chloroplasts. This wa
1 CONC s the evidence that convinced most scientists that there i
1 CONC s indeed a separate non-nuclear organelle genetic system. P
1 CONC erformed biochemical studies of the mechanism of exclusio
1 CONC n of paternal genes. Developed a system that makes geneti
1 CONC c mapping possible by permitting expression of paternal gen
1 CONC es. Developed several mapping methods and first published c
1 CONC ytoplasmic linkage groups and extensive mapping of an organ
1 CONC elle. She showed that the chloroplast DNA is circular. Demo
1 CONC nstrated with an in vitro system the basis of maternally in
1 CONC herited drug resistance. Discovered a eukaryotic restrictio
1 CONC n enzyme. Discovered that there is communication between nu
1 CONC cleus and organelles--they send molecular signals back an
1 CONC d forth. Showed that maternal DNA is methylated and paterna
1 CONC l DNA is not and proposed this difference as the basis of s
1 CONC elective destructive elimination of the paternal DNA. Of he
1 CONC r second career, cancer research, she said, "I had really w
1 CONC anted to work on cancer, but it seemed like a very difficul
1 CONC t thing to do. . . . We think that the first change in canc
1 CONC er is a genetic change--something acts to transform an indi
1 CONC vidual cell--whether that something be a viral infection o
1 CONC r a chemical or radiation."2 Entry into the subject was dur
1 CONC ing her sabbatical at the Imperial Cancer Research Fund i
1 CONC n 1972-73. From that time her career and her husband's wer
1 CONC e independent but highly mutually supportive. The questio
1 CONC n was: Which genes cause normal cells to become cancer cell
1 CONC s? Oncogenes, recently discovered at that time, were propos
1 CONC ed as the basis, but Sager championed inactivations of tumo
1 CONC r suppressor genes. "Nature's own approaches to cancer prot
1 CONC ection" are in addition deeply involved and are "a vast unt
1 CONC apped resource for anticancer therapy."2 Sager suggeste
1 CONC d a yin-yang balance of these for cellular homeostasis. Amo
1 CONC ng her cancer research accomplishments were the following
1 CONC : She developed a model system that allows detailed compari
1 CONC sons in the same culture medium between well-matched norma
1 CONC l and tumor Chinese hamster embryo fibroblasts (CHEF cells)
1 CONC . She emphasized the multigenic basis of tumorigenicity. A
1 CONC s with her earlier work she was among the first to champio
1 CONC n this then unpopular view at a time when all attention wa
1 CONC s on single oncogenes. In the late 1970s she initiated inve
1 CONC stigations into tumor suppressor genes. As with her researc
1 CONC hes on chloroplast genetics, " there was really no interes
1 CONC t in tumor suppressor genes at all until about mayb
1 CONC e . . . 1990."2 She demonstrated tumor suppression with cel
1 CONC l hybrids and cybrids. Remarkable examples are suppressor g
1 CONC enes that promote programmed cell death of defective cells
1 CONC , and these are inactivated in tumors. She showed an initia
1 CONC l example of increased genetic instability in cancer cells
1 CONC . A genetic change, amplification of the methotrexate resis
1 CONC tance gene, developed much faster in tumor cells than in no
1 CONC rmal cells. She decided that gene expression would best b
1 CONC e investigated in human cells. For this purpose she create
1 CONC d a workable human breast cancer cell culture system in whi
1 CONC ch normal and tumor epithelial cells could grow and at simi
1 CONC lar rates. She introduced the concept of expression genetic
1 CONC s, the study of changed gene expressions. Using subtractiv
1 CONC e hybridization, she discovered the IL-8-related gro gene a
1 CONC nd others whose mRNA level is modified in tumor cells. Sh
1 CONC e then shifted to the new, simpler differential display tec
1 CONC hnique to discover numerous additional potential tumor supp
1 CONC ressor genes, ones whose expression is lost in breast cance
1 CONC rs. And she began an investigation of the means to reactiva
1 CONC te their expression. Her favorite example was maspin, the g
1 CONC ene for a serine protease inhibitor, which is lost in advan
1 CONC ced breast cancers and inhibits tumor invasion and metastas
1 CONC is. Vigorous research on this gene continues in numerous la
1 CONC boratories, including those of several of her past student
1 CONC s and fellows. She found that these under-expressed genes w
1 CONC ere not mutated, unlike classical tumor-related genes. He
1 CONC r plan was to use these under-expressed genes as markers fo
1 CONC r detection and diagnosis, and she hoped for therapies base
1 CONC d on restoring their functions. Her later interests include
1 CONC d methylation of DNA and its specific enzymatic cutting an
1 CONC d chromosome rearrangements in tumor cells. Her legacy is e
1 CONC xpressed in the quotation:5 "For more than half a century R
1 CONC uth Sager has been a role model for women in health-relate
1 CONC d scientific research. . . . She demonstrated vision, insig
1 CONC ht and determination to develop novel scientific concepts i
1 CONC n the face of established dogmas. . . . Her pioneering rese
1 CONC arches and original ideas continue to make contributions t
1 CONC o biology." When asked near the end of her life what she co
1 CONC nsidered to be her most important contribution, she answere
1 CONC d, "Well, I don't think I've made it yet." Many colleague
1 CONC s have carried on her researches, and papers based upon the
1 CONC se researches continue to appear. She was a major construct
1 CONC ive force in the scientific and personal lives of her man
1 CONC y friends and students. She was a role model for many women
1 CONC , being among the earliest successful woman scientists in s
1 CONC pite of major career obstacles, but she was never highly ac
1 CONC tive in the women's liberation movement. When faced with th
1 CONC e built-in prejudice of the male scientific community again
1 CONC st women, she responded by saying there was nothing she cou
1 CONC ld do, except to be as good a scientist as possible. She ha
1 CONC d great concerns in 1994 about politics and the future of s
1 CONC cience. "The strong influence of fashions in scientific tho
1 CONC ught continues to play an inhibitory role in scientific pro
1 CONC gress. I think science is in a rut right now. The way grant
1 CONC s are given out just makes matters worse, because the exper
1 CONC iment has to be so obvious and practically done already bef
1 CONC ore they'll fund it."2 Her career twice demonstrated that s
1 CONC ome of the best science needs faith and support of novel id
1 CONC eas from the most creative minds. NOTES 1 The unattribute
1 CONC d quoted material in this memoir is derived from personal c

1 CONC BIRTH: 26 June 1934. David Grusin, born in Littleton, CO. [
1 CONC Source: Guiness Encyclopedia of Popular Music, Vol 2,  Pag
1 CONC e 1039, 1994.]
1 CONT
1 CONT OCCUPATION: Composer, musician. Plays both piano and keyboa
1 CONC rds. Arranger and producer of both jazz and pop. Wrote musi
1 CONC c for the films: The Graduate, On Golden Pond, etc. Has wo
1 CONC n many Grammy awards (The Graduate, etc) and been nominate
1 CONC d for an Academy Award. Composes music for movies and TV. C
1 CONC o-founder and co-owner of GRP Records. Pioneered all digita
1 CONC l recordings. Composed theme for the movie: On Golden Pond
1 CONC .  [Source: Guiness Encyclopedia of Popular Music, Vol 2, P
1 CONC age 1039, 1994.]
1 CONT
1 CONT  b. 26th June 1934, Littleton, Near Denver, Colorado, U.S.A.
1 CONT Dave Grusin was born far from the streets of New York, in t
1 CONC he small farm town of Littleton, Colorado just south of Den
1 CONC ver.
1 CONT His father Henri Grusin had also immigrated from Riga, Latv
1 CONC ia around 1913.
1 CONT He had married an American-born woman, Rosabelle.
1 CONT The Grusin's settled in Littleton and raised three children
1 CONC , Dave's brother Don (also a well-known keyboardist) an
1 CONC d a younger sister Dee.
1 CONT Henri Grusin was a watchmaker and jeweler by trade, but h
1 CONC e was also an accomplished violinist.
1 CONT There was always music in the Grusin home and, although Dav
1 CONC e had a very solid musical education before entering colleg
1 CONC e, he dreamed of being a veterinarian.
1 CONT In a number of interviews, he's explained that he switche
1 CONC d to music out of the guilt he felt for the immense effor
1 CONC t and expense his father had already spent on his musical e
1 CONC ducation.
1 CONT When Dave Grusin hit New York City in 1959 and enrolled i
1 CONC n the Manhattan School of Music he was planning a career a
1 CONC s a teacher.
1 CONT A chance to join the Andy Williams Show as music conducto
1 CONC r ended his plans for academia.
1 CONT It was during his stint with Andy Williams that Dave Grusi
1 CONC n met his future partner, Larry Rosen.
1 CONT During this period Grusin managed to debut as an artist rec
1 CONC ording two jazz trio albums, 'Subways Are For Sleeping' an
1 CONC d 'Piano, Strings and Moonlight.'
1 CONT Grusin left Andy Williams in 1964 and looked to his new asp
1 CONC iration.
1 CONT He wanted to write original music scores for motion picture
1 CONC s.
1 CONT His first film assignment was 'Divorce American Style' an
1 CONC d from there the work in motion pictures and for televisio
1 CONC n snowballed.
1 CONT Dave Grusin's career had taken off.
1 CONT While developing his career as a film score composer, Dav
1 CONC e Grusin and Larry Rosen began producing contemporary jaz
1 CONC z records for various companies.
1 CONT The two discovered and produced such artists as ŮCuŮDEarl Klu
1 CONC gh <Earl%20Klugh.html>ŮC/uŮD, ŮCuŮDAngela Bofill <Angela%20Bofi
1 CONC ll.html>ŮC/uŮD, Tom Browne, ŮCuŮDPatti Austin <Patti%20Austin.h
1 CONC tml>ŮC/uŮD, Lee Ritenour and others.
1 CONT Through Grusin-Rosen Productions they were helping to redef
1 CONC ine sophisticated contemporary jazz for a whole new generat
1 CONC ion. Their production company soon led to the creation of t
1 CONC heir own record company, GRP Records.
1 CONT In Hollywood, Dave Crusin was composing important film scor
1 CONC es including 'The Graduate', 'The Heart Is A Lonely Hunter'
1 CONC , 'Three Days Of The Condor' , winning Academy Award nomina
1 CONC tions for 'Heaven Can Wait', (1979) 'The Champ', (1980) 'O
1 CONC n Golden Pond', (1982) and 'Tootsie' (1983).
1 CONT The small screen was also a part of Grusin's compositiona
1 CONC l efforts. His credits included 'Baretta', 'Roots (Part 11)
1 CONC ', 'Good Times', 'The Name Of The Game', 'Dan August', 'I
1 CONC t Takes A Thief' and his most popular piece, the theme fro
1 CONC m 'St. Elsewhere' , the long-running NBC Network Televisio
1 CONC n series.
1 CONT Dave Grusin also remained a dedicated pianist, performing o
1 CONC n tour and with such albums as 'Mountain Dance,' 'Out Of Th
1 CONC e Shadows,' and 'Harlequin' with Lee Ritenour.
1 CONT It was 'Mountain Dance', produced in 1979 as a full digita
1 CONC l recording that convinced Dave Grusin and Larry Rosen tha
1 CONC t this was indeed the new sound recording technology of th
1 CONC e future.
1 CONT This digital philosophy became the cornerstone of their ne
1 CONC w record company.
1 CONT 'Mountain Dance' featured the old Ronnie Laws / David Olive
1 CONC r tune 'Friends And Strangers', featured on deejay Robbie V
1 CONC incents Radio London Shows of the late seventies / early ei
1 CONC ghties.
1 CONT Meanwhile, back in Hollywood, Dave Grusin captured his firs
1 CONC t Oscar for 'The Milagro Beanfield War' (1988) and won nomi
1 CONC nations again for 'The Fabulous Baker Boys' (1989) and 'Hav
1 CONC ana' (1990).
1 CONT Besides his numerous GRP releases, Grusin's also recorded f
1 CONC or Columbia, Sheffield Lab and Polygram.

1 CONC Lois R. Adler
1 CONT Lois Richman Adler, a resident of The Atriums in Overland P
1 CONC ark, died Saturday, November 25, 2006, at St. Luke's Plaza
1 CONC . Funeral services will be 3:00 pm Tuesday, November 28, a
1 CONC t The Temple, Congregation B'nai Jehudah, 12320 Nall Avenue
1 CONC , Overland Park, KS. Burial at Rose Hill Cemetery. Memoria
1 CONC l contributions in lieu of flowers are suggested to be mad
1 CONC e to the Frank J. Adler Leadership Endowment Fund of B'na
1 CONC i Jehudah or to the Arthritis Foundation of Greater Kansa
1 CONC s City, 1900 W. 75th St., Prairie Village, KS 66208. Mrs. A
1 CONC dler was born in Chicago in 1926, the daughter of Herman I
1 CONC . and Anna Handmaker Richman, and resided there until movin
1 CONC g to Kansas City in 1953. She received a Bachelor of Educat
1 CONC ion degree from the National College of Education, Evanston
1 CONC , IL, in 1948. After substitute teaching in Chicago publi
1 CONC c schools, she served as remedial reading teacher at the Ca
1 CONC tholic Youth Organization of Chicago, 1949-52. From 1963 t
1 CONC o 1967 she was the head teacher at the Kansas City Orthopti
1 CONC c Reading Clinic and did private tutoring. From 1967 to 198
1 CONC 4 she taught at Sunset Hill School. Mrs. Adler belonged t
1 CONC o Temple B'nai Jehudah since 1953. She was a member of th
1 CONC e Temple's Sisterhood and an Honorary Life Member of its bo
1 CONC ard. She was a Life Member of the National Council of Jewis
1 CONC h Women and a charter member of the U.S. Holocaust Memoria
1 CONC l Council. She also belonged to the Brandeis University Nat
1 CONC ional Women's Committee, the Midwest Center for Holocaust E
1 CONC ducation, and the Mainstream Coalition. Mrs. Adler is survi
1 CONC ved by these, her loving and devoted family: Frank J. Adler
1 CONC , her husband of 56 years; her sons, Daniel I. Adler of Lea
1 CONC wood and Dr. Paul D. Adler and daughter-in-law, Dr. Heidi M
1 CONC . Miller of East Longmeadow, MA; her grandchildren, Michell
1 CONC e Adler McLain and husband Bradley of Louisburg, KS; Davi
1 CONC d S. Adler of Prairie Village, and Jonathan A.M. and Kather
1 CONC ine L.M. Adler of East Longmeadow; greatgranddaughter, Mari
1 CONC ssa McLain; her brothers, Roland Richman and sister-inlaw
1 CONC , Miriam of Palm Springs, CA, and Donald I. Richman of Chic
1 CONC ago; her dear nieces and nephews and their offspring, and h
1 CONC er dear friend, Sue Fink. Online guest book @ www.louismemo
1 CONC rialchapel.com. (Arr: The Louis Memorial Chapel 816-361-521
1 CONC 1). Published in the Kansas City Star from 11/27/2006 - 11/
1 CONC 28/2006

1 CONC ŮCbŮDFrom New Orleans Times-Picayune 13 May 2008
1 CONT Dr. Martin Litwin, 78, a trainer of surgeons
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDTuesday, May 13, 2008
1 CONT By John PopeŮCbŮDStaff writerŮC/bŮD
1 CONT
1 CONT Dr. Martin Litwin, a Tulane University surgeon who was th
1 CONC e first medical director of its faculty-practice program, d
1 CONC ied Sunday of cancer at his New Orleans home. He was 78.  D
1 CONC r. Litwin was born in Florence, Ala., and had lived in Ne
1 CONC w Orleans since 1966. he trained several generations of sur
1 CONC geons.  "He wanted to encourage other people to view a care
1 CONC er in medicine as more of a calling than a job where you ge
1 CONC t a paycheck," said Dr. Douglas Slakey, chief of surgery a
1 CONC t Tulane University Health Sciences Center.  "He was one o
1 CONC f those rare surgeons who really felt like his life had mea
1 CONC ning by virtue of what he did for others," Slakey said. "H
1 CONC e really had this innate feeling that he wanted to give bac
1 CONC k to other people stimulation to have the same sort of enjo
1 CONC yment in a career that he had."  One of Dr. Litwin's protâeg
1 CONC âes was Dr. William L. Browder, chairman of the surgery depa
1 CONC rtment at Eastern Tennessee State College of Medicine in Jo
1 CONC hnson City.  "He was very loyal to the doctors, always repr
1 CONC esented them to the fullest when dealing with the hospital,
1 CONC " Browder said. "We always felt like he was one of us.
1 CONC "  A decade after Dr. Litwin arrived in New Orleans, Tulan
1 CONC e opened a $35 million hospital -- and needed money. He wa
1 CONC s part of the group that worked with Dr. John Walsh, the me
1 CONC dical center's chancellor, to develop the faculty-practic
1 CONC e program, which lets doctors see their private patients a
1 CONC t Tulane in return for a percentage of fees they receive
1 CONC .  The program succeeded. Dr. Litwin, who had a private pra
1 CONC ctice in general surgery, was its first medical director, a
1 CONC nd he held that post from 1976 to 2001.  "That took a lot o
1 CONC f leadership and trust on the part of the doctors, which h
1 CONC e had," Browder said.  Dr. Litwin earned undergraduate an
1 CONC d medical degrees at the University of Alabama. He later ea
1 CONC rned a master's degree in medical management at Tulane.  Af
1 CONC ter an internship at Michael Reese Hospital in Chicago, Dr
1 CONC . Litwin was a surgery resident -- and, eventually, chief r
1 CONC esident in that field -- at Peter Bent Brigham Hospital i
1 CONC n Boston. Dr. Litwin also trained for a year at St. Mary'
1 CONC s Hospital in London.  During the Vietnam War, he was draft
1 CONC ed and assigned to the surgeon general's office in Washingt
1 CONC on. While there, he saw people returning from Vietnam wit
1 CONC h napalm burns. As a result of his research on that topic
1 CONC , he developed a protocol for treating burns that has becom
1 CONC e the standard of care, said his wife, Nancy Kittay Litwin
1 CONC .  Dr. Litwin wrote "The Atlas of Advanced Surgery," as wel
1 CONC l as medical journal articles on such topics as trauma surg
1 CONC ery, skin cancer research and burn care.  In 1977, two frie
1 CONC nds and patients, Robert and Viola Lobrano, established a s
1 CONC urgery professorship for him.  Throughout his years at Tula
1 CONC ne, Dr. Litwin's devotion to the institution, his specialty
1 CONC , residents and students was impressive, Slakey said.  "I d
1 CONC on't think I've ever met anyone who was so devoted to the i
1 CONC nstitution where he worked," he said.  After he became chie
1 CONC f of surgery in 2006, Slakey increased Dr. Litwin's duties
1 CONC .  "You could see the light in his eyes when I gave him mor
1 CONC e of a role than he had had," Slakey said. "That rejuvenate
1 CONC d him."  In addition to his wife, survivors include two son
1 CONC s, Benjamin Bernard Litwin of Lafayette and Martin Mason Li
1 CONC twin of New Orleans; two daughters, Anna-Marie Litwin Jen
1 CONC e of San Francisco and Dr. Rebecca Litwin Newman of Glenvie
1 CONC w, Ill.; a brother, Dr. S. Bert Litwin of Boca Raton, Fla.
1 CONC , and Milwaukee; and two grandchildren.  A funeral will b
1 CONC e held Friday at noon at Temple Sinai, 6227 St. Charles Ave
1 CONC . Visitation will begin at 11 a.m. Burial will be private
1 CONC . Tharp-Sontheimer-Tharp Funeral Home is in charge of arran
1 CONC gements.
1 CONT

1 CONT Eileen specializes in a range of management disciplines inc
1 CONC luding corporate strategy, organizational development, an
1 CONC d strategic marketing. During her professional tenure she h
1 CONC as provided counsel to clients varying from technology star
1 CONC t-ups to blue chip pharmaceuticals, and has managed project
1 CONC s in both the domestic and international markets.
1 CONT
1 CONT Prior to starting Markstein Consulting, Eileen worked a
1 CONC s a business communications consultant in Washington, DC fo
1 CONC r leading companies including Burson-Marsteller, a top fiv
1 CONC e global perception management firm, and The ProMarc Agency
1 CONC , where she left her position as Associate Vice President t
1 CONC o pursue a graduate degree.
1 CONT
1 CONT Eileen earned her Masters in Business Administration wit
1 CONC h a focus in International Business from The George Washing

1 CONT Benjamin V. CohenŮC/bŮD (ŮCuŮDSeptember 23 ŮC/uŮD, ŮCuŮD1894 ŮC/uŮD (
1 CONC ŮCuŮDMuncie, Indiana ŮC/uŮD) - ŮCuŮDAugust 15 ŮC/uŮD, ŮCuŮD1983 ŮC/u
1 CONC ŮD (ŮCuŮDWashington, D.C. ŮC/uŮD)), who had a public service car
1 CONC eer that spanned from the early ŮCuŮDNew Deal ŮC/uŮD through an

1 CONT From TIME Magazine...Monday, September 12,1938

1 CONT ŮC/uŮDJewish-Irish- Muncie, Ind. is "Middletown," U. S. A. Be
1 CONC njamin Victor Cohen was born there in 1894, son of a scra
1 CONC p iron merchant. He broke all scholastic records at Univers
1 CONC ity of Chicago Law School (1915), took a postgraduate yea
1 CONC r at Harvard Law School and became secretary to U. S. Circu
1 CONC it Court Judge Julian Mack (receiverships). The War and th
1 CONC e Jews' plight brought Cohen into contact with Louis Dembit
1 CONC z Brandeis. He is still a director of Palestine Economic Co
1 CONC rp., wherein he first tasted planned economy. In the reckle
1 CONC ss 1920s he was not above playing the stockmarket. A killin
1 CONC g Chrysler stock (he was so excited about it at the time th
1 CONC at he used gleefully to point to every Chrysler he saw on t
1 CONC he street) made him temporarily rich. He kept enough pelf f
1 CONC or comfort, is not "socialistic because of the Crash." Revi
1 CONC siting Harvard in 1924, Ben Cohen walked into his old room
1 CONC . The current occupant was out. His name was Thomas Gardine
1 CONC r Corcoran. They did not meet until nine years later, whe
1 CONC n T. G. Corcoran had been for a year a cog in the legal sta
1 CONC ff of President Hoover's RFC. Ben Cohen had signed on to he
1 CONC lp James Landis draft the Securities Exchange Act. Thrown t
1 CONC ogether on this job, Corcoran & Cohen have been inseparabl
1 CONC e since.

1 CONT Reviews of William Lassiter book, "Benjamin Cohen, Architec
1 CONC t of the New Deal"
1 CONT ŮCbŮDFrom Library JournalŮC/bŮD
1 CONT Hardly a household name, Benjamin Victor Cohen (1894-1983
1 CONC ) was first an associate of Justice Louis Brandeis and the
1 CONC n one of Felix Frankfurter's "Happy Hotdogs" who played a m
1 CONC ajor role in FDR's New Deal. This is the first biography o
1 CONC f Cohen's life and career, and it is ably researched and wr
1 CONC itten by Lasser (political science, Clemson Univ.). Althoug
1 CONC h Cohen kept no diary, wrote no articles, made no speeches
1 CONC , saved few letters, and gave interviews only reluctantly
1 CONC , Lasser has rescued Cohen from becoming a "passionate anon
1 CONC ym" or a mere presidential assistant. During the entire Roo
1 CONC sevelt and Truman presidencies, Cohen, a chief architect o
1 CONC f the modern regulatory state, was also a model for futur
1 CONC e presidential assistants. He played a major role in the fi
1 CONC nancial legislation of the New Deal the Securities Act of 1
1 CONC 933, the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, and the Public Ut
1 CONC ility Holding Company Act of 1935. And he played a major ro
1 CONC le in the development of the Lend-Lease Act. Despite thes
1 CONC e New Deal accomplishments, Lasser makes clear that Cohen w
1 CONC as not a "radical liberal" crusader. Highly recommended fo
1 CONC r academic collections on the presidency and the New Deal
1 CONC . William D. Pederson, Louisiana State Univ., Shreveport
1 CONT Copyright 2002 Cahners Business Information, Inc.
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCbŮDBook DescriptionŮC/bŮD
1 CONT A key figure in the administrations of Franklin D. Roosevel
1 CONC t and Harry S. Truman, Benjamin V. Cohen (1894-1983) wa
1 CONC s a major architect of public policy from the first days o
1 CONC f FDR's presidency through the early days of the Cold War
1 CONC . Although he kept a low public profile, Cohen's influenc
1 CONC e extended across a wide range of domestic and foreign poli
1 CONC cy initiatives. In this biography, William Lasser offers th
1 CONC e first account of Ben Cohen's life and career, and an asse
1 CONC ssment of his contribution to the origin and development o
1 CONC f modern American liberalism. Cohen's life provides an extr
1 CONC aordinary lens through which to view the development of th
1 CONC e evolving political philosophy of the Roosevelt and Truma
1 CONC n presidencies. A brilliant lawyer noted for his good judgm
1 CONC ent and experience, Cohen was a leading member of FDR's "Br
1 CONC ain Trust," developing ideas, drafting legislation, lobbyin
1 CONC g within the administration and in Congress, and defendin
1 CONC g the New Deal in court. The book traces his contribution
1 CONC s to domestic financial policy, his activities during the w
1 CONC ar years in London and Washington, his service as counselo
1 CONC r to the State Department and member of the American delega
1 CONC tion to the United Nations after the war, and his role in t
1 CONC he American Zionist movement. From Cohen's life and work, L
1 CONC asser draws important insights into the development of th
1 CONC e New Deal and the evolution of postwar liberalism.

1 CONC Lessing was named after Lessing Rosenthal, his father's bes
1 CONC t friend, a Chicago attorney and a community activist. A
1 CONC s a youngster Rosenwald attended the University of Chicago'
1 CONC s elementary and secondary schools, then studied chemistr
1 CONC y at Cornell University (1909-1911).
1 CONT
1 CONT In September 1911, at age twenty, he began his career at Se
1 CONC ars as a shipping clerk. His father believed that his son s
1 CONC hould not be given any preferential job opportunities withi
1 CONC n the company. On Nov. 6, 1913, Lessing married Edith Goodk
1 CONC ind; they had five children.
1 CONT
1 CONT Rosenwald spent his entire working career at Sears. By doin
1 CONC g many different jobs, he gained a comprehensive view of th
1 CONC e company's operations. After serving in the United State
1 CONC s Navy during World War I, he returned to Sears in 1920, a
1 CONC s general manager of its Philadelphia plant. In 1930, afte
1 CONC r several promotions, he was named executive vice-president
1 CONC . Rosenwald also held several administrative posts, such a
1 CONC s vice-chairman of the board (January 1931-June 1932), an
1 CONC d chairman of the executive committee. When his father die
1 CONC d in 1932, he became chairman of the board. While chairma
1 CONC n of Sears, Rosenwald also was an industrial representativ
1 CONC e on the Regional Labor Board (1934). In January 1939, afte
1 CONC r seven years at the helm of Sears, he resigned (at age for
1 CONC ty-eight) in order to devote his life to philanthropy, coll
1 CONC ecting rare books and prints, and community activities.
1 CONT
1 CONT Although retired from corporate America, Rosenwald remaine
1 CONC d active in local and national affairs. For example, in 193
1 CONC 9 he served as a member of the Philadelphia Labor Mediatio
1 CONC n Tribunal and arbitrated labor disputes. In the early 1940
1 CONC 's he held a number of local and federal government posts
1 CONC : member of the local board of appeals of the Selective Ser
1 CONC vice System (October 1940), head of the Office of Productio
1 CONC n and Management's silk commodity section in Washington, D.
1 CONC C. (August 1941), chief of the Bureau of Industrial Conserv
1 CONC ation (September 1941), and its director when the bureau be
1 CONC came a division. In January 1943, after several major conse
1 CONC rvation efforts and accomplishments, he resigned when a num
1 CONC ber of federal agencies were reorganized to form the War Pr
1 CONC oduction Board.
1 CONT
1 CONT Upon his return to Philadelphia, Rosenwald devoted his atte
1 CONC ntion to collecting, philanthropy, and civic activities. I
1 CONC n early 1943, as president of the American Council for Juda
1 CONC ism, he opposed the establishment of a Jewish state in Pale
1 CONC stine. Rosenwald believed that such a state would not solv
1 CONC e the problems of Jews but, rather, would endanger Jews thr
1 CONC oughout the world and would create additional tension in th
1 CONC e Middle East. Instead, he sought to increase migration o
1 CONC f Jews to the United States and to other countries, in orde
1 CONC r to disseminate Jewish intellectuals throughout the world.
1 CONT
1 CONT Also in 1943, Rosenwald began donating valuable art and rar
1 CONC e books to the United States government. His largess includ
1 CONC ed gifts to the National Gallery of Art--some 6,500 print
1 CONC s and drawings--and to the Library of Congress--rare book
1 CONC s and reference materials. During the 1950's, Rosenwald con
1 CONC tinued his lifelong passion of collecting rare prints and b
1 CONC ooks. He amassed the largest collection of any twentieth ce
1 CONC ntury living collector and purchased many major pieces of a
1 CONC rt and rare books as he traveled throughout the world. Fo
1 CONC r example, while vacationing in Buenos Aires, Argentina, h
1 CONC e had bought a collection of German Expressionist prints an
1 CONC d drawings. His philosophy as a collector was to make purch
1 CONC ases for the love of art rather than for investment.
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1960, Rosenwald donated approximately 130 acres of his 1
1 CONC 59-acre estate (built in 1939) to Abington Township, Pa. H
1 CONC e retained about thirty acres for his personal use by conve
1 CONC rting an old greenhouse into a home where he resided with h
1 CONC is wife and maintained his collection. Over the next few ye
1 CONC ars, the township developed the land and dedicated it as Al
1 CONC verthorpe Park.
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1961, Rosenwald served as a honorary consultant on rar
1 CONC e books to the Library of Congress, to which he contribute
1 CONC d more than 700 rare volumes. In addition to his gifts, an
1 CONC d other philanthropic and community activities, Rosenwald s
1 CONC pent much of his time in the 1960's and the 1970's with hi
1 CONC s collection of rare books and prints, served on a number o
1 CONC f boards, and met with scholars and other collectors to dis
1 CONC cuss his collection. During his lifetime, he donated some 2
1 CONC 5,000 prints and drawings and nearly 8,000 rare books wort
1 CONC h $35 million to the United States government. Rosenwald, o
1 CONC ne of the greatest collectors of art in modern times, die
1 CONC d at his estate, Alverthorpe, in Jenkintown, Pa.

1 CONC Julius Rosenwald was born August 12, 1862, in Springfield
1 CONC , Ill. His father, Samuel, had traveled to Springfield seve
1 CONC ral years earlier to run a clothing store for his wife's fa
1 CONC mily.
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1885, Rosenwald teamed up with a cousin, Julius Weil, t
1 CONC o manufacture men's clothing. Coincidentally, one of the co
1 CONC mpany's clients was Richard Sears, who ordered men's clothi
1 CONC ng for sale in his catalogs.
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1890, Julius married Augusta Nusbaum, with whom he had f
1 CONC ive children: Lessing, Adele, Edith, Marion and William. Le
1 CONC ssing served as chairman of Sears upon his father's death i
1 CONC n 1932 until 1939.
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1895, Rosenwald became a partner in Sears, Roebuck and C
1 CONC o. when Richard Sears offered Rosenwald's brother-in-law, A
1 CONC aron Nusbaum, an interest in the company. Nusbaum recruite
1 CONC d Rosenwald to join the venture. In 1896, he became a vic
1 CONC e president of Sears, Roebuck and Co.
1 CONT
1 CONT From the moment he joined Sears, Roebuck and Co., Rosenwald
1 CONC 's abilities meshed amazingly well with those of Richard Se
1 CONC ars. He brought a rational management philosophy to Richar
1 CONC d Sears' well-tuned sales instincts. From 1895 to 1907, ann
1 CONC ual sales skyrocketed from $750,000 to $50 million. In 1908
1 CONC , Rosenwald was named president when Richard Sears resigned
1 CONC . Rosenwald continued to serve as president until 1924, whe
1 CONC n he became chairman of the board, a position he held unti
1 CONC l his death in 1932.
1 CONT
1 CONT After World War I, Sears was in dire financial shape and Ro
1 CONC senwald brought Sears back from the brink of bankruptcy b
1 CONC y pledging some $21 million of his personal fortune, in cas
1 CONC h, stock and other assets to rescue the company. By 1922, S
1 CONC ears had regained financial stability.
1 CONT
1 CONT Rosenwald insisted that the company's primary goal must b
1 CONC e responsibility to the customer. He established the "satis
1 CONC faction guaranteed or your money back" pledge and conducte
1 CONC d his business dealings by the creed "Sell honest merchandi
1 CONC se for less money and more people will buy." Under Rosenwal
1 CONC d's direction, the business positioned itself as a direct e
1 CONC xtension of the farmer's eyes, ears and wallet, making purc
1 CONC hasing decisions in the best interests of the farmer.
1 CONT
1 CONT After Rosenwald stepped down as Sears president in 1924, h
1 CONC e devoted most of his time to philanthropy. Over the cours
1 CONC e of his life, he donated millions of dollars to public sch
1 CONC ools, colleges and universities, museums, Jewish charitie
1 CONC s and black institutions.
1 CONT
1 CONT Of all his philanthropic efforts, Rosenwald was most famou
1 CONC s for the more than 5,000 "Rosenwald schools" he establishe
1 CONC d throughout the South for poor, rural black youth, and th
1 CONC e 4,000 libraries he added to existing schools. The networ
1 CONC k of new public schools subsequently employed more than 14,
1 CONC 000 teachers. In 1927, Rosenwald received a special gold me
1 CONC dal from the William E. Harmon Awards for Distinguished Ach
1 CONC ievement in Race Relations for his contributions to the edu
1 CONC cation of black youth.
1 CONT
1 CONT Rosenwald embodied the principles of modern, responsible ma
1 CONC nagement and corporate citizenship. He provided the means a
1 CONC nd the model for Sears to grow to ever-greater heights a
1 CONC s a company. More important, Rosenwald's life served as a b
1 CONC lueprint for doing everything in one's power to raise the f
1 CONC ortunes of those who otherwise could not do so.

1 CONC Herbert H. Lehman (1878-1963) was born on March 28, 1878, t
1 CONC he son of Meyer Lehman, one of the original partners of Leh
1 CONC man Brothers and a founder of the Cotton Exchange. He was e
1 CONC ducated at Dr. Sach's Collegiate Institute, New York, and a
1 CONC t Williams College, where in 1899 he received a A.B. In tha
1 CONC t same year he began work as a cotton-goods salesman at $5.
1 CONC 00 per week with the firm of J. Spencer Turner, of which h
1 CONC e later became treasurer and vice-president.
1 CONT After serving as a partner in Lehman Brothers and rising t
1 CONC o the post of Colonel in the U.S. Army in World War I, he e
1 CONC ntered politics in 1926, serving as Chair of the campaign t
1 CONC hat elected Alfred E. Smith Governor. Two years later he ac
1 CONC cepted the post of Chair of the Finance Committee of the De
1 CONC mocratic National Committee in the Smith Presidential Campa
1 CONC ign. In the same election he became Lieutenant-Governor o
1 CONC f New York, serving under his old friend, ŮCuŮDFranklin D. Ro
1 CONC osevelt <http://www.fdrlibrary.marist.edu/>ŮC/uŮD, who had be
1 CONC en elected Governor. Lehman stayed in Albany for the next 1
1 CONC 4 years. In 1932 he was elected Governor. In 1934, runnin
1 CONC g for re-election, he amassed a plurality of 800,000, the h
1 CONC ighest ever recorded up to that time in New York State hist
1 CONC ory.
1 CONT Three months before the expiration of his forth term as Gov
1 CONC ernor, Mr. Lehman was called to Washington by President Roo
1 CONC sevelt to become Director of Foreign Relief and Rehabilitat
1 CONC ion Operations, the organization which in 1943 was supplant
1 CONC ed by UNRRA, of which he was named the first Director Gener
1 CONC al. He held this post until March, 1946, when he resigned.
1 CONT In 1908 he entered Lehman Brothers as a partner and partici
1 CONC pated for the next two decades in the floating of more tha
1 CONC n five billion dollars worth of industrial securities as we
1 CONC ll as in the management of the Lehman corporation. He resig
1 CONC ned from both firms upon becoming Lieutenant-Governor in 19
1 CONC 29.
1 CONT When Mr. Lehman became Governor in 1933 he was confronted w
1 CONC ith a deficit of $100,000,000.
1 CONT When he retired from the Governorship in 1942 after havin
1 CONC g been four times elected by the people he served, he had n
1 CONC ot only wiped out that huge depression deficit, but left hi
1 CONC s successor with an $80,000,000 surplus.
1 CONT This remarkable financial success was achieved without th
1 CONC e sacrifice of any of the major aims to which Governor ha
1 CONC s always directed his efforts in public office. In fact, hi
1 CONC s tenure of office was one of the most progressive eras th
1 CONC e State of New York has ever experienced.
1 CONT The labor program that was developed during the Lehman admi
1 CONC nistration was one of the pioneering efforts in the field a
1 CONC nd has been used as a model for both Federal and State legi
1 CONC slation. It included a revitalized Workman's Compensation L
1 CONC aw, the creation of State Labor Relations and Mediation boa
1 CONC rds, unemployment insurance and savings bank life insurance.
1 CONT A great social security program was initiated and carried o
1 CONC ut during Governor Lehman's term of office that included as
1 CONC sistance to the blind, aid to dependent children, materna
1 CONC l and child welfare assistance, and aid to crippled childre
1 CONC n and to public health projects.
1 CONT Under Governor Lehman, New York embarked on the greatest pu
1 CONC blic housing project ever attempted by a State.
1 CONT Widely beneficial legislation for the regulation of publi
1 CONC c utilities was enacted in New York under his leadership.
1 CONT The greatest park system in the world was built and maintai
1 CONC ned during his administration.
1 CONT He also gave New York a vast and efficient network of impro
1 CONC ved highways, a modern and progressive prison system, gover
1 CONC nment departments that were universally recognized as the b
1 CONC est and most honestly administered in the land, and a syste
1 CONC m of unemployment insurance that was kept totally free of c
1 CONC orruption and partisan politics.
1 CONT When he became Director General of UNRRA in 1943, Mr. Lehma
1 CONC n had to build an organization from scratch. From the star
1 CONC t he was faced with the task of establishing, and then of o
1 CONC perating, the longest supply line in the world. The lives
1 CONC , health, hopes and futures of some 500,000,000 human being
1 CONC s depended on UNRRA.
1 CONT Not until after the peak of re-deployment had been passed w
1 CONC as he able to do anything more than beg for shipping space
1 CONC , and at the same time he was forced to contend with worl
1 CONC d wide shortages in food and other supplies. In spite of th
1 CONC ese handicaps, in 16 months (December, 1944, to April, 1946
1 CONC ) UNRRA had shipped 18,000,000 tons of food, clothing, medi
1 CONC cal supplies, farm equipment and essential lumber to virtua
1 CONC lly every country in Europe and Asia ravaged by war.
1 CONT With the help of public spirited citizenry he organized th
1 CONC e spectacular United National Clothing Collection which sen
1 CONC t 150,000,000 pounds of clothing, shoes and bedding to wa
1 CONC r stricken countries.
1 CONT Mr. Lehman brought to his work the rare combination of qual
1 CONC ities that made him one of the greatest governors any Stat
1 CONC e ever had. He solved the vast problems of business organiz
1 CONC ation, statesmanship, public administration, diplomacy an
1 CONC d sheer personal leadership which the job called for. He tr
1 CONC aveled halfway around the world making eight Atlantic cross
1 CONC ings, visiting devastated areas, many directly behind the a
1 CONC ctive fighting fronts, to be certain his solutions were rig
1 CONC ht. Above all, he recognized in UNRRA his country's first o
1 CONC pportunity to lead other nations to the understanding tha
1 CONC t peoples and nations could work together in peace as wel
1 CONC l as in war.
1 CONT When Mr. Lehman resigned in March of 1946 the major problem
1 CONC s had been solved and UNRRA was performing for the war tor
1 CONC n world a job without precedent.
1 CONT At the outbreak of World War I, Mr. Lehman applied for admi
1 CONC ssion to the first Officers Training Camp although he was a
1 CONC t that time about 10 years over the draft age. His assignme
1 CONC nt was held in abeyance, and he subsequently volunteers fo
1 CONC r work in the Navy Department under Franklin Roosevlt, the
1 CONC n Assistant Secretary of the Navy. He was commissioned an A
1 CONC rmy Captain in August 1917 and rose to the rank of Colone
1 CONC l attached to the General Staff., where he was directly con
1 CONC cerned with the shipping of supplies to the A.E.F.
1 CONT Immediately after the Armistice, he was appointed a specia
1 CONC l assistant to the Secretary of War, a member of the Boar
1 CONC d of Contract Adjustments, and a member of the War Departme
1 CONC nt Claims Board. For his services on these bodies he was la
1 CONC ter awarded the Distinguished Service Medal.
1 CONT The friendship between Lehman and Franklin D. Roosevelt, wh
1 CONC ich began during this period, was later to become as well k
1 CONC nown as it was warm. It was during the time when Roosevel
1 CONC t was Governor and Lehman his Lieutenant-Governor that th
1 CONC e former characterized him as "my good right arm." Roosevel
1 CONC t was later to give many special jobs to his "right arm."
1 CONT In 1932 and 1933 he called on him for active assistance i
1 CONC n the bank holiday. Just prior to the formation of UNRRA, t
1 CONC he President had appointed Lehman as his special advisor o
1 CONC n relief problems, and he was accustomed to draw deeply o
1 CONC n Lehman's knowledge both of banking and relief work.
1 CONT Mr. Lehman married Edith Altschul of California on April 28
1 CONC , 1910. They had three children, Hilda, Peter, and John. Pe
1 CONC ter was killed while on active duty in the American Air For
1 CONC ce during World War II. John became a lieutenant colonel i
1 CONC n the Army and served in the European Theatre during the wa
1 CONC r. Hilda was a corporal in the WAACs.
1 CONT Both he and Mrs. Lehman were well known for their intensiv
1 CONC e interest in charities. On his graduation from Williams Co
1 CONC llege, Mr. Lehman immediately volunteered as an instructo
1 CONC r in the Henry Street Settlement in New York City and it wa
1 CONC s there that he first met his good friend Alfred E. Smith
1 CONC . Later he became one of the founders of the Joint Distribu
1 CONC tion Committee, the foremost agency for the relief of Jewis
1 CONC h war victims and refugees in Europe.
1 CONT
1 CONT Herbert Henry Lehman served in World War I, volunteering a
1 CONC s a textile procurement specialist with the Navy Department
1 CONC , where he developed a close friendship with Franklin Delan
1 CONC o Roosevelt. In September, 1917, he was commissioned a capt
1 CONC ain in the Army and left the service two years later with t
1 CONC he rank of full colonel.
1 CONT Lehman was born on March 28, 1878, in New York City, the so
1 CONC n of Barbara and Mayer Lehman, who were German ŮCuŮDReform </
1 CONC jsource/Judaism/ref.html>ŮC/uŮD Jews. He attended Sachs Colle
1 CONC giate Institute in New York City and graduated from William

1 CONC rom college, he was employed by the J. Spencer Turner Co. t
1 CONC extile manufacturers. Iin 1908, he joined Lehman Brothers
1 CONC , an investment banking house that had been transformed fro
1 CONC m a cotton brokerage firm by his brother, Arthur. In 1910
1 CONC , Herbert Lehman married Edith Louise Altschul, the daughte
1 CONC r of the head of the New York branch of Lazard Freres, a re
1 CONC spected Paris-based banking house.
1 CONT Lehman's oldest son, Peter, was killed in World War II afte
1 CONC r flying 57 combat missions in Europe. Prior to serving i
1 CONC n the U.S. Air Force, he was on active duty with the Canadi
1 CONC an Air Force because the United States was not in the war a
1 CONC nd he felt very strongly about defeating ŮCuŮDHitler </jsourc
1 CONC e/Holocaust/hitlertoc.html>ŮC/uŮD. Peter Lehman received th

1 CONC uished Flying Cross was awarded to him posthumously.
1 CONT Lehman's daughter and other son also served overseas in th
1 CONC e armed forces. His son, Captain John Lehman, served with t
1 CONC he 20th Armored Force, and his daughter, Hilda Lehman de Va
1 CONC detzky, served with the Women's Army Corps in Algiers.
1 CONT Herbert Lehman became active in politics in 1920 and suppor
1 CONC ted Alfred E. Smith. When Smith won the Democratic Party no
1 CONC mination for president in 1928, Lehman became chairman of t
1 CONC he finance committee of the Democratic National Committee
1 CONC . It was in that year that he was elected lieutenant govern
1 CONC or of New York on a ticket headed by Franklin Delano Roosev
1 CONC elt.
1 CONT Lehman became Governor of New York in 1932, the year that R
1 CONC oosevelt became a candidate for president. Lehman served fo
1 CONC ur terms.
1 CONT In 1940. his brother, Irving, was elected chief judge of th
1 CONC e New York Court of Appeals, the first time in the histor
1 CONC y of New York that brothers headed the executive and judici
1 CONC al branches of government.
1 CONT Lehman became the Director of Foreign Relief and Rehabilita
1 CONC tion Operations in the State Department, Washington, D.C. i
1 CONC n 1943. He later became Director General of the United Nati
1 CONC ons Relief and Rehabilitation Administration and served fro
1 CONC m 1943 until 1946.
1 CONT Herbert Lehman ran twice for the U.S. Senate, and winning t
1 CONC he second time against, In 1950, he was elected to a full t
1 CONC erm in the Senate.
1 CONT In 1946, he was an unsuccessful candidate for election to t
1 CONC he United States Senate, losing to Irving M. Ives. Two year
1 CONC s later, he became a member of Public Advisory Board of th
1 CONC e Economic Cooperation Administration. In 1949, Lehman ra
1 CONC n a successful campagn for the Senate, this time in a speci
1 CONC al election against John Foster Dulles to fill the vacanc
1 CONC y caused by the resignation of Robert F. Wagner. Lehman ser
1 CONC ved from November 9, 1949, until January 3, 1957.
1 CONT Lehman was one of the earliest politicians to refute Senato
1 CONC r Joseph R. McCarthy and his witch-hunts. For his service t
1 CONC o the nation, he was awarded the Presidential Medal of Free
1 CONC dom.

1 CONC 63.

1 CONC e son of Julius Mortimer Alexander and Rebecca Ella Solomon
1 CONC s Alexander. His grandfather, Aaron Alexander, was the firs
1 CONC t Jew of American birth to settle in Atlanta.
1 CONT Henry Alexander attended the University of Georgia and rece
1 CONC ived his law degree from the University of Virginia in 1895
1 CONC . He was a member of the Georgia General Assembly from 1909
1 CONC -1910. In 1914, he was asked to help in the appeals proces
1 CONC s for Leo M. Frank. Throughout the years, Henry remained ac
1 CONC tive in both general and Jewish community activities and wa
1 CONC s a founder of the Atlanta Historical Society, a member o
1 CONC f the Disabled Emergency Officers of World War I, presiden
1 CONC t of the United Hebrew School, treasurer of the Jewish Welf
1 CONC are Fund and a member of the advisory board of the Union o
1 CONC f Sephardic Congregation in New York.
