1 CONT Cecil Alexander (b. 1918)ŮC/bŮD Cecil Alexander is an Atlant
1 CONC a architect and civic leader. As a partner in the prominen
1 CONC t architectural firm FABRAP, he was responsible for some o
1 CONC f the city's most notable public buildings. During the civi
1 CONC l rights movement of the 1960s and 1970s, he was a leader i
1 CONC n the movement to peacefully desegregate the city's publi
1 CONC c housing and local businesses. He also designed a new stat
1 CONC e flag that removed one of the last symbols of Georgia's ra
1 CONC cially divided past.  Early Life Cecil Abraham Alexander Jr
1 CONC . was born in Atlanta on March 14, 1918, the only son of pr
1 CONC osperous Jewish parents, Julia Moses and Cecil A. Alexander
1 CONC . The family lived in the Virginia Highland neighborhood o
1 CONC f Atlanta. His father was the owner of J. M. & J. C. Alexan
1 CONC der's Hardware in Atlanta, which is reputed to have been th
1 CONC e model for Scarlett O'Hara's store in the novel Gone Wit
1 CONC h the Wind (1936).  After graduating from high school, Alex
1 CONC ander enrolled in 1936 at the Georgia Institute of Technolo
1 CONC gy, where he spent one year before being accepted to Yale U
1 CONC niversity in New Haven, Connecticut. He earned a bachelor'
1 CONC s degree in architecture at Yale, then continued graduate s
1 CONC tudies at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Camb
1 CONC ridge. In 1941 he entered Civilian Pilots Training, a progr
1 CONC am established by U.S. president Franklin D. Roosevelt to c
1 CONC reate a backlog of pilots who would be available if the cou
1 CONC ntry became involved in World War II (1941-45). Around thi
1 CONC s time, Alexander met a couple who had fled Nazi Germany an
1 CONC d who told him firsthand of the horrors that Jews in German
1 CONC y, Poland, and Austria faced. He decided then that he wante
1 CONC d to fight the Nazis even though the United States had no
1 CONC t yet formally gone to war.  Military Service After obtaini
1 CONC ng a commercial pilot's license, AlexanderŮCiŮD ŮC/iŮDinterrupt
1 CONC ed his studies to enlist in the navy with the aim of becomi
1 CONC ng an aviator. While he was in flight training school, th
1 CONC e Japanese attacked Pearl Harbor, and the United States sub
1 CONC sequently entered World War II. As part of the top 10 perce
1 CONC nt of naval aviators, Alexander volunteered for the marine
1 CONC s and became a dive bomber pilot. Before shipping out for t
1 CONC he Pacific, he married Hermione Weil of New Orleans, Louisi
1 CONC ana. They eventually had three children.  During fifteen mo
1 CONC nths of service, Alexander flew a total of sixty missions a
1 CONC nd was awarded the Distinguished Flying Cross twice. As a d
1 CONC ecorated veteran returning to the states, he had a bright f
1 CONC uture ahead of him. In 1946 he enrolled in the graduate arc
1 CONC hitecture program at Harvard University in Cambridge, Massa
1 CONC chusetts, where he studied with Walter Gropius, the founde
1 CONC r of the Bauhaus school, which was a major influence on th
1 CONC e development of modern architecture. After earning his deg
1 CONC ree, Alexander worked in New York City as an architect. Soo
1 CONC n he and his wife returned home to Atlanta, where he bega
1 CONC n what would be a long and distinguished career in what wa
1 CONC s rapidly becoming the economic heart of the South.  Fighti
1 CONC ng for Equality As his practice thrived, he also became a
1 CONC n active participant in the civic and political life of Atl
1 CONC anta during the 1950s. He was appointed by Mayor William B
1 CONC . Hartsfield to chair the Citizen's Advisory Committee fo
1 CONC r Urban Renewal. Assembling the most powerful business, civ
1 CONC ic, and religious leaders from the black and white communit
1 CONC ies, the committee launched a program to eliminate slum hou
1 CONC sing and replace it with low-income public housing.  With H
1 CONC artsfield's retirement in 1961, businessman Ivan Allen Jr
1 CONC . defeated Lester Maddox to win the mayor's office. Progres
1 CONC sive and reform-minded, Allen tapped Alexander to head a va
1 CONC riety of programs designed to move the still-segregated cit
1 CONC y toward racial equality.  Alexander became a prominent fac
1 CONC e of the campaign against segregation, appearing frequentl
1 CONC y on television and in newspaper headlines. As chair of th
1 CONC e Allen administration's Housing Resources Committee, he mo
1 CONC bilized the construction of more than 22,000 low-income hou
1 CONC sing units to replace slums throughout the city. He guide
1 CONC d the Committee to Mediate Racial Unrest and formed the Atl
1 CONC anta Black Jewish Coalition with civil rights leader (and l
1 CONC ater U.S. congressman) John Lewis. He was also one of the o
1 CONC rganizers of a dinner to honor Martin Luther King Jr. afte
1 CONC r King was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1964.  After hi
1 CONC s wife was killed by a drunk driver in 1983, Alexander laun
1 CONC ched a program to educate young people about the dangers o
1 CONC f drinking and driving. He also campaigned for tougher law
1 CONC s to combat drunk driving. In 1985 he married Helen Eiseman
1 CONC n.  Architectural Career and Leadership In 1948 Alexander a
1 CONC nd Bernard Rothschild founded the Alexander and Rothschil
1 CONC d architectural firm, and two of their early projects wer
1 CONC e published in Architectural Record: the fully air-conditio
1 CONC ned Peachtree-Seventh Building (circa 1949-50, later Atlant
1 CONC a Federal Building and then Peachtree Lofts) and the Peacht
1 CONC ree Baker Building (circa 1956). During this period Alexand
1 CONC er also served periodically as a design critic for the Geor
1 CONC gia Tech Architecture School.  The firm merged in 1958 wit
1 CONC h Finch Barnes and Paschal to form FABRAP. As principal wit
1 CONC h FABRAP, Alexander worked on such high-profile projects a
1 CONC s the corporate headquarters for the Coca-Cola Company (197
1 CONC 0, 1979, 1981), Southern Bell (later BellSouth, 1980), an
1 CONC d Georgia Power Company, as well as the Atlanta-Fulton Coun
1 CONC ty Stadium (1965, razed 1997). In addition to commercial st
1 CONC ructures he also designed a number of prominent homes, incl
1 CONC uding a futuristic "round house" (1958) located on Mt. Para
1 CONC n Road in Atlanta, which was nominated for the National Reg
1 CONC ister of Historic Places.  Following his retirement from fu
1 CONC ll-time architectural practice with FABRAP in 1985, Alexand
1 CONC er collaborated with architect John Portman, a relationshi
1 CONC p that continued through the 1996 Olympic Games. He also co
1 CONC ntinued to campaign for racial equality. One of his goals w
1 CONC as to replace Georgia's 1956 state flag and its prominent C
1 CONC onfederate symbolism. As Atlanta prepared to host the 199
1 CONC 6 Olympics, he created a new flag design that incorporate
1 CONC d images from all of Georgia's historic flags. His design p
1 CONC roposal was ill received by legislators, but in 2000 Alexan
1 CONC der met with Governor Roy Barnes. Barnes supported Alexande
1 CONC r's design, with minor changes, and urged lawmakers to adop
1 CONC t it. By early 2001 a new state flag had been approved by t
1 CONC he Georgia General Assembly and was flying over the stateho
1 CONC use. Controversial from the start, the new flag proved to b
1 CONC e highly unpopular among Confederate heritage supporters. I
1 CONC t was replaced three years later with another design, whic
1 CONC h ended the controversy.  Alexander has received the Americ
1 CONC a Institute of Architects' Whitney M. Young Jr. Award for i
1 CONC mproving race relations, the Ivan Allen Award for communit
1 CONC y service, and the Yale Medal for distinguished alumni.  Th
1 CONC e novelist Pat Conroy has said that Alexander was the mode
1 CONC l for the character of Colonel Hudspeth in The Great Santin
1 CONC i (1976).  Suggested Reading Ivan Allen and Paul Hemphill
1 CONC , Mayor: Notes on the Sixties (New York: Simon and Schuster
1 CONC , 1971).  Harold H. Martin, Atlanta and Environs: A Chronic
1 CONC le of Its People and Events: Years of Change and Challenge

1 CONT From: Society for Industrial Archeology Newsletter - Volum
1 CONC e 34 Spring 2005 Number 2

1 CONT On the evening of Oct. 25, 1983, an inebriated 16- year-ol
1 CONC d drove head-on into a car driven by Atlanta architect Ceci
1 CONC l Alexander. Alexander was badly injured; his wife, Hermion
1 CONC e Weil Alexander, who was a passenger, died. As a tribute t
1 CONC o Mrs. Alexander, who was active in civic, philanthropic, a
1 CONC nd cultural activities in Atlanta and was the first femal
1 CONC e jury commissioner for Fulton County, the county commissio
1 CONC n named a bridge- Hermi's Bridge-  in her honor. This was e
1 CONC specially fitting as the Alexanders had been instrumental i
1 CONC n preserving the historic bridge when it had been threatene
1 CONC d with demolition nearly a decade prior to the accident. Co
1 CONC nstructed in 1903 by the Cotton States Bridge Co. for appro
1 CONC ximately $10,000, Hermi's Bridge is a two-span, 280- ft.-lo
1 CONC ng, pin-connected, Pratt, through-truss bridge that carrie
1 CONC d a single lane of traffic across the Chattahoochee River u
1 CONC ntil it was bypassed in 1972. Located near the private Love
1 CONC tt School, Hermi's Bridge is used by students to walk to at
1 CONC hletic fields. In addition, hikers and bicyclists use the b
1 CONC ridge as the only pedestrian-friendly crossing of the Chatt
1 CONC ahoochee in suburban Atlanta. Except for a new wooden dec
1 CONC k and a coat of paint provided by Fulton County, Hermi's Br
1 CONC idge has received little maintenance since it stopped carry
1 CONC ing motor vehicles over 30 years ago. Hermi's Bridge is no
1 CONC w in need of rehabilitation. The regional planning agency
1 CONC , the Atlanta Regional Commission, has made $320,000 of fed
1 CONC eral funds available for inspection and repairs; however, t
1 CONC his money requires a matching grant of $80,000, which count
1 CONC y funds will not cover. Cecil Alexander is heading an effor
1 CONC t to raise the matching funds. In a story in the Nov. 4, 20
1 CONC 04 Atlanta Journal- Constitution, Alexander explained that
1 CONC , "What I hope to do is publicize the situation so we coul
1 CONC d raise the money." In the same article, Alexander also exp
1 CONC lained the scope of the proposed work. "The first order o
1 CONC f business is an engineering check of the bridge to see i
1 CONC f it's safe….They'll need scuba divers to check the foundat
1 CONC ion. The second will be to replace any fasteners or steel t
1 CONC hat's inadequate." The third thing that needs to be done i
1 CONC s to remove the existing paint; this is likely to be an exp
1 CONC ensive undertaking since the paint is lead-based. And the
1 CONC n the bridge will be re-painted. Finally, the wood deck nee
1 CONC ds to be replaced and the approaches to the bridge need cle
1 CONC aring and landscaping as well. The PATH Foundation, a loca
1 CONC l non-profit trails group, has endorsed the rehabilitatio
1 CONC n of Hermi's Bridge. A lifetime Atlanta resident, Alexande
1 CONC r was an architect during the building boom in the 1960s an
1 CONC d 1970s, so he is wellknown. He now wants to use those "con
1 CONC nections" to aid in saving Hermi's Bridge. PATH is also rea
1 CONC ching out to those with interests in historic bridges fro
1 CONC m across the country. For information or to make a contribu
1 CONC tion: PATH Foundation, Box 14327, Atlanta, GA 30324; Attn
1 CONC : Ed McBrayor, Executive Director.

1 CONC From "Village Care New York" - 1993:
1 CONT Selma Jeanne Cohen. Cohen was born in Chicago in 1920, th
1 CONC e only child of Frank and Minnie Cohen. At a young age, sh
1 CONC e studied ballet under a teacher who knew, Cohen herself ad
1 CONC mitted, that she was not a naturally gifted dancer but wh
1 CONC o was nevertheless struck by her interest in dance. With th
1 CONC e teacher's encouragement, Cohen used her extensive librar
1 CONC y to learn about dance, leading Cohen to discover her calli
1 CONC ng - writing about the art of dance.
1 CONT
1 CONT Biography:
1 CONT Selma Jeanne Cohen was born in Chicago, IL in 1920. She rec
1 CONC eived an A.A. degree from Stephens College and an A.B., M.A
1 CONC ., and Ph.D. (in English) from the University of Chicago. C
1 CONC ohen was the only child of Frank and Minnie Cohen. Her inte
1 CONC rest in dance began early in her life, but her lack of natu
1 CONC ral talent turned her away from dancing to the study of th
1 CONC e history of dance. In the late 1940s, after earning her do
1 CONC ctorate in English Literature, she took her first teachin
1 CONC g assignment at University of California at Los Angeles. Sh
1 CONC e remained a teacher and lecturer throughout the 1950s an
1 CONC d began publishing articles on the history of dance and the
1 CONC ater in scholarly journals. Cohen took a position at the Hi
1 CONC gh School of the Performing Arts in New York in the 1950s
1 CONC . Cohen co-founded the ŮCiŮDDance PerspectivesŮC/iŮD quarterl
1 CONC y journal in 1959. One of the first journals dedicated to i
1 CONC nternational dance history, Dance Perspectives became a res
1 CONC pected publication with Cohen as its editor from 1966-1976
1 CONC . During these same years Cohen authored her three major bo
1 CONC oks, ŮCiŮDModern Dance: Seven Statements of BeliefŮC/iŮD in 196
1 CONC 6, ŮCiŮDDoris Humphrey: An Artist FirstŮC/iŮD in 1972 and ŮCiŮDDa
1 CONC nce as a Theater ArtŮC/iŮD in 1974. She also authored article
1 CONC s in numerous publications, journals, encyclopedias, and an
1 CONC thologies. Cohen's work with her Dance Perspectives Foundat
1 CONC ion culminated in her editing and compiling the ŮCiŮDInternat
1 CONC ional Dance EncyclopediaŮC/iŮD, a six volume, 4,000 page comp
1 CONC endium published by the Oxford University Press in 1998. Co
1 CONC hen spent much of the 1960s teaching at the Connecticut Col
1 CONC lege Dance Department, eventually becoming Founder-Directo
1 CONC r of the Connecticut College American Dance Festival Critic
1 CONC s' Conferences in 1970-1972. Cohen held positions at Smit
1 CONC h College and lectured for the Five College, Incorporated
1 CONC . In the 1970's, Cohen held graduate seminars at Sarah Lawr
1 CONC ence College and University of Chicago on dance history. Sh
1 CONC e was Founder-Director of the University of Chicago Seminar
1 CONC s in Dance History (1974-1976), a three-week course which w
1 CONC as the first intensive seminar on dance history. Cohen als
1 CONC o worked to development a dance history curriculum at Unive
1 CONC rsity of California Riverside. Among the awards and honor
1 CONC s received by Cohen have been a Rockefeller Foundation Rese
1 CONC arch Grant (1969), the Professional Achievement Award, Univ
1 CONC ersity of Chicago (1974), the American Dance Guild Award (1
1 CONC 976), and the Dance Magazine Award (1981). She was a charte
1 CONC r member of the Dance Panel of the National Endowment for t
1 CONC he Arts (1966-1971). Source: Title: Selma Jeanne Cohen Pape
1 CONC rs, 1942-1993  SOURCE;   Creator: Cohen, Selma Jeanne    Ca

1 CONC k    Abstract: The Selma Jeanne Cohen papers document the a

1 CONT Obituary: New York Times - 26 December, 2005:
1 CONT December 26, 2005 ŮCbŮDSelma Jeanne Cohen, 85, a Historian o
1 CONC f Dance, Is Dead ŮC/bŮDBy ŮCuŮDJACK ANDERSON ŮC/uŮD Selma Jeann
1 CONC e Cohen, a historian, editor and teacher who devoted her ca
1 CONC reer to proclaiming dance an art worthy of the same scholar
1 CONC ly respect traditionally awarded to painting, music and lit
1 CONC erature, died on Friday at her home in Greenwich Village. S
1 CONC he was 85. The cause was complications from Alzheimer's dis
1 CONC ease, said Leslie Steinau, her lawyer and a longtime friend
1 CONC . Ms. Cohen waged a tireless campaign against scholars wh
1 CONC o maintained that dance was inherently frivolous. Instead
1 CONC , she believed it had a rich history that could be fruitful
1 CONC ly analyzed from many philosophical viewpoints. Her effort
1 CONC s led her to become America's leading figure in dance schol
1 CONC arship. Her most ambitious achievement was her editing of T
1 CONC he International Encyclopedia of Dance for Oxford Universit
1 CONC y Press. Published in 1998 after two decades of planning, a
1 CONC nd encompassing all forms of dance, the six-volume work, mo
1 CONC deled on The New Grove Dictionary of Music and Musicians, r
1 CONC emains the most comprehensive guide of its kind. Eclectic s
1 CONC cholarly interests had prompted Ms. Cohen, A. J.Pischl an
1 CONC d Sheppard Black in 1959 to found Dance Perspectives, a jou
1 CONC rnal dedicated to monographs on various aspects of world da
1 CONC nce. Ms. Cohen became its sole editor in 1966. After it cea
1 CONC sed publication in 1976, the Dance Perspectives Foundation
1 CONC , which she had established, continued to award an annual p
1 CONC rize for the best dance book of the year. Ms. Cohen was bor
1 CONC n in Chicago, attended elementary and high school at the Un
1 CONC iversity of Chicago Laboratory School and went on to the un
1 CONC iversity itself, receiving a Ph.D. in English in 1946. Whe
1 CONC n a childhood friend started taking ballet lessons from Edn
1 CONC a McRae, a respected Chicago teacher, Ms. Cohen went along
1 CONC . Ms. Cohen soon realized she had no dancing talent. But sh
1 CONC e had great curiosity, and McRae had a dance library, whic
1 CONC h Ms. Cohen devoured. She began teaching English at the Uni
1 CONC versity of California at Los Angeles in 1946 and, at the sa
1 CONC me time, worked with the Los Angeles teacher and choreograp
1 CONC her Eugene Loring. After moving to New York in 1953, she de
1 CONC voted herself to dance and started reviewing for Dance Obse
1 CONC rver. From 1955 to 1958 she was a dance critic at The New Y
1 CONC ork Times, where she assisted John Martin, who was chief da
1 CONC nce critic. She was dance critic for The Saturday Review i
1 CONC n 1965 and 1966. Ms. Cohen was a member of the first Nation
1 CONC al Endowment for the Arts Dance Panel in 1966; organize
1 CONC d a critics' conference at the American Dance Festival in N
1 CONC ew London, Conn., in 1970; and taught over the years at man
1 CONC y colleges, including the University of Chicago, where sh
1 CONC e returned to head summer seminars from 1974 to 1976. Ms. C
1 CONC ohen wrote and edited several important books, among them "
1 CONC The Modern Dance: Seven Statements of Belief" (Wesleyan Uni
1 CONC versity Press, 1966), an anthology of essays by contemporar
1 CONC y choreographers; "Doris Humphrey: An Artist First" (Wesley
1 CONC an, 1972), a biography of a pioneering modern dancer; and "
1 CONC Next Week, Swan Lake" (Wesleyan, 1982), a study of dance ae
1 CONC sthetics. Her "Dance as a Theater Art" (Dodd, Mead, 1974)
1 CONC , a history of dance since the Renaissance, remains a popul
1 CONC ar college text. Ms. Cohen, a tiny soft-spoken woman, coul
1 CONC d be a rigorous teacher, but she delighted in presiding gra
1 CONC ciously over social events. Friends, colleagues and student
1 CONC s often met informally at her apartment, gatherings usuall
1 CONC y graced by Giselle, her long-lived and much-pampered cat
1 CONC . Ms. Cohen often jokingly said she hoped to write a treati
1 CONC se on feline aesthetics someday. No immediate family member
1 CONC s survive.  ŮCuŮDCopyright 2005
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮC/uŮDEstate provides gift to Benjamin V. Cohen Memorial Fun
1 CONC d (4/1/2008)
1 CONT The estate of Selma Jeanne Cohen has provided the Benjami
1 CONC n V. Cohen Memorial Fund with a monetary gift in excess o
1 CONC f $38,000.
1 CONT Selma, a New York resident, was the niece of Benjamin, a Mu
1 CONC ncie, Ind., native who was instrumental in organizing the U
1 CONC nited Nations and in constructing much of the New Deal legi
1 CONC slation. Selma was a noted scholar and critic of dance, dev
1 CONC oting her life to teaching and documenting its history an
1 CONC d importance until her death in 2005.
1 CONT The Cohen Memorial Fund was established in 1984 by Cohen'
1 CONC s nephew, Bernard Freund, an attorney and philanthropist. T
1 CONC he fund supports the Cohen Peace Fellowship Program, awardi
1 CONC ng funds to Ball State faculty and doctoral students for re
1 CONC search in fields related to progress toward a peaceful worl
1 CONC d. For more information about the Cohen Research Awards an
1 CONC d other programs, contact the Office of Academic Research a
1 CONC nd Sponsored Programs at 765-285-1601. There is not current
1 CONC ly an open call for proposals.

1 CONC y 22, 2004 at St. Vincent Hospital. He was born June 30, 19
1 CONC 28 in Baltimore, MD to Marie G. Efroymson and Charles F. Ef
1 CONC roymson, Sr. On December 1, 1968, he married Jacqueline C
1 CONC . Efroymson. He graduated from Shortridge High School in 19
1 CONC 46 and then served in the U.S. Air Force in post-World Wa
1 CONC r II Germany. After serving his country, he attended and gr
1 CONC aduated from Indiana University in Bloomington with a degre
1 CONC e in business. He was a commercial realtor in Indpls. for o
1 CONC ver 40 years and was responsible for several high profile a
1 CONC nd innovative real estate developments including the repurc
1 CONC hase and sale of the former H.P. Wasson's Department Stor
1 CONC e building and development of the "Miracle Mile" of Georget
1 CONC own Road. He remained very active in Indpls. real estate un
1 CONC til his death. He was active with several state and local r
1 CONC ealtor associations and served as Secretary and Treasurer o
1 CONC f the Metropolitan Indianapolis Board of Realtors in 1991-1
1 CONC 992. He was a lifelong member of Indianapolis Hebrew Congre
1 CONC gation where he held several committee positions and as a
1 CONC n Officer of IHC Brotherhood. He was a member of the Indpls
1 CONC . chapter of B'nai B'rith and Jewish War Veterans. He wa
1 CONC s a master bridge player and competed in contract bridge to
1 CONC urnaments throughout the Midwest. Survived by his wife, Jac
1 CONC queline C. Efroymson; children, Jonathon H. Efroymson, Bet
1 CONC h Efroymson Leventhal and Henry A. Efroymson; 5 grandchildr
1 CONC en.

1 CONC ŮCbŮDDr. Kenneth H. Kahn, DDS
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDDr. Kahn graduated in 1976 from the University of New M
1 CONC exico, and attended dental school at Indiana University Sch
1 CONC ool of Dentistry, where he earned his Doctor of Dental Surg
1 CONC ery in 1980. Upon graduation, Dr. Kahn was accepted into th
1 CONC e post-doctorate endodontic specialty studies program at Tu
1 CONC fts University School of Dental Medicine in Boston. There h
1 CONC e earned his Post-doctorate Certificate in Endodontics. I
1 CONC n 1989, he became a Diplomate of the American Board of Endo
1 CONC dontics.
1 CONT  Dr. Kahn was in private practice in Boston for 16 years, a
1 CONC s well as teaching clinical endodontics at Tufts Universit
1 CONC y School of Dental Medicine at both the pre-doctoral and po
1 CONC st-doctoral levels. In 1992, he received the honor of becom
1 CONC ing a Fellow in the International College of Dentistry. Th
1 CONC e College admits dentists who have shown outstanding clinic
1 CONC al, academic and leadership skills as well as giving back t
1 CONC o their communities. In 1996, he was honored by his peers b
1 CONC y being inducted into the exclusive Omicron Kappa Upsilon D
1 CONC ental Honor Society. Dr. Kahn volunteered for many years a
1 CONC t the Bridge Over Troubled Waters, a medical/dental facilit
1 CONC y for homeless youth. He provided dental service to the Bos
1 CONC ton Bruins hockey team and the World Cup Soccer league, whi
1 CONC le living in Boston.
1 CONT
1 CONT In 1998, Dr. Kahn and his wife Caren, returned to New Mexic
1 CONC o. He has maintained a private practice with offices in San
1 CONC ta Fe and Los Alamos since that time. In 2009, he began th
1 CONC e New Mexico Endodontic Center. Dr. Kahn has always been ac
1 CONC tive in various local and national dental organizations. Th
1 CONC ese include the American Dental Association, American Assoc
1 CONC iation of Endodontics, The International College of Dentist
1 CONC ry, and the American Board of Endodontics. He is past-presi
1 CONC dent of the Santa Fe District Dental Society as well as o
1 CONC n the Advisory Board of the Santa Fe Community College.

1 CONC Well-known in the Twin Cities community, Roland Minda i
1 CONC s a longtime public relations professional who, with his wi
1 CONC fe and partner, Merle Minda, operated their own agency, Min
1 CONC da Associates, for thirty years.
1 CONT
1 CONT His wide circle of contacts, friends and acquaintances, mat
1 CONC ched with his quizzical sense of curiosity and whimsy, ad
1 CONC d to the program's appeal.
1 CONT
1 CONT Roland hosted and produced "Strictly Seniors" for over 10 y
1 CONC ears, since 2000. The program has won numerous awards and m
1 CONC uch media attention. Major coverage has included the Minnes
1 CONC ota Sun Newspapers; Minneapolis Star Tribune, Southwest Jou
1 CONC rnal; Format Magazine; Skyway News, American Jewish World
1 CONC , and Hill & Lake Press.
1 CONT
1 CONT In June 2002, the program took first place in the senior ca
1 CONC tegory of the Alliance for Community Media's national compe
1 CONC tition. The Alliance's annual contest, the Hometown Video F
1 CONC estival, is the largest national video competition for loca
1 CONC l television cable programming in the United States and Can
1 CONC ada.
1 CONT
1 CONT Roland often serves as host and moderator for community pro
1 CONC grams and events, such as the Minneapolis Senior Center i
1 CONC n downtown Minneapolis.
1 CONT
1 CONT Minda was honored as the recipient of the Public Relation
1 CONC s Society of America's Donald G. Padilla Award for his 35 y
1 CONC ears of community service. Minda has served on the boards o
1 CONC f the Animal Humane Society, the American Red Cross, the Am
1 CONC erican Lung Association, Temple Israel, and the Public Rela
1 CONC tions Society of America (PRSA), among others.
1 CONT
1 CONT Roland was born in Minneapolis, growing up in the Kenwood/L
1 CONC owry Hill neighborhood. He is a graduate of West High Schoo
1 CONC l, Minneapolis, and Princeton University, with a degree fro
1 CONC m the Woodrow Wilson School of Public and International Aff
1 CONC airs. He served in the U.S. Army in World War II, in New Gu
1 CONC inea and the Philippines.

1 CONT "In 1971, (Chicago Mayor Richard) Daley faced Richard Fried
1 CONC man, a highly-touted reform Democrat running on the GOP tic
1 CONC ket.  Friedman had been the president of the Better Governm
1 CONC ent Association, a municipal watchdog organization that rec
1 CONC eived substantial funding from the city's major corporation
1 CONC s.  He solicited these corporate acquaintances for financia
1 CONC l support, but they were nearly unanimously committed to Da
1 CONC ley.  Friedman also had the endorsement of the Rev. Jesse J
1 CONC ackson, the Chicago-based civil rights leader, but this pro
1 CONC ved to be of little value.  Daley received 70 percent of th
1 CONC e vote, including 75 percent of the Black vote.  Friedman w
1 CONC on just two of the 50 wards.

1 CONC  ŮCbŮDTammy Jo Bialosky obtained her undergraduate degree fro
1 CONC m Wheaton College (1981), a law degree from Northwestern Un
1 CONC iversity (1985) and a Masters of Education from John Carrol
1 CONC l University (2000). After law school, Tammy enjoyed a succ
1 CONC essful career as a lawyer at Thompson Hine in Cleveland
1 CONC , a prestigious, national law firm. She traded this for th
1 CONC e opportunity to make a difference in the lives of teenager
1 CONC s as a high school teacher. When the family settled in Bloo
1 CONC mfield Hills in 2000, Tammy accepted a position at Avondal
1 CONC e High School to teach advanced placement history. As an Av
1 CONC ondale educator, Tammy influenced the lives of Avondale Hig
1 CONC h School staff, colleagues and students. In 2005 she was di
1 CONC agnosed with a rare form of cancer which she battled with g
1 CONC race, determination and courage. Unfortunately, she lost he
1 CONC r battle with cancer in 2006. Throughout her battle with ca
1 CONC ncer, she drew great comfort and strength from her relation
1 CONC ships with Avondale students, teachers and staff. ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT ŮCbŮDEstablished in 2006, the Tammy Jo Bialosky Scholarship p
1 CONC rovides a scholarship for a graduating senior who has bot
1 CONC h (1) demonstrated excellence in the field of history at Av
1 CONC ondale High School, and (2) embodied the passion of Tammy J
1 CONC o Bialosky for learning. ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT ŮCbŮDThe scholarship is available to Avondale High School sen
1 CONC iors who have demonstrated good citizenship and maintaine
1 CONC d a minimum cumulative 3.0 GPA in high school. The scholars
1 CONC hip is for students enrolling in a full-time degree progra
1 CONC m at a four-year accredited college. In the case of student
1 CONC s receiving special services, the scholarship may be awarde
1 CONC d to students enrolling in a two-year accredited college. T
1 CONC he Avondale Foundation will administer the disbursement o
1 CONC f funds on behalf of the Tammy Jo Bialosky Scholarship. ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT ŮCbŮDEach year one scholarship of up to $10,000 will be award
1 CONC ed. Twenty-five percent (25%) of the scholarship will be pa
1 CONC yable on September 1 of each year during which the studen
1 CONC t is enrolled in an accredited four-year college, provide
1 CONC d the student has maintained at least a cumulative 3.0 grad
1 CONC e point average. In the case of an award to a student recei
1 CONC ving special services to attend a two-year college, fifty p
1 CONC ercent (50%) of the scholarship will be payable on Septembe
1 CONC r 1 of each year during which the student is enrolled in a
1 CONC n accredited two-year college, provided the student has mai
1 CONC ntained at least a cumulative 3.0 grade point average. Th
1 CONC e cumulative grade point average will be determined as of t
1 CONC he end of the previous academic year. ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT ŮCbŮDThe scholarship recipient will be determined by a Schola
1 CONC rship Committee consisting of the Avondale High School prin
1 CONC cipal, the chair of the social studies department and Davi
1 CONC d L. Bialosky (Tammy's husband) from the top three applicat
1 CONC ions selected by the principal and chair of the social stud
1 CONC ies department. ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT ŮCbŮDIn exceptional circumstances, the Scholarship Committe
1 CONC e will have the authority to split the award into scholarsh
1 CONC ips for two recipients provided that the total of both awar
1 CONC ds may not exceed $10,000. ŮC/bŮD

1 CONT 11 December 2007 Paid Notice:
1 CONT Deaths Stanley R. Died December 8, 2007 at age 89. Loving h
1 CONC usband of Zelma Becker and father of Hilary Oran, Joni Beck
1 CONC er and Betsy Salinger. Devoted husband of Betty Ann and ste
1 CONC pfather of Michael and Kim; loving grandfather to Olivia, G
1 CONC annon, Gregory, Avery, Oliver, Holly and Anna. Served a
1 CONC s a Naval Officer in World War II on the U.S.S. Maumee fuel
1 CONC ing Allied conveys in the Atlantic. Graduated from Union Co
1 CONC llege in 1940, and maintained a 65 year relationship contri
1 CONC buting to the revitalization of the school's infrastructur
1 CONC e and providing financial support for the education of nume
1 CONC rous students. A successful real estate developer, he retir
1 CONC ed to pursue his love affair with the sea. An adventurous o
1 CONC ffshore sailor, he sailed across the Atlantic, and at age 7
1 CONC 0 survived an encounter with Hurricane Grace in the Perfec
1 CONC t Storm. Interested in education and healthcare, he serve
1 CONC d on the Boards of Southampton Hospital and The East Hampto
1 CONC n Heathcare Foundation. A memorial service will be held a
1 CONC t 12pm on Wednesday, December 12, at Yardley & Pino, 94 Pan
1 CONC tigo Road, East Hampton, New York. In lieu of flowers, dona
1 CONC tions can be made to The East Hampton Healthcare Foundation
1 CONC , 200 Pantigo Place, East Hampton, NY 11937 and UJA-Federat
1 CONC ion of New York.
1 CONT
1 CONT Stanley R. Becker '40, a retired developer and investor fo
1 CONC r whom the College's career planning center is named, die
1 CONC d Dec. 8, 2007. He was 89.
1 CONT Becker twice funded the renovation of the former Old Gymnas
1 CONC ium. In 1981, the building was dedicated as the Stanley R
1 CONC . Becker Hall, home to Admissions and Financial Aid. In 200
1 CONC 2, after he funded a conversion, the building was re-dedica
1 CONC ted as the Stanley R. Becker Career Center.
1 CONT Becker once gave the following career advice: "Select a fie
1 CONC ld of employment that you will get great pleasure from, sta
1 CONC y with it and work hard because the competition out there i
1 CONC s keen."
1 CONT In 1978, Becker established the Stanley R. Becker Scholarsh
1 CONC ip, to juniors and seniors in political science who sho
1 CONC w a promise of contributing to the public good.
1 CONT He received an honorary degree from the College in 1980, an
1 CONC d the Alumni Gold Medal in 2000. He also served as class he
1 CONC ad agent, chairman of the Annual Fund, and member of the Tr
1 CONC ustees Board of Advisors, the national committee for the Ca
1 CONC mpaign for Union and the Terrace Council.
1 CONT Becker, who earned his bachelor's degree in science, joine
1 CONC d the U.S. Department of the Interior as a junior topograph
1 CONC er after graduation. He joined the U.S. Navy in 1942 and wa
1 CONC s commissioned as an ensign.
1 CONT When he left the service in 1945, he began as a junior cons
1 CONC truction supervisor on Long Island. As a self-employed buil
1 CONC der and real estate developer, he owned the Beckitt Constru
1 CONC ction Corp. and was owner and operator of Howard Johnson Mo
1 CONC tor Lodges in Stratford and Hartford, Conn., and at JFK Air
1 CONC port in New York City. In 1969, he was elected chairman o
1 CONC f the board of Howard Johnson National Operators Council. H
1 CONC e retired in 1984 and formed Stanley R. Becker Investments.
1 CONT Among his community activities, he was on the board of dire
1 CONC ctors of the East Hampton Healthcare Foundation, presiden
1 CONC t of Halsey's Marina of East Hampton, N.Y.; on the board o
1 CONC f governors of the Saugatuck Harbor (Conn.) Yacht Club; an
1 CONC d past president of the Rotary Club at JFK Airport.
1 CONT His interests included trans-Atlantic sailing and collectin
1 CONC g historical documents and nautical antiques.

1 CONC ary Oran, Joni Becker and Betsy Salinger; two stepchildren
1 CONC , Michael and Kim; and seven grandchildren.

1 CONC Emigrated from Pyrmont, Waldeck Germany (near Hamburg)to Ka
1 CONC lamazoo, Michigan, in 1855 to work for his cousin,  Manne
1 CONC s Israel, in M. Israel Dry Goods...a large  variety store t
1 CONC hat Mannes Israel owned and operated there.   The store lat
1 CONC er became Rosenbaum & Speyer.  The "Rosenbaum" was Simon.
1 CONT It is not known what brought Mannes Israel to Kalamazoo i
1 CONC n 1844(?), but he was definitely an EARLY settler...,"the f
1 CONC irst Jew to arrive in Kalamazoo".
1 CONT Mannes was much admired and loved by the Rosenbaums (a "fir
1 CONC st cousin" of Simon and Samuel, ,although precise nature o
1 CONC f the relationship is not established.)
1 CONT Simon married Rieckchen Romberg of Berlin, Prussia, and ha
1 CONC d one daughter, Emma.
1 CONT
1 CONT Database: Full Context of Michigan Biographies, 1878
1 CONT American Biographical History of Eminent and Self-Made Me
1 CONC n with Portrait Illustrations on Steel, Volumes I-II
1 CONT
1 CONT Name: Simon Rosenbaum Dry-Goods Merchant, Kalamazoo, Michig
1 CONC an, is a son of Susman and Caroline (Bermann) Rosenbaum. H
1 CONC e was born in Pyrmont, Waldeck, Germany, April 13, 1832. Hi
1 CONC s early educational advantages were confined to the commo
1 CONC n schools of his native place. After leaving school, wher
1 CONC e he was distinguished for his rapid progress in learning
1 CONC , he continued his self-education by careful study of Germa
1 CONC n literature, devoting his attention to the works of its mo
1 CONC st eminent authors, with an interest bordering on enthusias
1 CONC m. At the age of fourteen, he entered the dry-goods store o
1 CONC f his father, in which he continued, as clerk, for nine yea
1 CONC rs; and, by his untiring industry, contributed greatly to i
1 CONC ts financial success. At the end of this time, he receive
1 CONC d from a friend an urgent invitation to come to America, wh
1 CONC ere be arrived in 1855, and found a situation awaiting hi
1 CONC m in the dry-goods store of Mr. Israel, in Kalamazoo, Michi
1 CONC gan. Here, by his business talent, energy, and fidelity, h
1 CONC e soon won for himself the confidence and friendship of hi
1 CONC s employer. In 1865 he was admitted as a partner; and, at t
1 CONC he death of Mr. Israel, in 1868, took the entire charge o
1 CONC f the business. He has conducted it ever since, giving, b
1 CONC y his untiring zeal and sterling integrity, full satisfacti
1 CONC on to all. He is noted for the salutary influence he exert
1 CONC s upon the young men in his employment. He is frugal in hi
1 CONC s habits, strict towards himself, but lenient in his judgme
1 CONC nt of others. He has been several times to Europe, and ha
1 CONC s visited all the important cities on this continent. He wo
1 CONC rships with the Israelites, but evinces no prejudice toward
1 CONC s those of a different faith. Since he became a citizen o
1 CONC f the United States, he has always voted with the Republica
1 CONC n party. He married, February 27, 1865, Rieckchen Romberg
1 CONC , a lady of superior intellectual culture, having receive
1 CONC d her education in the most celebrated schools of Berlin, P
1 CONC russia. They have one child,--a daughter. Mr. Rosenbaum i
1 CONC s unassuming in his manners, and shrinks from parade of an
1 CONC y kind. The late financial depression has left him unharmed
1 CONC , and he is now in a more prosperous condition than ever be
1 CONC fore.
1 CONT

1 CONC Edward Israel was born in Kalamazoo on 1 July 1859. He die
1 CONC d almost 25 years later on 27 May 1884 in a tent at Cape Sa
1 CONC bine on Ellesmere Island far to the north of the Arctic Cir
1 CONC cle. The story of how he came to die so young in such a for
1 CONC saken place is a long and fascinating one. In fact, two ful
1 CONC l length books have been written on the subject of the Gree
1 CONC ly Expedition, of which Israel was a member. Here there i
1 CONC s only space for a brief sketch of the life of Kalamazoo'
1 CONC s own Arctic explorer and the part he played in the ill-fat
1 CONC ed expedition that, for a brief time, captured the attentio
1 CONC n of the world.
1 CONT Mannes Israel, Edward's father, was the first Jewish citize
1 CONC n to reside in the village of Kalamazoo. He arrived in 184
1 CONC 4 and established a dry goods store on the corner of Michig
1 CONC an Avenue and Rose Street. Edward's mother was Tillie Israe
1 CONC l. She raised the children and continued the family busines
1 CONC s after the death of her husband. Edward attended the Kalam
1 CONC azoo Public Schools and then continued his education at th
1 CONC e University of Michigan. It was there, just a few months b
1 CONC efore he was to graduate in 1881, that Israel was nominate
1 CONC d by one of his professors for a position as the astronome
1 CONC r on a United States government expedition to the Arctic. I
1 CONC srael excitedly accepted the nomination, was granted his di
1 CONC ploma by the university, and returned home to Kalamazoo t
1 CONC o bid farewell to his family. On 28 April 1881 Israel lef
1 CONC t Kalamazoo for Washington. He would not return alive.
1 CONT
1 CONT The Lady Franklin Bay Expedition (also commonly called th
1 CONC e Greely Expedition after its leader Lieutenant Adolphus Gr
1 CONC eely) was chartered by the federal government as part o
1 CONC f a worldwide plan to send groups to the polar regions to c
1 CONC onduct scientific experiments and measurements. Greely, Isr
1 CONC ael, and twenty three other men set out from Washington, D.
1 CONC C., for Newfoundland on 9 June 1881. From there they boarde
1 CONC d a special ship that took them to their final destinatio
1 CONC n at Lady Franklin Bay, well north of the Arctic Circle. Fr
1 CONC om a base they christened Fort Conger, the men spent two lo
1 CONC nely years conducting their measurements and experiments i
1 CONC n about as much comfort as one could expect in such a froze
1 CONC n and barren place. Israel was responsible for taking hundr
1 CONC eds of readings each day, including astronomical, pendulum
1 CONC , magnetic, and meteorological observations. The men had pl
1 CONC enty of food, a sturdy structure to keep the elements at ba
1 CONC y, and even some luxuries (each man was given a quart of ru
1 CONC m on his birthday).
1 CONT The first sign that something was wrong came in the summe
1 CONC r of 1882 when the ship that was supposed to resupply the e
1 CONC xpedition failed to arrive. However, they still had plent
1 CONC y of provisions for the following year. Another year passe
1 CONC d and the summer of 1883 crawled by in the never-ending day
1 CONC light of an arctic summer. Supplies were beginning to run o
1 CONC ut, and so, according to a predetermined plan Greely decide
1 CONC d to lead his men back south across the ice to Cape Sabin
1 CONC e where they could make use of a cache of supplies and mak
1 CONC e themselves more easily rescued. It was 9 August 1883 whe
1 CONC n the men finally left Fort Conger with all the supplies th
1 CONC ey could carry loaded into small boats and set off southwar
1 CONC ds. Israel's readings were the only way the party could det
1 CONC ermine their location on the constantly shifting ice. Witho
1 CONC ut Israel the entire party would probably have been lost. E
1 CONC ventually the expedition reached Cape Sabine where they rec
1 CONC eived the worst possible news. The relief ship had been cru
1 CONC shed in the ice and sunk, and only a fraction of the necess
1 CONC ary supplies had been delivered. The Greely expedition woul
1 CONC d have to suffer through another arctic winter, this time w
1 CONC ith almost no supplies and no proper shelter.
1 CONT Nine months passed, and all but six of the expedition membe
1 CONC rs slowly succumbed to frostbite, exposure, and starvation
1 CONC . Edward Israel died about three weeks before the remainin
1 CONC g men were finally rescued by a U.S. Navy ship that had man
1 CONC aged to break through the ice. One of the survivors wrote o
1 CONC f Israel's passing, "Everyone was his friend. He had no ene
1 CONC mies. His frankness, his honesty, and his noble generosit
1 CONC y of nature had won the hearts of all his companions. His u
1 CONC nswerving integrity during these months of agony has bee
1 CONC n a shining example; and although his sacrifices were los
1 CONC t to a few, still the effect has produced good fruit. For l
1 CONC ack of strength we could not bury him today."
1 CONT Edward Israel's body was returned to Kalamazoo on 11 Augus
1 CONC t 1884. Thousands turned out at the railway station to pa
1 CONC y their respects, and the businesses of the town were all c
1 CONC losed. He was buried in the Jewish section of Mountain Hom
1 CONC e Cemetery. A historical marker was erected near his graves
1 CONC ite in 1972 to commemorate Kalamazoo's Arctic explorer.

1 CONC Clifford Orwin is a professor of political science, a fello
1 CONC w of St. Michael's College, and director of the Program i
1 CONC n Political Philosophy and International Affairs at the Uni
1 CONC versity of Toronto. He is a regular contributor to the ŮCiŮDG
1 CONC lobe and MailŮC/iŮD, Canada's national newspaper of record, a
1 CONC s well as to numerous publications in the United States. H
1 CONC e is the author of ŮCiŮDThe Humanity of ThucydidesŮC/iŮD (Princ
1 CONC eton University Press, 2nd ed., 1997) and coeditor and coau
1 CONC thor of ŮCiŮDThe Legacy of RousseauŮC/iŮD (University of Chicag
1 CONC o Press, 1997) and has written dozens of articles on classi
1 CONC cal, modern, contemporary, and Jewish political thought. H
1 CONC e is currently completing a book for the general reader o
1 CONC n the role of compassion in modern politics. He received hi
1 CONC s B.A. in history from Cornell University and his M.A. an
1 CONC d Ph.D. in political science from Harvard University and ha
1 CONC s taught as a visitor at Harvard and Chicago as well as i
1 CONC n Jerusalem, Paris, and Lisbon.

1 CONC New York TIMES - Published: May 25, 1990
1 CONT Margaret Sedlis, Architect, Weds
1 CONT Margaret J. Sedlis, the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Edward G
1 CONC . Sedlis of New York, was married yesterday to Matthew Gold
1 CONC stein, a son of Mr. and Mrs. Philip Goldstein of New York
1 CONC . Rabbi Balfour Brickner performed the ceremony at the Step
1 CONC hen Wise Free Synagogue in New York.
1 CONT The bride, a graduate of the Parsons School of Design, rece
1 CONC ived a Master of Architecture degree from Washington Univer
1 CONC sity. She is the director of interiors and a senior associa
1 CONC te at the New York architectural firm of Davis, Brody & Ass
1 CONC ociates.
1 CONT
1 CONT
1 CONT
1 CONT Education Update Online
1 CONT APRIL 2009
1 CONT Architecture: A Passion & A Career Maggi Sedlis Goldstein B
1 CONC y Joan Baum, Ph.D.  Maggi Sedlis Goldstein, a licensed arch
1 CONC itect, "evolved" into the project management business fro
1 CONC m a career as a practicing architect, and has been at the h
1 CONC elm of her Project Management business, Sedlis Goldstein Gr
1 CONC oup, LLC, since 1995. Projects have ranged from $1,000,00
1 CONC 0 to a current workload of several projects including two c
1 CONC urrent 100-million dollar projects. She credits the succes
1 CONC s of her business to "relationship building" and her divers
1 CONC ified career path.  A graduate of the High School of Musi
1 CONC c and Art, where she took classes in fashion design, she we
1 CONC nt on to study at the Parsons School of Design/The New Scho
1 CONC ol for Social Research, where she received a BFA in Environ
1 CONC mental Design, a program that was a mixed bag of architectu
1 CONC re, interior design, landscape and urban design. The variet
1 CONC y of disciplines was eye opening and exciting.  Having alwa
1 CONC ys wanted to be an architect, she fondly recalls a childhoo
1 CONC d fascination with drawing floor plans ("I still have copie
1 CONC s of them), and parents who would take her on weekend excur
1 CONC sions to see model houses and apartments (kind of a goofy i
1 CONC nterest for a 9 year old!). However, she received mixed sig
1 CONC nals about pursuing this dream because a) she was a girl, a
1 CONC nd b) she was not good in math. While at Parsons, she was e
1 CONC ncouraged by one of her instructors, ("you can do it!"), an
1 CONC d she applied for a Master's in Architecture at Washingto
1 CONC n University in St. Louis. Having never been west of Philad
1 CONC elphia or south of Washington D.C., St Louis was as far a
1 CONC s she had the courage to go and it seemed like an adventure
1 CONC . At the time, there were no women on the faculty, and sh
1 CONC e was the only female in her graduating class. Though Fran
1 CONC k Lloyd Wright was a background influence ("I loved his sen
1 CONC se of scale, context, environment"), her career began wit
1 CONC h hands-on work in construction. After her Master's, she we
1 CONC nt to Culebra, Puerto Rico where she actually did construct
1 CONC ion and built wood frame houses. Then, after a few years ba
1 CONC ck and forth between New York City and St. Louis, she perma
1 CONC nently returned to New York. This being the late 70s, earl
1 CONC y 80s, jobs were scarce, but she landed a job in White Plai
1 CONC ns working on corporate interiors. These were the early day
1 CONC s of "Affirmative Action", and though she sensed that she w
1 CONC as being hired for gender rather than expertise, her attitu
1 CONC de was, "hire me!" Indeed, she seems always to have moved w
1 CONC ith opportunity, whatever its motive or source. The resul
1 CONC t was picking up practical knowledge. She did at first resi
1 CONC st going into interior design (it seemed like such a clich
1 CONC âe for a "girl"), but she recognized that this area would gi
1 CONC ve her practical design and management experience. And sh
1 CONC e picked up more than practical knowledge -- she learned ho
1 CONC w to manage projects and clients! Confidence brought an app
1 CONC reciation of the savvy customer. "Early in my career, we us
1 CONC ed to joke that the projects would be great if we didn't ha
1 CONC ve clients! Then as I gained more experience, I realized th
1 CONC at the best, most successful, projects had the most 'educat
1 CONC ed clients', those who were willing to learn and had done t
1 CONC heir due diligence." She is always looking to take advantag
1 CONC es of opportunities and leads as they presented themselves
1 CONC , and has built her career on this. One of her current clie
1 CONC nts, New York Presbyterian Hospital, came about years ago t
1 CONC hrough a former associate. She was originally recommended t
1 CONC o provide project management training to the facilities tea
1 CONC m (that didn't materialize); but what did, was something be
1 CONC tter. She was asked to provide Project Management service
1 CONC s on a project at the hospital, a move that would give he
1 CONC r expertise in hospital construction and regulatory require
1 CONC ments —  drawing on a wide range of design and financial ac
1 CONC tivities, among them developing and adhering to standard
1 CONC s —  and has been consulting with the Hospital ever since
1 CONC . Her client list, which includes both residential and comm
1 CONC ercial projects, exemplifies her firm's diversity —  co-ops
1 CONC , educational institutions (Barnard, NYIT, The New School)
1 CONC , healthcare, not-for profits, media organizations and art
1 CONC s clubs. She prides herself on bringing projects in on tim
1 CONC e and within budget, and very happy clients. Maybe she ough
1 CONC t to work for the Feds. #

1 CONC y peacefully in her home from natural causes Saturday morni
1 CONC ng.  Born and raised in New York City, Dannie graduated fro
1 CONC m Boston University in 1972 and subsequently worked for app
1 CONC arel manufacturer David Crystal in its New York showrooms
1 CONC .  At Crystal, Dannie met her husband, Bill, and they wer
1 CONC e married in 1979. In 1982, the couple relocated to Berks C
1 CONC ounty, where their daughter, Emily, was born.  Dannie estab
1 CONC lished herself as a homemaker and developed a wide circle o
1 CONC f friends who relied on her keen insights, emotional suppor
1 CONC t and wonderful sense of humor.  Despite suffering from a m
1 CONC yriad of illnesses over the last few years, Dannie continue
1 CONC d to selflessly offer time and display interest in her fami
1 CONC ly and friends around her, who will all miss her greatly
1 CONC .  She leaves her husband, Bill, Wyomissing; her daughter
1 CONC , Emily, New York City and her mother, Georgette Wlodawer o
1 CONC f New York City.  A Memorial Service will be Tuesday at 3:3
1 CONC 0 p.m., with a visitation beginning at 3:00 p.m. in the Edw
1 CONC ard J. Kuhn Funeral Home, Inc., 739 Penn Ave., West Reading

1 CONC New York TIMES - Published: May 25, 1990
1 CONT
1 CONT Margaret J. Sedlis, the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Edward G
1 CONC . Sedlis of New York, was married yesterday to Matthew Gold
1 CONC stein, a son of Mr. and Mrs. Philip Goldstein of New York
1 CONC . Rabbi Balfour Brickner performed the ceremony at the Step
1 CONC hen Wise Free Synagogue in New York.
1 CONT The bride, a graduate of the Parsons School of Design, rece
1 CONC ived a Master of Architecture degree from Washington Univer
1 CONC sity. She is the director of interiors and a senior associa
1 CONC te at the New York architectural firm of Davis, Brody & Ass
1 CONC ociates.
1 CONT
1 CONT Mr. Goldstein, a graduate of City College, received a maste
1 CONC r's degree from Rutgers University and a Ph.D. in mathemati
1 CONC cal statistics from the University of Connecticut. He is th
1 CONC e president of the Research Foundation, which coordinates r
1 CONC esearch projects at the City University of New York. His pr
1 CONC evious marriage ended in divorce.
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCbŮDBiography of Dr. Matthew GoldsteinŮC/bŮD
1 CONT  Dr. Matthew Goldstein was unanimously approved on July 22
1 CONC , 1999 as Chancellor of the City University of New York b
1 CONC y its Board of Trustees, becoming the first CUNY graduate t
1 CONC o lead the nation's largest urban public university. His ap
1 CONC pointment is effective on September 6, 1999.
1 CONT Matthew Goldstein has served as President of Adelphi Univer
1 CONC sity since June 1998.
1 CONT From September 1991 to June 1998 he was President of Baruc
1 CONC h College/CUNY. Prior to that, he was President of the Rese
1 CONC arch Foundation of The City University of New York from 198
1 CONC 2 until 1991.In addition, he was the Acting Vice Chancello
1 CONC r for Academic Affairs for the University from December 199
1 CONC 0 until September 1991; and before that, he held faculty po
1 CONC sitions in mathematics and statistics at a number of differ
1 CONC ent universities. He has been a professor of Statistics a
1 CONC t Baruch College and a member of the CUNY doctoral facult
1 CONC y since 1978.
1 CONT He was a member of the Research and Development Subcommitte
1 CONC e for the Governor's Conference Committee on Science and Te
1 CONC chnology and Governor-Elect George Pataki's Higher Educatio
1 CONC n Transition Task Force. In addition, he served on the Advi
1 CONC sory Council on Economic Information and Research of the Ne
1 CONC w York State Department of Economic Development, on the Ne
1 CONC w York State Senate Higher Education Committee's Advisory C
1 CONC ommittee, on the Board of Directors and Research Policy Com
1 CONC mittee of CORETECH (the Council on Research and Technology)
1 CONC , and on the New York City Partnership's Technology Executi
1 CONC ve Council.
1 CONT Currently, he serves as a member of the Board of Trustees o
1 CONC fthe Albert Einstein School of Medicine, and of the Bronx-L
1 CONC ebanon Hospital Center; he is a Director of Health-Chem Cor
1 CONC poration, and a former Director of Audits and Surveys World
1 CONC wide; an ex-officio Trustee of the Jean Cocteau Repertory
1 CONC ; a member of the American Council on Education's Commissio
1 CONC n on Leadership Development, and was recently appointed t
1 CONC o the New York State Education Commissioner's Advisory Coun
1 CONC cil on Higher Education.
1 CONT In October of 1994 he was the recipient of the Jewish Natio
1 CONC nal Fund Tree of Life Award. He is a member of Beta Gamma S
1 CONC igma and the Golden Key Honor Society.
1 CONT Dr. Goldstein's research specialty is discrete multivariat
1 CONC e analysis, particularly the modeling of cross-classified c
1 CONC ategorical arrays and discrimination procedures, applying t
1 CONC he data analytic procedures to natural, behavioral and soci
1 CONC al science problems. He is the co-author of three books o
1 CONC n statistical theory and methods and author or co-author o
1 CONC f numerous published articles in leading journals in applie
1 CONC d and mathematicalstatistics.
1 CONT Dr. Goldstein earned his bachelor's degree from The City Co
1 CONC llege of New York/CUNY, his master's degree from Rutgers Un
1 CONC iversity and his doctorate in mathematical statistics fro
1 CONC m the University of Connecticut.
1 CONT Born on the Lower East Side of Manhattan, he was raised i
1 CONC n Manhattan and Brooklyn. He is married to Maggi Sedlis, a
1 CONC n architect, and has two sons, Brian and Seth from a previo
1 CONC us marriage.

1 CONC Among the leading merchants of Winchester may be classed th
1 CONC e Hainsfurther Brothers. They have been reared in the busin
1 CONC ess and from early childhood merchandising has been almos
1 CONC t a second nature with them. The name Hainsfurther is almos
1 CONC t a household word in Scott county. Way back yonder in 185
1 CONC 9 Moses and Solomon Hainsfurther embarked in business i
1 CONC n a moderate way in the Chapman building on the south sid
1 CONC e of the square. For nine years they dealt with the peopl
1 CONC e from this building and then moved to the Flynn building o
1 CONC n the west side of the square, where they remained for thir
1 CONC teen years. Their trade increased and with that increase ca
1 CONC me the increase in stock, and, in order to obtain more room
1 CONC , rented two rooms in the Smith building, just south of the
1 CONC ir old stand and opened out with a large stock of dry goods
1 CONC , carpets and notions in one room and boots, shoes and clot
1 CONC hing in the other. In 1890 they built a new store buildin
1 CONC g (now occupied by Sol Hainsfurther as a shoe store) and us
1 CONC ed it exclusively for clothing, hats, caps and gents furnis
1 CONC hing goods. The business thus divided into three department
1 CONC s they made many improvements and added largely to their st
1 CONC ock. In June, 1889, they purchased the shoe business of Dan
1 CONC iel Smith, on the north side of the square and for a year c
1 CONC onducted business there in conjunction with their other sto
1 CONC res, but finally merged the stock into their own store on t
1 CONC he west side. After the fire, in 1895, they, sharing with D
1 CONC aniel Smith, erected a splendid two story building, and whe
1 CONC n completed, moved their goods and occupied the entire buil
1 CONC ding. During this same year (1895) they incorporated unde
1 CONC r the name of the Hainsfurther Mercantile Co., with the fol
1 CONC lowing incorporators: Solomon Hainsfurther, Sr., Moses Hain
1 CONC sfurther, Albert Hainsfurther, David Hainsfurther and Solom
1 CONC on Hainsfurther, Jr. This continued until February, 1901, w
1 CONC hen the corporation ceased to exist and the shoe departmen
1 CONC t was sold to Solomon Hainsfurther, Jr., and the clothing
1 CONC , furnishings and dry goods departments to Albert and Davi
1 CONC d Hainsfurther.
1 CONT
1 CONT Solomon Hainsfurther, father of the Hainsfurther brothers
1 CONC , was born in Ichenhausen, Germany, December 22, 1824, an
1 CONC d came to the United States in 1850, and to Winchester, Sco
1 CONC tt county, Illinois, 1857. June 2, 1855, he was married t
1 CONC o Miss Yetta Kirschausen, daughter of Benedict Kirschausen
1 CONC , and to them seven children, four girls and three boys, we
1 CONC re born. One of the sons is dead. Solomon Hainsfurther die
1 CONC d July 14, 1900.

1 CONC ed it exclusively for clothing, hats, caps and gents furnis
1 CONC hing goods. The business thus divided into three department
1 CONC s they made many improvements and added largely to their st
1 CONC ock. In June, 1889, they purchased the shoe business of Dan
1 CONC iel Smith, on the north side of the square and for a year c
1 CONC onducted business there in conjunction with their other sto
1 CONC res, but finally merged the stock into their own store on t
1 CONC he west side. After the fire, in 1895, they, sharing with D
1 CONC aniel Smith, erected a splendid two story building, and whe
1 CONC n completed, moved their goods and occupied the entire buil
1 CONC ding. During this same year (1895) they incorporated unde
1 CONC r the name of the Hainsfurther Mercantile Co., with the fol
1 CONC lowing incorporators: Solomon Hainsfurther, Sr., Moses Hain
1 CONC sfurther, Albert Hainsfurther, David Hainsfurther and Solom
1 CONC on Hainsfurther, Jr. This continued until February, 1901, w
1 CONC hen the corporation ceased to exist and the shoe departmen
1 CONC t was sold to Solomon Hainsfurther, Jr., and the clothing
1 CONC , furnishings and dry goods departments to Albert and Davi
1 CONC d Hainsfurther.

1 CONC Among the leading merchants of Winchester may be classed th
1 CONC e Hainsfurther Brothers. They have been reared in the busin
1 CONC ess and from early childhood merchandising has been almos
1 CONC t a second nature with them. The name Hainsfurther is almos
1 CONC t a household word in Scott county. Way back yonder in 185
1 CONC 9 Moses and Solomon Hainsfurther embarked in business i
1 CONC n a moderate way in the Chapman building on the south sid
1 CONC e of the square. For nine years they dealt with the peopl
1 CONC e from this building and then moved to the Flynn building o
1 CONC n the west side of the square, where they remained for thir
1 CONC teen years. Their trade increased and with that increase ca
1 CONC me the increase in stock, and, in order to obtain more room
1 CONC , rented two rooms in the Smith building, just south of the
1 CONC ir old stand and opened out with a large stock of dry goods
1 CONC , carpets and notions in one room and boots, shoes and clot
1 CONC hing in the other. In 1890 they built a new store buildin
1 CONC g (now occupied by Sol Hainsfurther as a shoe store) and us
1 CONC ed it exclusively for clothing, hats, caps and gents furnis
1 CONC hing goods. The business thus divided into three department
1 CONC s they made many improvements and added largely to their st
1 CONC ock. In June, 1889, they purchased the shoe business of Dan
1 CONC iel Smith, on the north side of the square and for a year c
1 CONC onducted business there in conjunction with their other sto
1 CONC res, but finally merged the stock into their own store on t
1 CONC he west side. After the fire, in 1895, they, sharing with D
1 CONC aniel Smith, erected a splendid two story building, and whe
1 CONC n completed, moved their goods and occupied the entire buil
1 CONC ding. During this same year (1895) they incorporated unde
1 CONC r the name of the Hainsfurther Mercantile Co., with the fol
1 CONC lowing incorporators: Solomon Hainsfurther, Sr., Moses Hain
1 CONC sfurther, Albert Hainsfurther, David Hainsfurther and Solom
1 CONC on Hainsfurther, Jr. This continued until February, 1901, w
1 CONC hen the corporation ceased to exist and the shoe departmen
1 CONC t was sold to Solomon Hainsfurther, Jr., and the clothing
1 CONC , furnishings and dry goods departments to Albert and Davi
1 CONC d Hainsfurther.

1 CONT The Park City News - Thursday, December 7, 2006
1 CONT
1 CONT Meredith Ann Hainsfurther is the daughter of Mr. & Mrs. A
1 CONC . Michael Hainsfurther,  She is the granddaughter of Mr
1 CONC . & Mrs. Bob Peterson of Dallas, and Mr. & Mrs. Albert Hain
1 CONC sfurther of Winchester, Illinois.  Meredith was a Duchess o
1 CONC f the La Fiesta de las Seis Banderas.  She has two brothers
1 CONC , Nick and Joes.
1 CONT
1 CONT A Highland Park High School graduate, Meredith is currentl
1 CONC y a junior at Texas A&M, pursuing a major in Accounting.  S
1 CONC he is the Philanthropy chair at Kappa Kappa Gamma, serves a
1 CONC s a Greek Ambassador, and teaches Sunday School at Grace Bi
1 CONC ble Church.  Meredith was recently selected for the Mays Bu
1 CONC siness School Fellows Program.  She also participates wit
1 CONC h Aggies in Mission, the Abbott Family Leadership Conferenc
1 CONC e, and has spent her summers studying abroad in Italy and a
1 CONC s a counselor at Camp Ozark.
1 CONT
1 CONT Meredith likes to spend time with friends, who she says se
1 CONC e her as outgoing, happy, determined, and passionate.  Sh
1 CONC e is an avid runner, completing the Nashville Marathon wit
1 CONC h her dad in 2004.  When asked what one thing she would lik
1 CONC e to change about the world, Meredith said she wished "ever
1 CONC yone knew of Christ's love."
1 CONT
1 CONT The Dallas Symphony Orchestra League is proud to present 20
1 CONC 07 Debutante, Meredith Ann Hainsfurther.
1 CONT

1 CONT Movie: Father of the Bride
1 CONT Song: Defying Gravity - Wicked
1 CONT City: Sorrento, Italy
1 CONT Restaurant: The Mansion

1 CONC r beautiful lips, speak only words of kindness; and for poi
1 CONC se, walk with the knowledge that you are never alone."  Aud
1 CONC rey Hepburn

1 CONT 001454ŮCtabŮDHart - Van Eaton Hart, a retired denim manufactu
1 CONC rer in Montgomery, Ala., died March 17 in
1 CONT Montgomery. He was 78. Mr. Hart was born in Deridder and li
1 CONC ved In Montgomery for almost 50 years.
1 CONT
1 CONT He was a graduate of Isidore Newman School and Tulane Unive
1 CONC rsity.
1 CONT
1 CONT He was a Lieutenant In the Navy during World War Il.
1 CONT
1 CONT Survivors Include his wife, Carol Lobman Hart; two Sons, Ju
1 CONC lian Hart of Washington, D.C., and Van E. Hart Jr. of San F
1 CONC rancisco; two Daughters, Michal Hart Hillman and Alice H. W
1 CONC ertheim, both of Atlanta; two brothers, Carol B. And Elli
1 CONC s T. Hart of Winona, Miss.; and eight grandchildren.
1 CONT
1 CONT A Memorial Service Was Held March 19 In Montgomery. 04-02-1

1 CONC American Club of the Costa del Sol ŮCiŮDŮCtabŮDŮCtabŮDŮCtabŮD
1 CONT Written by Ann McGeehan
1 CONT Tuesday, 13 April 2010 ŮCtabŮDŮCtabŮD
1 CONT
1 CONT Of special mention is the passing of one of our own very de
1 CONC ar and lifetime members. Lillian Matthews died in her ninet
1 CONC ies after having lived a very full, wonderful life which in
1 CONC cluded such incredible memories as being an American Club f
1 CONC ounder, an incredible bridge player and a loving wife and m
1 CONC other, grandmother, and great-grandmother. She was truly a
1 CONC n amazing lady. Many of us dearly miss Lillian and share fo
1 CONC nd memories of how over 40 years, she and her husband Sydne
1 CONC y organized parties, bridge conferences and bridge cruises
1 CONC . Because of her efforts in so many areas, she was consider
1 CONC ed an Ambassador for the Costa del Sol and the Bridge Clu
1 CONC b affectionately referred to Lillian as ŮCbŮD"The Bridge Quee
1 CONC n." ŮC/bŮDŮC/iŮD In order too appreciate more of the significan
1 CONC t contributions Lillian made to the American Club and to Ma
1 CONC rbella greater society, please kindly refer to the accompan
1 CONC ying celebration of some of the details of her life, a spec
1 CONC ial feature prepared together with this month's newslette
1 CONC r especially for you, our members. Lillian's star burns bri
1 CONC ghtly among us all.

1 CONC t in the stock market and held extensive interests in manuf
1 CONC acturing securities.

1 CONC New York Times
1 CONT Published: Thursday, January 28, 1988
1 CONT Obituary - Section B, Page 5, New York editionŮCbŮD
1 CONT
1 CONT Mab Moltke Dies at 78; Ex-Publicity Executive
1 CONT ŮC/bŮDMab Wilson Moltke, a former advertising and publicity e
1 CONC xecutive in New York, died Jan. 1 at her home in Blackrock
1 CONC , County Dublin, after a long illness. She was 78 years ol
1 CONC d and had moved to Ireland in 1970.
1 CONT Mrs. Moltke, a native of San Francisco, began her career i
1 CONC n New York with Saks Fifth Avenue and Vogue. She was the ma
1 CONC gazine's beauty editor in 1941 when Bergdorf Goodman appoin
1 CONC ted her advertising director.
1 CONT In World War II, she went overseas for the Office of War In
1 CONC formation. Posted in Stockholm, she met Count Carl Adam Mol
1 CONC tke, a member of the Danish underground, who became her sec
1 CONC ond husband in 1944.
1 CONT Mrs. Moltke, known professionally as Mab Wilson, was direct
1 CONC or of publicity at Tiffany & Company in 1968 when she rejoi

1 CONC The following biography is from the American National Biogr
1 CONC aphy, published by Oxford University Press. Copyright 199
1 CONC 9 ACLS.
1 CONT
1 CONT Zacharias, Ellis Mark (1 Jan. 1890-28 June 1961), naval off
1 CONC icer, was born in Jacksonville, Florida, the son of Alron Z
1 CONC acharias, a tobacco farmer, and Theresa Budwig. A graduat
1 CONC e of the U.S. Naval Academy in 1912, Zacharias began a four
1 CONC -decade-long career in which he distinguished himself as a
1 CONC n intelligence officer and as one of the navy's foremost au
1 CONC thorities on Japan. Zacharias's interest in Japanese langua
1 CONC ge and culture began at the academy and led to his appointm
1 CONC ent to Tokyo in 1920 as assistant naval attache. During hi
1 CONC s three-year tour in Japan Zacharias became fluent in Japan
1 CONC ese. He also became an enthusiastic student of the craft o
1 CONC f intelligence. Although most officers preferred sea duty o
1 CONC r other staff positions to service in the Office of Naval I
1 CONC ntelligence (ONI), Zacharias eagerly pursued a career as a
1 CONC n intelligence specialist. After a brief assignment as a co
1 CONC mmunications intelligence officer on the USS Huron (Nov.-De
1 CONC c. 1923), he served in several unrelated posts at sea and o
1 CONC n shore in Latin America and with the Asiatic Fleet. In 192
1 CONC 4, while he was on duty in the Panama Canal Zone, he met an
1 CONC d married Clara Evans Miller, an aviator. They had two sons
1 CONC , both of whom served in the navy. In 1928 he returned t
1 CONC o a brief but controversial tour in Tokyo. Accused by the n
1 CONC aval attache of trying to usurp his authority during a temp
1 CONC orary illness, Zacharias returned to the United States to b
1 CONC ecome chief of the Far Eastern Division of ONI until 1931
1 CONC . Following two years of sea duty Zacharias completed the s
1 CONC enior course at the Naval War College and returned to ONI a
1 CONC s head of the Far East Division (1934-1936). Following mor
1 CONC e sea duty he resumed his intelligence work, this time as d
1 CONC istrict intelligence officer in the Eleventh Naval District
1 CONC , San Diego (1938-1940). Zacharias commanded the heavy crui
1 CONC ser USS Salt Lake City during the early months of the war a
1 CONC gainst Japan and earned a Letter of Commendation for the bo
1 CONC mbardment of Wotje Island in the Marshall Islands. In Jun
1 CONC e 1942 he left the Pacific to become deputy director of ONI
1 CONC , an assignment he believed to be a preliminary to his appo
1 CONC intment as director. By this time Zacharias had built a rep
1 CONC utation as the navy's best intelligence officer, but he ha
1 CONC d his detractors as well. Impatient with those who did no
1 CONC t share his vision for ONI, Zacharias feuded with colleague
1 CONC s and ran afoul of J. Edgar Hoover, director of the Federa
1 CONC l Bureau of Investigation (FBI). Zacharias's difficulties w
1 CONC ith other intelligence officials probably explain his bein
1 CONC g passed over for the coveted director's position in favo
1 CONC r of the inexperienced but bureaucratically competent Capta
1 CONC in Harold Train. Disappointed by this turn of events, Zacha
1 CONC rias nevertheless continued his efforts to reinvigorate th
1 CONC e navy's sluggish intelligence agency. Emphasizing aggressi
1 CONC ve action, he moved ONI into the areas of propaganda, psych
1 CONC ological warfare, counterespionage, and operational intelli
1 CONC gence. Of particular significance was the Special Warfare o
1 CONC r "W" branch, which produced the successful Commander Norde
1 CONC n broadcasts aimed at demoralizing German U-boat crews. Zac
1 CONC harias's efforts to reform ONI once again brought him int
1 CONC o conflict with his fellow officers, including Captain Trai
1 CONC n, who believed that Zacharias was after his job. Zacharia
1 CONC s was removed as deputy director in August 1943. He spent t
1 CONC he next year in the Pacific as commander of the battleshi
1 CONC p USS New Mexico and returned to the states as chief of sta
1 CONC ff to the commandant of the Eleventh Naval District in Octo
1 CONC ber 1944. In April 1945 Secretary of the Navy James Forrest
1 CONC al brought Zacharias to Washington for what was to be his m
1 CONC ost controversial assignment. Working out of the Office o
1 CONC f War Information, and identifying himself as an official s
1 CONC pokesman of the U.S. government, Zacharias made fourteen br
1 CONC oadcasts, in both Japanese and English, in which he attempt
1 CONC ed to convince the Japanese high command to surrender. In t
1 CONC he twelfth of these, Zacharias implied that if the Japanes
1 CONC e surrendered the emperor would be spared. Although many U.
1 CONC S. officials had privately concluded that they would need t
1 CONC o use the emperor to ensure the surrender of Japanese troop
1 CONC s, President Harry S. Truman opposed any public modificatio
1 CONC n of the unconditional surrender formula. Consequently, Zac
1 CONC harias was instructed to drop the words "official spokesman
1 CONC " from his remaining broadcasts. The impact of Zacharias'
1 CONC s broadcasts on Japan's surrender is difficult to assess. N
1 CONC evertheless, the controversial Zacharias maintained that th
1 CONC e Japanese could have been persuaded to surrender without t
1 CONC he use of the atomic bombs. In 1946 he published Secret Mis
1 CONC sions, a memoir of his intelligence exploits, and retired w
1 CONC ith the rank of rear admiral. In retirement he wrote Behin
1 CONC d Closed Doors: The Secret History of the Cold War (1950)
1 CONC , a plea for a more aggressive psychological warfare campai
1 CONC gn in the Cold War, and contributed articles to United Nati
1 CONC ons World, Readers Digest, and Saturday Evening Post. He su
1 CONC pported the United Nations and criticized reports that it w
1 CONC as being used as a cover for hundreds of foreign agents ope
1 CONC rating in the United States, and he opposed supporting an
1 CONC y dictatorship in China, whether Nationalist or Communist
1 CONC . He died in West Springfield, New Hampshire.
1 CONT
1 CONT Bibliography There is no biography of Zacharias, but Jeffer
1 CONC y M. Dorwart, Conflict of Duty: The U.S. Navy's Intelligenc
1 CONC e Dilemma, 1919-1945 (1983), contains the most authoritativ
1 CONC e secondary account of his intelligence career. See also Ro
1 CONC bert J. C. Butow, Japan's Decision to Surrender (1954), an
1 CONC d Marc Gallicchio, The Cold War Begins in Asia: American Ea
1 CONC st Asian Policy and the Fall of the Japanese Empire (1988).
1 CONT
1 CONT An obituary is in the New York Times, 29 June 1961.

1 CONC Rebecca Solomons Alexander (1854-1938) was born in Charlest
1 CONC on, South Carolina, and spent much of her youth in Savannah
1 CONC . Following her marriage to Julius Mortimer Alexander, sh
1 CONC e moved to Atlanta where she remained for the rest of her l
1 CONC ife. In her memoir she recalled several incidents during th
1 CONC e Civil War, including the following:
1 CONT
1 CONT  "All talk was of war and as if it were yesterday I remembe
1 CONC r being told the war had started - 12 April when Anderson w
1 CONC as fired upon at Fort Sumter. It was Saturday and my nurs
1 CONC e was putting the finishing touches to my toilet for Synago
1 CONC gue and I was waiting for my cousin Nellie, Uncle Joe's dau
1 CONC ghter and my boon companion who always came for me and wen
1 CONC t to services together as she came in eight I called to he
1 CONC r 'Nellie the war has begun' upon which she called up to m
1 CONC e 'then I am going straight back home.' I did not want to r
1 CONC un into danger either, but my gentle mother re-assured me
1 CONC , and together she and I went to pray 'that the South woul
1 CONC d win.
1 CONT '
1 CONT Rebecca Solomons Alexander was the first president of the A
1 CONC tlanta Section, National Council of Jewish Women, and remai
1 CONC ned in that position from 1895-1902. Under her direction, t
1 CONC he section was actively involved with the founding of the A
1 CONC tlanta Free Kindergarten Association that was later house
1 CONC d at the Jewish Educational Alliance. She also establishe
1 CONC d a Sabbath School on Decatur Street for the children of ne
1 CONC wly arriving immigrants.

1 CONC Obituary: Wessick, Betty (Peabody)
1 CONT Nov 08, 2000
1 CONT Betty (Peabody) Wessick, 72, of Third Beach Road, Middletow
1 CONC n, died November 8, 2000 at home. She was the wife of Rober
1 CONC t E. Wessick for 49 years. Born in Newport on September 2
1 CONC , 1928, he was the daughter of the late Restcom Peabody an
1 CONC d the late Marguerite (Beebe) Peabody. She was a descendan
1 CONC t of the founders of Newport and at the 350th celebration o
1 CONC f the founding of the city she signed the book for the desc
1 CONC endants of the founder. She was a graduate of the Sargent C
1 CONC ollege in Boston University, Class of 1950. Prior to her ma
1 CONC rriage she taught at St. Michaels School of Newport as a Ph
1 CONC ysical Education teacher. During the 1970s and 1980's she a
1 CONC ssisted her mother in the operations of Peabody's Third Bea
1 CONC ch. She was resident of Natick, Mass. for 15 years prior t
1 CONC o returning to Middletown in 1970. She was a member of th
1 CONC e Middletown Republican Town Committee, a Life Member of th
1 CONC e Middletown Historical Society and the Friends of the Midd
1 CONC letown Library, member of the Norman Bird Sanctuary, a form
1 CONC er member of the RI Republican State Central Committee, th
1 CONC e Preservation Society of Newport County, and a member of t
1 CONC he Wanumet onomy Golf and Country Club. She was also know
1 CONC n as an avid gardener. She is survived by her husband Rober
1 CONC t E. Wessick of Middletown; two daughters Cynthia L. W. Coh
1 CONC en of Orlando, FL and Polly L. Wessick of Warwick and Middl
1 CONC etown; one grandson Theodore W. Cohen; and her brother Rest
1 CONC com E. Peabody, Jr. of Middletown. She was preceded in deat
1 CONC h by her son Theodore P. Wessick. Funeral Services were hel
1 CONC d in the St. Columba Berkeley Memorial Chapel, Vaucluse Ave
1 CONC nue Middletown. Burial followed in Middletown Cemetery, Tur
1 CONC ner and Wyatt Roads, Middletown. Donations in her memory ma
1 CONC y be made to Hospice Care of Rhode Island, 143 Main Street
1 CONC , Wakefield, RI 02879 or to the Robert Potter League for An
1 CONC imals, PO Box 412, Newport, RI 02840

1 CONC David Behrend was born in 1792 in Hanover and came to Engla
1 CONC nd in 1809. He seems to have arrived in London penniless
1 CONC . A German nephew of his recounts that during a visit to En
1 CONC gland in 1856, his uncle showed him in one of the slums o
1 CONC f the City of London the "most miserable of all houses wher
1 CONC e he had once as a hapless youth at the beginning of the ce
1 CONC ntury found shelter at night with money obtained by begging
1 CONC ." He may have been in Liverpool by 1812, and he was soon p
1 CONC rosperous enough to rent a seat in Liverpool's Seel Stree
1 CONC t Synagogue. In 1816/17 he is on the books of the Liverpoo
1 CONC l Philanthropic Society as a donor, not a recipient. He ma
1 CONC y have already been working for Charles Bahr, a Dane, who h
1 CONC ad established a ship-broking agency (i.e. an agency arrang
1 CONC ing the shipment of goods). In 1835, he became a partner. T
1 CONC he firm of Bahr Behrend exists to this day in Liverpool.
1 CONT David married twice: Priscilla Aaron of Liverpool in 1823 a
1 CONC nd, two years after her death, in 1835, Maria Hess, who bel
1 CONC onged to a leading Liverpool family. He had two sons by Pri
1 CONC scilla, George Henry and Henry Michael, and one son by Mari
1 CONC a, Samuel Hesse. David was Senior Treasurer of the Old Hebr
1 CONC ew Congregation's Seel Street Synagogue in 1845-46 and Seni
1 CONC or Warden twice: 1851-52, 1855-57.
