1 CONC Childrens' Hospital of Wisconsin
1 CONT HEALTH MATTERS - Volume 10, Issue 2
1 CONT January, 2007
1 CONT
1 CONT ŮCbŮDS. Bert Litwin, MD
1 CONT ŮCiŮDMaryanne Kessel, RN, MBA,ŮC/bŮD director, Herma Heart Cent
1 CONC er, Children's Hospital of Wisconsin.ŮC/iŮD
1 CONT This month we say farewell to S. Bert Litwin, MD, after mor
1 CONC e than three decades of caring for the children of Wisconsi
1 CONC n. He has done this both through his skill as a leading car
1 CONC diothoracic surgeon and as a philanthropist. From his homet
1 CONC own of Jasper, Ala., to home base of Children's Hospital o
1 CONC f Wisconsin, Dr. Litwin helped thousands of children live w
1 CONC ith heart disease and defects.
1 CONT Dr. Litwin earned his undergraduate degree at the Universit
1 CONC y of Alabama and his medical degree from Harvard Medical Sc
1 CONC hool, Boston. He completed his clinical and research fellow
1 CONC ship in Surgery at Harvard Medical School and Massachusett
1 CONC s General Hospital in Boston, with a postdoctoral fellowshi
1 CONC p at the National Heart Institute, National Institutes of H
1 CONC ealth.
1 CONT In 1968 he became a clinical instructor in Surgery at Harva
1 CONC rd Medical School and assistant in Cardiovascular Surgery a
1 CONC t Children's Hospital Medical Center in Boston. In 1968 an
1 CONC d 1969 he was certified by the American Board of Surgery an
1 CONC d American Board of Thoracic Surgery.
1 CONT In 1972, Dr. Litwin came to Milwaukee to accept positions a
1 CONC s director of Cardiovascular Surgery at Children's Hospita
1 CONC l of Wisconsin and associate professor of Surgery at the Me
1 CONC dical College of Wisconsin. He has since served as chief o
1 CONC f staff at Children's Hospital and professor of Surgery a
1 CONC t the Medical College.
1 CONT Before his arrival, most children with heart disease and de
1 CONC fects simply could not be cared for at Children's Hospita
1 CONC l of Wisconsin.
1 CONT Dr. Litwin has continued to foster the growth of the progra
1 CONC m he began 34 years ago. He has held the position of medica
1 CONC l director emeritus, Cardiothoracic Surgery, for the Herm
1 CONC a Heart Center. The center was established in 2003 when Car
1 CONC diology and Cardiothoracic Surgery were formally combined i
1 CONC nto one integrated center to provide seamless care for chil
1 CONC dren with congenital heart disease and their families. Herm
1 CONC a Heart Center now is recognized as a leading pediatric hea
1 CONC rt program in the nation. The center's excellence in hear
1 CONC t care is one of many factors that has led Children's Hospi
1 CONC tal of Wisconsin to be recognized nationally as well, thank
1 CONC s to the foundation of the cardiac program developed by Dr
1 CONC . Litwin.
1 CONT Dr. Litwin's dedication to the children who turn to Childre
1 CONC n's Hospital of Wisconsin for care goes far beyond the oper
1 CONC ating room. He has been an advocate and champion of all asp
1 CONC ects of growth of the Children's Hospital and Health Syste
1 CONC m as it has expanded care throughout the state and region
1 CONC . We wish him well. He will be dearly missed.

1 CONC "Sol" Hainsfurther, as he is known by nearly everybody in t
1 CONC he county, is a native of Scott county, and was born at Win
1 CONC chester, October 20, 1869. He is a son of Moses and Harrie
1 CONC t (Strauss) Hainsfurther. His father was born in Ichenhause
1 CONC n, Germany, March 3, 1823, and came to this country in 1848
1 CONC , and to Winchester in 1857. In 1855 he was married to Harr
1 CONC iet Strauss and to them nine children have been born, fou
1 CONC r girls and one boy now living. Sol was educated in the Win
1 CONC chester schools and at St. Louis. After he came out of scho
1 CONC ol he worked for three years at Osage, Iowa, and in 1889 re
1 CONC turned to Winchester and entered the employ of M. & S. Hain
1 CONC sfurther, taking charge of the shoe department. When the Ha
1 CONC insfurther Mercantile Company was organized in 1895, he wa
1 CONC s a member of that corporation and remained there until tha
1 CONC t company went out of business in 1901, at which time he pu
1 CONC rchased the shoe stock of the Hainsfurther company and move
1 CONC d it to the building he now occupies, and today has one o
1 CONC f the most neatly appointed and best arranged shoe stores t
1 CONC o be found in this section of Illinois. Mr. Hainsfurther i
1 CONC s thoroughly conversant with his business and is giving i
1 CONC t his undivided attention.
1 CONT Solomon Hainsfurther and Miss Victoria Sachen, daughter o
1 CONC f Albert and Julia Sachen, were married at Chicago, April 1
1 CONC 0, 1895. Mrs. Hainsfurther was born in New York City, Decem
1 CONC ber 3, 1869. They have two children, (twins) Herbert and Be
1 CONC atrice. Mr. Hainsfurther is a Mason, an Odd Fellow, a K. o
1 CONC f P., a Woodman, a Rebekah and a D. O. K. K. His church mem
1 CONC bership is with the synagogue at Quincy. Though but a youn
1 CONC g man Sol Hainsfurther has proven himself and readily take
1 CONC s rank with the responsible, progressive business men of th
1 CONC e county.

1 CONT On the evening of Oct. 25, 1983, an inebriated 16- year-ol
1 CONC d drove head-on into a car driven by Atlanta architect Ceci
1 CONC l Alexander. Alexander was badly injured; his wife, Hermion
1 CONC e Weil Alexander, who was a passenger, died. As a tribute t
1 CONC o Mrs. Alexander, who was active in civic, philanthropic, a
1 CONC nd cultural activities in Atlanta and was the first femal
1 CONC e jury commissioner for Fulton County, the county commissio
1 CONC n named a bridge- Hermi's Bridge-  in her honor. This was e
1 CONC specially fitting as the Alexanders had been instrumental i
1 CONC n preserving the historic bridge when it had been threatene
1 CONC d with demolition nearly a decade prior to the accident.

1 CONC The Atlanta Journal-Constitution, by H.M. Cauley, Decembe
1 CONC r 9, 2004.
1 CONT
1 CONT "Helen Alexander is a woman with a wild imagination. From t
1 CONC he curves, dots and dashes that make up stenography, she ca
1 CONC me up with a line of plush toys that almost defy descriptio
1 CONC n."
1 CONT In 1939, at the Pierre Hotel in New York City, Helen Eisema
1 CONC nn Alexander began designing a collection of doodlies now k
1 CONC nown as Squinkles Ă. At age 18, Helen was a budding Broadwa
1 CONC y actress, taking a shorthand course in Manhattan, New York
1 CONC . Back in the Stone Age, when administrative assistants sti
1 CONC ll wrote everything by hand, Helen studied shorthand as a b
1 CONC ackup to her acting aspirations. When she practiced the sho
1 CONC rthand symbols, Helen began daydreaming and doodling. Thos
1 CONC e doodles turned into almost 100 fanciful offbeat stuffed a
1 CONC nimals for adults and children called Squinkles. Six decade
1 CONC s later, after as many years of studying, including at th
1 CONC e American Academy of Dramatic Arts, and acting on Broadwa
1 CONC y and in films and television under her stage name, Helen E
1 CONC astman, and raising a big family, Helen has brought severa
1 CONC l of those drawings to life. Children love these sweet, hug
1 CONC gable critters. The multi-limbed, big-lipped, bug-eyed crea
1 CONC tions come in a rainbow of colors. Adults enjoy their sass
1 CONC y attitudes and the poems included on the tag. And everyon
1 CONC e is tickled by their fanciful names.

1 CONT ARNOLD RICE RICH was born in Birmingham, Alabama in 1893 an
1 CONC d died in 1968 in Baltimore, Maryland shortly after his sev
1 CONC enty-fifth birthday. He was the second of two children i
1 CONC n a prosperous Southern family. His father, the owner o
1 CONC f a mercantile business, allowed Arnold a free choice in de
1 CONC ciding his future. Arnold's older sister, now Mrs. Leonel R
1 CONC . Weil of Montgomery, Alabama, never ceased to show interes
1 CONC t in his career. After elementary school education in Birmi
1 CONC ngham, Arnold was sent to a military preparatory academy
1 CONC -    The Bingham School-    in North Carolina. Whereas thi
1 CONC s military training did little to change Arnold's inheren
1 CONC t dislike of regimentation and all forms of physical exerci
1 CONC se, it probably was responsible for his erect bearing and s
1 CONC lim figure. His trim appearance was pleasing in spite of hi
1 CONC s perpetual pallor, which mirrored a sedentary life. His mo
1 CONC st characteristic expression was a quizzical smile, whethe
1 CONC r in accord or dispute with his companions. Following his p
1 CONC reparatory school education, Arnold entered the Universit
1 CONC y of Virginia. An elastic curriculum permitted a free choic
1 CONC e of any number of subjects at one time and Rich, after a s
1 CONC hort sojourn of only two years, was given his A.B., and on
1 CONC e year later his M.A. degree, and was elected, as well, t
1 CONC o Phi Beta Kappa. While at college, Rich had considered bec
1 CONC oming a min- ing engineer, but since he detested mathematic
1 CONC s, his alternative choice for a career was biology. His Mas
1 CONC ter's research, carried out in the zoology department at th
1 CONC e University of Virginia, concerned the reactions of the pr
1 CONC obiscus of a flatworm ŮCiŮD(Planaria albissima ŮC/iŮDVejdovsky)
1 CONC ; this work, completed in Virginia, was published during Ri
1 CONC ch's third year at medical school. Rich entered the Johns H
1 CONC opkins Medical School in the fall of 1915 and received hi
1 CONC s M.D. in 1919 along with membership in Alpha Omega Alpha
1 CONC . He was associated with Hopkins for the remainder of his l
1 CONC ife. His specialization in pathology was accidental. In med
1 CONC ical school he came under the influence of Dr. William H. H
1 CONC owell and was fascinated by this extraordinary scholar whos
1 CONC e interest at that time was coagulation of the blood. Ric
1 CONC h was soon immersed in related research projects; his findi
1 CONC ngs on the "Nature of Metathrombin" and the "Changes in th
1 CONC e Clotting Power of Oxalated Plasma on Standing" (see bibli
1 CONC ography, 1917) were published while he was still a medica
1 CONC l student. He did not allow the school curriculum to interf
1 CONC ere with his research to any great degree. One unexpected i
1 CONC nterruption did occur because of the participation of the U
1 CONC nited States in World War I. The medical students, in the f
1 CONC all of 1918, were regimented into the Johns Hopkins Unit o
1 CONC f the Students Army Training Corps and because of Rich's pr
1 CONC evious military training, he was made a sergeant. Althoug
1 CONC h the military regime did not hinder his pursuit of knowled
1 CONC ge or further any athletic development, the war did chang
1 CONC e Rich's interest from theory to more practical medical pro
1 CONC blems; he therefore decided to specialize in experimental s
1 CONC urgery. To this end, Dr. William Halsted, the Professor o
1 CONC f Surgery, insisted that Rich devote himself to pathology f
1 CONC or a year as preparation for a surgical internship. It wa
1 CONC s thus that Rich came under the influence of Dr. William G
1 CONC . MacCallum, and surgery lost its brilliant prospect to pat
1 CONC hology. Except for a sabbatical year studying with Dr. Han
1 CONC s Eppinger in Vienna, Rich remained in the Hopkins Patholog
1 CONC y Department for his entire career. Flattering invitation
1 CONC s from other institutions were always refused. Rich loved w
1 CONC orking at Johns Hopkins and living in Baltimore. He was app
1 CONC ointed Professor of Pathology in 1944, and in 1947 he becam
1 CONC e the third Baxley Professor of Pathology, Chairman of th
1 CONC e Pathology Department, and Pathologist-in-chief of the Joh
1 CONC ns Hopkins Hospital, adding his distinguished services to t
1 CONC hose of his predecessors, Drs. William H. Welch and Willia
1 CONC m G. MacCallum. Although Rich became Professor Emeritus i
1 CONC n 1958, he retained his interest in pathology until his dea
1 CONC th in 1968, which followed a long illness beset by cardiova
1 CONC scular complications. I first met Arnold Rich when I wa
1 CONC s a medical student. I was fortunate to have had him for m
1 CONC y instructor, and he always remained my preceptor, associat
1 CONC e, and friend. Few could resist his enthusiastic teaching
1 CONC . His contagious interest in the study of disease changed m
1 CONC any of his students into embryonic pathologists. To his stu
1 CONC dents he embodied the ideal teacher whose standard was exce
1 CONC llence in all spheres. This he did by example: Rich taugh
1 CONC t superbly and lectured brilliantly, vividly describing hi
1 CONC s material in his soft, slightly Southern-tinged tones. Hi
1 CONC s meticulous autopsy dissections, similarly accompanied b
1 CONC y flowing lucid analyses, always drew a large audience of s
1 CONC tudents and staff. Rich's influence was felt throughout th
1 CONC e medical school and hospital, and he was consulted by memb
1 CONC ers of all departments. This, in spite of the fact that a
1 CONC s a careful and meticulous worker himself he might seem ove
1 CONC r-critical and discouraging. If Dr. Rich approved your wor
1 CONC k and encouraged its publication, you were assured of its w
1 CONC orthiness; but he was ruthless in red-penciling observation
1 CONC s he considered incomplete, equivocal, uncontrolled, or uni
1 CONC mportant. Rich's power of critical evaluation was especiall
1 CONC y apparent at the weekly Journal Club meetings of the patho
1 CONC logy department. 334 BIOGRAPHICAL MEMOIRS A junior staff me
1 CONC mber would report and criticize the original contribution
1 CONC s in a specific journal, but it was Dr. Rich who always mad
1 CONC e the pertinent comments on the value of each. To underscor
1 CONC e his evaluations, he delighted in arguing ŮCiŮDagainst ŮC/iŮDh
1 CONC is true opinion to develop perspicacity of judgment in hi
1 CONC s young staff members. Rich's critical ability was furthe
1 CONC r appreciated and utilized as a member of the editorial boa
1 CONC rd of the ŮCiŮDBulletin of the Johns Hopkins Hospital ŮC/iŮDan
1 CONC d as a member of the Hopkins Research Society. His opinion
1 CONC s were sought not only by colleagues, but by former associa
1 CONC tes in advanced positions in other institutions. A tribut
1 CONC e to Dr. Rich's popularity was the overflowing audience o
1 CONC f students and staff that attended his weekly clinicalpatho
1 CONC logical conferences (CPC), probably the most popular hour i
1 CONC n the school curriculum. Dr. William Thayer was his first c
1 CONC linical opponent, then for many years Dr. Louis Hamman, an
1 CONC d finally Dr. A. McGehee Harvey. Each of these clinicians a
1 CONC dded a personal delightful variation to the session. It wa
1 CONC s Dr. Rich's function to select the cases to be shown, an
1 CONC d these were always instructive. In addition, Rich delighte
1 CONC d to choose patients who could illustrate a hitherto unreco
1 CONC gnized problem or lesion. Many of the CPC cases were subseq
1 CONC uently published and frequently/ served as a basis for rese
1 CONC arch. It was in his research that Rich made his greatest im
1 CONC pact on the field of pathology. He was instrumental in inte
1 CONC resting his students and young pathology staff members in h
1 CONC is work and utilized their aid in his extensive experimenta
1 CONC l investigations. His first important contribution elucidat
1 CONC ed the origin of bilirubin and the bile pigments. This impo
1 CONC rtant physiological process had previously been poorly unde
1 CONC rstood and controversial. Rich's studies culminated in hi
1 CONC s classic review, "Formation of Bile Pigment," for the ŮCiŮDP
1 CONC hysiological Reviews ŮC/iŮD(1925). In this he concluded tha
1 CONC t hemoglobin, derived from destroyed red blood cells, is th
1 CONC e sole source of bile pigment; its normal site of origin i
1 CONC s in reticuloendothelial cells alone, especially the Kupffe
1 CONC r cells, and the epithelial liver cells have no role in th
1 CONC e formation, but only in the excretion of the bile pigment
1 CONC . Additional studies in this field resulted in Rich's delin
1 CONC eation of jaundice into two types on the basis of pathogene
1 CONC sis. The first, retention jaundice, results from overproduc
1 CONC tion of bile pigment in conditions that are associated wit
1 CONC h a decrease in the excretory power of the liver, such as i
1 CONC mmaturity, anoxemia, and fever. The second type-    regurgi
1 CONC tation jaundice-    is caused by reflux of bile from the li
1 CONC ver canaliculi into the blood stream in the presence of duc
1 CONC t obstruction or liver cell necrosis. Published in 1930, th
1 CONC is work remains, with only slight modification, the basis f
1 CONC or the present concept of jaundice, its clinical diagnosis
1 CONC , and its treatment. Dr. Rich's next consuming interest wa
1 CONC s in the field of inflammation and hypersensitivity, especi
1 CONC ally as related to tuberculous infection. This motivated hi
1 CONC s investigations for many years. With the assistance of sev
1 CONC eral co-workers, he was able to demonstrate that acquired r
1 CONC esistance in the host is independent of the hypersensitiv
1 CONC e inflammatory reaction, and the latter, injurious to the h
1 CONC ost, may be eliminated by desensitization without impairmen
1 CONC t of immunity. These findings were summarized in the ŮCiŮDPhy
1 CONC siological Reviews ŮC/iŮDin 1941. Continued research clarifie
1 CONC d the pathogenesis of the spread of the tubercle bacilli i
1 CONC n the body and revolutionized the concept of the disease "t
1 CONC uberculosis" and its myriad manifestations. These monumenta
1 CONC l studies were published as a deservedly famous book, ŮCiŮDTh
1 CONC e Pathogenesis of Tuberculosis, ŮC/iŮDin 1944, revised in 195
1 CONC 1 and subsequently translated into Spanish and Japanese. Ex
1 CONC tensive investigation of the mechanisms of hypersensitivit
1 CONC y and immunity led to additional knowledge in pneumococca
1 CONC l infection and syphilis. But of greatest importance was Ri
1 CONC ch's demonstration that the lesions of periarteritis nodosa
1 CONC , rheumatic carditis and pneumonitis, and some forms of glo
1 CONC merulonephritis 336 BIOGRAPHICAL MEMOIRS were caused by th
1 CONC e anaphylactic type of hypersensitivity. This work was inst
1 CONC rumental in stimulating other scholars to investigate the b
1 CONC asic mechanisms of the pernicious interactions of antigen a
1 CONC nd antibody which produce disease in the human body. Durin
1 CONC g Rich's long career, in addition to basic research, he mad
1 CONC e numerous important observations in the field of pathologi
1 CONC cal anatomy and histology and clarified the pathogenesis o
1 CONC f previously poorly understood conditions. With his colleag
1 CONC ues, he demonstrated that portal cirrhosis in rabbits coul
1 CONC d result from repair following liver cell necrosis caused b
1 CONC y a diet deficient in vitamins B1; B2, B6, and nicotinic ac
1 CONC id. By time-lapse cinemicrography of cells ŮCiŮDin vitro, ŮC/i
1 CONC ŮDhe first depicted the characteristic locomotion and natur
1 CONC e of the "acute splenic tumor" cell. He helped clarify th
1 CONC e pathogenesis of acute hemorrhagic pancreatitis by provin
1 CONC g that activation of trypsinogen by enterokinase is not a n
1 CONC ecessary step in the production of this lesion, which may r
1 CONC esult therefore from liberation of unactivated pancreatic e
1 CONC nzymes in the parenchyma following rupture of ductules. Ric
1 CONC h showed the relation of the "tubular" lesions of the adren
1 CONC al cortex to acute infection, described a peculiar focal in
1 CONC terstitial form of nephritis that may occur in acquired syp
1 CONC hilis, and first noted the obstructing pulmonary arteriola
1 CONC r lesions that occur in tetralogy of Fallot. His descriptio
1 CONC n of idiopathic interstitial fibrosis of the lungs was mad
1 CONC e in conjunction with the clinical observations of Dr. Loui
1 CONC s Hamman, and this condition now bears the name "Rich-Hamma
1 CONC n disease." Dr. Rich was the recipient of many honors. In 1
1 CONC 954 he was elected a member of the National Academy of Scie
1 CONC nces. Prior to that time he was a consultant to the Chemica
1 CONC l Warfare Service, to the Surgeon General of the United Sta
1 CONC tes Army, and to the Tuberculosis Control Division of the U
1 CONC nited States Public Health Service. In 1947, Rich receive
1 CONC d the certificate of honor of the American Academy of Tuber
1 CONC culosis. In 1951, France gave him its top award, making hi
1 CONC m a Chevalier of the Legion of Honor. He was a committee me
1 CONC mber of the National Research Council from 1947 to 1952. I
1 CONC n 1951, Rich became the Chairman of the Scientific Advisor
1 CONC y Board of the Armed Forces Institutes of Pathology. He wa
1 CONC s the U.S. Department of State delegate to the Internationa
1 CONC l Congress of Allergy, Zurich, at which time he was grante
1 CONC d an honorary M.D. by the University of Zurich and was mad
1 CONC e a fellow of the International Association of Allergists
1 CONC . Further honors followed. In 1952, Rich was appointed an h
1 CONC onorary committee member to celebrate the 50th Anniversar
1 CONC y of the discovery of anaphylaxis in Paris. The Universit
1 CONC y of Toronto honored him in 1956 as the man who during th
1 CONC e preceding ten years had contributed most toward practica
1 CONC l knowledge in medical arts and science. In this year he al
1 CONC so became an honorary fellow of the British Royal Society o
1 CONC f Medicine. The Kober Medal of the Association of America
1 CONC n Physicians was presented to Rich in 1958. Even in his ret
1 CONC irement, Rich continued to receive further honors. He was m
1 CONC ade an honorary Mickle fellow of the University of Toronto
1 CONC , and the Gardner award was given him at this University fo
1 CONC r his research on the allergic effects of certain drugs. H
1 CONC e was presented the Gordon Wilson Medal by the American Cli
1 CONC nical and Climatological Association, the Trudeau Medal b
1 CONC y the National Tuberculosis Association, an honorary plaqu
1 CONC e by the Japanese Society of Tuberculosis, the Seaman awar
1 CONC d by the Association of Military Surgeons, and an award b
1 CONC y the American College of Physicians. The stupendous number
1 CONC s of honors and awards received by Rich for his work in med
1 CONC ical science might suggest that his interests were confine
1 CONC d to this field. Not so! His talents were notable in many d
1 CONC iverse directions, and it is difficult to separate his scie
1 CONC ntific from his personal life. Rich met his future wife, He
1 CONC len Jones, in 1915, while still at the University of Virgin
1 CONC ia, through a mutual interest in music. Miss Jones continue
1 CONC d her musical education and career and did not marry Dr. Ri
1 CONC ch until 1925. Mrs. Rich remains a talented pianist and com
1 CONC poser. There are two daughters and five grandchildren. Th
1 CONC e elder daughter is Adrienne Rich, the famous poetess who
1 CONC , to date, has received twenty prizes and awards for her pu
1 CONC blished works. Cynthia Rich Glauber, four years younger, wr
1 CONC ites and also teaches creative writing at Harvard Universit
1 CONC y. Both girls received a unique, unconventional classical e
1 CONC ducation from their parents. The Baltimore census did not d
1 CONC iscover their existence until Adrienne was nearly eleven an
1 CONC d Cynthia seven. At this point, the truant officers of th
1 CONC e Baltimore School System insisted they attend conventiona
1 CONC l school. Their placement in a proper class was not facilit
1 CONC ated by their ignorance of mathematics, their fluency in la
1 CONC nguages-    modern and classical-    and a remarkable appre
1 CONC ciation of history, art, and world conditions. The Rich hos
1 CONC pitality was delightful. Dr. Rich enjoyed entertaining hi
1 CONC s staff members and held many of the Pathology Journal Clu
1 CONC b meetings in his attractive home. Once the discussion of c
1 CONC urrent journals was completed, Mrs. Rich, aided by her daug
1 CONC hters, would treat our group to delicious homemade cakes an
1 CONC d potent punch (a secret recipe) and join in the general co
1 CONC nversation. Often an informal musicale would follow with Mr
1 CONC s. Rich at the piano, Dr. Rich playing his violin or viol
1 CONC a d'amore, and a junior staff member playing a cello or a w
1 CONC ood instrument. These sessions were delightful and lasted w
1 CONC ell into the night: invariably the pathology staff would ar
1 CONC rive late for work the following morning. This passed unnot
1 CONC iced by Rich, who abhorred the early morning and whenever p
1 CONC ossible arrived for work near noon, but remained in his off
1 CONC ice or laboratory until any hour at night. He enjoyed detai
1 CONC ning an associate with him to discuss, in an informal manne
1 CONC r, current problems in the department, music, literature, p
1 CONC olitics, or ethics. Time would pass heedlessly while famili
1 CONC es at home awaited a delayed supper. The scope of Dr. Rich'
1 CONC s interest was unlimited, and he did not limit the time h
1 CONC e devoted to others. Probably the two main nonscientific co
1 CONC ncerns of Rich were music and literature. He was a member o
1 CONC f the Chamber Music group that included the late "Bard of B
1 CONC altimore," H. L. Mencken, and he enjoyed as well the rich m
1 CONC usical environment offered by the City of Baltimore. He rea
1 CONC d voraciously in the classics, English, and foreign literat
1 CONC ure. His sharp critical ability was evident in his analyse
1 CONC s of modern writings. He himself wrote with ease, and altho
1 CONC ugh his compositions seemed as uncontrived and fluent as hi
1 CONC s speech, he admitted that he rewrote every sentence innume
1 CONC rable times before satisfied of its clarity. He did thoroug
1 CONC h research in any subject that drew his interest; his studi
1 CONC es on the "Source of the Nile" were almost as extensive a
1 CONC s those of Alan Moorehead. One further quality in his writi
1 CONC ng must be added: he had a delightful sense of humor. Thi
1 CONC s was always apparent in his original presentations, and wa
1 CONC s exemplified by an elfin twinkle as he read a treatise suc
1 CONC h as "In Defense of the Double Bed." Rich was modest, but h
1 CONC is vision was wide and clear. He was a free thinker and i
1 CONC n two fields was known as a nonconformist. In an era of spe
1 CONC cialization in the medical sciences, Rich advocated compreh
1 CONC ensive knowledge without splintering of activities. His int
1 CONC erest in pathology was universal, enveloping all facets o
1 CONC f disease. He did not limit his studies to a specific sex
1 CONC , age, or portion of the body. He was proud to be a "genera
1 CONC l" pathologist. Rich's nonconformity was apparent in a seco
1 CONC nd direction. His stimulus to work was love of work; moneta
1 CONC ry rewards were unimportant to him. He urged this precept o
1 CONC n his juniors, but unfortunately lost many a staff member w
1 CONC ho was unable to survive on the meager salary provided by R
1 CONC ich. Government grants were anathema. He would not conside
1 CONC r applying for outside funding which might necessitate modi
1 CONC fication of his work or its direction. As a result, the pat
1 CONC hology department, supported entirely by Johns Hopkins fund
1 CONC s, remained small during Rich's tenure. This was in keepin
1 CONC g with the Rich precepts of quality and excellence which in
1 CONC fluenced not only his immediate associates but also sprea
1 CONC d far afield to other institutions and countries. He was re
1 CONC sponsible for directing many promising students into the sp
1 CONC ecialty of pathology. Arnold Rice Rich will be remembered b
1 CONC y them and by his peers in this field as a great pathologis
1 CONC t.

1 CONT Published: January 4, 1991, Page A19
1 CONT Irving M. Riffin Dies; Anesthesiologist, 77ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT
1 CONT Dr. Irving M. Riffin, a family doctor and anesthesiologis
1 CONC t for almost 50 years, died on Friday at a vacation home i
1 CONC n Boca Raton, Fla. He was 77 years old and lived in Montcla
1 CONC ir, N.J.
1 CONT He died of congestive heart failure, his daughter Jane said
1 CONC .
1 CONT Dr. Riffin graduated from New York University Medical Schoo
1 CONC l in 1940 and served with the Army Medical Corps, primaril
1 CONC y in Europe, during world War II.
1 CONT He was in private practice in Montclair and was affiliate
1 CONC d with the former St. Vincent's Hospital there, where he on
1 CONC ce headed the department of anesthesiology. He was a forme
1 CONC r president of the New Jersey State Society of Anesthesiolo
1 CONC gy.
1 CONT Besides his daughter, Jane Susswein of Montclair, the finan
1 CONC cial vice president of the Federation of Reconstructionis
1 CONC t Congregations and Havurot, he is survived by his wife, Ba
1 CONC bette, a former member of the Montclair Board of Education
1 CONC ; another daughter, Ann, who disappeared in 1982 while o
1 CONC n a painting vacation in Mora, N.M.; a brother, Dan, of Liv
1 CONC ingston, N.J.; a sister, Rosalie Sussman of Bethesda, Md.
1 CONC , and two grandchildren.

1 CONT BRIGNAC Janet Perrault Brignac died on Sunday, March 28, 20
1 CONC 10 at home after a lengthy illness. Age 73 years. Beloved w
1 CONC ife of Edmond M. Brignac, Jr. for fifty years. Mother of Mi
1 CONC chele Brignac, DVM, Aimee Brignac, Denise Brignac Smith an
1 CONC d Nicole Brignac Barnes (Donnie). Daughter of Aimee Boudoi
1 CONC n Perrault and the late Joseph A. Perrault. Sister of Josep
1 CONC h A. Perrault, Jr. Grandmother of Wilson Smith, Corinne Smi
1 CONC th, GiGi Smith, Aimee Barnes, Langley Barnes, Trey Barnes a

1 CONC h a special interest in microtones. He graduated with honor
1 CONC s from Lawrence University and Conservatory with a BA in En
1 CONC glish and a BM in theory/ comp from the studio of Joanne Me
1 CONC tcalf, and completed his masters in composition at the Univ
1 CONC ersity of Buffalo studying with Cort Lippe and Jeffrey Stad
1 CONC elman. In the fall he will attend University of Minnesota o
1 CONC n a fellowship working towards his Ph.D in composition.He w
1 CONC as a runner up for the Hicks Prize for poetry in 2003 for "
1 CONC Charcoal Woman" and was a participant in the Interlochen Co
1 CONC mposer's Institute in 2006. He has studied composition wit
1 CONC h Philippe Bodin, Jason Hoogerhyde, Joanne Metcalf, Cort Li
1 CONC ppe, and Jeff Stadelman; he studied percussion with Dane Ri
1 CONC cheson. He was an editor for the literary magazine Tropos a
1 CONC nd the editor-in-chief of the literary magazine The Wit. H
1 CONC e was a five year long member of LUPE, a percussion ensembl
1 CONC e that plays chamber music as well as the traditional musi
1 CONC c of Africa, Brazil, and Cuba.

1 CONT  You are viewing page ŮCbŮD1ŮC/bŮD
1 CONT
1 CONT Carol Bernard Hart, a lifelong New Orleans attorney, socia
1 CONC l activist and leader of Jewish causes, passed away Sunday
1 CONC , Sept. 6, 2009. He was 83.
1 CONT He is survived by his adoring wife, Ricka Brown Hart of Atl
1 CONC anta; two children, Richard Hart, 54, a journalist, of Durh
1 CONC am, N.C., and Susan Sandler, 52, a social worker, of Atlant
1 CONC a; their spouses, Sally Hicks, a journalist, and Rabbi Nei
1 CONC l Sandler of Ahavath Achim Congregation in Atlanta; five gr
1 CONC andchildren, Lillie Hart, 16, Isaac Hart, 11, Ariel Sandler
1 CONC , 23, Aliza Sandler, 21, and Joshua Sandler, 19; a brother
1 CONC , Ellis Hart of Lake Caroline, Miss.; a sister-in-law, Glyn
1 CONC n Brown of New Orleans; and more than 40 nieces, nephews, g
1 CONC reat-nieces and nephews, and great-great nieces and nephews
1 CONC .
1 CONT Carol Hart was born in Beaumont, Texas, to Lillie Oppenhei
1 CONC m Hart and Van Eaton Hart, on October 11, 1925, the younges
1 CONC t of four sons; his brothers were Julian, Ellis and Van. Th
1 CONC eir father died when Carol was an infant, and Lillie move
1 CONC d to New Orleans, where she found work in a department stor
1 CONC e. The three youngest boys were placed in the Jewish Childr
1 CONC en's Home there to be raised and cared for. Carol and his t
1 CONC wo brothers remained in the Jewish home through high schoo
1 CONC l and attended Isidore Newman School, which was founded t
1 CONC o serve the home.
1 CONT He attended Tulane University and received degrees in busin
1 CONC ess administration and law while working fulltime as a spor
1 CONC tswriter at The Times-Picayune and then as Tulane's Sport
1 CONC s Publicity Director, editing the Tulane football program
1 CONC , the "Greenie." After graduating in 1948, he went into pri
1 CONC vate practice as an attorney.
1 CONT He married Ricka Brown on June 20, 1953. His family was hi
1 CONC s passion, but equally important in his life were the wellb
1 CONC eing of New Orleans and all its residents, and giving bac
1 CONC k to the Jewish community that raised him. In 1978, he ra
1 CONC n citywide for judge on the Louisiana state Court of Appea
1 CONC l after being outraged to hear that former D.A. Jim Garriso
1 CONC n was running for the spot. He was active in the Louisian
1 CONC a Coalition against Racism and Nazism to stop the electora
1 CONC l efforts of David Duke, and volunteered on a number of oth
1 CONC er political campaigns. He pursued pro bono the case of Cor
1 CONC dell Lee, the son of his children's caregiver, who was wron
1 CONC gly on Louisiana's death row, and helped gain his freedom.
1 CONT He loved good food, traveling, and making new friends. In t
1 CONC he 1990s, he saw the Petoskey (Mich.) High School Steel Dru
1 CONC m Band perform while on vacation there and dedicated the ne
1 CONC xt year to bringing them to New Orleans to play at Mardi Gr
1 CONC as.
1 CONT He was president of the Jewish Children's Regional Servic
1 CONC e (the successor to the Jewish Children's Home), presiden
1 CONC t of the Southern Jewish Historical Society, president of t
1 CONC he New Orleans B'nai B'rith Council, president of the advis
1 CONC ory board of Tulane-Newcomb Hillel Foundation, vice-preside
1 CONC nt of B'nai B'rith of Louisiana, member of the board of Isi

1 CONC Rabbi Neil Sandler is a native of Minneapolis, Minnesota wh
1 CONC ere his family was an active member of the Adath Jeshurun C
1 CONC ongregation. His warm experiences in the congregation, espe
1 CONC cially when he served as a leader in USY, contributed direc
1 CONC tly to his eventual decision to enter the rabbinate.
1 CONT Rabbi Sandler is a graduate of the University of Minnesot
1 CONC a where he majored in Hebrew Language and Literature. His f
1 CONC inal year of undergraduate study was spent in Jerusalem a
1 CONC t The Hebrew University. Thereafter, Rabbi Sandler entere
1 CONC d the Jewish Theological Seminary from which he receive
1 CONC d a Master's degree and Rabbinic Ordination. Upon his ordin
1 CONC ation, Rabbi Sandler completed a Master of Science degree i
1 CONC n Social Work at Columbia University. In his more than twen
1 CONC ty years of service to the Jewish community Rabbi Sandler h
1 CONC as served congregations in Richardson, Texas, St. Louis, Mi
1 CONC ssouri, Des Moines, Iowa and Marlboro, New Jersey. He gaine
1 CONC d his greatest contentment in the rabbinate from the sacre
1 CONC d moments he has shared with his congregants in their time
1 CONC s of joy and sorrow. Rabbi Sandler has also been actively i
1 CONC nvolved in Camp Ramah, the camp of the Conservative Movemen
1 CONC t. For many years, he and his wife served on the staff of C
1 CONC amp Ramah in Wisconsin.
1 CONT Rabbi Sandler is married to Susan Hart Sandler, a social wo
1 CONC rker who has concentrated on Jewish communal service. Nei
1 CONC l and Susan are the parents of Ariel, a student at the Univ
1 CONC ersity of Wisconsin, Aliza and Joshua, both high school stu
1 CONC dents.
1 CONT As a Conservative congregation, Ahavath Achim Synagogue str
1 CONC ives to support its members in attaining a meaningful commi
1 CONC tment to Jewish life and tradition in the 21st Century. Rab
1 CONC bi Sandler's desire is to nurture our caring and egalitaria
1 CONC n religious community to deepen its commitment to Jewish le
1 CONC arning, living, and values

1 CONC leveland Ohio.  A dinner and reception followed the ceremon
1 CONC y. The bride wore a beautiful Patau model of ivory satin wi
1 CONC th enveloping tulle veil and shower of lilies of the valle
1 CONC y and orchids.  Mrs Joseph Sharwell, mother of the bride, w
1 CONC as the matron of honor and wore a smart model of gold crep
1 CONC e combined with brown and matching accessories.  Miss Ina L
1 CONC esser was the maid of honor and was frocked to an eggshel
1 CONC l satin combined with American Beauty crepe and Carried a
1 CONC n armful of roses.  Michael Arnovitz was best man and the u
1 CONC shers included Gabriel Leeb and the following brothers of t
1 CONC he bride: Edwin, Sherman and Raymond Sharwell

1 CONC Max R. Dendy PERRYTON - Max R. Dendy, 70, died Sunday, Marc
1 CONC h 16, 2003, in Amarillo. Services will be at 2 p.m. today i
1 CONC n First Christian Church with Dr. Jeff Knighton and the Rev
1 CONC . O.K. Bowen officiating. Burial will be in Ochiltree Cemet
1 CONC ery by Boxwell Brothers Funeral Home. Mr. Dendy was a lifet
1 CONC ime resident and farmer in the Perryton area. He was a memb
1 CONC er of First Christian Church of Perryton. He married his fi
1 CONC rst wife, Christeen Mae Records in 1950 at Clayton, N.M., a
1 CONC nd she passed away in 1973. He then married Karyn Jean Wrig
1 CONC ht in 1974 at Perryton. He served in the Navy from 1951 t
1 CONC o 1955 and was stationed in Bethesda, Md., San Diego and ha
1 CONC d a tour of Japan. He was a charter member of Elks Lodge an
1 CONC d VFW. He was preceded in death by his parents, John Troy a
1 CONC nd Treva Mary Dendy; his first wife and the mother of his c
1 CONC hildren in 1973; and a son-in-law, Sam Lilly. Survivors inc
1 CONC lude his wife; three daughters, Connie Lilly of Perryton, a
1 CONC nd Mary Lou Batlan and Sally Brackett, both of Austin; a so
1 CONC n, Donnie Dendy of Perryton; a brother, Fred Dendy and wife
1 CONC , Linda, of Perryton; a grandson, Jason Lilly of Perryton
1 CONC ; four granddaughters, Rayne Lilly of Amarillo, and Rebecc
1 CONC a Batlan, Katharine Batlan and Ashley Brackett, all of Aust
1 CONC in; and a great-grandson, Tatum Lilly of Perryton. The fami
1 CONC ly suggests memorials be to Ochiltree Hospice, 3101 Garret
1 CONC t Drive, Perryton, TX 79070; or American Cancer Society, 24
1 CONC 05 Utica Lane, Perryton, TX 79070. Amarillo Globe-News, Mar
1 CONC ch 18, 2003

1 CONT Dan (Dino) Glattstein, age 79, passed away on Sunday, Novem
1 CONC ber 26, 2006 at 1:30 a.m.
1 CONT
1 CONT He was a member of Congregation Adath Jeshurun and the Jewi
1 CONC sh Community Center.
1 CONT
1 CONT A Holocaust survivor, Dan knew no bitterness but blessed th
1 CONC e lives of his innumerable friends with his understanding
1 CONC , humor, compassion and ability to live life to its fullest
1 CONC . He enriched the lives of all who were privileged to kno
1 CONC w him.
1 CONT
1 CONT He is survived by: his wife, Gila; his son Uri (Marilyn); a
1 CONC nd grandchildren, David and Emily Glattstein.
1 CONT
1 CONT He was preceded in death by his son, Roni.
1 CONT
1 CONT The funeral will take place on Tuesday, November 28, 2006
1 CONC , at 2 p.m. at the funeral home. Visitation will begin afte
1 CONC r 1 p.m. Tuesday.
1 CONT
1 CONT Expressions of sympathy may be given to one of the followin
1 CONC g: Yad Vashem: The Holocaust Martyrs' and Heroes' Remembran
1 CONC ce Authority Museum in Israel, the United States Holocaus
1 CONC t Memorial Museum in Washington, D.C. or to a charity of on
1 CONC e's choice.

1 CONC ELLEN B. SEDLIS '74, 49, of Florence, died Wednesday (7-11-
1 CONC 01) at Cooley Dickinson Hospital in Northampton followin
1 CONC g a lengthy illness. Sedlis was founder, shelter-services c
1 CONC oordinator and board member of the New England Learning Cen
1 CONC ter for Women in Transition in Greenfield. Born in Boston
1 CONC , June 28, 1952, she was the daughter of Herbert and Mildre
1 CONC d Sedlis of Boca Raton, Fla., and the late Sumner C. Sneier
1 CONC son. She grew up in Boston, was educated in Wayland school
1 CONC s and graduated from Wayland High School. She moved to th
1 CONC e Pioneer Valley to attend the University of Massachusett
1 CONC s at Amherst in 1970. She graduated cum laude in 1974 fro
1 CONC m UMass with a bachelor's degree concentrating on psycholog
1 CONC y and social change. SH earned a master's degree in socia
1 CONC l work in 1989 from Boston University, receiving the Kolodn
1 CONC y Prize for Academic Excellence. Sedlis also was coordinato
1 CONC r of the Sexual Abuse Intervention Project for the Departme
1 CONC nt of Social Services in the Franklin-Hampshire area office
1 CONC s and worked as an intern at the Northampton Center for Chi
1 CONC ldren and Families. Most recently, she worked as a clinicia
1 CONC n at the Children's Clinic, affiliated with the Northampto
1 CONC n Center for Children and Families and as a psychotherapis
1 CONC t in private practice, specializing in work with adolescent
1 CONC s. She had lived in the Pioneer Valley for most of the las
1 CONC t 30 years, with brief stays in Barrington, RI, while worki
1 CONC ng in Swansea, and in San Francisco. She was a member of th
1 CONC e Jewish Community of Amherst and also attended services a
1 CONC t Beit Ahavah in Florence. For the past four years, she wa
1 CONC s involved in the Hampshire County Relay for Life, this yea
1 CONC r serving as an honorary co-chairwoman and leading the open
1 CONC ing lap undertaken by cancer survivors. Besides her parents
1 CONC , she leaves her husband, Allan Doe; a son, Samuel Thomas D
1 CONC oe, and a daughter, Michaela Gray Doe, both at home; a brot
1 CONC her, Steven Sedlis of Cambridge; a sister, Shari Sedlis Bea
1 CONC l of Bay Harbor Island, FL; a paternal grandmother, Rose Se
1 CONC dlis Beal of Bay Harbor, and nieces and nephews. . .

3 CONC , Switzerland, graduated from Choate Rosemary Hall and fro
3 CONC m Sarah Lawrence College with a B.A. degree in art history
3 CONC . The future bride, a jewelry designer for ZEBE Ltd. in Ne
3 CONC w York and Los Angeles, formerly was an assistant and late
3 CONC r a cataloguer in the old master's painting department fo
3 CONC r Sotheby's in New York. Her father, who is retired, was a
3 CONC n independent commercial real-estate developer in New Yor
3 CONC k and Connecticut.
3 CONT Mr. Salinger, an actor professionally known as Matt Salinge
3 CONC r, is currently appearing in the movie ''The Revenge of th
3 CONC e Nerds.'' He graduated from Phillips Andover Academy, atte
3 CONC nded Princeton University and received a B.A. degree in ar
3 CONC t history and drama from Columbia University. His father, t
3 CONC he writer, is author of ''The Catcher in the Rye,'' publish
3 CONC ed in 1951 by Little Brown & Company. His mother is a psych
3 CONC ologist in San Francisco.

2 NOTE  Live Oak Cemetery This cemetery is between Dallas Avenue a
3 CONC nd Selma Avenue in Selma, Dallas Co., AL. From Broad Street
3 CONC , turn west on Dallas Avenue. Turn left onto King Street in
3 CONC to the cemetery, which lies on either side of this street
3 CONC . The portion east of King street, called "Old Live Oak Cem
3 CONC etery," was reserved as a cemetery in 1829. The western por
3 CONC tion, called "Live Oak Cemetery," was purchased by the cit
3 CONC y of Selma in 1877 from Sallie Ann Jones. Most people cal
3 CONC l the entire cemetery "Old Live Oak Cemetery" as there i
3 CONC s a newer cemetery a few miles down the road called "New Li
3 CONC ve Oak Cemetery." The cemetery office is in New Live Oak an
3 CONC d the people there are happy to help you find a grave. Th
3 CONC e original eastern part of the cemetery, called the West Se
3 CONC lma Cemetery in the mid-1800s, was laid out haphazardly, t
3 CONC o the dismay of many of the townspeople. By 1865 the Selm
3 CONC a City Council passed an ordinance calling the cemetery "
3 CONC a nuisance" and prohibited any more burials. Burials were t
3 CONC o be done in the East Selma Cemetery built in 1855 and main
3 CONC tained by the city. It was used predominately for area slav
3 CONC es who died in a small pox epidemic in 1860. Some confedera
3 CONC te soldiers are buried there. In 1858 the city council deci
3 CONC ded that West Selma Cemetery should be resotred and the cem
3 CONC etery put "in good order." The city added to the cemetery w
3 CONC hen it purchased the land in 1877. The western section is l
3 CONC aid out neatly by sections, blocks, and rows with roads an
3 CONC d walkways, making it easy to walk around and easy to fin
3 CONC d the occupants
